question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of a women's health strategy.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out any assessment of the economic impact of Ulster University's decision to cut 450 jobs from its workforce.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she was made aware that Ulster University had decided to cut 108 jobs from its Magee campus.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration their Department has taken, with Executive colleagues, of the wider economic and regional implications of proposed job losses at Ulster University.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with Executive colleagues regarding supporting the delivery of regional balance, in light of proposed job losses at a key institution in the North West.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support that will be made available to staff who may be affected by redundancy at Ulster University, including access to retraining and employment support programmes.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether any assessment has been made on the absence of a long-term funding model for higher education contributing to the current financial pressures facing Ulster University.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has engaged with the Minister for the Economy regarding the financial sustainability of universities in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In order to inform the development of a draft multi-year Budget, I met the Minister for the Economy to discuss the full range of budgetary pressures within her Department. This included discussion around funding pressures facing the university sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact that staffing reductions at Ulster University could have on the planned expansion of its Magee campus.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department has considered to provide emergency financial support for higher education institutions facing financial pressures.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with Ulster University regarding its proposed staff redundancy scheme, including any steps taken to prevent job losses.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress made in developing a sustainable higher education funding model for Northern Ireland, including a timeline for its delivery.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the potential economic impact of proposed job losses at Ulster University, particularly in the North West.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the drafting of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will be completed.
answer_text: The drafting of the Employment Rights Bill will be completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has given any consideration to mirroring the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has given any consideration to the introduction of paid climate leave, similar to that which has recently been introduced in Spain.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has sought Executive agreement on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to update health and safety standards within the workplace.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the renovation project at the Triangle Estate in the Foyle constituency, including a review of the original proposals.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that it has carried out extensive remedial works to Waterside Triangle through the Response Maintenance Contract, to alleviate the water ingress issues. The original brief for the Planned Maintenance Scheme has since been reviewed with new proposals outlined to provide a long-term solution for the external elements of the properties. NIHE has engaged with the appointed Consultant to review and agree on the revised proposals, and these proposals are currently going through internal approvals.Subject to the necessary approvals, the works are expected to be tendered in the summer. Subject to a successful procurement exercise and obtaining relevant approvals, commencement is anticipated in the early part of 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any action he is taking to increase the budget for the Mobuoy Remediation Project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the drafting of the Good Jobs Employments Rights Bill will be completed.
answer_text: The Employment Rights Bill is currently being drafted by the Office of the Legislative Counsel and this will be completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme contribution will be increased to 20 per cent.
answer_text: I have extended the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%, however, as the Budgets for 2026-27, including the final allocation for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, have not yet been set by the Executive,  I am not in a position to provide any commitments to increase the percentage contribution for the NICSS.Any decision regarding future increases will be considered in the context of the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the PSNI, in relation to the granting of greater powers to address street drinking.
answer_text: Street drinking can have a significant impact on communities, contributing to anti-social behaviour (ASB) and public disorder in some areas.Responses to a joint DoJ and Department for Communities (DfC) public consultation on ASB legislation, including issues of street drinking, were published in April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform. Since then, I have been in direct communication with the Chief Constable of the PSNI and other stakeholders seeking views on proposals developed with DfC exploring the potential of powers allowing for the seizure and disposal of open and closed containers of alcoholic drinks in designated areas and in certain public order situations. As drinking in public is a cross-cutting issue, my Department and DfC will continue to work with one another, and with the PSNI and other key partners to determine next steps towards identifying a proportionate and workable solution, with a view to bringing forward legislation in the next assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of legislative proposals from her Department, which are awaiting Executive approval.
answer_text: The Executive has agreed a total of five Department for the Economy (DfE) Bills for inclusion in the 2025–26 legislative programme.All five Bills have secured Executive policy agreement:Renewable Heat Incentive (RHI) Bill;Onshore Petroleum Exploration and Licensing Bill;Insolvency (Amendment) Bill;Employment Rights Bill; and Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of visits Invest NI hosted which originated in Derry in the last five years.
answer_text: The term originated, in Invest NI records, refers to the source market of the visiting company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has engaged with Invest NI in relation to how they define remote working when measuring jobs created.
answer_text: At my meeting with the Economy Committee in January and in recent AQWs, I committed to keeping Invest NI’s targets and, by extension, associated definitions, under review.  I understand this was also discussed at your recent meeting with the Invest NI Chief Executive and his colleagues, at which they restated this commitment.		Invest NI is currently developing its next Business Strategy, due to go live on 1 April 2027.  As part of this, Invest NI is reviewing its approach to remote working investments. 		My officials and I will work closely with Invest NI to agree a final position on its key strategic targets as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Local Economic Partnerships.
answer_text: All eleven Local Economic Partnerships have submitted Action Plans and Letters of Offer have issued to ten Partnerships with the remaining one expected to issue in the coming week. Departmental and Invest NI officials continue to work closely with and support all the partnerships in the delivery of their Action Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any immediate steps he is taking to ensure that the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is not discontinued in April 2026.
answer_text: The continuation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme into 2026/27 was announced on 11 March: Education Minister announces continuation of Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme | Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 40898/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has undertaken an assessment of her Department's budget for 2025-26, in regard to the continued expansion of Ulster University Magee campus.
answer_text: The Magee expansion is a priority for me and this is reflected in my 2025/26 budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: Drafting of the Bill continues and, when complete, the draft Bill will be brought to the Executive for agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: Further to my response to your Assembly question on 25 February 2026, I can confirm that the £150 million Enhanced Investment Zone programme has now received British Government Ministerial approval.The focus is now on delivery, with work underway to secure and finalise individual project approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. I have received the business case for the enhanced services and will consider these as part of my budget decisions on the Department’s Final Budget for the coming year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Investment Strategy.
answer_text: The Executive is focused on delivery, on improving lives, on supporting economic growth and on ensuring that our infrastructure meets the needs of our people.  A significant amount of work has already gone into shaping the draft Investment Strategy.  Alongside this, considerable work is also progressing on the enabling actions needed to tackle some of the barriers to delivery and ensure we are well placed to realise the ambition that will be set out in the strategy.Once the strategy is finalised, the timeline for approval and publication will then be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will carry out a traffic light assessment of the Bradley’s Pass junction, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to make direct contact with your office to discuss the traffic signal functionality at the Bradley’s Pass junction in more detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to increase the availability of respite provision for carers of children with severe learning disabilities, within the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. A number of the recommendations from Professor Jones’ Review relate to the expansion of services for children with disabilities (CWD), and the regional Ministerial Investment of £13.1 million to the CWD service that was announced in October 2024 has supported all five HSC Trusts with this. The Western HSC Trust (WHSCT) are developing a new Children with Disability (CWD) Short Break Service, which will provide planned, time limited short breaks for children with a disability who are supported by the Children’s Disability Team, and who are not subject to Looked After Children arrangements. The service will enhance family resilience and attempt to prevent crisis or family breakdown, by providing regular, planned short breaks which support families caring for children with a disability, ensuring placements are flexible, needs led and reflective of each family’s circumstances.  Children will be offered a positive, safe and enriching short break experience.   All five HSC Trusts have agreed to the Regional Adoption and Fostering Model for Short Breaks, and a recruitment campaign for Carers will be launched regionally from 29th May – 12th June 2026. Information and resources are currently being agreed. Work is ongoing in the development of the Regional Website to include short breaks for children with disability.  The Short Break Team are working closely with the WHSCT’s Family and Childcare Fostering Team, and are currently co-working on its first assessment.  Staff are attending recruitment events arranged for March and April.   A launch of the new service is planned for May 2026.The WHSCT’s Children with Disability Service has enhanced its provision of community social care supports with the development of a Specialist Support Service, which provides crisis led wrap around intensive support for a time limited period to prevent placement breakdowns.  The Trust has also enhanced clinical and therapeutic service provision with the recruitment of additional nursing, behavioural support and psychology staff.  The WHSCT’s Children with Disability Service have also increased the level of supports provided by the community and voluntary sector, delivering a wide range of short break activities for young people across the geography of the Trust.  The Trust has also procured a number of additional residential overnights with NI Hospice.  The WHSCT are working to restore residential short break provision, however, the Trust are cognisant that this is a complex process and there are many multifactorial challenges impactin

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are aware of other international examples of Delivery Units, in which the head of government has chaired meetings of the work of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: In establishing the Delivery Unit, we have drawn on international principles and learning, while designing an approach that reflects our own distinctive circumstances. The Unit is intentionally flexible and will continue to evolve as local experience grows and lessons are learned through delivery.Ministers have been closely engaged in the development and operation of the Unit, reflecting the priority attached to this work, with regular stocktakes with the Delivery Unit team to review progress and ensure a continued focus on outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why he would not consider a gradation of the red flag system for breast screening services to ensure that those with a history of virulent breast cancer can be prioritised.
answer_text: The red flag system operates within the symptomatic breast assessment pathway. The National Institute for Health and Excellence (NICE) guideline NG12 recommends that all patients who meet urgent suspected cancer criteria are assigned the same priority to maintain a consistent and equitable national approach. Clinical information is reviewed at triage to ensure correct pathway allocation, and once designated as a red flag, patients are scheduled strictly in date order through the regional booking system. Some Trusts manage symptomatic patients with relevant family history within their Family History services, while others triage them through the regional breast assessment pathway. These arrangements are intentionally not standardised, allowing Trusts the flexibility needed to manage services according to capacity.The introduction of a tiered or graded red-flag system is not supported by clinical evidence and would conflict with the approach outlined in NICE guideline NG12. The Department is unable to endorse the implementing of this approach at the current time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Minister of Health has directly engaged with the Delivery Unit.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Visit Derry review.
answer_text: An external review of tourism management and marketing in Derry was completed in September 2025, on behalf of Tourism NI and Derry City and Strabane District Council. The review findings were shared with the Board of Visit Derry in October 2025. The Board continue to consider the way forward for Visit Derry alongside securing additional monies in the short term to stabilise the organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Utility Regulator (Support for Decarbonisation Preparation) Bill.
answer_text: I hope to introduce the Bill, subject to Executive agreement, to the Assembly in the coming weeks, and refer the member to my answer to AQO 3140 22/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme legislation.
answer_text: I have submitted a paper to the Executive to agree the final policy.Once Executive approval is secured, my department aims to introduce the required primary legislation for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme to the Assembly before summer recess 2026. Once the Primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how frequently single-let temporary accommodation used to house homeless individuals is formally inspected to ensure the properties consistently meet required safety and quality standards.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to introduce emergency legislation during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate, in order to address street drinking.
answer_text: Street drinking can have a significant impact on communities, contributing to anti-social behaviour (ASB) and public disorder in some areas.The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) can address several alcohol-related public order offences via Penalty Notices provided for in the Justice Act (NI) 2011. This allows officers to quickly address offences such as being drunk in a public place, disorderly behaviour, or conduct likely to cause a breach of the peace. In addition, the Confiscation of Alcohol (Young Persons) Act 1997 gives police the power to require those under 18 to surrender alcohol in public, with refusal itself constituting an offence.Councils can prohibit drinking in designated public areas by way of local byelaws made under the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 1972. Consuming alcohol in these areas is an offence. Police can take an offender’s name and address, and prosecutions can be brought by councils. At present, I have no plans to introduce emergency legislation in relation to street drinking nor would the Department have the additional resources required to do so without halting progress on other key areas of delivery. Responses to a joint DoJ and Department for Communities (DfC) public consultation on ASB legislation, including issues of street drinking, were published in April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform. My Department is currently working with the PSNI and DfC to determine appropriate next steps towards identifying a proportionate and workable solution, with a view to bringing forward legislation in the next assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any progress made by his Department to proactively tackle, and improve, air quality in schools; and (ii) whether he will consider providing air purifiers for schools.
answer_text: Both the Education Authority (EA) and my Department rely on the expert advice of the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) when considering matters such as ventilation and the potential role of air filtration systems. The EA issued ventilation guidance to schools in December 2021. Although this guidance was developed as part of the Covid-19 response, it is fundamentally about improving ventilation and remains relevant to school settings.Considerable work has been taken forward to ensure that windows in schools can open unrestricted to allow for natural ventilation. In addition, all schools were provided with carbon dioxide monitors to identify classrooms or other areas potentially subject to poor ventilation.My Department continues to face significant financial pressures, and the distribution and installation of air purifiers to all school settings is currently not under consideration. If a school considers it has an issue with ventilation, it should contact the EA in the first instance to determine whether any action is necessary, other than local action by the school. The priority remains to keep schools open and safe and that the EA complies with relevant statutory requirements in relation to the safety of school premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his definition of disproportionately, in the context of the increase in childcare fees following the introduction of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: In the context of increases in childcare fees, the term disproportionate is not intended to imply a fixed numerical threshold. It is used to refer to increases that are not broadly aligned with the underlying cost pressures affecting the childcare sector.Childcare in Northern Ireland operates largely as a private market, with providers facing different cost structures and business models. As a result, fee changes will vary. While some individual increases may be perceived as disproportionate when viewed in isolation, determining whether a specific increase is truly disproportionate would require detailed insight into a provider’s operating costs staffing model and financial sustainability.  My Department does not have the remit, resources or expertise to collect this provider-level data. It is therefore not possible to apply a single definition at an individual provider level.As part of the data provided through the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, the issue is considered by looking at overall fee trends. The evidence indicates that, to date, fee trends following the introduction of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme have broadly reflected wider cost pressures impacting the sector with an average increase of fees at approximately 12%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of information contained within the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, to detail the largest fee rise out of the 202 private day nursery providers, as a percentage rise.
answer_text: Fee analysis contained in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy is based on 202 private day nursery providers who had provided data for both September 2024 and April 2025. This indicated that the average provider-level increase across the group was approximately 12%. Within that sample, the largest increase was from an average fee of £58 per day to £71 per day, an increase of 22%. This figure should be interpreted in context, as it reflects an individual provider within a wider dataset, which shows that most providers recorded smaller changes and the overall distribution of fees remained broadly stable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has provided carbon dioxide monitors to all schools; and (ii) any guidance issued to schools on the use and maintenance of carbon monoxide monitors.
answer_text: Carbon dioxide monitors have been distributed to schools, with the rollout facilitated by the Education Authority (EA).  Ventilation guidance was issued to schools in December 2021 that provides clear advice on the correct use of the monitors. Although originally developed as part of the Covid-19 response, the guidance is fundamentally about improving ventilation and remains relevant to school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of how greater price transparency in the costs of childcare, across all providers, would improve market competition, and drive self regulation in pricing.
answer_text: As set out in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, our proposals will require providers operating the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to publish their fees on an annual basis.  I would expect having childcare costs in the public domain would lead to greater awareness of the price of childcare services across all providers and therefore create more competition.   This transparency will also ensure that families have the information they need to make informed decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department had with providers in the childcare sector during the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Sector, in relation to the introduction of fee capping.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken extensive engagement with the childcare sector throughout the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy.As well as formal engagement through the ELC Stakeholder Engagement Forum (SEF) my officials have also held discussions with providers and sector organisations on an individual basis on a range of issues relevant to the strategy development, including childcare costs and parental fees.  In addition, my officials have engaged with their counterparts in other jurisdictions, including in the Republic of Ireland, to understand different approaches to parental fees and fee management.Alongside this engagement, the Department has drawn on independent evidence that directly involved provider input, including the RSM Review of Childcare Services and the Department for the Economy’s commissioned market research on targeted business support for childcare providers. This combined programme of engagement and evidence gathering has shaped the draft Strategy and helped ensure that provider perspectives are reflected in its development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to funding for projects from the Education Authority's Youth Grant Funding Scheme, to detail any actions he is taking to protect youth services, in the absence of ringfencing, and to prevent youth services being a target for future cuts.
answer_text: Adequate provision for youth services is determined through the Education Authority’s (EA) regional assessment of need process, and the statutory requirement allows for the EA to provide these youth services in conjunction with the community and voluntary sector.At present, Executive Departments’ Budgets for 2026-27, and subsequent periods, have not yet been agreed.  Following confirmation of my department’s 2026-27 final opening budget and subsequent agreement of the budget strategy, the Education Authority (EA) will receive a composite block grant budget allocation for both schools and youth services.  The EA and its Board will make decisions on how best to deploy the EA’s budget which will include consideration of youth organisations currently at conditional offer stage for the period 2026-2029.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to establish a dedicated menopause clinic for women in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is considerable focus being given to gynaecology services, which include the development of a Women’s Health Action Plan and the key priorities identified, and these will require a sharp focus going forward.   The Department’s Women’s Action Plan will focus on primary, intermediate and secondary care elements of service provision for women.The Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum will oversee ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate and secondary care level for local and regional populations, as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. This will include specific work around services, including Menopause, and the most appropriate pathway and services to support women with this condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any steps her Department has taken to improve bus connectivity between Derry and Dublin; and (ii) any further improvements that are planned.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink provide seven scheduled services per day Monday to Sunday (49 scheduled services per week) in both directions between Derry and Dublin City and Dublin Airport on our X3/X4 route. Last summer Translink supplemented the above service level with an additional departure at 04.00 from Derry to Dublin Airport, operating during July and August in recognition of demand for connectivity to early flight departures from Dublin Airport.   Translink also provided increased capacity via assist services on specific dates throughout the year to meet spikes in demand for travel on this route when major events are taking place. Recently Translink improved the online ticket booking system to allow passenger self-serve and enhanced flexibility on ticket options.    Translink also provide services from Belfast to Dublin City and Dublin Airport on its X1/X2 route and passengers from Derry and Mid Ulster have the further option of interchanging between a 212 service and an X1/X2 service in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of referrals for eating disorder treatment that were (a) accepted; (b) not accepted; and (ii) the type of treatment received, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he and his officials have had with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions regarding the disregard of payments from the Republic of Ireland, made under the Mother and Baby Institutions Payment Scheme, in relation to benefit entitlement.
answer_text: Social security legislation and policy is generally maintained in parity with that in place under the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in Great Britain. Officials are continuing to liaise with colleagues in DWP on the potential for an indefinite disregard to be introduced in Great Britain. The most recent engagement by officials was in early February 2026.A decision on the introduction of a disregard in Northern Ireland will be made once there is clarity on the DWP position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has sought Executive agreement on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: I have sought agreement from the Executive on the policy decisions underpinning the Bill.Once drafting is complete, the Bill will be presented to the Executive for their agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: The £150 million Enhanced Investment Zone programme has been approved by the Executive.  British Government ministerial approval is anticipated to follow shortly.  No projects have been approved at this stage, as it is important to secure full programme approval before moving to individual project approvals. However, a number of high-potential projects have been identified, on which we are continuing to engage with private-sector partners. Once the necessary business cases are completed and approved, I will be in a position to announce individual projects.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the reason they have not chaired a meeting of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: The Delivery Unit is part of The Executive Office structure and operates as an agile team established to support delivery of the Executive’s priorities.It is not a formal committee or board requiring ministerial chairing. In the same way that Ministers do not routinely chair meetings of other units within their Departments, the Delivery Unit conducts its work with departmental officials, delivery partners and other stakeholders while maintaining clear lines of accountability to us as Ministers.We continue to meet with and receive updates from the Unit at regular intervals and maintain close oversight of its work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Delivery Unit will carry out work on other priority areas of focus, alongside their work on reducing health service waiting times, before the end of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Alongside efforts to reduce waiting lists, the Unit is actively overseeing delivery of six transformation projects across health, education, justice and infrastructure. It has also supported the Public Sector Transformation Board to assess a range of additional transformation proposals and will continue to play a central role in ensuring these projects achieve their potential and strengthening connections across individual projects to maximise impact. The Unit also continues to engage with a range of other stakeholders to identify opportunities where it can add value and support positive change.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any priority areas of focus the Delivery Unit has considered.
answer_text: Following Executive consideration, it agreed the Delivery Unit should prioritise hospital waiting lists. Officials in the Delivery Unit have continued to work closely with DoH officials and delivery partners since then. Alongside efforts to reduce waiting lists, the Unit is actively overseeing delivery of six transformation projects across health, education, justice and infrastructure. It has also supported the Public Sector Transformation Board to assess a range of additional transformation proposals and will continue to play a central role in ensuring these projects achieve their potential and strengthening connections across individual projects to maximise impact. The Unit also continues to engage with a range of other stakeholders to identify opportunities where it can add value and support positive change.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when the Delivery Unit last provided a paper to the Executive.
answer_text: Only Ministers bring papers to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the Ministers who have attended a meeting of the Delivery Unit, in their own Ministerial capacity.
answer_text: The Delivery Unit is a small, agile team within The Executive Office, established to drive reform and transformation. It is not a formal committee or board which Ministers chair or attend, although we receive regular updates on its work and last met with Unit officials this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the student loan repayment threshold freeze, announced by the British Government, will apply to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The policy in respect of student loan repayment is a devolved matter, I can confirm that the proposal to freeze repayment thresholds in England will not apply to borrowers from here. The repayment threshold here will see an inflationary increase from £26,095 to £26,900 in April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has secured the £169 million needed to increase the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme rate to 30 per cent, by April 2030.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet agreed funding for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme beyond 31 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38084/22-27, to detail the location of Quilt Fayre Retreat Ltd.
answer_text: Quilt Fayre Retreat Limited is a British registered business with a registered business address of Units 20 & 22 Stockwood Business Park, Redditch, England, B96 6SX. Its North of Ireland address is 26 Bayswater, Derry,BT47 6JL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will extend the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme beyond March 2026.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, currently issued for public consultation, proposes the continuation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme beyond March 2026.  However, this is subject to Executive funding being made available.I am committed to further improving the affordability of childcare for parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38193/22-27, to detail the number of visits, hosted by Invest NI, that originated in Belfast.
answer_text: The term originated, in Invest NI records, refers to the overseas location of the visiting company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact that the proposed delivery of the Local Growth Fund will have on the Community and Voluntary Sector, particularly for services in the Foyle constituency.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his Department's current budget for the Mobuoy Remediation Project.
answer_text: Safeguarding public health, ensuring safe drinking water, and reducing the environmental impact of the Mobuoy site is of paramount importance to me. To that end, NIEA continues to implement a comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme at the site and plan for remediation.The public consultation on the draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site closed on 2 October 2025. Responses have been carefully considered, and the Integrated Consultancy Team appointed by my department, has completed a comprehensive technical evaluation of these responses. The consideration of the responses will now guide the necessary updates to the draft Remediation Strategy document.Currently, for 2025/26, a budget of £0.8 million has been allocated to support the extensive Environmental Monitoring Programme and ongoing remediation planning. This includes approximately £0.5 million in staffing costs and £0.3 million for monitoring activities and other project-related expenditure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail an update on the review into the work of social services, including his engagement with the family court system.
answer_text: I wrote to the Chief Executive Officer of the Regulation and Inspection Authority (RQIA) on  15th January 2026 to request that RQIA, undertakes an urgent review across the five Health and Social Care Trusts Children’s Court Services of practice in Private Law cases before the Family Courts where Domestic Abuse has been alleged or established and Social Services have had involvement in that context, specifically with regard to child contact issues and related child safeguarding issues.I have also replied on the 13th January 2026 to a request from the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, Geraldine Hanna, who had requested nominations of officials from my Department to participate in a Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Working Group and I am awaiting a response form the Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timeline for the completion of the review into the work of social services, including his engagement with the family court system.
answer_text: I wrote to the Chief Executive Officer of the Regulation and Inspection Authority (RQIA) on 15th January 2026 to request that RQIA undertakes an urgent review across Children’s Court Services in the five Health and Social Care Trusts. The review will consider HSCT practice in private law cases where domestic abuse has been alleged or established and Social Services have been involved with related child contact issues and child safeguarding issues.Work is ongoing between the DoH and RQIA to agree terms of reference and a methodology for the review.A firm timeline for the review will not be established until the above are agreed. However, I have asked that it is completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the scope of the review into the work of social services.
answer_text: I have asked the RQIA to undertake a review of the five Health and Social Care Trusts Children’s Court workers and social workers practice in Private Law cases before the Family Courts where Domestic Abuse has been alleged or established and Social Services have had involvement in that context, specifically with regard to child contact issues and related child safeguarding issues.RQIA are developing Terms of reference and a methodology to undertake the review. I expect the review to assess the quality of HSC Trust social services assessments and reports against relevant best practice, standards, polices and procedures and to ascertain if the voices, wishes and feelings of children and relevant adults are accurately considered and reflected upon in social services reports to the courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how evidence used for the review into social services will be gathered; and (ii) whether lived-experience cases will be considered as part of the review’s evidential base.
answer_text: I have asked the RQIA to undertake a review of the five Health and Social Care Trusts Children’s Court workers and social workers practice in Private Law cases before the Family Courts where Domestic Abuse has been alleged or established and Social Services have had involvement in that context, specifically with regard to child contact issues and related child safeguarding issues.RQIA are developing Terms of reference and a methodology to undertake the review. I expect the review to undertake an overview of social services case files and records to assess the quality of HSC Trust social services assessments and reports against relevant best practice, standards, policies and procedures and to ascertain if the voices, wishes and feelings of children and relevant adults are accurately considered and reflected upon in social services reports to the courts.I have not specifically asked that the review engages directly with persons with lived experience of social services involvement in cases before the Family Courts as that has already been reported upon in the Queen’s University Report “Totally Invisible: The experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims/survivors and children engaging with private law family court processes in Northern Ireland” that was commissioned by the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38304/22-27, to detail the allocation of the 736 jobs, broken down by (i) company; (ii) number of jobs attributed to each company; (iii) assistance offered to each company; and (iv) the number of jobs yet to be fulfilled at each company.
answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal provides detail on individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI that will answer points (i), (ii) and (iii) of this request; however, some offers may not be included due to the reasons outlined in the Invest NI Open Data Guidance.In relation to point (iv) Invest NI is unable to disclose the number of jobs yet to be fulfilled by individual supported businesses while a project is still live. This level of detail is considered commercially sensitive and releasing it could prejudice the commercial interests of those businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 36734/22-27, to detail the address to which the Propel Industries Pvt Ltd investment will be attributed to.
answer_text: In June 2024 Propel Industries PVT Ltd announced its first investment in the North through an equity investment into Omega Crushing & Screening Ltd which, at the time, was based in Castlederg in Derry City & Strabane District Council.   This investment was recorded against this DCA.  The business has subsequently moved its operations to Omagh and currently employs 13 people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38194/22-27, to detail why new inward investment visits, hosted by Invest NI, are not counted by origin of visit.
answer_text: Invest NI does count new inward investment visits by origin. As outlined in the original response for AQW 38194/22-27, Invest NI records for each visit the date, agreed itinerary, locations visited, sector, and the origin, defined as the source market of the visiting company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 38499/22-27, to detail when a decision will be made on the revised Translink business case.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of addressing regional imbalance and improving connectivity for communities across the region, including those in the North West. My officials are currently considering the revised business case for enhanced rail services to the North West. The outcome of this review will be presented to me in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38306/22-27, to detail the allocation of those 681 jobs, broken down by (i) company; (ii) number of jobs attributed to each company; (iii) assistance offered to each company; and (iii) the number of jobs yet to be fulfilled at each company.
answer_text: Invest NI is unable to disclose the number of jobs created by individual supported businesses while a project is still live. This level of detail is considered commercially sensitive and releasing it could prejudice the commercial interests of those businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 38306/22-27, to detail the number of the 929 new jobs that have been advertised.
answer_text: Invest NI does not collect data from businesses on the number of supported jobs that they advertise; however, through conditions in its Letters of Offer, Invest NI stipulates that supported jobs must be new or additional to the Client’s current workforce. Compliance with this condition is tested through supporting evidence after the company has recruited the roles and submitted a grant claim to Invest NI.In addition, all offers of support are conditional on the business complying with all applicable laws or regulations, including employment law relating to recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 38603/22-27, to provide a detailed breakdown of the amounts paid to the1,866 registered childcare providers by the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, broken down by (i) location; (ii) provider; and (iii) amount paid.
answer_text: My Department is committed to supporting transparency in the use of public funding. However, it must also comply with its obligations under the Data Protection Act 2018 and UK GDPR. Disclosing the amounts paid to individual providers would release personal financial information relating to identifiable organisations or individuals. Releasing such information would not meet the requirements of Principle 1 (lawfulness, fairness and transparency) and the NICSS Privacy Notice does not provide a lawful basis or expectation that individuallevel financial data will be made public.For similar reasons, the Department cannot provide a breakdown of expenditure by location combined with provider type as this could allow individual providers to be indirectly identified.The Department will continue to publish appropriate aggregate information on NICSS while ensuring compliance with data protection legislation and protecting personal information.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have chaired a meeting of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: The Delivery Unit is a small, agile team within the Executive Office, established to drive reform and transformation. It is not a formal committee or board which Ministers chair or attend, although we receive regular updates on its work and last met with Unit officials in December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) day care and after-school providers registered in Foyle; and (ii) these which enable parents to avail of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the expenditure of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, broken down by provider, since the scheme was introduced.
answer_text: Since the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) was introduced, 1,866 registered childcare providers have submitted at least one claim for a 15% reduction on parent’s childcare bills. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has sought Executive agreement on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will be brought to the Executive for agreement as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the work of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: Alongside its ongoing work with the Department of Health on the Executive priority to reduce hospital waiting times in the immediate and short term, the Delivery Unit is supporting implementation of the Programme for Government commitment to the reform and transformation of public services. The Unit is currently overseeing delivery of six funded transformation projects, including a project to expand multidisciplinary teams in GP practices as a critical strand of wider health reforms. The Delivery Unit has also supported the Public Sector Transformation Board in finalising its recommendations on the allocation of remaining public sector transformation funding. It will be central to ensuring that public sector transformation projects fulfil their potential and that connections are made across individual projects. The Delivery Unit has also been engaging with a range of other stakeholders to identify discrete projects where its involvement can add value and make a positive difference. To this end, it is in the early stages of work with Belfast City Council to test how it can help break down public sector silos to improve outcomes for vulnerable people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. Translink submitted a revised business case in September 2025 for the provision of additional services in the North West rail corridor. This is being considered by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why her Department has not provided a five year financial stability plan to the Department of Finance.
answer_text: My Department has provided its five-year financial stability plan to the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why regional median earnings has been chosen by her Department as the topline measure of success, for regional balance.
answer_text: Median wages are a clear indicator of relative living standards and reducing regional disparities in living standards is the key objective of the Regional Balance agenda. Wage growth is also strongly linked with increased productivity, another priority of the Economic Vison. Median wages are therefore an appropriate headline measure of success that is aligned and consistent with the wider goals of the Economic Vison.While this is the headline measure, the overall success of the Economic Vison will be measured against an array of supporting indicators. Each of these indicators will be considered within the context of sub-regional performance, ensuring that a broad range of issues are considered in my department’s approach to promoting Regional Balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how many of the 466 jobs to be created in East Derry from 2020-21 to 2024-25, as evidenced by Invest NI's open data tables, have been (i) created; and (ii) fulfilled.
answer_text: Over the period 1st April 2020 to the 31st March 2025 Invest NI offered support to businesses within the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency area which will lead to the creation of 466 new jobs over the 3 to 5 years following offer acceptance.  To date 363 of these jobs have been created within the constituency area.The projects within this period are at different stages of completion; therefore, the figures presented should not be interpreted as an indication of performance against final project targets. Instead, they represent the status of each project at a specific point in time. All projects will continue to be monitored in accordance with Invest NI policy, and a final outcome will be recorded for each once the postproject evaluation has been completed.For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Open Data NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how new inward investment visits, hosted by Invest NI, are counted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale used within her Department to determine whether a remote working investment should be considered as outside the Belfast Metropolitan Area.
answer_text: Invest NI set out Regional Targets in its Business Strategy to deliver 65% of its investments to businesses located outside of the Belfast Metropolitan Area in Year 3 of its Business Strategy, often referred to as “outside BMA” . This measure is applied to all investments, not just new inward investments. 	To measure its progress towards this target, Invest NI has attempted to split investments logically between (i) those primarily targeted at the Belfast Metropolitan Area and, (ii) those investments which target the Rest of the North or offer North of Ireland wide opportunities, i.e. through remote working.  		This approach has helped Invest NI to encourage a greater spread of investments which might previously have favoured locating in Belfast. 		However, I continue to keep the measures of economic impact under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rational as to why remote working investments and jobs are considered outside the Belfast Metropolitan Area, within Invest NI inward investment figures for 2024-25.
answer_text: Invest NI set out Regional Targets in its Business Strategy to deliver 65% of its investments to businesses located outside of the Belfast Metropolitan Area in Year 3 of its Business Strategy, often referred to as “outside BMA” . This measure is applied to all investments, not just new inward investments. 	To measure its progress towards this target, Invest NI has attempted to split investments logically between (i) those primarily targeted at the Belfast Metropolitan Area and, (ii) those investments which target the Rest of the North or offer North of Ireland wide opportunities, i.e. through remote working.  		This approach has helped Invest NI to encourage a greater spread of investments which might previously have favoured locating in Belfast. 		However, I continue to keep the measures of economic impact under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's definition of remote working.
answer_text: Invest NI defines a remote working project as one in which an inward investor creates employment opportunities that allow people in the North to work from home where there is no centralised office in the North.These remote roles contribute directly to the North’s economic activity and to the overall number of jobs created in the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how Invest NI engages with the City of Derry Airport.
answer_text: Invest NI engages with the City of Derry Airport through the Local Economic Partnership and NI Connections.The Managing Director of City of Derry Airport is an important stakeholder in the Local Economic Partnership (LEP) in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area. LEPs bring together central government, the business community, universities and colleges, local enterprise agencies, and civil society in the area to identify the main barriers to economic development and the priority interventions that will build the region’s value proposition to help deliver a more regionally balanced economy. Invest NI also engages with the City of Derry Airport through its partnership with Northern Irish Connections (NIC). This collaboration uses the region’s international connectivity to strengthen engagement with the North’s global diaspora. By showcasing the North West’s strategic location, talent, and crossborder advantages, NIC and the airport work together to promote economic opportunities in trade, investment, education, and business tourism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the four existing investments by externally owned investors into the Derry City and Strabane local council area, broken down by the (i) name of the company; (ii) value of each individual investment; and (ii) number of jobs created for each.
answer_text: Over the period 1st April 2024 to the 31st March 2025, Invest NI supported three externally owned companies with four business expansion and development projects in the Derry City and Strabane Council area. The table below details these projects.Invest NI Inward Expansion and Development Projects in Derry City and Strabane Council Area – 2024-25Client NameTotal Assistance £Total Planned Investment £Total New JobsType of SupportExpansionSeagate Technology (Ireland)15,036,255114,713,96933Investing in Innovation & R&DBusiness DevelopmentAlchemy Technology Services Ltd98,227156,4510Investing in Innovation & R&DAlchemy Technology Services Ltd8508500Driving Global Ambition & Overseas TradeQuilt Fayre Retreat Ltd5,8238,3190Investing in Innovation & R&D		While business development investments in innovation, R&D, and international trade may not lead directly to job creation, they strengthen overall competitiveness and ultimately support sustainable growth and the creation of new employment opportunities.		For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Open Data NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) steps her Department has taken to improve bus connectivity between Derry and Dublin; and (ii) planned further improvements.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink provide seven scheduled services per day Monday to Sunday (49 scheduled services per week) in both directions between Derry and Dublin City and Dublin Airport on our X3/X4 route. Last summer Translink supplemented the above service level with an additional departure at 04.00 from Derry to Dublin Airport, operating during July and August in recognition of demand for connectivity to early flight departures from Dublin Airport.   Translink also provided increased capacity via assist services on specific dates throughout the year to meet spikes in demand for travel on this route when major events are taking place. Recently Translink improved the online ticket booking system to allow passenger self-serve and enhanced flexibility on ticket options.    Translink also provide services from Belfast to Dublin City and Dublin Airport on our X1/X2 route and passengers from Derry and Mid Ulster have the further option of interchanging between a 212 service and an X1/X2 service in Belfast. Translink plan to reintroduce the additional 04.00 departure from Derry again this July and August on our X3/X4 route.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the full roof repairs necessary at Nazareth House Primary School in Derry.
answer_text: The Education Authority is currently progressing urgent remedial works to make the roof of the main Nazareth House Primary School building safe, with the aim of allowing the whole school community to return to learning on their own site by half-term break in February 2026.Officials from my Department are engaging with technical advisers in Construction and Procurement Delivery to agree the scope of a procurement package for an Integrated Consultancy Team to deliver the more extensive roofing works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering lowering the age for bowel cancer screening, in line with GB and Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and to lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. Currently in Northern Ireland, men and women aged between 60 and 74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel cancer screening. Work to meet the commitments to expand the bowel screening programme is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Where a Screening Programme expands its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, including diagnostic capacity, workforce and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.I remain committed to ensuring that the NI Bowel Screening Programme is expanded in line with UK National Screening Committee recommendations and as outlined in my three-year HSC Reset Plan, subject to future departmental spending priorities.Regardless of age, anyone currently experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee legislation.
answer_text: The Final Scheme Design for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) scheme was published by my Department in September 2025. I will bring forward a paper to the Executive in the coming weeks to agree the final policy.Once Executive approval is secured, my department aims to introduce the required primary legislation for the REPG to the Assembly before summer recess 2026. Once the Primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when he expects a statutory rule to be introduced, to ensure that payments made under the Mother and Baby Institutions Payment Scheme in the Republic of Ireland are disregarded for the purposes of benefit entitlement in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Social security legislation and policy is generally maintained in parity with that in place under the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in Great Britain. Officials are continuing to liaise with colleagues in DWP on the potential for an indefinite disregard to be introduced in Great Britain. A decision on the introduction of a disregard in Northern Ireland will be made once there is clarity on the DWP position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the water and wastewater treatment network capacity in Foyle.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that there are currently no water demand issues in the Foyle area. However, NI Water  is currently undertaking work on the Craigavon Bridge which includes lagging of an existing watermain on the northern side of the bridge and will commence work in February to replace the 600mm diameter pipe which has reached end of life.During Price Control 21, NI Water is proposing to invest approximately £43m in key water infrastructure in the Foyle area. With regard to wastewater treatment capacity in the Foyle area, there are currently no wastewater capacity constraints at Donnybrewer and Nixon’s Corner wastewater treatment works or associated networks.  There are also no wastewater capacity constraints at Culmore wastewater treatment works, however, there are significant capacity constraints within the wastewater network in the Culmore catchment. This is due to the presence of a large number of discharging assets within this area that are classified as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges, which means that they discharge more frequently than designed.  To help improve key wastewater infrastructure in the Foyle area, NI Water is proposing to invest approximately £57m in Price Control 21. This investment includes an additional £11m which I secured through June monitoring, to deliver the Skeoge(Coshquin) wastewater pumping station, which is required to facilitate new connections at the H2 site.In addition, NI Water is working closely with the University of Ulster regarding its proposed expansion of the campus and has advised that although the wastewater catchment has some capacity constraints, it has confirmed to the University that a connection is possible for all proposed expansion sites subject to storm water offsetting.   	 Furthermore, my Department’s Living With Water in Derry Plan, which details the drainage and wastewater issues in the area as well as the potential opportunities for solutions, will be published in February 2026. 	 I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development. Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office we have enabled the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North including 3,000 in the Derry area. I also note that the second quarter of 2025 saw the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15-year high in the North in the same period. This is positive news for people, jobs and the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any immediate steps his Department is taking to address the structural roof safety concerns, which have caused the temporary closure of Nazareth House Primary School, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The temporary closure of Nazareth House Primary School was necessary to ensure the health and safety of the pupils, staff and parents. Years of underinvestment in our ageing school estate have left us facing difficult decisions and, in this case, the only option was to temporarily close the school.The Education Authority (EA) has commenced planning for urgent remedial works to make the roof of the main school building safe, with the aim of allowing the whole Nazareth House school community to return to learning on their own site by half-term break in February 2026.Following intensive work with the school leadership, the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and the EA, it has been agreed that from 7 January, pupils and staff of Nazareth House Primary School, including the nursery, will share a site with St Anne’s Primary School. In parallel with these temporary contingency arrangements and the remedial works, my Department has commenced planning for the more extensive roofing works, which will be prioritised for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of the £34.1 million, allocated to her Department for water services, public transport, rail and road projects, that will be allocated to the Translink business case, for rail improvements in the North West.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. Translink submitted a revised business case in September 2025 for the provision of additional services on the North West rail corridor. This is being considered by my officials.At the outset of the financial year, I over planned against my Department's opening 2025-26 capital budget, maximising funding at the start of the year. The £34.1m allocated to my Department in the December Monitoring Round is in respect of this overplanning commitment and will allow current capital projects right across my Department and its Arms Length Bodies to continue as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has secured Executive agreement on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 35855/22-27 published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 5th December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the £12.6 million, allocated to her Department for further and higher education in the 2025-26 December Monitoring Round, will be spent.
answer_text: The additional £12.6 million Capital DEL funding allocated to my Department in the December Monitoring Round will be used as follows:£4.5 million to address a capital overcommitment arising from the June Monitoring Round, when I committed to fund an additional £9.0 million for quality-related research in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). This step was necessary to provide financial stability for HEIs after the Executive provided only £2.5 million in extra funding at that time;£6.3 million to provide further support for Higher Education; and£1.8 million for minor capital works across the Further Education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether, during the development of her Department's Sub-Regional Economic Plan, was consideration given to using electoral wards, rather than International Territorial Levels, Level 3, when comparing data and trends between areas.
answer_text: The selection of International Territorial Level (ITL) 3 / LGD regions was a policy decision on the basis that detailed and robust analysis was required and therefore good data across a range of sources.  As ITL regions are the standard statistical geographic unit used by most publications for the presentation of sub-regional estimates a decision was taken to use ITL regions. Sample based estimates at lower geographic unit levels such as electoral wards are typically subject to a greater degree of statistical uncertainty (and heightened risk of suppression due to disclosure rules). This is particularly the case where the sample for a source survey is not intended to be representative at a very low geographical unit level. Statistical uncertainty can make it difficult to judge whether differences in comparative regional performance are authentic. While the census provides robust data at District Electoral Area and other lower levels of geographic unit for some indicators this is only available every decade.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale for using International Territorial Levels, Level 3, within her Sub-Regional Economic Plan, rather than electoral wards.
answer_text: The selection of International Territorial Level 3 / LGD regions was a policy decision on the basis that detailed and robust analysis was required and therefore good data across a range of sources.  As ITL regions are the standard statistical geographic unit used by most publications for the presentation of sub-regional estimates we chose to use ITL regions. Sample based estimates at lower geographic unit levels such as electoral wards are typically subject to a greater degree of statistical uncertainty (and heightened risk of suppression due to disclosure rules). This is particularly the case where the sample for a source survey is not intended to be representative at a very low geographical unit level. Statistical uncertainty can make it difficult to judge whether differences in comparative regional performance are authentic. While the census provides robust data at District Electoral Area and other lower levels of geographic unit for some indicators this is only available every decade.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the SONI Cost and Performance Report October 2020 to September 2024.
answer_text: As system operator, SONI’s responsibility is to plan and operate the electricity transmission system. The delays in delivering new infrastructure, which is an important enabler of power decarbonisation, outlined in the Utility Regulator’s report, are disappointing.To help address this, I have established the Grid Development Monitoring Group with senior representatives from SONI, NIE Networks, the Utility Regulator, which is charged with accelerating power decarbonisation including delivering more renewable electricity onto the system.

The table below highlights 17 of these investors by District Council area, outlining the total planned investment for each project and the sector of activity.
