question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27334/22-27, whether she has asked the Ulster University Magee Taskforce to meet directly with her since taking office.
answer_text: I have not asked the Ulster University Magee Taskforce to meet directly with me since I took office, however, I would be glad to accept any invitation to meet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department is working to ensure that the success of the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme is not limited by the capacity of the current grid system.
answer_text: My Department contracted Aurora Energy Research to provide the required technical expertise and knowledge of the electricity system to support the development of a Renewable Electricity Support Scheme. Aurora conducted extensive analysis and engaged with SONI and NIE Networks on the capacity of the grid system to inform the design of the first auction under the scheme. My Department is also establishing a Grid Development Monitoring Group, comprising the Department, SONI, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator. This group will track and monitor the key grid developments and upgrades planned for 2030 and beyond. Finally, the Utility Regulator approved last year the largest sum in our history for network development, strengthening and operation in the RP7 price control period from 2025 to 2031 (£2.23B).  This will go a long way to delivering the low carbon network we need to deliver the statutory target of 80% electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Net Zero Accelerator Fund.
answer_text: Invest NI commissioned an independent market needs analysis to assess the need for projects that could make a significant contribution towards reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the North. This stage of the project is nearing completion, and the final report is expected by the end of June 2025. Once this analysis is complete a number of official processes will need to be completed, commencing with the development of an outline business case. It is anticipated a fund will be launched in 2026, subject to business case approvals processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail: (i) the total funding given to all schools in Foyle for maintenance and repairs in the past 15 years, broken down by school, for each year up to 2024/25; and (ii) an overall total for each school across the entire period.
answer_text: As funding is not allocated to individual schools, nor by constituency, resource expenditure detail has been provided. The Education Authority (EA) has statutory responsibility for undertaking repairs/maintenance work in maintained and controlled schools.  My Department allocates the EA resource funding each financial year to take this forward.In line with record retention policies, details of the final outturn for schools’ repairs/maintenance expenditure is only available for each of the last seven financial years for maintained and controlled schools in the Foyle constituency and is set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.Please note that the 2024/25 maintenance figure is provisional outturn expenditure and is likely to be subject to change. Landlord maintenance activities are not included as they are funded by schools from their LMS budget and Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated Schools are responsible for their own maintenance works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail: (i) the total minor works funding given to all schools in Foyle for maintenance and repairs in the past 15 years, broken down by school, for each year up to 2024/25; and (ii) an overall total for each school across the entire period.
answer_text: As funding is not allocated to individual schools, nor by constituency, capital minor works expenditure detail has been provided. In line with record retention policies, details of the amount spent on minor works for schools in the Foyle constituency is only available for each of the last seven financial years and is set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 26396 22/27, to explain the delay in Local Economic Partnerships bringing forward projects to her Department, given that the Sub-Regional Economic Plan states that this was meant to have happened by the end of March 2025.
answer_text: My first priority in this work is that it is done as a genuine partnership with councils and other local stakeholders. This has meant listening to the feedback of others around the need to identify the right projects. While there has been some slippage due to different factors, including business case approval, I am confident that we are building the right foundations to ensure maximum impact over the years ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department is working to leverage global connections to attract philanthropic (i) investment; and (ii) support, for key initiatives in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: On my recent international trip to the United States, I saw firsthand the passion, commitment and energy of our diaspora community. It is important that we harness that energy in a focussed way aligned with our strategic economic agenda. The role of diaspora engagement to boost trade and investment is recognised in Invest NI’s 2024-2027 Business Strategy and through the delivery of its NI Connections programme. NI Connections engages the diaspora to share their expertise and experience with businesses to support trade and investment opportunities. This is in a non-renumerated, voluntary capacity. The establishment of both Net Zero and Life and Health Sciences Advisory Boards in recent months are excellent examples of how we are working through Invest NI and its NI Connections programme to harness the contribution that the diaspora can bring to the future economic growth of the North. I and my officials will continue to engage with Invest NI and others to tell the positive story of our diaspora and the role it can play in driving forward our economic agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department is taking to actively encourage philanthropy, particularly in terms of fostering connections with the Irish diaspora.
answer_text: On my recent international trip to the United States, I saw firsthand the passion, commitment and energy of our diaspora community. It is important that we harness that energy in a focussed way aligned with our strategic economic agenda. The role of diaspora engagement to boost trade and investment is recognised in Invest NI’s 2024-2027 Business Strategy and through the delivery of its NI Connections programme. NI Connections engages the diaspora to share their expertise and experience with businesses to support trade and investment opportunities. This is in a non-renumerated, voluntary capacity. The establishment of both Net Zero and Life and Health Sciences Advisory Boards in recent months are excellent examples of how we are working through Invest NI and its NI Connections programme to harness the contribution that the diaspora can bring to the future economic growth of the North. I and my officials will continue to engage with Invest NI and others to tell the positive story of our diaspora and the role it can play in driving forward our economic agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the policy scoping work undertaken by her Department in relation to the family court system.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his awareness of any existing match-funding initiatives.
answer_text: As part of Budget 2025-26 the Executive agreed to provide match-funding as set out below:DescriptionMatch FundingResource DELCapital DELPeace PlusEuropean Union£17.8m£6.9mTackling ParamilitariesTreasury£8.0mCity and Growth Deals 1 Treasury-£41.3mInclusive Future FundingTreasury-£4.7m1 Deal partners also contribute

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what steps his Department has taken to encourage philanthropic giving in the region,  in light of the 2024 Charities Aid Foundation UK Giving report finding that three of the top ten giving constituencies in the UK were in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is keen to explore any opportunities that might arise to encourage philanthropic giving in Northern Ireland; we have engaged with American philanthropists through the NI Bureau, and the importance of attracting alternative sources of funding has also been highlighted to the Arm’s Length Bodies sponsored by my Department.I continue to explore options to secure philanthropic funding through connections made during my visits to Washington.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to expand match-funding initiatives to stimulate greater giving and partnership with the private sector.
answer_text: I am willing to consider any match funding initiatives brought forward by departments.  Any proposals will need agreement from the respective co-funder, and must be considered by the Executive in the context of available funding and all other competing priorities as part of the upcoming multi-year Budget process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the recent National Philanthropy Policy in the Republic of Ireland, which includes objectives such as improving communication, incentivising giving, and enhancing partnerships with the government, whether they will commit to developing a Philanthropy Strategy.
answer_text: The Head of the Civil Service is currently leading engagement to explore means by which philanthropic collaboration could support Programme for Government objectives to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the recent National Philanthropy Policy in the Republic of Ireland, which includes objectives such as improving communication, incentivising giving, and enhancing partnerships with the government, whether they see value in similar actions being taken here.
answer_text: We recognise the potential for maximising change through shared endeavour and are keen to leverage opportunities for philanthropic partnerships. We met recently with the Social Change Initiative to discuss effective ways to secure and maximise the impact of philanthropic investment. The Head of the Civil Service is currently leading engagement to explore means by which philanthropic collaboration could support Programme for Government objectives to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This includes the funding available for the public transport network. The timetabling of services is primarily an operational matter for Translink.  The Public Service Agreement allows Translink to amend services, but they must do so in a cost-effective manner, consistent with the required level of quality and performance, and minimise costs.In June 2024 Translink submitted a business case to my Department:Introduce an hourly Sunday Service.Provide an earlier morning departure from Derry to connect with the 0800 hours Enterprise service to DublinExtend the 22.40 service from Belfast to terminate in Derry rather than ColeraineMy Department is working with Translink to better understand the associated costs. This will then enable my Department to consider these proposals and to examine what service changes could be introduced, subject to budget availability, to improve connectivity to the North-West.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Green Growth Strategy.
answer_text: My Department has been leading on the development of the Green Growth Strategy, on behalf of the Northern Ireland Executive, collaborating with other departments, local government and key stakeholders from across the business, voluntary and community sectors.		The draft Strategy is the Executive’s multi-decade strategy for climate, the environment & the economy.  Its aim is to ensure future government policy making here has climate and environmental action at its core, embracing and enabling science and innovation to drive solutions.	The Strategy sets out our long-term vision for a future where Northern Ireland transitions from being a high to a net zero emissions society; where we can enjoy the longer term economic, social, health and environmental benefits that this brings. One in which society enjoys a low carbon, nature rich future reaping the advantages from jobs in the expanding green economy and consequent health and wellbeing advantages.		In October 2021, the then Executive agreed the text of a draft Green Growth Strategy. The strategy was issued for an eight-week public consultation, revised and updated to incorporate feedback from the consultation exercise and the impact of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) which received Royal Assent on 6 June 2022. While the updated strategy was agreed by the former DAERA Minister, it was not possible to secure approval in the absence of an Executive.		In the interim, the draft Strategy has been further updated to ensure its accuracy and relevance and it has now been shared with Ministerial colleagues prior to being tabled at an Executive meeting for approval at the earliest opportunity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what interim plans are in place to support maternal mental health services across Health and Social Care Trusts in the absence of a Mother and Baby Unit; and (ii) whether any alternative or enhanced resources will be made available to strengthen existing provision in the meantime.
answer_text: (i) Interim Support Across Health and Social Care TrustsIn the absence of a dedicated regional Mother and Baby Unit (MBU), Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented a range of interim measures to ensure the continued support of women experiencing moderate to severe perinatal mental health difficulties. These include:Specialist Community Perinatal Mental Health Teams established in all Trust areas, offering assessment, treatment, and ongoing management of mental health conditions during pregnancy and the postnatal period.Enhanced training for maternity, health visiting, and mental health staff to promote early identification and referral.Collaborative working with general adult acute care (both inpatient and home treatment services) to provide care where admission is required, ensuring mother-baby contact is maintained where possible and clinically appropriate.Strong integration with primary care and voluntary sector services to support stepped-care models of intervention and peer support.(ii) Alternative and Enhanced ResourcesWhile the development of an MBU remains a longer-term strategic objective, the Department of Health has committed to strengthening existing provision in the interim through:Investment in community-based specialist perinatal mental health services.Upskilling of perinatal clinical staff, including midwives, mental health nurses, family nurses  and health visitors .Implementation of the Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathways and associated guidanceImplementation of crisis intervention pathways and out-of-hours support for women experiencing acute distress.Ongoing engagement with service users and advocacy groups to ensure care remains person-centred and responsive to the needs of mothers and families. Development of a Service User Leaflet to explain the Perinatal Mental Health Plans for Service development work between Specialist Perinatal Teams and Perinatal Services to look at early interventions I was delighted to launch the updated Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathway in March 2025 which has integrated the community perinatal mental health teams into the pathway of care. It provides guidance to all health and social care professionals who come into contact with women in the antenatal and postnatal period across all settings, inclusive of primary care.These measures reflect a commitment to delivering safe, accessible, and high-quality maternal mental health care until such time as a dedicated MBU can be established.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) why more than 18 months after the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust’s Mother and Baby Unit (MBU) proposal was declined in favour of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust site due to capital readiness, there has been no apparent progress on the MBU build; (ii) the current stage of development; and (ii) whether a timeline is in place.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) her Department’s process for following up on road defects that have been marked for repair; and (ii) what measures are in place to ensure that such defects are addressed within a reasonable timeframe, particularly where initial markings have worn away before repairs are carried out.
answer_text: My Department’s officials undertake regular inspections of the road network and road defects that meet the current intervention criteria in accordance with the Department’s policy will be recorded and marked for repair. Our current standards specify a range of response times for the repair of defects, depending on factors such as their severity and the volume of traffic on the road. They range from one calendar day for the most serious defects, to periods of five working days and four weeks for less serious defects. If it becomes apparent that the relevant response time cannot be met, then my Department has the option of installing signs to warn road users of a possible danger.My officials can run reports to monitor and identify defects which are nearing, or are past, their due date for completion and all defects are recorded with a description of their location and dimensions to enable the contractor to identify and repair the correct defect. Instructed repairs remain ongoing until confirmation is received that a permanent repair is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the conversations she has had with the Irish Government in relation to the resumption of air travel between the City of Derry Airport and Dublin Airport, in the last six months.
answer_text: In the New Decade New Approach Agreement, the Irish Government committed to taking forward a review of the potential for Government support for air routes from Cork to Belfast and Dublin to Derry.This review was led by the Department of Transport in Dublin in cooperation with the Department for Transport in London which is responsible for Public Service Obligation policy.  At a recent meeting with the Secretary of State for Transport, I asked for an update on progress and have written to the Minister for Transport in Dublin emphasising the significant economic and social opportunities of reopening the Dublin-Derry route for the North West and asking when the government’s response to their review into the route will be made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why maternal mental health peer support providers, including the Maternal Advocacy & Support Project and Action on Postpartum Psychosis, were not engaged in the development of the Mother and Baby Unit business plan despite a commitment to include lived experience input; and (ii) whether the strategic outline and successful business case will be shared.
answer_text: The development of a business case in respect of this is a purely factual exercise.  Service users and people with lived experience will be closely involved in the design of the Mother and Baby Unit and the development of the service model.At present, there are no plans to share the strategic outline and successful business case due to potential commercial sensitivities and the fact that progress of the Mother and Baby Unit remains subject to the necessary funding being identified.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has now matched the £1.5m in funding provided by the Irish Government to the North West Regional Development Fund.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Executive provided the equivalent of €2.5m to Phase 1 of the North West Development Fund. Phase 1 of the fund ended on 31 March 2025. Going forward we want to focus on the transformational change that is required in the Region and how best to utilise current structures like the North West Strategic Growth Partnership, to support that change. Officials continue to engage with Irish Government counterparts on how best to continue to support the Region, and we are currently considering next steps in relation to this which will involve considering additional funding requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26618/22-27, whether she has met with the Ulster University Magee Taskforce since she assumed her position as Minister for Infrastructure.
answer_text: I have not received any meeting requests from the Ulster University Magee Taskforce,  however, if an invitation was received I would be happy to accept. My officials continue to engage with the University on issues around their expansion plans

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to lift the cap on the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: I increased the cap in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme by 10% from 1 April 2025, raising the maximum claimable amount from £167 to £184 per child per month to reflect childcare provider fee increases.  As a result, the amount that parents can claim annually has increased from £2,000 to £2,208.  The cap remains an important control in the operation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.  I will keep the level it is set at under review to ensure an effective and fair distribution of public funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a targeted business support scheme for the childcare sector.
answer_text: The cross-Departmental Steering Group has received the draft Final Research Report on Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers and is considering its findings. Once this is complete, officials will provide me with advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any conversations he has had with the Minister for the Economy regarding the development of a targeted business support scheme for the childcare sector in the last month.
answer_text: On 22 July 2024, I wrote to the Minister for the Economy, asking him to lead on the development of a targeted business support scheme for the childcare sector.  The Economy Minister responded on 1 August 2024, offering to lead on a scoping exercise to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements of the sector and help inform the appropriate government response.  My officials have contributed to this work and I understand the report is currently being finalised.On 15 May 2025, the Executive approved my plan for allocating the £55m Early Learning and Childcare funding available in 2025-26. This includes £2m earmarked for childcare business support, subject to the recommendations of the Department for the Economy scoping study.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any work that is taking place to address gaps in equality legislation.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 24635/22-27
answer_text: AQW 24635/22-27 was answered on 26 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Wellbeing Dashboard, as referenced in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The PfG Wellbeing Framework dashboard was launched alongside the consultation on the draft PfG on 9 September 2024. By the end of March 2025, the number of indicators had risen to 51, with new indicators looking at good jobs, processing times for criminal cases, self-efficacy among young people, and life satisfaction among young people added, to address stakeholder feedback from the consultation process. In addition to these four new indicators, 31 indicators have had a data update between launch and the end of March 2025. The PfG Wellbeing Framework is publicly available at: www.northernireland.gov.uk/wellbeing

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 21539/22-27
answer_text: AQW21539/22-27 was answered on 29 September 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an assessment of whether the current procurement restrictions imposed by the Education Authority on schools in the controlled sector are fit for purpose; and (ii) whether consideration will be given to increasing school-level autonomy to ensure timely and cost-effective purchases, particularly in subject areas like Drama and Special Educational Needs that require flexible and responsive sourcing.
answer_text: As the managing authority for controlled schools, the funding authority for all grant-aided schools and the Centre of Procurement Excellence (CoPE) for education, the Education Authority (EA) is responsible for ensuring that effective procurement practice is embedded across the education sector.All schools are required to operate within the procurement legal framework and adhere to Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and all relevant Northern Ireland Procurement Policy Notes.  Notwithstanding, I share the concerns of our school stakeholders in relation to procurement processes and the benefit of offering our schools greater flexibility and reduced bureaucracy in this area.As education’s CoPE, the EA conducts thorough consultations with schools before establishing contracts. Currently, there are no contracts or frameworks specifically for Drama-related goods or services. However, direct procurement from suppliers for lower-value purchases is possible, in accordance with PPN 04/21.Additionally, the EA has confirmed that it has established a Framework for Special Educational Needs Equipment and Furniture.  This Framework includes a list of suppliers offering a wide range of specialist equipment and furniture to meet the diverse needs of SEN pupils across all schools.The recent revision of PPN 04/21 in March 2025 increased the thresholds which determine the procurement processes for lower value purchases which all contracting authorities, including schools, must follow. The threshold at which a contracting authority must evidence two price checks has increased from £5,000 to £10,000.  Given this update to PPN 04/21, I have recently instructed the EA to increase the schools’ procurement control limit to £10,000 for purchases which require two evidenced price checks.  This change will provide schools with more flexibility for those procurements below £10,000 and which are not already available through an existing framework.  This change has been communicated to schools via numerous EA channels including C2K and the EA Procurement portal.In addition to this change, I have instructed my officials to engage with the EA to explore further options available specifically in relation to schools’ use of frameworks.  The EA is exploring options for providing schools with greater flexibility in this area of spend, including an assessment of the potential benefits, risks and impact of any changes which may be considered or piloted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) why controlled schools are not permitted to procure classroom resources and subject-specific materials from suppliers offering best value for money; and (ii) what safeguards are in place to ensure the Education Authority's procurement framework does not lead to excessive costs or delays in educational provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) serves as both the funding authority and the Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for all controlled schools in Northern Ireland. As such, it is responsible for ensuring that all procurement activity is compliant with public procurement legislation, Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy (NIPPP) and associated Procurement Policy Notes (PPNs).All EA frameworks and contracts, including those for classroom resources, are established through open and competitive tendering processes, in full compliance with NIPPP and PPNs. These frameworks are designed to deliver value for money (VfM) by way of selecting the most advantageous tender (MAT).  MAT is not defined solely by cost and quality, but also sustainability. Current procurement policy also incorporates key objectives such as the removal of barriers for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and microbusinesses.Under the revised  PPN 04/21 Procurement Control Limits, schools now have greater flexibility for lower-value procurement. Where goods or services are not available through existing EA contracts or frameworks, schools may procure independently for requirements up to £10,000, provided a school obtains two price checks and can demonstrate VfM.  This provides:Flexibility to source from alternative suppliers;		Reduced administrative burden (no formal quotations required below £10,000);Opportunities to support local SMEs; andGreater autonomy and clarity in managing procurement expenditure.For procurements exceeding £10,000 that fall outside existing frameworks, the EA, as CoPE, will continue to undertake formal tender processes on behalf of schools. This ensures that procurement is led by commercial and subject matter experts, maintaining compliance with legislation and policy frameworks.(ii)	The EA seeks to avoid excessive costs or delays in educational provision by:Pre-Market Engagement: Prior to launching the Classroom Resources Framework in Autumn 2024, the EA conducted a comprehensive pre-market engagement exercise to raise awareness and stimulate supplier competition.Competitive Tendering and Savings: The resulting framework, awarded through a transparent and competitive process, has delivered over £1.4m in annual savings. The framework includes a list of competitively priced core products based on actual school usage and requirements.Extensive Product Range and Discounts: In addition to core items, the framework provides access to a wide range of products via supplier catalogues.School Representation in Evaluation: Tender evaluation panels include school representatives, including seconded and former principals, to better ensure that schools’ needs are embedded in the procurement strategy.Benchmarking for Value Assurance: The EA regularly benchmarks framework pricings against other public sector organisations and leading retailers (e.g. Tesco and Amazon). A recent benchmarking exercise confirmed that the EA’s Classroom Resources Framework offers significantly

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment on whether the current pay offer for further education lecturers, while matching the schoolteachers’ percentage increase, does not advance pay parity and therefore amounts to a standstill.
answer_text: My predecessor provided a budget envelope last year which allowed for an 11.2% pay increase for Further Education lecturers for 2023/24, which aligned with that provided for teachers. This represented a step change in the approach, and I am pleased that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer as a further step towards parity with teachers. I remain fully supportive of equitable remuneration for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will commit to funding the implementation of any agreement reached between unions and employers on a salary package for further education lecturers.
answer_text: I have been able to commit funding within the 2025/26 financial year to support the agreed pay offer for Further Education lecturers of a 5.5% pay increase for 2024/25 and a 3% pay increase for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will ensure that her Department (i) engages constructively with a review of the industrial relations framework; and (ii) will give due consideration to its recommendations.
answer_text: I am pleased that Further Education Colleges and Trade Unions have reached agreement on a pay deal for lecturers that recognises the critical role these staff play in improving our skills base. I am aware that one of the conditions agreed between employers and Trade Unions is to review the industrial relations framework and I will ensure that my Department engages constructively in this process and consider recommendations as relevant to its role as sponsor of FE Colleges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why the review into the terms and conditions and pay parity of further education lecturers, as committed to by her predecessor, has not yet been progressed.
answer_text: The responsibility for any review of terms and conditions and pay parity of Further Education lecturers lies with the Further Education colleges, as employers. I am pleased that the multi-year pay offer for Further Education lecturers has been agreed for 2024/25 and 2025/26, and I have been clear that I want to see progress at pace regarding the associated conditions, including pay parity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department will assess the traffic lights at Great James Street, Derry, as agreed by her predecessor.
answer_text: Officials in my local Network Development team would be pleased to examine this matter further if more information could be provided. They can be contacted on the following e mail: TrafficDCS@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will raise tuition fees during the remainder of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Tuition fees are raised every year in line with inflation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will engage with Echo Echo Dance Theatre Company in Foyle to discuss why they were not awarded funding via the Arts Council of Northern Ireland's Annual Funding Programme, despite meeting the eligibility criteria.
answer_text: Allocation of funding through the Annual Funding Programme is a matter for the Arts Council which has a review process available to unsuccessful applicants.It would be inappropriate for me to engage with Echo Echo to discuss why they were not awarded funding while due process is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department maintains roads after a road defect, such as a pothole, is fixed to ensure the issue does not occur again.
answer_text: My Department’s local maintenance offices inspect a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of my Department’s internal Contractor are also undertaken.The longevity of pothole repairs can be impacted by weather conditions, particularly heavy rainfall or very low temperatures, as well as the quality of the adjacent road surface. Additionally, there may be an operational need to carry out a temporary repair at certain sites to remove the defect until arrangements are made for a permanent repair. In general, however, the minimum expected longevity of a repair would be two years.However, over a decade of sustained underfunding and the resultant inability to upgrade road surfaces which have come to the end of their serviceable life has seen a continued deterioration in our infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department will provide to Echo Echo Dance Theatre in Foyle to ensure they will not be forced to close, following the decision by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland not to award them funding despite meeting the eligibility criteria.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland is the funding and development body for the arts in Northern Ireland which is responsible for the disbursement of funding provided by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what work her Department is undertaking to increase (i) export intensity; and (ii) the number of firms that export.
answer_text: There is a strong, positive relationship between exporting, innovation and productivity – and I am working with Invest NI, InterTradeIreland and others to ensure we harness our unique dual market access arrangements to grow the export base wider and deeper. We are making good progress in that regard: in the year ending December 2024, the North increased the value of its exports while Scotland, England and Wales experienced decreases.Invest NI is the main provider of export support in the North. Since the start of this year, it has delivered 12 international trade missions and exhibitions, collectively supporting 87 businesses across multiple sectors such as Food and Drink, Materials Handling, Aerospace, and Cleantech to accelerate export success in Europe, the Americas, and IMEA.Invest NI also provides tailored trade support to help local businesses to develop their footprint overseas and to increase export intensity. Support programmes such as Grow Beyond and Going Dutch have been tailored to the needs of early-stage exporters and SME’s.	The current uncertainty around tariffs is unwelcome and creates additional complexity for businesses trading internationally. I am engaging with business representatives to understand the issues at play and shape my asks of the British and Irish Governments as the situation evolves.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a lifelong learning strategy.
answer_text: My officials have been working directly with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning to co-design approaches to support a culture of lifelong learning. In April, we launched ‘Measuring the Impact of Adult Education in NI: Current Practice and Ways Forward’ (MeasuringtheImpactofAdultEd2025.pdf)– a report developed in partnership with the Alliance. We are now considering how best to progress the associated recommendations and will continue to work closely with the Alliance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the conversations she has had with the Minister of Education regarding expanding the curriculum to reflect the use of Artificial Intelligence in the workforce.
answer_text: Equipping the workforce with the necessary skills to utilise Artificial Intelligence (AI) is one vital component of how we harness the economic opportunities arising from the technology. Officials within my Department and the Department of Education collaborated in the development of the Digital Skills Action Plan launched last year, which seeks to create alternative pathways into the digital sector. Ministerial colleagues recently discussed AI and these discussions will continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the curriculum to ensure it adequately addresses Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum being carried out by Lucy Crehan considers all areas of learning across the Curriculum.The Review is nearing completion, with the report expected to be published in the coming weeks. The Review’s report will set out the core principles for curriculum re-design as well as detail any subject or phase specific issues that need to be addressed.To date, the Review engaged with a range of business and industry representatives, and other stakeholders. This engagement has raised a number of relevant issues, including the importance of digital skills, the potential impact of Artificial Intelligence and the future needs of the Northern Ireland economy.Artificial Intelligent (AI) has the potential to transform the delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. It can support curriculum planning, create interactive learning environments, and provide real-time assessments and feedback, helping students understand their progress and areas for improvement. AI-powered language learning tools can assist in teaching new languages, reading, and writing skills, adapting to the learner's pace and proficiency. However, it is also important to note the risks associated with the use of AI to support the delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, namely that content produced by AI can be inaccurate, inappropriate or biased.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps she is taking to address Northern Ireland's low productivity rate.
answer_text: Improving the North’s productivity is one of my priorities, along with more good jobs, improving regional economic balance and delivering decarbonisation commitments, and there is work taking place across my department to improve our productivity performance across the economy.  The department is delivering sectoral action plans to focus our support on the sectors that will drive up our productivity levels.  These sectors and businesses tend to produce tradeable goods and services, and Invest NI is promoting our export capacity and targeting foreign direct investment in these areas.Promoting innovation activity also underpins my approach to productivity growth, and my officials are working with Innovate UK and Invest NI to produce a joint innovation action plan to support more firms to invest in R&D and to innovate.  Additionally, the Business Innovation Grant launched its first competitive call at the end of 2024.  This is a £4.3 million scheme designed to support innovation in micro-businesses, sole traders, and SMEs.  By making innovation more accessible, the scheme is helping local businesses to modernise and expand.  Ensuring that our businesses have access to the skills they need to improve their productivity is also a key aspect of this work.  Improving skills, particularly in leadership, project management, analytics and business improvement techniques and mentoring, is crucial to enhancing productivity.  These areas are essential for adapting to change and driving innovation.  Developing more sector-specific skills in ICT, AI and green technologies is also strategically important, especially in the small and micro-business sector. As just one example of the support available for businesses to develop their skills profile, the SME Productivity Booster, launched in November 2024, offers a road map for SMEs to increase productivity while delivering the skills that will see them achieve this goal.  The initiative received a £350,000 investment as part of the wider Skills Fund to address the productivity gap by providing targeted upskilling to more than 70 local SMEs operating in key economic areas such as fintech, healthcare and advanced manufacturing. Our productivity performance is a long-standing issue, and these are just examples of the kinds of policies and interventions I am taking forward to address this.  It is important to recognise the scale of this challenge, and to note that the work of other departments also needs to align with the steps I am taking.  Improving productivity requires a healthy and well-educated workforce, and the infrastructure that businesses need to innovate and grow.  The Programme for Government places productivity at the centre of our aim to grow a globally competitive and sustainable economy and I will work across the Executive to make these aims a reality.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the action (i) her Department; and (ii) Invest NI, will take following the news of BT's decision to close its office in Derry.
answer_text: Following the announcement made by BT on 30 April 2025 I, along with officials from Invest NI, immediately met with BT senior management to discuss the matter and to impress upon them the impact of this regrettable announcement and the importance of working collectively and constructively to affect the best outcome possible for staff affected.I understand that BT has now commenced a consultation process with staff and their representatives and will guarantee employment until certain prescribed exit dates later this year as part of that process. In addition to this guarantee, staff affected will be offered the option to transfer to alternative jobs within BT Group, with BT offering to pay additional travel costs for a two-year period. I understand that BT will now also open an enhanced voluntary exit scheme for staff in Derry. A similar offer was made to staff last year in Enniskillen with over 90% opting to accept. However, for those staff members that do not wish to voluntarily leave the business my officials will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Derry City and Strabane District Council, Enterprise NI, Local Enterprise Agency and other local businesses and stakeholders, to provide advice and identify opportunities for alternative employment, self-employment and/or re-training.Invest NI will continue to engage proactively with BT senior management over the coming months, and throughout the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the discussions she has had with Invest NI regarding the closure of the BT office in Derry.
answer_text: I was extremely concerned at the announcement made by BT on 30 April 2025 regarding the future of its desk-bound operations in Derry, and on foot of that announcement I, along with officials from Invest NI, immediately met with BT senior management to discuss the matter. During that meeting I impressed upon BT the impact of this regrettable announcement and the importance of working collectively and constructively with my officials to affect the best outcome possible for staff affected. In line with my direction I can confirm that those engagements have commenced, and my officials from Invest NI met face-to-face with BT senior management following my meeting of 30 April 2025. I understand that further communications have taken place between Invest NI and BT during week commencing 05 May 2025 including where introductions were made to key Invest NI personnel in the agency’s Derry office so that local officials could be fully engaged with BT during its consultation process. Invest NI will continue to work with the company’s management over the coming weeks and months. For those staff members that do not wish to voluntarily leave the business my officials will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Derry City and Strabane District Council, Enterprise Northern Ireland, Local Enterprise Agency and other local businesses and stakeholders, to provide advice and identify opportunities for alternative employment, self-employment and/or re-training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering these proposals to examine what service changes could be introduced, subject to budget availability, to improve connectivity to the North-West

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any recent engagement she has had with the Ulster University Magee Taskforce.
answer_text: My Department is committed to continuing to engage with the Taskforce and other stakeholders on the planned Ulster University expansion at Magee.My predecessor, Minister O’Dowd, previously met with the Taskforce on 9 December 2024. More recently, the Taskforce presented a public meeting on the 6 February 2025 chaired by Stephen Kelly at which the department was present along with other stakeholders.My officials regularly attend the Student Community Relations Meeting which typically take place each month, the most recent of which took place on 8 May 2025. The minutes of these meetings are shared with the Magee Taskforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support her Department can provide to employees impacted by the closure of the BT office in Derry.
answer_text: Immediately following BT’s announcement on 30 April 2025 I, along with officials from Invest NI, met with BT senior management to discuss the matter. During the meeting I impressed upon them the impact of this regrettable announcement and the importance of working collectively and constructively to affect the best outcome possible for staff affected.I understand that BT has now commenced a consultation process with staff and their representatives and will guarantee employment until certain prescribed exit dates later this year as part of that process. In addition to this guarantee, staff affected will be offered the option to transfer to alternative jobs within BT Group, with BT offering to pay additional travel costs for a two-year period. I understand that BT will now also open an enhanced voluntary exist scheme for staff in Derry. A similar offer was made to staff last year in Enniskillen, which over 90% of staff opted to accept. For those staff that do not wish to voluntarily leave the business my officials will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Derry City and Strabane District Council, Enterprise Northern Ireland, Local Enterprise Agency and other local businesses and stakeholders, to provide advice and identify opportunities for alternative employment, self-employment and/or re-training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the membership of the Local Economic Partnerships that have been established, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Department shared guidance on membership and draft Terms of Reference (ToR) with councils in November 2024. The Guidance stipulated membership must include representatives from FE colleges, Local Enterprise Agencies, local businesses /Chamber of Commerce and third Sector. Membership outside of this will vary, depending on local economic needs and will be for local councils and LEPs to agree.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether funding for the Local Economic Partnerships has been released.
answer_text: The Regional Balance Fund Business Case required Department of Finance approval prior to my Department being able to make funding available to the Local Economic Partnerships. That approval has now been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the projects to be brought forward by Local Economic Partnerships by the end of March 2025.
answer_text: Decisions on which projects to bring forward will be made by Local Economic Partnerships. The Department and Invest NI are continuing to work closely with all councils and LEPs to support them in bringing forward projects that will help address regional imbalance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review into further education.
answer_text: I am very supportive of the critical role played by Further Education Colleges in delivering the skills which the economy needs by providing learners with high quality vocational and technical education. With courses available from access level to post graduate level, colleges have courses to suit learners of all ages. Additionally, our colleges provide support to many businesses to help them to innovate and grow. This locally based provision is critical to ensuring all areas are able to benefit from economic growth. My Department will be taking forward a co-designed programme of work in conjunction with Further Education Colleges over the next 24 months to review the Further Education delivery model. This work aims to promote greater consistency in the provision of technical and vocational educational pathways as well as innovation support for employers in pursuit of my Economic Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Mobuoy site.
answer_text: A draft remediation strategy has been developed, in line with an industry standard process, for the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site. The next step will be a public consultation. I approved the launch of the public consultation on a draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site with the expectation that it would launch in springtime. However, it has been necessary to delay the launch of the public consultation until after the completion of court proceedings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how she plans to ensure the practice of zero-hours contracts does not continue.
answer_text: I intend to end the use of exploitative zero hours contracts by providing workers who work consistent and regular hours with the same employer with the right to move to a banded hours contract.  	Additionally, I intend to introduce the right to reasonable notice of shifts, and compensation for when shifts are curtailed or cancelled on short notice for zero hours workers.  I will also ban exclusivity clauses in these contracts.My proposals aim to end exploitative zero hours contracts, while providing for work that is genuinely casual or seasonal. My proposals offer a balanced approach.  The proposals aim to provide security for those workers who are effectively working consistent and regular hours with the same employer and who wish to make the switch to a banded hours contract.  The proposals also aim to support proportionate flexibility for employers who rely on genuinely casual or seasonal work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale as to why she did not take the decision to fully ban the practice of zero-hour contracts.
answer_text: I carefully considered the responses to the consultation questions on zero hours contracts when setting my policy in this area. Some businesses rely on genuinely casual or seasonal work and some workers wish to work on this basis. I have therefore decided to permit a limited number of exemptions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail a timeline for the publication of her Department's research into the potential costs and benefits of paid carer’s leave.
answer_text: I have published the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) briefing paper on carer’s leave. It can be accessed via this link: Introducing Carer’s Leave in Northern Ireland briefing paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department engaged with Carers NI prior to her decision to provide the legislative basis for paid carer’s leave.
answer_text: I can confirm that since April 2024 departmental officials have been constructively and openly engaging with Carer’s NI at regular intervals and will continue to do so.My predecessor, Minister Murphy, met with CarersNI in August 2024, just prior to the launch of the Good Jobs consultation. As part of my stakeholder engagement, I also met with Carers NI to provide a briefing in advance of my Ministerial statement on Monday 28th April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the British Government in relation to introducing a statutory entitlement to paid carer’s leave.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with counterparts from the Department for Business and Trade regarding their Employment Rights Bill and proposed review into the operation of carer’s leave in Britain. In addition, officials have engaged with HMRC regarding the system changes which would be necessary to operationalise a paid carer’s leave entitlement in the north of Ireland. My predecessor, Minister Murphy, and I have also discussed the issues impacting unpaid carers and the desirability of paid carer’s leave with the responsible Minister, Justin Madders MP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has costed the introduction of paid carer's leave.
answer_text: An independent review of the costs and benefits of paid carer’s leave was undertaken by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) on behalf of my Department.  I have now published the UUEPC briefing paper on carer’s leave. It can be accessed via this link: Introducing Carer’s Leave in Northern Ireland briefing paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she expects to introduce paid carer's leave.
answer_text: In the first instance my Department must be given the legal powers to introduce a paid carer’s leave entitlement. I hope this will be provided during this mandate. The entitlement will then be introduced when the Executive agrees to fund it from within its own resources, or when funding is provided because it has been introduced by the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11610/22-27, (i) for an update on her Department's Active Travel Delivery Plan; and (ii) whether there are any plans to extend the footway and cycleway from Drumahoe towards Altnagelvin on the right-hand side of the road.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan including network maps for our larger towns and cities. The extension of the footway and cycleway at this location was included in the draft plan published for consultation.Public consultation on the draft plan, including consultation on the Department’s draft priority routes within the plan, closed on 28 February 2025. My officials are currently reviewing the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn.I plan to give careful consideration to all feedback before publishing the plan later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will introduce legislation in relation to whistleblowing in the workplace.
answer_text: In order to provide further assurance that prescribed persons are fulfilling their responsibilities in relation to protected disclosures, my Department intends to legislate, using existing powers under the Employment Rights Act 2016, to introduce a requirement for prescribed persons to produce annual reports and submit them to the Department. This proposed change will also be a useful means of capturing information on disclosure trends.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why her statement, made on the 28 April 2025, did not include any mention of changing industrial action notice periods.
answer_text: I do not plan to amend the current industrial action notice period of seven days within the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in the last 12 months in relation to the introduction of minimum unit pricing.
answer_text: ANSWERI have not had any direct engagement with the Health Minister on the minimum unit pricing of alcohol to date. I am of course happy to engage with Minister Nesbitt on this matter.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they are taking to address the budget deficit in relation to the Mute Meadows project in Ebrington, Derry.
answer_text: The Department is liaising with Derry City and Strabane District Council on the repair of Mute Meadows and is supportive of the project.  We understand a concept design exists for relighting the artwork and a cost estimate has been compiled.  On receipt of costs from Council we will consider a contribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Health in the last 12 months in relation to the introduction of minimum unit pricing.
answer_text: During the last 12 months neither I nor my predecessor Minister O’Dowd have had any engagement with the Minister of Health in relation to the introduction of minimum unit pricing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Health in the last 12 months in relation to the introduction of minimum unit pricing.
answer_text: I have not had any direct engagement, outside of written correspondence and discussion at Executive, with the Minister of Health in the last 12 months in relation to the introduction of minimum unit pricing; however, my Departmental officials have met with Department of Health (DoH) officials in relation to this issue. I remain supportive of the introduction of minimum unit pricing.Going forward, my Department will remain engaged not only with DoH, but also the PSNI and the Department for Communities as the policy develops and aspects particularly relevant to my Department are firmed up, such as enforcement and any potential costs or savings to the Justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of inward investment visits that have taken place in each month over the last five years, broken down by (i) constituency; and (ii) local council area.
answer_text: Tables 1-5 show the number of visits hosted by Invest NI by potential foreign direct investors which took place in each Parliamentary Constituency Area in each of the years between 2020-2021 and 2024-2025. Tables 6-10 show the number of visits hosted by Invest NI by potential foreign direct investors which took place in each District Council Area in each of the years between 2020-2021 and 2024-2025. A potential investor may choose to make a number of visits, and to several council areas, before deciding on a location for their investment project.  While Belfast shows a higher number of visits, these figures include initial meetings which are most often held at Invest NI’s head office in Belfast. This can be due to the investors’ travel plans, other meetings taking place as part of their visit, or time constraints.
