Number of Foreign Direct I

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the delay the roll-out of the Renewable Electricity Scheme will have on her Department's ability to meet the targets contained in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to deliver the 80% by 2030 target. The introduction of a renewable electricity support scheme is crucial in enabling delivery of additional renewable electricity capacity and I plan to bring primary legislation before the Assembly this year, with subordinate regulations to follow as we move towards implementation. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the 80% by 2030 target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 25442/22-27, for his assessment of the drop in birth rate.
answer_text: ANSWERAs noted in AQW 25442/22-27, fertility rates have declined in the last decade while first time mothers are waiting longer to have children.Evidence shows that while part of this may be explained by the age of marriage increasing, nearly as many births now happen outside marriage here. Up to the early 1980’s more than 90% of births registered each year were to a married couple. Since then, the proportions have almost evened out with 54% of birth registrations in 2023 to married couples or couples in a civil partnership, and 46% either to unmarried couples or single people.Wider UK research indicates that in addition to having children at older ages, young adults are tending to stay longer in their parental home, which could contribute to delaying starting a family. The potential reasons for staying at home longer and making decisions around when and if to start a family, such as education, living costs, lifestyle etc, would require investigation covering a number of themes cutting across several Departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 24533/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 24533/22-27 was answered on 23 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the allocation to childcare in the 2025-26 Budget, for his assessment of his Department's ability to fully fund an early learning and childcare strategy.
answer_text: The allocation for early learning and childcare in the 2024-25 budget will enable me to fund a package of interim measures in the current financial year.  I do not have baseline budget to maintain these in subsequent years or fund the more comprehensive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I intend to bring forward this autumn.  It will be for the Executive to decide on the level of funding it is prepared to allocate to the strategy, consistent with the commitment in the Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the roll-out of the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme.
answer_text: I plan to bring primary legislation before the Assembly this year, with subordinate regulations to follow as we move towards implementation. The upcoming publication on the final scheme design will provide a full update including eligibility criteria, delivery roles and responsibilities, and an outline of the contract allocation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on (i) the consultation currently being undertaken; and (ii) the consultation proposed to be undertaken as part of the Energy Strategy Mid-Term Review which was due to commence in January 2025.
answer_text: The mid-term review of delivery of the Energy Strategy commenced in January 2025 and will complete within the year. The review considers progress from the Strategy’s launch in December 2021 to 2025 and will establish the key milestones to deliver in the period 2026 to 2030.  My officials will establish a core stakeholder group of DfE’s energy delivery partners and independent expert advisors, to provide their insight at a workshop in this quarter (Q2 2025). We will engage with wider stakeholders through a range of communication methods as the review progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the proposed membership of the Grid Development Monitoring Group, as referenced in the Energy Strategy The Path to Net Zero Energy Action Plan 2025.
answer_text: The terms of reference and structure for this group are currently being developed and as such have not been finalised. The group itself will have both the Department and Utility Regulator as members.  SONI and NIE Networks will update the group regularly on progress in relation to key grid developments. Any decisions relating to the regulation of these companies will be for the Utility Regulator.  Where clarity or feedback is required in relation to energy policy, this will be considered by the Department.  Other key industry stakeholders may be invited to join at the appropriate times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the meeting and reporting structure of the Grid Development Monitoring Group, referenced in the Energy Strategy The Path to Net Zero Energy Action Plan 2025.
answer_text: The meeting and reporting structure for this group is currently being developed and as such has not been finalised.  The group itself will have the Department and Utility Regulator as members. SONI and NIE Networks will update the group regularly on progress in relation to key grid developments. Any decisions relating to the regulation of these companies will be for the Utility Regulator.  Where clarity or feedback is required in relation to energy policy, this will be considered by the Department.  Other key industry stakeholders may be invited to join at the appropriate times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether EU Regulation 2023/1115 on products related to deforestation will apply in Northern Ireland from 30 December 2025; and (ii) any preparation that has taken place.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 on products related to deforestation no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and now comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her Department's assessment of the role that long duration energy storage will play in supporting the delivery of the renewable electricity consumption target of at least 80 per cent by 2030.
answer_text: One of my priorities is to reduce carbon emissions and generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change.It is important to build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to deliver the 80% by 2030 target.  I recognise the important role that long duration energy storage will play in decarbonising the electricity grid, in particular its ability to utilise excess renewable electricity and export power back to the grid at times of high demand.The Department has commissioned research on interconnection and energy storage here. This research will include analysis on the effect of energy storage on the cost to local consumers, security of supply and decarbonisation, and help inform policy development on both short and long duration energy storage. The Department aims to complete policy development in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to support addiction services in Derry.
answer_text: A Population Needs-Based Assessment for Substance Use in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area was completed in Autumn 2023, providing insights into local challenges. PEACEPLUS funding of 8M Euros has been secured for a Community Detox Plus (CAD+) project to support individuals with alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland’s border counties including the North West.A recently completed Independent Review of Tier 4 Regional In-patient Detoxification and Residential Rehabilitation Service has recommended the development of a Regional Specialist Assessment, Treatment, and Recovery Network.This Review was subject to public consultation and highlights the need for evidence-based, regionally consistent, equitable, and value-for-money services. The Review’s highest priority recommendation is the development of a sustainable network of specialist recovery and treatment services across our region.All of the above work streams will be prioritised by officials in line with agreed timelines and availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre in Foyle following the decision to withdraw core grant funding.
answer_text: Firstly, I want to assure you that I value the work of the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre, and I want to place on record my thanks for their continuing work to support those in need. The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives. The Scheme was launched in November, along with confirmation that the budget would be £1.8m. With 259 bids totalling over £13m being submitted to the Department, demand was clearly always going to outstrip supply. The general standard of applications was incredibly high, and the selection of 25 high scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on the agreed scoring criteria rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The scoring criteria can be found in the guidance for applicants, available on my Department’s website.The Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre have not been successful in their application for Core Grant in 2025/26, which I understand will be disappointing for the organisation. My officials have been in contact with Northlands to offer feedback, which will assist them in applying again in future funding rounds. Indeed, I expect to issue another call for applications this Autumn for 2026/27 funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for the decision to withdraw core grant funding to the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre, Derry.
answer_text: Firstly, I want to assure you that I value the work of the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre, and I want to place on record my thanks for their continuing work to support those in need. The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives. The Scheme was launched in November, along with confirmation that the budget would be £1.8m. With 259 bids totalling over £13m being submitted to the Department, demand was clearly always going to outstrip supply. The general standard of applications was incredibly high, and the selection of 25 high scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on the agreed scoring criteria rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The scoring criteria can be found in the guidance for applicants, available on my Department’s website.The Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre have not been successful in their application for Core Grant in 2025/26, which I understand will be disappointing for the organisation. My officials have been in contact with Northlands to offer feedback, which will assist them in applying again in future funding rounds. Indeed, I expect to issue another call for applications this Autumn for 2026/27 funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department will facilitate access to cross-border addiction services in the North West.
answer_text: Addressing alcohol and drug-related harm is a key priority for the Department of Health, who lead on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy, ‘Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery’.To achieve the Strategy's vision, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have co-produced the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-2028. This Plan outlines strategic priorities for improving services for those affected by substance use, including families and carers,  and is being progressed subject to available funding.The Department of Health is also progressing several related workstreams to address the needs of people seeking addiction services in the North West and across Northern Ireland. These include:A Population-Based Needs Assessment for Substance Use in the Western Health & Social Care Trust Area.An Independent Review of Tier 4 Substance Use Services, including In-patient Detoxification and Residential Rehabilitation Services.Additionally, under the European PEACEPLUS Programme's theme ‘Healthy & Inclusive Communities, Investment Area 4.1 Collaborative Health and Social Care: Addiction Services’, CAWT partner organisations have secured €8 million for the COMMUNITY DETOX PLUS Project. This project aims to:Increase access to community detoxification in border communities.Reduce unplanned and unsupervised detoxifications.Shorten hospital stays following medical alcohol detoxification.Provide rapid community follow-up to enhance service attendance rates and early intervention.The Department of Health continues to collaborate with counterparts in Ireland and the UK through the British Irish Council and the North South Ministerial Council to share learning and coordinate actions on substance use issues at a national level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress made on the development of a second North South Interconnector
answer_text: The North South Interconnector Project is essential for the local economy, and timelines associated with it are vital.  SONI estimates that the cost to local electricity consumers due to the absence of the interconnector are currently estimated at £19m a year. My Department has recently been informed by SONI that the completion date has been revised from 2028 to 2031.  EirGrid, the system operator in the South, has advised that this is due both to delays on processing land access and on actual construction time. The Department is concerned at the ongoing significant cost implication for electricity customers in the North. Therefore, we are engaging with officials in the Southern Government, to explore options for mitigating or reducing these delays.NIE Networks advises that it is on track to meet its projected construction date of 2028 and has commenced construction. My Department has a statutory responsibility to consider applications for compulsory wayleaves and has to date, granted 29 necessary wayleaves.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Samaritan's March 2025 report Lost Futures: Poverty, Inequality, and Suicidality in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I fully agree with the findings within the report. The report adds further to feedback received during stakeholder engagements for the review of the Protect Life 2 Action Plan.Protect Life 2 has long recognised suicide as a major inequality and the disparity between suicide rates in the most deprived areas and the least deprived areas is stark. For this reason the Action Plan focuses investment on those areas where rates are highest and the greatest difference can be made. The new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and Implementation Plan will underline the importance of a cross-departmental approach to bring down the rates of suicide and this cohesive approach was agreed at the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention on 3 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has undertaken any research into the decline of the birthrate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ANSWEROfficial statistics on birth registration numbers and fertility rates here are produced by the NISRA, using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO). Based on birth registrations information, NISRA statistics show that the total period fertility rate (TPFR) has reduced from 1.95 in 2013 to 1.64 in 2023. In practical terms, this means that the average family size here has been decreasing in the last decade. Additionally, the mean age of first-time mothers in general has steadily increased from 24.5 in 1983 to 29.5 in 2023, indicating that women are waiting longer before starting families.NISRA also supports the development and maintenance of the NI Longitudinal Study (NILS) for accredited research purposes. The NILS is a wide-ranging database of people and their major life events covering 28% of the population and which can support a range of research including fertility trends.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the low carbon hydrogen policy consultation, due to be published in 2025, will focus only on green hydrogen proposals.
answer_text: The NI Executive’s Energy Strategy states that we can utilise increasing levels of renewable electricity for green hydrogen production to make our electricity system more flexible. The hydrogen consultation will focus on low carbon hydrogen which is defined in the British Government’s Low Carbon Hydrogen Standard. This will include green hydrogen and other renewable methods of producing hydrogen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out research in relation to the impact the Electronic Travel Authorisation scheme will have on the economy.
answer_text: I wrote to Seema Malhotra MP, Home Office Minister for Migration and Citizenship last month in advance of the scheme going live for EU Nationals on 2 April. I urged Minister Malhotra to reconsider the implementation of the ETA scheme including the potential to introduce an exemption to protect our valuable tourism industry, as proposed by the NI Tourism Alliance (NITA). I also communicated that the scheme is already having a damaging impact on our local tourism sector. I will continue to press the British Government on this issue, to reiterate my own strong concerns, and to seek a response to the correspondence issued to Minister Malhotra from Minister Murphy in December and January which sought a pragmatic solution.My Department and officials from Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI have been working alongside industry representatives and key stakeholders regarding the potential impact on our tourism sector from the introduction of the ETA Scheme. This has included close engagement and discussions with representative bodies including NITA and the Inbound Tour Operators Association (ITOA). The implementation of the ETA scheme is already having a damaging impact on the local tourism sector. NITA is reporting the impacts on its members, especially tour operators, with tours and trips being cancelled and the North being removed from itineraries due to the requirement for visitors to have an ETA.NITA and ITOA are jointly working with other industry stakeholders to further assess the impact that the requirement for an ETA is having on businesses offering travel to the North. This will add to its evidence base to support its call for an exemption for tourists travelling from the South into the North. In addition, Tourism Ireland will continue to monitor key overseas markets in relation to ETA.  This includes a Sentiment Tracker Survey which indicated that 1 in 5 people (coming from North America) and 1 in 4 (coming from Europe) are likely not to travel due to an ETA.I am ambitious to grow the value of our tourism economy to mitigate any impacts of the ETA Scheme. In January my Department published a 10 Year Tourism Vision & Action Plan, which set a Strategic Goal that the economic value of our tourism expenditure from overnight visitors will exceed £2 billion by 2035.  The Vision and Action Plan outlined 17 actions to drive sustainable, regionally balanced economic growth in the sector, including: Incorporating local regions into Failte Ireland’s regional brands; Developing peace tourism;Ensuring an adequate mix of accommodation right across the north;Developing skills in the industry;Development of Destination Stewardship Plans; andRestoring appropriate funding to Tourism Ireland to increase tourism revenue from overseas. It has launched exciting international marketing plans for 2025.In addition, my officials and colleagues from Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI continue to engage with Home Office and other partners, to inform their development and roll-

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: The timetabling of services is primarily an operational matter for Translink.  The Public Service Agreement allows Translink to amend services, but they must do so in a cost-effective manner, consistent with the required level of quality and performance, and minimise costs.In June 2024 Translink submitted a business case to my Department:Introduce an hourly Sunday Service.Provide an earlier morning departure from Derry to connect with the 0800 hours Enterprise service to DublinExtend the 22.40 service from Belfast to terminate in Derry rather than ColeraineMy officials are currently considering the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has engaged with Translink in relation to increasing the number of trains from Derry to Belfast on a Sunday.
answer_text: Officials frequently engage with Translink on provision of services including the potential amendments to scheduled services. As this is an operational matter for Translink I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:As part of the Belfast to Derry Enhancement Railway Programme, the Strategic Outline Business Case (SOC) is considering increased journey frequencies. The SOC is expected to be submitted to my department by Autumn 2025.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of a redress scheme for those injured by vaginal mesh.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact of the UK Chancellor's Spring Statement 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to address health inequalities.
answer_text: Health inequalities are one of my areas of focus as Health Minister. It needs sustained collaboration across NI Executive Departments and Agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled to lead healthy lives. The next stage of MLB is currently being considered, taking into account the many recent developments in this space, whilst also considering the need for the most effective and efficient use of existing resources.MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. These policies also have a focus on health inequalities and are implemented on the ground by the Public Health Agency (PHA).I have also introduced a new targeted, place-based approach called Live Better which has been designed to help address health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most. Live Better is being delivered in two initial test areas in Derry/Londonderry (Fountain, Bogside, Brandywell and Creggan) and in Belfast (Lower Shankill, Lower Falls and Grosvenor Road).Live Better is pulling existing Health and Social Care initiatives together so that they can be delivered intensively in communities to make a real difference. Stakeholder engagement has been undertaken and outcomes for each area have been identified – focusing on Starting Well, Living Well, and Ageing Well. The delivery stage of Live Better began in earnest in January, and programmes are underway, supported by health fairs/checks, health literacy training, and health inequalities training.While the Live Better approach is currently focused entirely within the two selected areas, the evaluation of this initial phase will inform the future development and mainstreaming of the approach into business as usual for the benefit of all communities across Northern Ireland. The Live Better approach is referenced in the Programme for Government and will be reflected in the next stage of MLB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24532/22-27, to detail why his Department has not undertaken any research into the decline of the birth rate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Education has not undertaken research into the decline of the birth rate but is aware of the work the Department of Finance undertake on population estimates and projections.  The Department of Finance have advised that official statistics on birth registration numbers and fertility rates in Northern Ireland are produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA), using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO). NISRA has not conducted research into reasons for the reduction in fertility as it involves a wide range of factors which are not captured as part of the birth registration process. NISRA use birth registration data to produce mid-year population estimates and population projections.  Officials in the Department of Education’s Statistics and Research Team use NISRA’s population projections to produce school population projections. The Education Authority also uses NISRA’s birth rates and population projections, along with the Department’s school population projections to inform area planning, predominantly in the primary phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to recruit more gynaecologists with a speciality in endometriosis.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time.  In this regard my Department has secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time Report on gynaecology and progressing implementation of its recommendations. This will include the progression of these recommendations in the NCEPOD.The GIRFT report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through a coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully. Where regional commissioning/investment is required, the timescale for implementation will be dependent on the complexity and scale of the issue, available resource and other commissioning priorities.   One of the regional actions was in respect of establishing a Task and Finish Group to develop and implement an approved pathway for endometriosis.  This group has recently been established and work is underway with key stakeholders to commence this recommendation, to ensure an improved pathway for women.  This includes those women diagnosed with endometriosis and those on the pathway to get a diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address regional imbalance.
answer_text: Reform is one way we can ensure better and equitable access to healthcare for everyone and address any regional imbalances. I have said before that the pressures evident in our health and social care system underscore the necessity for change. But it is not change for change’s sake. The goal is to ensure people have access to the right services at the right time, resulting in better outcomes.Last December I set out a three-year plan to improve primary and hospital care, social care, productivity, and patient safety. Not only does the plan set ambitious goals to improve services such as Mental Health and Elective Care. It also brings a stronger focus on reducing health inequalities.  The HSC has experienced unprecedented financial challenges in recent years. This has meant the ability to address equity issues has been limited.  The financial outlook undoubtedly remains challenging; however, reform and having a three-year road map will go some way to help mitigate the impact of an inadequate budget.On a more technical front, my Department uses a formula to determine fair shares of funding provided to HSC Trusts. This takes account of issues such as population, age/gender weighting, additional need and rurality.  My Department then carries out detailed equity comparisons of Trust baseline funding, to inform a strategy to address any gaps between fair shares and Trust baseline funding.It is also important to note that robust Equality and Rural Needs assessment processes are in place to ensure any potential impact of policies or other decisions on people and rural areas are fully considered.The Hospital Network consultation outlined a set of high- level principles to maximise collaborative working and ensure that people receive the right care, in the right place, at the right time.  It is not possible for all hospitals to do all things, so different hospitals will provide different services.  I also want to see more care moving closer to people’s homes, to be delivered in the community, rather than in our acute hospitals. Moving to an increasingly networked hospital approach should mean that hospital services should be equally accessible to everyone for example through the creation of regional lists. This would also mean that some people may have to travel further to receive services. The goal is equal access and safer, more sustainable services. My officials are now analysing the responses from the consultation which I will then consider before producing a report setting out the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of gynaecologists with a speciality in endometriosis across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, as women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  The service model for endometriosis recognises the impact of this condition on the health and well-being of women.  The basis of the care pathway model is delivery of care by a Specialist Multidisciplinary Team in order to meet the holistic needs of women undergoing diagnosis and treatment.  The MDT is best able to provide responsive, appropriate care throughout the patient journey.  Each member of the team functions as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, or other professional lead delivering care. All consultant gynaecologist provide care for patients with endometriosis.  Identifying the number of endometriosis specialists in place cannot be confined to a single profession, as this is not how care is provided and not in line with best practice.   My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time.  In this regard my Department has secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time Report on gynaecology and progressing implementation of its recommendations. This will include the progression of these recommendations in the NCEPOD.The GIRFT report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through a coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully.One of the regional actions was in respect of establishing a Task and Finish Group to develop and implement an approved pathway for endometriosis.  This group has recently been established and work is underway with key stakeholders to take forward this recommendation and ensure an improved pathway for women.  This includes those women diagnosed with endometriosis and those on the pathway to get a diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the research project, Delivering balanced regional growth in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This project, being delivered by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre is progressing well and is scheduled for completion in April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 23679/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 23679/22-27 was answered on 2 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently recorded as homeless in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The quarterly Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin, published on 27 February, contains information on homelessness and is available at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-housing-bulletin-october-december-2024Table 2.2 contains details on social rented sector waiting lists by local government district and provides a breakdown of the total number of applicants, including those in housing stress and those with Full Duty Applicant status as at 31 December 2024. I am, however, unable to provide a breakdown in the specific format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the data his Department collects in relation to homelessness.
answer_text: My Department does not collect homelessness data directly. All data published by my Department via the biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin and the quarterly Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin is collected by the Housing Executive and provided to my Department’s Analytics Division in aggregate form.The official statistics include data on homelessness presentations, acceptances and temporary accommodation as well as social rented sector waiting lists and allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Homelessness in Northern Ireland report by the Northern Ireland Audit Office.
answer_text: The Department welcomes the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report into Homelessness in Northern Ireland.This report recognises the complexity of the issue of homelessness and acknowledges the considerable effort and substantial investment which goes towards addressing this problem.The Department will now take time to consider its findings and recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the resurfacing work to be carried out in the Foyle constituency during the summer period.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including those in the Foyle constituency, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is responsible for regulating fees for private nursing homes.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the regulation of fees for private nursing homes but does have responsibility for setting regional tariff rates across Programmes of Care for residents placed within both nursing and residential homes. The regional tariff rate is a guide to what the HSC considers to be fair and affordable, it is not a fixed price for a care placement. While the regional tariff rate is a matter for the Department it has no role in establishing the rates paid for care. Separate to establishing the regional tariff rate, independent care providers are responsible for setting their own care home fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the reorganisation of Invest NI.
answer_text: A restructure of the Invest NI leadership team is still progressing with interviews for the positions of Chief Operating Officer, Chief Development Officer and Chief Commercial Officer recently conducted. Offers have been made to the successful candidates, and we hope to be in a position to communicate further information within the next fortnight. It is anticipated that most of the appointees will take up post by the end of Q1 2025/26. Transition arrangements remain in place to ensure business continuity throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 23012/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the projects to be brought forward by Local Economic Partnerships by the end of March 2025.
answer_text: Councils in some areas are still in the process of forming Local Economic Partnerships (LEPs), those that have agreed membership and focus are now discussing potential projects. Once these are identified, my officials and Invest NI Regional Managers will work with the Partnership to agree an action plan for each LEP.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how they ensured that all responses to the Programme for Government 2024-2027 consultation exercise were carefully considered and provided to all Executive Ministers.
answer_text: The Executive Ministers met to discuss consultation findings on the 21 November 2024 and a robust process was followed thereafter between The Executive Office (TEO) and all departments. Analysis by NISRA and TEO resulted in the production of a consultation report which includes a summary of the feedback provided by more than 1,400 individuals and organisations. Intensive analysis and cross-checking between quantitative and qualitative responses was undertaken in order to identify key and emerging issues. The Programme for Government was approved by the Assembly on Monday 3 March 2025, and was made public immediately, as was the consultation report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the British Government in relation to the proposed welfare benefit cuts.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 24250/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the roll-out of the Renewable Electricity Support Scheme.
answer_text: I plan to bring primary legislation before the Assembly this year, with subordinate regulations to follow as we move towards implementation. The upcoming publication on the final scheme design will provide a full update including eligibility criteria, delivery roles and responsibilities, and an outline of the contract allocation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with trade unions in relation to her Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: My department has engaged with trade unions on numerous occasions in relation to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill. This engagement has included introductory meetings with relevant officials, a Ministerial led engagement forum meeting and three workshop style meetings prior to consultation launch. It has also included official attendance at four Labour Relations Agency Engagement Forum meetings.My predecessor met with trade union representatives during the consultation period and trade unions also attended an academic event run by my department prior to the consultation closing. Many of these events were also attended by representatives of business organisations.Since the consultation closed my predecessor met with union representatives to discuss the UNI Europa event in Belfast from 24th to 26th March and its relevance to the Good Jobs Bill.  I spoke at this conference to delegates of trade union representatives from across Europe.A departmental official attended the Irish Congress of Trade Unions biennial delegate conference in November of last year.Eighteen responses to the consultation were received from trade unions and the main trade union representative body.My officials and I will continue to liaise with key stakeholders, including trade unions, as the planned Bill passes through the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the £50 million earmarked for early years and childcare in the Executive's Draft Budget 2025-26 will be spent.
answer_text: I am currently considering options.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues for approval.  Only at that point will the £50m be available to my department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether any of their Department's arm’s-length bodies are currently being reviewed; and (ii) whether any such reviews are planned to be commissioned or completed during the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: An independent reviewer has recently completed a statutory review of the operation of the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA).  We are currently considering the reviewer’s report and recommendations.  A review of the Strategic Investment Board was completed in 2024.  The final draft report is also currently under consideration.  There are plans to review the Equality Commission of Northern Ireland (ECNI), which will likely take place in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has undertaken any research into the decline of the birthrate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Education has not undertaken any research in relation to the declining birth rate in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much of the £25 million package of measures to support children, parents and providers with early learning and childcare in 2024-25 represents the expenditure of net direct subsidy to parents under the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: As of 20 March, £7.8 million has been paid out under the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to reduce parents’ childcare bills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23951/22-27, how much of the £1.9 million total cost for the scheme was the net administration costs paid to providers.
answer_text: £1.3m of the £1.9m referenced in AQW 23951/22-27 was paid to childcare providers to administer the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the policy scoping work undertaken by her Department in relation to the family court system.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated to Lurgan Junior High School for the replacement pitch works.
answer_text: Under an existing licence agreement between the Education Authority (EA) and Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council, the overall cost of the project for replacement of synthetic surfaces and shock pads to the 2G hockey pitch and 3G football pitch at Lurgan Junior High School will be split 50/50 between the two organisations. The EA’s contribution to the total funding will be £396,724.56.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any discussions with the UK Treasury on the possible provision of Treasury reserve funding to the PSNI in order to meet the cost of claims against the organisation.
answer_text: I raised this issue in my bilateral with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) on 27 February 2025. The issue had also been raised by my predecessor Dr Caoimhe Archibald.The CST current position is that the Executive would be expected to absorb this from within its current budget. This is not a position I agree with, as these are exceptional costs which could not be absorbed within existing budgets without inflicting considerable damage on public services. Therefore, I will continue to engage the Treasury on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the administration costs for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The total administration costs for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme paid up to 21 February 2025 (the latest date available) are just over £1.9 million.  This includes payments to childcare providers for operating the scheme and the delivery agent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has engaged with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to the possible proposals that cash payments of Personal Independence Payment will be replaced by vouchers.
answer_text: My officials are in the early stages of engaging with officials in the Department for Work and Pensions to understand any proposed changes to Personal Independence Payment and will assess the impact in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much of the £9 million allocated to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme has been used.
answer_text: The final spend position is not yet available.  However, monitoring to date indicates that the profile spend is broadly in line with the earmarked allocation.  I will provide the final expenditure position at the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered replacing cash payments of Personal Independence Payment with vouchers.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 23949/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether it is still her policy to expand Ulster University’s Magee campus to at least 10,000 students; and (ii) why the wording in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 states that the expansion of Magee will be up to 10,000 students.
answer_text: My policy remains firmly focussed on expanding Ulster University’s Magee campus to 10,000 students in line with the NDNA commitment and Programme for Government.  I am fully committed to ensuring its successful delivery as reflected in my Department’s Business Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to establish a night-time economy taskforce.
answer_text: The night-time economy is a valuable aspect of our broader economy, with impacts on our tourism and hospitality industry and our sense of place. Its success requires collaboration between a range of actors. My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts, have recently commissioned research to understand the value of our night-time economy and the issues facing it. The research will include recommendations on how we develop a thriving, safe and diverse night-time economy and will point to best practice elsewhere. My Department will review the research findings and share the recommendations through existing fora on the night-time economy such as the Purple Flag Committee and the Night-Time Economy Commission, both of which the Department has representation upon. The research will inform decisions on whether there is a requirement for a night-time economy taskforce in addition to these existing fora. I remain committed to engaging with stakeholders and assessing the research findings as we seek to develop our night-time economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: I anticipate that my policy proposals following the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill consultation will be published in Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20893/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider implementing a Court of Protection to allow parents of a young person with disabilities to get a mental capacity assessment before the age of 18 years old.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA), a piece of legislation brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice, provides a framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they may lack capacity.The MCA has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. When the relevant justice provisions are commenced, the High Court in Northern Ireland will assume equivalent powers to those of the Court of Protection in England and Wales in relation to mental capacity. The court will have powers to make a declaration about the mental capacity of persons who are 16 years old or older, and to appoint a deputy for such persons. As these provisions fall within the remit of the Department of Justice, commencement would be a matter for the Justice Minister to consider. While there are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA, my Department is currently working collaboratively with Department of Justice officials to develop an updated implementation plan to better understand the resource need for full MCA implementation, which will help to guide future Departmental decision making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the research project, Delivering balanced regional growth in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This project, being delivered by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre is progressing well and is scheduled for completion in April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail where the 5,850 new build social homes, as outlined in the Programme for Government 2024-2027, will be developed, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Social Housing Development Plan (SHDP) is a three-year rolling programme of planned social housing schemes based on the identification and analysis of housing need by geographical area. The Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group (DPG) manages the SHDP on behalf of my Department. DPG is responsible for in-year management of the SHDP through close working relationships with our housing association development partners.Programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure planning approval. Also, schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.For these reasons I can’t confirm the exact locations of where these schemes will be located.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will be introduced to the Assembly by the end of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Subject to agreement from my Executive colleagues, I intend to introduce an Employment Rights Bill in the Assembly by January 2026, or earlier if there is scope to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what steps he is taking to ensure people who are eligible for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme benefit from savings from reduced childcare costs.
answer_text: I have secured £50m for Early Learning and Childcare provision in the draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation.  This will allow the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) to continue into the next financial year.  	I am also aware that many childcare providers are planning to increase their fees over the coming weeks.  Following any price increases applied by providers, eligible parents will continue to benefit from bills which are 15% lower than they would otherwise have been, with a further 20% subsidy applied from TFC.  Together, these schemes ensure that the vast majority of working families will continue to receive a 32% reduction on their total childcare bills, even when fees increase.		Additionally, I have decided to increase the monthly subsidy cap for the Scheme by 10% to help ensure a greater proportion of parents benefit from the full 15% reduction to their bill.  		I will also be increasing the administration payments for participating providers in recognition of their increased operating costs and to ensure these are not passed on to parents.  The Early Years Organisation, the delivery agent for the scheme, will shortly advise providers and parents of the full details of the updated subsidy cap and the enhanced administration rates, both of which will take effect from 1 April 2025.	I remain committed to ensuring that the scheme continues to deliver real benefits for working families.  To that end, I am considering what further enhancements would be affordable within the allocation I have secured in the draft Executive budget for 2025-26.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the review of the Small Business Rate Relief scheme.
answer_text: As part of the Strategic Review process announced by my predecessor in December, the review of both the Small Business Rate Relief and Non-Domestic Vacant Rating exclusions will commence at the start of the forthcoming 2025/26 rating year. Critically that process will build on evidence from the Cost of Doing Business research being undertaken by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre. I will be engaging with business stakeholders later in the week on this and a range other key issues of interest to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how much funding from the special educational needs capital budget has been allocated towards minor works schemes, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The total capital SEN budget for 2024/25 is currently £50.443m.  Funding is not allocated by strand or constituency.£42.243m has been allocated to the EA to deliver the SEN capital investment programme in special schools and controlled mainstream schools across the following four strands:Strand 1 - annual maintenance and equipment programme Strand 2 - new specialist provisions in mainstream Strand 3 - extension and refurbishment of special schoolsStrand 4 - new special schools £8.2m was allocated to the SEN capital investment team in my department for specialist provision and associated works in non-controlled schools (Strand 2) as my department has budget responsibility for capital works in these schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children in receipt of free school meals, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: School Census information shows the number of children entitled to free school meals broken down by constituency of school location. This information relates to census day, 11 October 2024.ConstituencyTotal pupils entitled to free school mealsBelfast East                       4,079 Belfast North                       9,511 Belfast South and Mid Down                       5,015 Belfast West                     10,644 East Antrim                       3,140 East Londonderry                       4,552 Fermanagh and South Tyrone                       4,093 Foyle                       8,275 Lagan Valley                       3,480 Mid Ulster                       4,200 Newry and Armagh                       5,979 North Antrim                       4,225 North Down                       2,785 South Antrim                       3,418 South Down                       4,021 Strangford                       3,351 Upper Bann                       5,588 West Tyrone                       4,579 Total                     90,935 Source: NI School censusFigures include nursery schools, primary (including preschool age pupils enrolled in Specialist Provision in Mainstream, nursery classes, reception and year 1 - 7 classes), post primary and special schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with special educational needs at post-primary schools in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: There are 1,782 pupils with special education needs enrolled in post primary schools in the Foyle constituency.  This information relates to the 2024/25 academic year and is sourced from the annual school census. Census day this academic year was 11 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how schools were selected to participate a pilot to increase specialist provision capacity within mainstream schools.
answer_text: The member has clarified that the questions are in relation to the SEN Pilot - Specialist provision in mainstream schools (October 2020) (link attached).The aim of SEN Pilot (2020) was to develop, test and agree a new process that facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at Mainstream Schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).The pilot was undertaken by Education Authority (EA) on the Department’s behalf.   The Education Authority identified nine projects, involving 10 schools, which had:existing temporary provisions established for more than four years with proven demand and delivery of high-quality specialist provision in a mainstream setting.major capital announcements, with existing provision, which provide the opportunity for enhanced provision in modern fit-for-purpose built accommodation.major capital announcements, without existing provision, where there is identified local need.The outcome of the 2020 SEN Pilot exercise was two-fold. Firstly, the new process to establish, close or change specialist provision in mainstream schools (without the need for a Development Proposal) was approved and secondly the proposed Specialist Provisions at the pilot schools were approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools participating in a pilot to increase specialist provision capacity within mainstream schools.
answer_text: The member has clarified that the questions are in relation to the SEN Pilot - Specialist provision in mainstream schools (October 2020) (link attached).The aim of SEN Pilot (2020) was to develop, test and agree a new process that facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at Mainstream Schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).The pilot was undertaken by Education Authority (EA) on the Department’s behalf.   The Education Authority identified nine projects, involving 10 schools, which had:existing temporary provisions established for more than four years with proven demand and delivery of high-quality specialist provision in a mainstream setting.major capital announcements, with existing provision, which provide the opportunity for enhanced provision in modern fit-for-purpose built accommodation.major capital announcements, without existing provision, where there is identified local need.The outcome of the 2020 SEN Pilot exercise was two-fold. Firstly, the new process to establish, close or change specialist provision in mainstream schools (without the need for a Development Proposal) was approved and secondly the proposed Specialist Provisions at the pilot schools were approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how the workforce is being taught the necessary skills to address the green transition.
answer_text: My Department, in conjunction with the Green Skills Delivery Group, has assessed the current green skills provision and developed a Green Skills Action Plan. It provides a comprehensive framework to strengthen the green skills ecosystem to meet the evolving and complex skills demand of industry. The Plan will be launched in the next few weeks. A sub-group of the Green Skills Delivery Group, with representatives from business, academia and across government, is being established to advise the department on future green skills requirement and the timeline for delivery.  Additionally, the delivery of the skills required to support the green transition, has already commenced through the Skill Up programme, with over 50 free green skills courses available through our colleges and universities and the Skills Focus programme which provides training support for SME’s.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of properties that will benefit from Project Gigabit in the Foyle constituency, broken down by postcode.
answer_text: There are 184 eligible premises in the Foyle constituency in the target intervention area for Project Gigabit. Because Project Gigabit is still at the procurement phase, I am unable to provide the details on the precise numbers of premises that will benefit. These premises will only be known once the procurement is completed and if a supplier is appointed.
