question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Draft Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan, particularly in relation to offshore wind energy.
answer_text: My Department published an updated version of the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) as part of the consultation on the OREAP: Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) Environmental Report and Report to Inform Appropriate Assessment (RIAA) on 27th February 2025.  I have attached a link to the updated OREAP for ease of reference:Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the roll-out of the £100 winter fuel payment.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 21433/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 22441/22-27, to detail the work of the Regional Balance Unit in the last six months.
answer_text: Since establishment on 1st October 2024, the Regional Balance Unit has been progressing the creation of the Local Economic Partnerships and the establishment of the £45m Regional Balance Fund.  It has also been working closely with Community Wealth Building partnerships to explore collaboration opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered establishing a central licensing authority to support courts, regulators, enforcement agencies and operators in understanding, navigating and interpreting the licensing system.
answer_text: This is a recommendation in the report of the independent review of the licensing system including the surrender principle which was published on 21 February 2025. I have asked my officials to consider carefully the report and to provide me with advice on each of the 26 recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how the lack of transport infrastructure is causing premises to not make widespread use of the later opening hours permitted under the 2021 Licensing Law amendments.
answer_text: The first departmental review of the implementation and effectiveness of the provisions within the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, which includes the changes permitting later opening hours in licensed premises, is ongoing. The closing date for responses to online surveys and data requests is 13 April 2025, after which my officials will provide me with an analysis of the responses for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of the licensing system including the surrender principle was published on 21 February 2025. I have asked my officials to consider carefully the report and to provide me with advice on each of the 26 recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to address the high rates of alcohol-related deaths.
answer_text: While the reported reduction in alcohol-specific deaths in the 2024 NISRA report (since the previous year) is welcome, the increase in such deaths over the last decade remains a concern. These figures serve as a stark reminder of the devastating impact that alcohol can have on individuals and families across Northern Ireland. Each and every one of these deaths is preventable. Sadly, the figures also confirm that these impacts continue to be felt by some parts of our community more than others, with the percentage of alcohol-specific deaths in our most deprived areas (37.6%) almost four times that of the least deprived areas (9.8%). My Department continues to lead on the implementation of the cross-departmental strategy to prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use in Northern Ireland. The strategy includes measures to reduce the availability and accessibility of alcohol and other substances. It is vital that people understand the harm that alcohol can cause. Therefore, my Department and the Public Health Agency promote the UK Chief Medical Officers’ low risk guidelines, which state that to drink at low risk, men and women should drink no more than 14 units of alcohol a week. We also promote specific awareness-raising events such as Dry January and Alcohol Awareness Week. Drug and Alcohol Coordination Teams (DACTs) also facilitate education and prevention activity across Northern Ireland which addresses drug and alcohol issues. These services are available in settings such as in schools, workplaces, community and youth centres and clubs providing free drug and/or alcohol focused awareness-raising advice, materials, sessions, or programmes. This is in addition to brief interventions and information provided during engagement with health service professionals and targeted prevention with young people most at risk of substance use. There are also a range of services in place that can help and support anyone who thinks they might have an issue with alcohol and/or their families – you can see details of awareness-raising activity and local services at: http://services.drugsandalcoholni.info/. Finally, it is exactly because of these unacceptable levels of harm and health inequalities that I have indicated my intention, subject to Executive and Assembly agreement, to bring forward legislation to introduce Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) of Alcohol in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will make a decision on the business case of integration status for Culmore Primary School.
answer_text: On 10 September 2024, the Education Authority (EA) published Development Proposal 737 (not a business case) proposing that:-“Culmore Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status, with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter.”The statutory two-month objection period in relation to this proposal concluded on 11 November 2024.I am informed that my officials are in the final stages of preparing a report for my consideration. This has involved significant research, engagement with stakeholders, consultation on a Case for Change, objections received within a two-month statutory objection period, advice from policy teams within the Department; the professional advice of the Education and Training Inspectorate, and any other information considered pertinent to the proposal.  I am therefore unable to provide you with a precise date by which a decision will be taken. I do wish to assure you that I am aware of your desire for early clarity on this issue and I will take a decision once the report has been presented to me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of consultant gynaecologists with a speciality in endometriosis in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, as women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  The service model for endometriosis further recognises the impact of this condition on the health and well-being of women.  The basis of the model is holistic management for the needs of women undergoing diagnosis and treatment by delivering care through a multidisciplinary team best able to provide responsive, appropriate care throughout the patient journey.  Each member of the team is functioning as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, radiologist, pain specialist, or other professional lead in delivering care. Belfast Trust have two consultants who have a special interest in Endometriosis.In the Southern Health and Social Care Trust there are no consultants with specialist training in advanced endometriosis treatment.South Eastern Trust has 4 gynaecologists with specialist training in endometriosis treatment and access to a consultant surgeon with a special interest who often performs joint cases with gynaecologists.Northern Trust do not have any consultant gynaecologists who have specialist training in advanced endometriosis treatment.The Western Trust currently has two Consultants with specialist training in Endometriosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an assessment will be carried out to ensure that the traffic light phasing at the Spencer Road junction, Derry, is optimal, given current levels of congestion caused by work undertaken by NI Water.
answer_text: My officials have been monitoring traffic flows affected by this essential lane closure on the lower deck of Craigavon Bridge.  Following this review, the direction of the road closure has been amended to facilitate traffic moving into the city centre, resulting in an easing of the congestion.Monitoring of this closure will continue to determine if further interventions are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to clarify the decision taken in relation to funding for the Samaritans from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what progress has been made to meet key activities in his Department's 2024-25 Climate Change Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department delivers a diverse range of functions that impact on the lives of everyone in our society and the Departmental Climate Change Action Plan sets out the work my Department will take over this financial year to address the challenges that climate change presents to our people, our communities, and our places.  Key activities of the Climate Change Action Plan have been grouped into 3 areas based on the themes of Mitigation, Adaptation and Behavioural Change, with most of them completed or progressing on track.There are 17 key activities under Mitigation, 4 have been completed, with the other 13 in progress and on track for completion.  Actions under this theme include the Fuel Poverty Strategy public consultation which closes on 6th March and, I am pleased to confirm that the Northern Ireland Housing Executive is progressing well with their actions to deliver 300 units as part of the Low Carbon Retrofit Programme. This includes improved energy efficiency measures, renewable technology and low carbon heating with better energy advice to the households.Adaptation has 12 key activities, of which 3 have been completed and the other 9 are on track to be completed. The Historic Environment Division have completed its objective of publishing much needed guidance for owners on retrofitting historic buildings. My officials in Belfast Regeneration Division have piloted a Risk and Vulnerability Assessment which analyses potential climate hazards and evaluates existing vulnerabilities to understand the seriousness of the potential impacts on people, assets, services, livelihoods, and the environment. 18 key activities have been allocated to Behavioural Change, with 8 completed and the remaining 10 in progress. Climate literacy has been increased across my Department through Climate Smart training, the development of an online climate change resource area, hosting a series of policy awareness seminars and conducting a staff climate change survey.In total all 47 key activities have been completed or are progressing as planned. My Department will publish the outcomes of all activities at the end of the plan’s term.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she will ensure that the interests of consumers in Northern Ireland are adequately represented through the governance arrangements of GB Energy.
answer_text: Officials are working closely with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero and early engagement has taken place at a senior level with GB Energy. A key consideration for us is how GB Energy will fund and support projects here, to ensure there is alignment with the Executive’s Energy Strategy which recognises the importance of protecting consumers in the energy transition. My Department has secured assurances in the GB Energy Bill that the organisation will operate in a way that meets our strategic aim. An amendment to the Bill ensures that any elements  of the strategic direction statement that the Secretary of State must lay at Westminster, which impact on energy policy here, must have the consent of this administration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for a timeline on her Department's development of an interconnection policy; and (ii) to detail her assessment of whether the lack of an interconnection policy will lead to delays on the delivery of the LirlC interconnector.
answer_text: The Department has commissioned research to provide evidence for the development of interconnection and energy storage policy. This will include analysis on the effect on the cost to local consumers, security of supply and carbon emissions under specified scenarios. It is expected to be completed in April 2025. The output of this research will inform the development of both interconnector and energy storage policy. DfE is also working with the Utility Regulator and relevant industry players to enable timely progress in the policy development in parallel to the research. The Department aims to complete policy development in 2026.The Department must ensure that robust analysis is carried out on the proposed LirIC interconnector and that its impact on consumers is determined before any decision is taken on the provision of consumer support for this project.  We continue to engage with the Utility Regulator so that they can carry out their analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of whether her Department’s Energy Division is appropriately resourced to deliver on the net zero targets; and (ii) to detail any current plans she has to allocate additional resource to the division.
answer_text: There are significant budgetary and consequently staffing pressures across all departments. My Department is proactively managing resourcing priorities and also working closely with NICSHR to  progress recruitment efforts robustly to fill positions within Energy Group.  At the same time, we are prioritising budgets strategically to allocate resources where they are most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline when the final terms of reference will be published for the review into the case of Jonathan Creswell.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract.  Those suppliers who meet the published criteria of the selection questionnaire will be invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender for the project. The procurement will seek a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in the coming months, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether a new Regional Property Programme, as mentioned in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan, has been established.
answer_text: Please see link https://www.investni.com/sites/default/files/2024-12/invest-northern-ireland-regional-property-programme-2024-2034.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether a new Regional Balance Unit has been established.
answer_text: The Department established a new Regional Balance Unit on 1st October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any plans to restructure Invest NI.
answer_text: Restructuring of Invest NI’s leadership team is currently progressing with interviews for the positions of Chief Operating Officer, Chief Development Officer and Chief Commercial Officer taking place in February and March 2025.  It is anticipated that all, or most, of the appointees will be in post by June/July 2025/26. Invest NI has put in place transition arrangements in place to ensure business continuity throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 21878/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 21878/22-27 was answered on 14 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support currently being offered to adults seeking an ADHD diagnosis.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD. Southern Health and Social Care Trust and Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) currently do not accept adult ADHD referrals. However, WHSCT confirmed if a person is diagnosed with ADHD in children’s services and required ongoing medication management by a Consultant Psychiatrist, they will continue to receive regular reviews by a Consultant Psychiatrist in adult mental health services. Waiting times for ADHD diagnosis in adult services is not recorded or reported on in the WHSCT as there is no commissioned service in place. Although there is no commissioned Adult ADHD service Belfast, Northern and Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts provide a level of provision. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust reconfigured a small resource (0.5 Consultant Psychiatry) from within adult teams to process these referrals. Current waiting is > 3000, waiting times for an ADHD assessment based on current provision is 8 years from the point of referral.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) in response to demand for Adult ADHD services, the NHSCT decided to progress with a small, unfunded pilot for service users who have been referred to the Trust, to be placed on a waiting list and then offered an appointment with the Trust. Part of the pilot process is detailed pre-screening to ensure assessments are offered, where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. All patients on any waiting lists remain in under the care of their GP. This pilot approach has taken time to establish given the preparatory work required. Communication with individuals on the register commenced in December 2023, in chronological order of date of original referral.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) in response to demand, SEHSCT has progressed a small unfunded service for those patients who have transferred with an existing diagnosis and who are prescribed medication and require monitoring as per NICE Guidelines.  For patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.SEHSCT receives referrals for Adult Mental Health Services via Mental Health Assessment Centres located in Newtownards, Lisburn and Downpatrick.  Individuals, including those with a potential/confirmed co-morbid ADHD diagnosis are offered a mental health assessment and are managed via the adult mental health core care pathway, where appropriate.As stated previously there is no commissioned service for Adult ADHD. Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts. All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Careers Portal project.
answer_text: The development of a new Careers Portal is an ambitious and complex IT enabled system transformation project. Successful delivery will help every part of the careers system to work together and improve access to high quality careers information and services. Following extensive research and engagement work to identify priority needs of the wide range of end users and stakeholders, the project moved into an IT Discovery phase in autumn 2024. During this phase, the project team worked with external IT professionals to bring together user-focused engagement and research, with an aim to identify the optimum technical solutions for a new Careers Portal. A number of workshops were held between October 2024 and January 2025. These workshops were attended by representatives of multiple stakeholder groups including young people, teachers, career professionals, parents and the voluntary and community sector, each representing a different user persona on a career journey. With the conclusion of these workshops, the project is nearing the end of the IT Discovery phase. A final report is expected soon, which will record the findings from the workshops and will include recommendations for the technical solutions required to deliver the Portal. The report will inform the next stages of the Careers Portal project of business case development and a pre-market engagement exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Education in relation to the Developing a more strategic approach to 14-19 Education and Training - a framework to Transform 14-19 Education and Training Provision.
answer_text: Since taking office, I have not had any direct engagement with the Minister of Education regarding this matter. I can advise that my predecessor and the Minister for Education had met to discuss the approach to this work and that they identified areas of joint strategic importance and established shared priorities. Officials have been progressing work in this context. I look forward to engaging with the Minister in the near future to continue this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a timeline for the publication of the joint Department for the Economy and Department of Education Careers action plan.
answer_text: Officials from both my Department and the Department of Education are in the final stages of developing the joint Careers Action Plan. It is anticipated that, as a cross-cutting issue, this will be shared with the Executive in March 2025. The Action Plan will set out the steps to establish a new system that improves the service that young people, adults and those who are furthest from the labour market receive. The Plan aims to establish a more cohesive, vibrant, and sustainable careers ecosystem based on effective partnership working. The key to this will be collaborative engagement across all providers of careers services to share resources, knowledge and embrace new technologies. The Action Plan draws on themes and recommendations emerging from a range of important documents including the ‘Independent Review of Careers’ and ‘14-19 Framework’. Dr Deirdre Hughes OBE, author of the ‘Independent Review of Careers’, briefed the Education and Economy committees in May 2024 in a joint session. This document has been a key evidence source for the Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Skills Barometer 2023-33.
answer_text: The NI Skills Barometer is a valuable resource servicing a wide array of stakeholders within the skills ecosystem. This comprehensive report provides an up-to-date assessment of the future skills needs of our economy, highlighting both opportunities and challenges. The report anticipates that there will be significant challenges ahead as future skills constraints will not meet the demands of the labour market. However, this projection arises from a positive context; the continued growth of workforce jobs which are projected to increase to almost one million by 2033.It is particularly encouraging to note that the sectors projected to experience the highest forecasted growth rates align closely with my Department’s seven priority sectors. This alignment indicates that our strategic focus is well-placed to support our future skills needs. Officials in the Department will continue to use this important tool to inform and shape policy to ensure our skills ecosystem remains adaptive and responsive to emerging trends and demands. Later this year UUEPC will also be commencing work on a Sub-Regional Skills Barometer that will undertake a more detailed skills analysis at a localised level within the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a breakdown of the funding supplied to community radio in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has no responsibility for community radio as broadcasting and spectrum (the range of electromagnetic frequencies allocated for transmitting television and radio signals) are reserved matters.  However, I am aware that local stations may apply to schemes such as the Department for Digital, Culture Media and Sport’s Community Radio Fund, to support core service costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Sligo, Leitrim, Northern Counties Railway Greenway project.
answer_text: Leitrim County Council are leading on the development of the cross-border Sligo, Leitrim, Northern Counties Railway Greenway project in partnership with Sligo County Council and Fermanagh and Omagh District Council. Departmental officials are represented on the cross-border steering group alongside officials from The Department of Transport in the South to support the councils in the development of the project, and regular updates are provided to the North South Ministerial Council- Transport Sectoral meeting.A preferred route corridor for the Greenway in both jurisdictions and it is planned to consult the public later this year. Following agreement of a preferred route corridor, development of a more detailed preferred route can commence. Once suitably developed, this will also be subject to future public consultation.The Department delivers annual funding rounds to councils to support capital investment in greenway and active travel enabling projects; and will continue to work with councils on any forthcoming proposals. Alongside this, the Department has been developing plans to support increased investment in active travel including the Active Travel Delivery Plan out for public consultation until 28th February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many endometriosis specialists are currently in place.
answer_text: Endometriosis is a chronic long-term condition that develops when cells similar to the ones normally found in the womb are present elsewhere in the body such as the bladder or bowel, with many women not having any symptoms.  Endometriosis is usually associated with pelvic pain, painful periods and difficulty getting pregnant. The pathway with this condition includes symptom management, diagnostics to assess severity and progression of the condition to inform next steps. These can involve ongoing monitoring through to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention with management of fertility throughout.All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, as women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  The service model for endometriosis further recognises the impact of this condition on the health and well-being of women.  The basis of the model is holistic management for the needs of women undergoing diagnosis and treatment by delivering care through a specialist multidisciplinary team best able to provide responsive, appropriate care throughout the patient journey.  Each member of the team is functioning as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, or other professional lead in delivering care.Identifying the number of endometriosis specialists in place cannot be confined to a single profession, as this is not how care is provided and would be outwith best practice.  My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time.  In this regard my Department has secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time Report on gynaecology and progressing implementation of its recommendations. This will include the progression of these recommendations in the NCEPOD

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to provide support networks for women affected by endometriosis.
answer_text: As you may be aware, my Department has recently published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology. The report identifies 20 general recommendations to improve gynaecology services through coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver.My officials have established a regional forum, with Trust, primary care and Public Health Agency (PHA) representation, to take forward these recommendations.  It is also planned that a regional engagement forum will also be set up to garner expert user feedback and ensure that patient’s voices are heard. My Department recognises reform and improvement in the delivery of Endometriosis services are a priority and a task and finish group has been formed to focus on this service area. You will be aware that the Belfast and Western HSC Trusts previously held provisional accreditation as a British Society of Gynaecological Endoscopy (BSGE) Endometriosis Centre and this accreditation was impacted on during Covid. Part of this group’s remit will be to determine how quickly accreditation can be achieved again in Northern Ireland, while recognising the need to balance theatre capacity for urgent or cancer surgeries, acknowledging our funding constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he is taking to mitigate the impact on businesses in Northern Ireland's border counties due to the decision by the Republic of Ireland to reduce the VAT rate for food-led hospitality.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 21919/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timeline on the publication of the Women's Health Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when Lisneal College was made aware that their pitch facilities posed a health and safety risk.
answer_text: A pitch inspection at Lisneal College was carried out by the Education Authority’s (EA) Grounds Maintenance staff on 12 December 2019, which identified that the pitch was in very poor condition and needed replaced.I have been informed by the Education Authority that the issue was referred to its Minor Works Team on 14 January 2020 and that EA contacted the school on 29 July 2021. The EA has advised that the delay in progress between January 2020 and July 2021 was due to the Covid Pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the health and safety risk assessment for Lisneal College was made.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that a pitch inspection was carried out on 12 December 2019. This identified that the pitch was in very poor condition and had the potential for slips and accidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of injuries that have been recorded at Lisneal College football pitch since 2019.
answer_text: Lisneal College has reported 10 accidents to the Education Authority since 2019 in relation to the use of the 3G pitch.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the review of further education student support will include a review of the Education Maintenance Allowance.
answer_text: The ongoing Review of Further Education Student Support within my Department does not include a review of the Education Maintenance Allowance.The Educational Maintenance Allowance is administered across both my Department and the Department of Education - around 60% of recipients are in school, with 40% of recipients attending a Further Education College. The Education Minister and my predecessor Minister Murphy agreed to take forward a joint programme of work drawing from the 14-19 framework and starting with careers as a priority. However, support for learners - including Educational Maintenance Allowance, will need to be considered as a feature of a future work programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will publish the Health and Safety risk assessment report carried out for a new football pitch at Lisneal College.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that a pitch inspection was carried out on 12 December 2019. This identified that the pitch was in very poor condition and had the potential for slips and accidents.The findings of the pitch inspection were:•	The carpet has deteriorated and has torn in various areas; rips have been temporarily repaired;•	The pile on the carpet is at the end of its useful life and lies flat creating the potential for slips and accidents;•	Once the pile deteriorates in this manner it cannot be revived and must be replaced;•	The base is of blinded hard-ore with no shock-pad or open-textured bitmac:•	The school have documented accidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools currently engaged in Minor Works in excess of £500,000, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: I have deposited a table in the Assembly Library which details the schools currently engaged in Minor Works in excess of £500,000, broken down by constituency. The table details projects at various stages from business case approval through to those on site and those where final account has yet to be agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will develop a robust regional workforce strategy aimed at supporting the professionalisation of the childcare sector.
answer_text: Actions to support the early learning and childcare workforce will be included within the draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process his Department carried out to establish a 100 per cent inflationary increase on the Minor Works scheme.
answer_text: In October 2017 the Department increased the Education Authority’s (EA) capital delegation from £500k to £1m. In reviewing the delegated limit, the Department considered the level of expenditure involved and the quality of business cases reviewed. To inform this, the Department undertook a test drill of EA business cases and a standard business case template was subsequently developed, to provide the necessary consistency. This effectively means that since October 2017 all EA capital projects below the £1m threshold are carried out by the EA without reference to the Department. The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works. Projects above £1m can still be undertaken but require approval by the Department’s Finance Director.Similarly, all capital projects above £1m in the non-controlled sector, which are managed by the Department’s Investment and Infrastructure Directorate, are subject to approval by the Department’s Finance Director. All capital projects are subject to a value for money business case and relevant statutory approvals. Costs associated with the schemes are reviewed and assessed by Quantity Surveyors to ensure value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools in Foyle that are awaiting the beginning or completion of capital projects, including new builds; (ii) the progress, status and expected completion date of each of these capital build projects; and (iii) the current ranking for the prioritisation of the completion of outstanding capital works for schools in Foyle.
answer_text: There are currently three Major Capital Works projects and three School Enhancement Projects in the Foyle constituency.The construction of a new school for Ardnashee School and College is well advanced and is due to be handed over to the school this month. The total capital cost of the build is estimated at £42m. In April 2024 I also announced capital planning would begin for a second new build campus for Ardnashee.Lumen Christi College and St Brigid’s College were announced under the 2022 Major Capital Works Programme. Unfortunately, these, along with nineteen other projects, are currently on hold because of ongoing budget pressures.There are three schools progressing under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) in Foyle. Integrated Consultancy Teams have been appointed for each of these projects which are at various stages of design and planning as shown below.Chapel Road Primary School - RIBA Stage 4 Technical design Greenhaw Primary School - RIBA Stage 4 Technical design Holy Child Primary School - RIBA Stage 1 Technical FeasibilityWhile funding is available to take SEP projects through the design and planning process, progress to construction will be dependent on availability of funding. It is not therefore possible to provide expected completion dates for these projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when schools were made aware of the 100 per cent inflationary increase on the Minor Works scheme.
answer_text: There is no current call for planned minor works projects. The constrained capital budget position has meant that in recent years, minor works have been restricted to Ministerial Priority 1 i.e. those works that meet inescapable statutory requirements, such as health and safety, fire protection, safeguarding and statutory obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act. Therefore, schools currently submit Unavoidable Minor Works applications based on an urgent need within the school. Alternatively, a need will be identified through statutory inspections, site visits, or approved development proposals. Projects are scoped and a Technical Feasibility Report prepared, which will provide an estimate of the cost. The business case will then be completed, which identifies a preferred option, based on an assessment of costs, non-monetary benefits and risks.  Costs associated with projects are reviewed and assessed by quantity surveyors. In October 2017 the Department increased the Education Authority’s (EA) capital delegation from £500k to £1m. This effectively meant that from that date, all EA capital projects below that threshold were carried out by the EA without reference to the Department. The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works. Projects above £1m can still be undertaken but require approval by the Department’s Finance Director.Similarly, all capital projects above £1m in the non-controlled sector, which are managed by the Department’s Investment and Infrastructure Directorate, are subject to approval by the Department’s Finance Director. Based on available records, the first EA Minor Works business case over £500k was approved in April 2016 for additional teaching accommodation at Bunscoil Mhic Reachtain. The first non-controlled Minor Works business case over £500k was approved in November 2015 for Fire Risk Works at Mount Lourdes Grammar School.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline when the Housing Executive’s review of Primary Social Needs criteria and points for all victims of violence, abuse and trauma will be completed.
To ask the Minister for the Economy how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the work of his Department.
answer_text: AI is one of the most significant technological developments in recent times. I believe AI brings substantial opportunities to transform operations and drive efficiencies across not just my Department but also the wider public sector. My Department has successfully delivered an AI Proof of Concept (PoC) to explore economic data, policy documents and to assist with research. Officials in my Department are actively exploring other opportunities for the use of AI in the work of the Department.I am aware that a number of PoCs are being taken forward by other departments in the North, as well as in Great Britian. These PoCs are at various stages of development and officials are working with colleagues in both jurisdictions to learn from that work and explore how my Department can deploy relevant solutions in a controlled and measured way, that adds value. These opportunities are being considered in the light of concerns and possible risks identified around the use of this technology including data privacy, security, bias and reliability.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the work of their Department.
answer_text: We have established the AI and Digital Unit.  The work of this unit will be informed and guided by the work being taken forward by the delivering on the commitment made in the Programme for Government (PfG), the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser (CSTA) established and chaired a Task-and-Finish (T&F) Group to help inform and shape the Office of AI and Digital. The T&F group comprised representatives from government departments, industry and academia. Contributions thus far have been invaluable in identifying key challenges and opportunities associated with adoption of AI across the public sector.   FM/dFM formally launched the Office of AI and Digital on Wednesday 11 June at the AI Collaboration Centre in Belfast.  The Office of AI and Digital, alongside an AI Advisory Panel & the Government AI Network will develop:A draft an AI strategy and action plan to 2030.A detailed Business Plan for an AI Hub for delivery.A risk framework for AI projects. An AI for Citizens plan for engagementThe completion of stage 2 is scheduled for late Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the work of his Department.
answer_text: I recognise that AI offers opportunities to examine how processes and procedures could be improved to drive efficiencies and effectiveness.   My Department relies upon the Department for Work and Pensions in Great Britain and the Department of Finance in Northern Ireland for the vast majority of its digital infrastructure and systems.  My Department is working closely with both on shared opportunities as they arise. All opportunities will be explored and will follow the Generative AI Framework provided by Cabinet Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the work of his Department.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure is actively investigating and trialling the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI).  Research is taking place in both administrative and operational areas to identify processes where AI can realise efficiencies and assist in decision-making.  All instances of AI trialled to date have been deployed internally only, to facilitate safe testing in a closed, secure environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how Artificial Intelligence is being incorporated into the work of his Department.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Generative AI in particular, offers opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality-of-care services delivered. Anticipated benefits include freeing up capacity to enhance services through improved communications and tailored treatment plans.Future plans include testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts. However, the potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users’ needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The HSC has been considering how the emerging capabilities of AI could be utilised to improve efficiency in a range of activities through the HSC AI steering group.  My Director of Digital Health & Care Northern Ireland chairs the NI health and Social Care AI/GENAI Strategy Steering Group and a departmental AI Working group reviewing how my department could avail of the potential benefits of the technology and the governance risks to be addressed ensuring the adoption of safe and responsible AI for the citizens of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has plans to remove the cap on the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme in order that parents of all school age children can avail of the scheme.
answer_text: The administrative data generated from the operation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) enables movements in the cost of childcare to be tracked over time.  All options to ensure that the subsidy remains effective in reducing the costs of childcare for parents are being considered.Currently, registered childcare providers who offer the NICSS, reduce the normal fee charged to eligible parents by 15% up to the scheme’s cap.  The billing process ensures transparency in that the parent’s invoice shows the normal fee, the 15% subsidy, and the net amount to be paid.The draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation has earmarked £50m for Early Learning and Childcare.  I am currently considering what is affordable within this allocation.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered introducing a cap on childcare fees for parents.
answer_text: The administrative data generated from the operation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) enables movements in the cost of childcare to be tracked over time.  All options to ensure that the subsidy remains effective in reducing the costs of childcare for parents are being considered.Currently, registered childcare providers who offer the NICSS, reduce the normal fee charged to eligible parents by 15% up to the scheme’s cap.  The billing process ensures transparency in that the parent’s invoice shows the normal fee, the 15% subsidy, and the net amount to be paid.The draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation has earmarked £50m for Early Learning and Childcare.  I am currently considering what is affordable within this allocation.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he plans to ensure that the 15 per cent savings from the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme reaches parents.
answer_text: The administrative data generated from the operation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) enables movements in the cost of childcare to be tracked over time.  All options to ensure that the subsidy remains effective in reducing the costs of childcare for parents are being considered.Currently, registered childcare providers who offer the NICSS, reduce the normal fee charged to eligible parents by 15% up to the scheme’s cap.  The billing process ensures transparency in that the parent’s invoice shows the normal fee, the 15% subsidy, and the net amount to be paid.The draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation has earmarked £50m for Early Learning and Childcare.  I am currently considering what is affordable within this allocation.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any Financial Transactions Capital funding will be made available to any councils through Local Economic Partnerships.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to engage in constructive discussions with local Councils to co-design arrangements to deliver funding for the Local Economic Partnerships, based on the allocations I announced on Monday.These discussions will cover approaches to the type and profile of funding. As this process is ongoing it is not possible at this stage to detail the specific nature of funding. Financial Transactions Capital can only be deployed by the public sector as a loan to, or equity investment in, a private sector entity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to implement biomethane within the gas network.
answer_text: The first injection of locally produced biomethane into the gas network took place at Dungannon in November 2023.  Following a Call for Evidence on developing local biomethane production in 2024, my Department is currently analysing responses and working towards publishing a draft policy consultation for biomethane later this year.  Key considerations will include the economics of producing biomethane and treatment of costs for connecting to the gas network.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure the savings generated from the Northern Ireland Subsidy Scheme will not be made redundant due to childcare providers raising their fees in order to deal with increased costs.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) adopts a similar approach to Tax Free Childcare (TFC) in that the amount that can be claimed is a percentage of total childcare costs up to a maximum value or CAP.  My Department will continue to monitor the scheme’s parameters to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs to families.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered developing a fair and equitable pricing structure for the production of biomethane, in order to be of benefit to both producers and users.
answer_text: Following a Call for Evidence on developing local biomethane production in 2024, my Department is currently analysing responses and working towards publishing a policy consultation on biomethane later in the year.  Our considerations will include how best we might develop the biomethane sector in a way which is economically sustainable and provides renewable gas to consumers at a fair price.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support his Department is providing to companies working towards net zero targets to ensure legislative commitments are met by 2050.
answer_text: My Department, in partnership with Invest NI, launched an Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Businesses in May 2024. The ERE Programme includes a range of support for businesses in the form of Technical Consultancy, resource Matching Through Industrial Symbiosis, a Resource Efficiency Capital Grant and an Energy Efficiency Capital Grant. The Programme helps support businesses to optimise and future-proof their operations by implementing green efficiencies to achieve cost and carbon savings in the transition to net zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many of the actions in the Environmental Improvement Plan for Northern Ireland, with a delivery date of 2024, have been completed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the publication of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 20996/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 24 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the services available to support young people leaving secondary education with complex needs.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of health and social care services to support young people with complex needs leaving secondary education, aligned to assessed needs.A young person with complex needs covers a broad spectrum of needs including physical, learning or mental health needs and potentially these young people may require support across multiple services and agencies. Complex needs may also arise from adverse childhood experiences or trauma which can necessitate support and service provision across Children’s and Adult Services. At the point of leaving secondary or special needs school a variety of services provided by Health and Social Care Trusts to young people with complex needs, in accordance with their assessed need may include:Gateway Children’s Service, single point or entry provisionFamily Intervention / Family Support ServicesSafeguarding ServicesLooked After Children ServicesLeaving and After Care ServicesFostering and AdoptionResidential Homes Regional Secure Centre  Children’s Disability ServicesChild Health (Nursing led services)Psychological ServicesAllied Health Professionals/Nursing and Medical PractitionersChild and Adolescent Mental Health ServicesTransition Co-ordinator/ Named Worker to support transitions from schoolTransition Programmes (to develop key skills and knowledge to help prepare for leaving school and manage increasing levels of independence and associated risk. This may focus on areas such as mental health and anxiety, drug and alcohol awareness, sexual health, and positive friendships).Vocational Training and Supported EmploymentVolunteering Further EducationCommunity Based Day Opportunities including Day Opportunity Bases/Hubs and activities such as Social Farming, Performing Arts Programmes.Building Based Day CareDomiciliary Care package in own homePermanent accommodation in Nursing home, residential or Supported Living as appropriateRespite/Short Breaks Service/Carer SupportShared Lives ServiceDirect PaymentsManaged Budgets Advocacy/Carer/Service User SupportSupport from a Community Mental Health Team to support and encourage as appropriate the young person to remain in education or when leaving school.  Voluntary Services offering courses and programmes e.g. Action Mental Health. I am aware that leaving school for young people, their parents and families and the associated transition from Children to Adult Services can present as challenging. My Department is leading on work to enhance transitions for young people. My Department is currently finalising the Children with Disability Framework and the Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. As part of this work a draft Transitions Protocol for young people with learning disabilities has been developed. An Easy Read of the draft Transitions Protocol is being developed to support consultation with young people and families, with a pilot of the Transition Protocol being considered.Within the

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any support available to families who engage in legal processes to allow their children with disabilities to access their Child Trust Funds.
answer_text: There is guidance on the Department’s website in relation to the process for applying to the High Court for legal authority to manage the financial affairs of a person lacking capacity, including information relating to accessing a child trust fund. The Department’s website also has information about applications for a fee exemption or remission. General advice on accessing a child trust fund when a child turns eighteen is available on the GOV.UK website, as HMRC is responsible for the Child Trust Fund scheme. This advice covers Northern Ireland and signposts the information on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value of successful applications to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme in the Foyle constituency since September 2024.
answer_text: From the launch of the scheme in September 2024 to the 17 January 2025, childcare providers have submitted claims for 382 children with a home address in the Foyle Constituency. The total value of these claims was £153,041.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of unsuccessful applications to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme in the Foyle constituency since September 2024.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is available to all parents with children below primary school age who are eligible for and in receipt of Tax Free Childcare (TFC).  All parents that meet these criteria can register for the NICSS.  Registered childcare providers must be signed up with HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) as a TFC provider to allow eligible parents using their services to avail of the subsidies available.  		It is a decision for registered childcare providers to decide whether or not they wish to register for both TFC and NICSS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the debate on 21 January 2025, to provide a deadline for the publication of a new early learning and childcare strategy.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward a draft strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the scope of the review into higher education.
answer_text: Last year I agreed in principle to proceed with a Review of Higher Education Funding and instructed officials to plan for a Review involving three strands of work.  The strands of work are:1) financial sustainability of the HE sector;2) the teaching funding model; and 3) student support finance.  Officials continue to engage with the sector on the Review and further detail on the scope of each strand of work will be available in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider providing further training on autism profiles to include recognition of the Pathological Demand Avoidance profile to better equip medical staff to understand the profile.
answer_text: Training is key to ensure that appropriate adjustments are made for each autistic person, and to ensure that our services are person-centered. Health and Social Care (HSC) staff have access to a range of autism training, which includes Trust-run training events, Trust-commissioned training and regionally available training.  Staff are encouraged to take up this training.  My Department is also working with colleagues from the Middletown Centre for Autism to design and provide HSC with three training modules that are research and practice/strengths based.  It is envisaged that the first module will be launched in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a timeline for the establishment of the Tourism Partnership Board.
answer_text: I established the Tourism Partnership Board in September 2024. Details on the membership and its terms of reference are available on my Department’s website: Tourism Partnership Board | Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the representatives on the Tourism Partnership Board.
answer_text: I established the Tourism Partnership Board in September 2024. Details on the membership and its terms of reference are available on my Department’s website: Tourism Partnership Board | Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of IBD UK's December 2024 report Crohn's and Colitis Care in Northern Ireland: A Vision for Change, whether he will commit to conducting a comprehensive workforce census, including all Health and Social Care Trusts, to ensure improvements in inflammatory bowel disease care through a better understanding of local requirements and the provision of targeted support.
answer_text: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) conditions are managed in primary care and also in secondary care by both Gastroenterologists and General (Colorectal) Surgeons. Close collaboration between Gastroenterology and Colorectal teams, multidisciplinary team working and maximising opportunities for non-surgical management have been fundamental in improving outcomes for patients with bowel disease-related illnesses. Operational workforce planning to meet population health needs is for the Health and Social Care Trusts. The Review of General Surgery (2022) and the Elective Care Framework (2024) provides the strategic context for the delivery of equitable and timely care for all general surgical conditions including IBD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the risk assessments in place for all Emergency Department waiting areas.
answer_text: My Department recognises the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department.Following a review of Emergency Medicine in NI, my Department is taking forward a number of actions to improve pressures on the wider unscheduled care system to reduce overcrowding and delays in emergency departments.  Trusts were required to develop winter preparedness plans for 2024/25 in conjunction with NIAS to improve patient flow through urgent and emergency care services.  This included specific actions to reduce ambulance delays, improve patient flow through emergency departments and the wider hospital system, and to ensure timely discharge for patients who are medically fit for discharge and no longer require hospital care.  In addition, Trusts have been asked to develop alternative pathways with access to NIAS as an alternative to conveyance to emergency departments.  As patient safety remains a priority, careful consideration is made in the identification of patients who are deemed appropriate for allocation to an additional bed/care space, using an agreed set of exclusion criteria.  A patient who is placed in an additional care space receives both medical and nursing care dictated by their clinical need to the same safety standard of all patients placed within a designated ward bed space. Trusts will increase staffing levels for these respective areas so that patients have access to appropriate care, although mitigations against deficits in the privacy and dignity of patients are much more challenging.  All Trusts have risk assessments in place that cover Emergency Department waiting areas.Belfast HSC Trust has a General Health and Safety Risk Assessment in place for its Emergency Departments (ED) and Children’s ED waiting area.  The Trust uses a risk, audit and assessment tool to measure and record compliance in managing health and safety and other risks associated with the delivery of services across all wards, departments, facilities and services within the Trust.  The tool has a total of 23 different standards with associated risk assessments that ensure compliance.  Risk assessments for ED waiting areas are completed as part of the tool’s general risk assessment category.All patients are triaged on arrival to the Northern HSC Trust’s Emergency Departments using the Manchester Triage scoring system and assigned by clinical priority. This involves a full set of observations including blood pressure, temperature and heart rate.  A dynamic risk assessment is then completed by the nursing staff (with medical involvement if needed) until they are seen by a doctor. This is patient specific on their presentation. Patients in the waiting room are under observation and have ongoing observations completed to ensure they are safe to remain in the waiting room.The South Eastern HSC Trust uses a General Risk Assessment (GRA) for the Emergency Department’s main waiting rooms and paediatric waiting room. This GRA include

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on road infrastructure in Derry in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Expenditure on road infrastructure is not analysed on a city or town basis, rather it is by District Council area. A summary of the amount spent in the Derry & Strabane area is provided below. 2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24£7.7m£10.0m£11.5m£11.5m£11.0mThis expenditure does not include spend on major projects such as the A6 Dungiven to Drumahoe Dual carriageway.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 19917/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 19917/22-27 was answered on 20 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 19916/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 19916/22-27 was answered on 28 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 18577/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 18577/22-27 was answered on 24 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the extent to which regional economic imbalances have widened in the last ten years.
answer_text: In October 2024 I published my Sub-Regional Economic Plan which set out a new strategic approach to economic development.  This included a technical annex (Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan - Technical Annex) which presented the extent to which regional imbalances have changed over the last ten years for key economic indicators including labour productivity, employment rate and economic inactivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether data is collected on the impact of poverty on the proportion of individuals missing hospital appointments.
answer_text: HSC Trusts do not routinely collect this information; however, one Trust has undertaken some research in to the issue.The short paper by the Western Trust’s Assistant Director for Nursing Research & Development, indicates that “Across the UK annually, non-attendance of hospital appointments cost the NHS £1 billion. The higher prevalence of missed appointments in people from socially deprived areas is a common occurrence in the recent literature. This trend is similar in both GP and hospital appointments”. The Western HSC Trust suggests that two of the top four reasons given by patients for missing appointments is due to letters being missed or not received, however as “our process includes a text reminder to supplement the letter.  This makes us believe there could be other underlying reasons for non-attendance”.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 19917/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 19917/22-27 was answered on 20 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of a statutory inquiry and a financial redress scheme for those affected by Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on inquiry and redress scheme closed on the 19th September 2024. We would like to thank all those who responded. A draft report was prepared and shared with members of the Consultation Forum in December 2024. The final consultation report was published on the TEO website in January 2025.While we are pleased with the many areas of broad agreement highlighted in the consultation, further discussions with Executive Colleagues are needed on complex and sensitive policy issues before any final decisions can be made. It is also important for us to continue to engage and listen with victims and survivors in this period.We hope to introduce as soon as possible in the 2024/25 legislative year. We remain committed, along with the many members of the Assembly, to help victims and their loved ones get the answers and support they deserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any equality protections in place for people with disabilities navigating the employment tribunal system.
answer_text: The Employment Tribunals adhere to the Equal Treatment Bench Book which enables effective communication and participation by all parties. This is a guide for Judges on how to ensure equal treatment and fairness for all users and covers topics such as disabilities, diversity and capacity. “Ground Rules” hearings are often used within the tribunals to agree on reasonable adjustments which will enable the parties to be on an equal footing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps she has taken to deal with the decrease in numbers of dedicated domestic violence police officers in the North West area.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational responsibility of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.However, my officials are currently considering a business case from PSNI on workforce recovery and recruitment.  I remain committed to working with the Chief Constable, the Minister of Finance and my Executive colleagues to ensure that PSNI is funded appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the PSNI is still required to report domestic violence cases to the Public Prosecution Service.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the PSNI and not in the remit of my department to comment on such areas.  I advise that you write to the Chief Constable who is best placed to provide information regarding this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the announcement of an additional 2500 full time preschool places for the 2025-26 academic year, for a breakdown of these places by constituency.
answer_text: Information on the pre-schools transitioning to full-time provision from September 2025 is published by constituency on the Department website at Minister’s Decisions on Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition from September 2025 | Department of Education.  The number of funded pre-school education places in local areas is set annually by the Education Authority, based on demand and is therefore subject to change from year to year.  A summary of the number of additional full-time places by constituency, based on the 2024-25 patterns of demand, is set out below.ConstituencyPlaces Belfast East 37Belfast North 140Belfast South and Mid Down 74Belfast West22East Antrim 160East Londonderry251Fermanagh and South Tyrone276Foyle76Lagan Valley162Mid Ulster152Newry and Armagh 144North Antrim 125North Down 170South Antrim 180South Down 173Strangford78Upper Bann135West Tyrone167Total2522

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of classroom assistants employed in the Foyle constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The vast majority of classroom assistants in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA).  The EA has provided the following information for the Foyle constituency:Year1No. of Classroom Assistant Assignments220209672021999202210782023118920241244Notes:Data is provided at 30 September in each year.Number of Assignments – this is the count of posts/positions held and accounts for staff who have multi-jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she plans to introduce the Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: As you will be aware, it was not possible to introduce any legislation or progress key policy decisions in the absence of Ministers, the Executive and NI Assembly during a significant proportion of the early part of this mandate. Following revisions to my planned legislative programme to allow for this significantly shortened mandate, I secured Executive agreement to commence drafting the Sentencing Bill on 19th December and aim to introduce it in the autumn of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions she is taking to prevent retail crime.
answer_text: Business and retail crime has a real impact on local businesses, staff, individual consumers and the wider community, and a partnership approach is central to preventing and addressing retail crime. The assessment of, and response to any criminal activity is primarily an operational matter for the Chief Constable and policy responsibility for the retail sector lies with the Department for Economy.  Additionally, businesses have a key role to play in partnership with Government, the police and local communities, in tackling crime and building safer communities for all. My Department is fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), and other Departments and organisations to ensure that we have a community where we all feel safe to live and work.  My Department is a member of the Business Crime Partnership (BCP).  The BCP, chaired by the PSNI, consists of representatives from the Department of Justice, Northern Ireland Policing Board, Northern Ireland Retail Consortium, Federation of Small Businesses, Retail NI, Belfast City Centre Management, Trading Standards and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).  The BCP is a networking, information sharing and engagement forum, set up to share best practice and crime prevention advice to support businesses.  Through the BCP, I am aware that PSNI ran a Safer Business Action Week from 14 to 20 October 2024 which resulted in 23 arrests, and operation “Seasons Greetings” ran from 15 November 2024 to 12 January 2025.  The operation was specifically for the PSNI to provide support and guidance to retailers and advise them on measures to reduce risk over the Christmas period.Additionally, my Department, alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board provides approximately £4.25m annual operational funding to PCSPs to support a range of community safety initiatives.  Each PCSP addresses the issue of business crime within their respective action plans and ongoing initiatives include on-street projects such as Community Safety Warden schemes, radio link and text alert schemes as well as the Business Watch scheme which provides crime prevention advice. Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. However, in light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, including retail workers, I also intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty, or providing services to the public.  The new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault, which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation.  Alongside this new offence, I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be requi

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any actions her Department has taken to help businesses who are facing the increasing cost of doing business.
answer_text: I have been engaging with businesses and listened to the cost pressures they are facing.  I have therefore commissioned Ulster University Economic Policy Centre to carry out an independent sectoral study into the cost of doing business here. A terms of reference for the research has been published on the department’s website - Cost of Doing Business Research - Terms of Reference. I am particularly concerned about the planned changes to National Insurance contributions in April. Devolved Governments do not have the financial capacity to compensate for decisions made on reserved taxation matters. I have written to the British Government on the need for support or mitigations to ease the impact of these changes. This research, which will include an assessment of the impact across sectors, will allow me to present as strong a case as possible to Treasury on this important matter. In addition, my department, through Land & Property Services (LPS), offers a wide range of rate support schemes providing £275 million of support to business ratepayers, alongside offering alternative payment options to assist those who need it with rating debt.  I have also written to Executive colleagues in October 2024 to encourage them to ensure their departments and Arms’ Length Bodies are doing everything they can to improve prompt payment performance to businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of classroom assistants vacancies in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The vast majority of classroom assistants are employed by the Education Authority (EA).  The EA has advised that they do not hold centralised data on the number of vacancies across schools, as the management of staffing is delegated to the Board of Governors of each school.  However, the EA does support schools with the delivery of recruitment processes to fill their vacancies. The following table provides information on the number of recruitment exercises for Classroom Assistants that the EA supported in each year, 2020-2024, by constituency area. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of times his officials have met with Innovate UK to work on publishing an action plan that will help improve research and development in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are working collaboratively with Innovate UK to make the Action Plan a reality. Officials from both organisations have been meeting on a weekly basis to develop strategic actions and to work towards publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will establish an all-Ireland higher education coordinating body.
answer_text: Higher education was not included in the Good Friday Agreement as one of the north-south bodies. However my officials and I engage with counterparts in the South on a range of issues related to higher education and I look forward to building on that to make further progress on issues of common interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his phased approach to the introduction of social inclusion strategies, including (i) a timeline for the publication; and (ii) the strategies being prioritised for Executive approval.
answer_text: Work with Executive Departments is ongoing on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies and I intend to bring both draft Strategies to the Executive early in 2025.Details of the proposed timetable for the next steps in developing the Gender Equality and Sexual Orientation Strategies will follow in due course.Whilst my Department is leading on the Executive’s Social Inclusion Strategies, final decisions on their content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19646/22-27, whether she will consider publishing the equality impact assessment that helped inform the draft Budget for 2025-26.
