answer_text: A cumulative Equality Impact Assessment was published alongside other Draft Budget 2025-26 documents, and is available at:https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/consultations/draft-budget-2025-26-consultation

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the University of Ulster's Magee Taskforce will continue to operate after the end of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The Ulster University Magee Taskforce, as outlined in its Terms of Reference, was established for a duration of three years.  Any decision regarding the extension of its role beyond this timeframe will be for the Minister at that time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered launching any further public safety awareness initiatives, similar to that of Ask for Angela, in order to help women who are distressed in public spaces.
answer_text: Outcome 3 of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is that women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhere.The Framework is intended to amplify, accentuate, and accelerate existing work without duplication. Ask for Angela is a PSNI led safety initiative being rolled out across bars, clubs and other licensed businesses across Northern Ireland.  As part of the first Delivery Plan 2024-26 a number of sectoral groups have been established to look at specific areas of intervention, one of which is the Safer Socialising Steering Group (SSSG).  The PSNI and a broad range of stakeholders are represented on this group, chaired by Hospitality Ulster, which will develop an Action Plan to improve the safety of women and girls when socialising.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will appoint a Commissioner for Public Appointments.
answer_text: Preparations to appoint a Commissioner for Public Appointments are well progressed, and the competition will be launched shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support mechanisms that have been introduced to support transgender youth following the announcement of the permanent ban on puberty blockers.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with the Belfast HSC Trust to address the issues of waiting times, resources, and to develop a new model of care for Gender Identity Services for transgender people living in NI. I am committed to improving wider gender identity service provision for children, young people and adults in NI. This will require investment in gender identity services through a business case which is currently being considered. Despite the very challenging financial position facing my Department, I have advised my officials that I will prioritise the development of this service to ensure the appropriate advice and support is available to individuals and their families.Support is available from the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) in each Health Trust and the Knowing our Identity (KOI) service as the Regional CAMHS Gender Service.Furthermore, in recent discussion with the Rt Hon Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, England, I have secured agreement that children and young people in Northern Ireland will have equitable access to planned NHS clinical trials and the Knowing our Identity Services here  will  benefit from close working with the clinics in England  regarding best practice learning and the provision of peer support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the steps she is taking to prevent online sextortion.
answer_text: I am conscious of the abhorrent behaviour involving financially motivated sexual extortion, or ‘sextortion’ as it is more widely known, and because of the particular behaviour it involves, of the devastating and long-lasting impact it has on its victims.  I understand that most of this type of behaviour is carried out online, which can make it easier for perpetrators both locally and from outside of the Northern Ireland jurisdiction to prey upon their victims.Tackling sexual exploitation and abuse remains a key priority for me and while there are existing offences which are used to deal with related behaviours, I continue to look for opportunities where I can improve upon, or strengthen, our response. Online safety is a cross-cutting issue which goes further than my Department.  This includes those areas that fall within the reserved space, such as the recently enacted Online Safety Act 2023. My Department worked closely with the UK Government during its development to help ensure that Northern Ireland’s interests were adequately represented.My Department also forms part of the cross-departmental implementation group established to consider the Executive’s 2020 – 2025 Online Safety Strategy: Keeping Children and Young People Safe.  The strategy seeks to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way. I understand the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) is leading on a number of actions from the strategy, and that between January and March 2024 it ran a social media campaign for young people and adults about online dangers and reporting pathways. This focused on sextortion, online grooming and the risks around sharing nude imagery.Additionally, in April 2024, the SBNI collaborated with the National Crime Agency to coordinate an education alert about sextortion, which featured information for professionals and advice for parents/carers on how to talk to their child about sextortion and how to support them if they become a victim, signposting to the PSNI and Online Safety Hub for further support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether all Health and Social Care Trusts use both HPV primary screening and cytology in cervical screening processes; and (ii) to detail the number of severe adverse incidents in relation to cervical smear tests there have been in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Primary HPV testing was officially introduced into the Northern Ireland cervical screening programme from 11 December 2023 and along with a secondary cytology test, was used by all Trust laboratories.  A new single regional laboratory service has since been established from 1 November 2024. Only samples which are HPV positive proceed for a cytology test.  A Framework on the Audit of Invasive Cervical Cancers was published by the Public Health Agency (PHA) in 2019 and aimed to standardise the approach to audit and disclosure.  The number of invasive cervical cancer audits reported since 2019 to November 2024 as a category 3 outcome (defined as an unsatisfactory review which constitutes a patient safety incident) are as follows:Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) <5 Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) <5  Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) 13 South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) <5  Western Health and Social care Trust (WHSCT) 15* Anyone diagnosed with cervical cancer before 2019 who wants further information about the review of their screening history, can contact screening.cervical@hscni.net*As outlined in my Written Ministerial Statement on 25 July 2024, 12 cases (with a category 3 outcomes and notified as SAIs) occurred as a result of the WHSCT applying a different threshold than others when categorising cervical cancer audits with an ‘unsatisfactory’ outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the Youscreen and HPValidate research studies, whether he will consider the utilisation of at-home cervical smear tests in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), which advises Ministers and health services in the four nations on screening matters, has not made a recommendation on the use of HPV self-sampling for cervical screening.The UK NSC has an open consultation relating to HPV self-sampling in under-screened people, eligible for Cervical Screening (closing 26 February 2025): https://view-health-screening-recommendations.service.gov.uk/cervical-cancer/. The UK NSC will consider the responses and any potential recommendation at a future meeting and I will await their advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps he will take to ensure that women can have confidence in the accuracy of cervical screening tests; and (ii) whether he will commence a public inquiry into cervical cancer screening in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Over 99% of cervical cancers are caused by the human papilloma virus (HPV). Primary HPV testing, with cervical cytology as a second line test for those women who test positive for HPV, was fully implemented into the NI Cervical Screening programme from 11 December 2023. Primary HPV testing is an automated test which identifies the presence of HPV and was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than cervical cytology alone. The Public Health Agency (PHA) is monitoring awareness, confidence and barriers to participation in cervical screening and will continue to use this information to inform future promotional activities.  Following the publication of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcomes Report and the Cervical Cancers in the Southern Trust a detailed analysis of the findings of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcomes Report will be commissioned by the PHA. It is expected that this analysis, supported by appropriate independent experts, will be completed in January 2025.  These reports will help inform my next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Cervical Cytology Review Activity and Outcomes Report, whether he will commence a public inquiry into cervical cancer screening in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the publication of the two factual reports a detailed analysis of the findings of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcomes report will be commissioned by the PHA. It is expected that this analysis, supported by appropriate independent experts, will be completed in January 2025.  These reports will help inform my consideration of the next steps.  As Health Minister I remain committed to finding the most appropriate method to understand what happened and to learn lessons to ensure that our screening programmes deliver on behalf of our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the December 2024 Southern Health and Social Care Trust Cervical Cytology Review Activity and Outcomes Report.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcome Report and note that a detailed analysis of the findings is being commissioned by the PHA. It is expected that this analysis, supported by appropriate independent experts, will be completed in January 2025.  These reports will help inform my next steps.  As Health Minister, I remain committed to understanding what happened and to ensure that our cervical screening programme delivers on behalf of our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the 2025-26 budget will contain gender-based budgeting measures.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that it fulfils its Section 75 duties, and an equality impact assessment has informed the draft Budget for 2025-26.  This includes gender considerations alongside all other Section 75 groups.As part of the work on the Executive’s Budget Sustainability Plan, my department will consider ways in which gender budgeting can contribute to better budget outcomes for women.In addition, the development of the Executive's Social Inclusion Strategies, including the Gender Equality Strategy, will be crucial in enhancing our gender considerations in budgets and help drive long-term benefits of equal participation of women in all aspects of society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the amount of any increase in student places for entry at Ulster University Magee campus in 2024-25; (ii) any increase above the Maximum Student Number cap; and (iii) a breakdown of whether these places represent an expansion of existing courses or the establishment of new courses.
answer_text: The University has advised that it is too early to provide a definitive enrolment figure for the 2024/25 academic year, as the validation and verification of enrolment data is still ongoing. However, the Magee student population is projected to be between 5,900 and 6,000 learners, an increase from the actual enrolment of 5,337 in the previous year.Regarding the 500 additional funded MaSN (Maximum Student Number) places allocated to support the growth of Magee, Ulster University has delivered an increase of 475 places to date.The majority of this MaSN growth has been in existing courses. However, several new programs have welcomed their first cohorts in 2024/25. These include LLB (Hons) Law with Criminology, BSc (Hons) Accounting and Finance, BSc (Hons) Accounting with Computing, and BSc (Hons) Irish with Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps her Department is taking to ensure the adoption of new technologies and ways of working do not inadvertently hinder access to justice for individuals with specific needs.
answer_text: Technological approaches to the delivery of services afford a range of opportunities to address barriers to justice, principally by offering low-cost options that are accessible to users whatever their location.  The benefit of this expanded range of options is dependent on maintaining access for people who are less able to access technology-enabled routes to justice.  For this reason, traditional service delivery models will remain an important part of my Department’s approach to access to justice, even as we explore the potential of new approaches to improve access.In particular, when developing online service delivery models, my Department routinely applies the latest Web Content Accessibility Guidelines to ensure the maximum level of accessibility of its online service offering.  Additionally, new proposals to enhance access to justice will be subject to equality impact assessment requirements, affording a further opportunity for methods to enhance access for potentially excluded groups to be identified and adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered amending the Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999 to ensure that all children allegedly involved in an offence are granted anonymity at every stage of the criminal justice process, including the pre-charge stages.
answer_text: Under Article 22 of the Criminal Justice (Children) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, an automatic reporting restriction is granted in respect of children involved in criminal proceedings in the youth court.  For those appearing in the magistrates’ court or the Crown Court, a power is granted to the courts to determine whether a reporting restriction is required.  While there is legislative provision to grant anonymity to children at the pre-charge stage within Section 44 of the Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999, this has not been commenced due to concerns that its implementation could prevent open reporting of incidents involving young victims and, in cases such as suspected abduction, put children’s lives at risk.  In order to offer a level of protection to children in pre-charge cases, the Independent Press Standards Organisation has committed, as a rule, not to name any young person under the age of 18, who has been arrested before they appear before the youth court.Given that compliance with this code of practice remains strong, I have no plans to seek commencement of Section 44 of the Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999 to extend the restrictions on media reporting pre-charge.  This position will be kept under review by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department has taken to mitigate against future flooding events.
answer_text: My Department is the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to significant flooding, and my officials have worked with partner organisations to develop a multi-agency severe weather plan for emergencies that occur anywhere across the North. This plan details the pre-planned arrangements for a coordinated multi-agency response as a result of a warning for severe weather. The Department’s preparations for the response to severe weather, including flooding, is an ongoing process throughout the year. The unpredictable nature of weather events means heavy rainfall can occur at any time and as winter arrives there can be an increased risk of adverse weather which could lead to flooding conditions. Through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG), which is co-chaired by my Department and local government, there continues to be engagement with over 40 community groups in many areas across the North. These communities are primarily in flood risk areas or have flooded in the past and the work of the RCRG helps them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies. Through this community resilience work my Department seeks to provide an extra layer of support in addition to the normal response for those who may be impacted by flooding in periods of severe weather.My Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at more remote locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding. In addition, as the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary. The most effective method of managing flood risk for the public is to prevent insofar as is possible the risk occurring in the first place and advising developers and Planning Authorities in relation to flood risk is a key area of work through my River’s Directrorate’s role as a Statutory Consultee in the planning process.Advice on planning and flood risk matters is provided to Planning Authorities, for both present day and climate change flood risk scenarios, in line with Planning Policy. This advice, which is a material consideration for the Planning Authorities in their decision-making process, is to ensure that new development, where possible, is kept out of flood risk areas and does not contribute to an increase in flood risk elsewhere.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 18577/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 18577/22-27 was answered on 24 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the Ulster University Magee Taskforce Action Plan will be published.
answer_text: The Ulster University Magee Taskforce’s Report and Action Plan launch event, to which you have received an invite, is to be held on 16 December 2024, at the Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Derry City and Strabane District Council in the last six months in relation to the expansion of Ulster University's Magee campus to at least 10,000 students by 2030.
answer_text: Over the past six months, several meetings have taken place between Departmental officials and the Derry City and Strabane District Council. These discussions have focused on various Taskforce issues, including the integration of City Deals into the university’s expansion plans and the Council’s contributions to the "Destination City" section of the Taskforce action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Ulster University Magee Taskforce.
answer_text: The Ulster University Magee Expansion Taskforce has met ten times since its inception and have been progressing works to demonstrate how the Magee campus can grow to accommodate 10,000 students. Its final Report and Action Plan, which was formally launched on 16 December 2024, at the Magee campus, can be found here: Expansion of the Derry-Londonderry campus of Ulster University report and action plan | Department for the Economy

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the publication of the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the most recent iteration of the OREAP will be published alongside the upcoming public consultation on the Strategic Environmental Assessment/Habitats Regulations Assessment of the North’s marine area. This public consultation has been delayed as there are spatial matters that need to be resolved with the Ministry of Defence. The process that we are working through now will have benefits for the future and ensure a sustainable approach for offshore renewable energy development in the North. The Department and our key stakeholders continue to work collaboratively towards delivery of the actions as outlined within the draft OREAP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the names and organisations of those involved in the Social Enterprise Action Plan Co-Design Group.
answer_text: The Social Enterprise Action Plan was published and launched on the 4th of December 2024 and is available at the link below. Page 4 of the action plan lists the members of the Social Enterprise Co-Design Group alongside their organisation.  https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/social-enterprise-action-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of funding allocated to the full delivery of his Department's Social Enterprise Action Plan.
answer_text: The Social Enterprise Action Plan (SEAP) aims to increase the commercial capacity of social enterprises and, in turn, strengthen and grow the sector across the North. The five objectives set out in the SEAP are Awareness of the Sector, Leadership and Development, Investment Support, Procurement and Awareness of Support. 	As this activity will be carried out across a range of organisations and will involve changes to existing activity to be more inclusive of social enterprises as well as new activity, it is not yet possible to detail the budget involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the legal obligation to achieve net zero by 2050, to detail the implications if Northern Ireland does not reach its target by 2050.
answer_text: Under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, all North of Ireland departments share the legal obligation to achieve net zero emissions by 2050. Achieving this target is both a legal requirement and a moral obligation to the wellbeing of future generations, which is why decarbonisation is central to my economic plans. My Department will continue to collaborate across government and with wider stakeholders, including industry, in order to ensure we reduce carbon emissions and transition to a greener and more sustainable economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the companies currently involved in onshore petroleum exploration and production in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Petroleum (Production) Act (NI) 1964, my Department is responsible for the award of onshore petroleum licences. There are no current petroleum licences in place: the last active licence was relinquished on 28 April 2020.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the implementation of the recommendations made in the High Street Task Force.
answer_text: On 10th October we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.Responses have now been received from all four departments, indicating a willingness to take forward recommendations which sit within their remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how feedback from stakeholders is being incorporated into the lifelong learning policy.
answer_text: My Department considers and incorporates feedback from a variety of sources. It has been working closely with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning as detailed in my responses to AQW 16217/22-27 and AQW 18820/22-27. In addition, the Skills Council facilitates collaboration between various stakeholder groups to assess how the skills system can better support economic development in the north. This includes work being conducted through its sub-committees, which have considered the contribution lifelong learning can make. The Skills, Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-Committee of the Skills Council was set up to specifically examine issues with regards to access and participation in skills and education. SEDI membership is based on those who can best represent individuals or who have lived experience of skills inclusion issues coming from underrepresented groups. Its membership includes government, academia, women, disability, business, the manufacturing sector and policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the report on Innovation Skills international developments and Northern Ireland economic priorities.
answer_text: Innovation and Research & Development (R&D) are recognised key policy levers that will support delivery of my economic vision and its four priorities of productivity, regional balance, good jobs and decarbonisation.To drive innovation, we need a sufficient pipeline of skilled individuals to support the specific needs of innovating companies and respond to new and emerging opportunities.   I welcome the report on ‘Innovation Skills: International Developments and Northern Ireland Economic Priorities’ which undertakes analysis on the skills and capabilities required to support innovative growth across our economy.It includes a deeper focus on the skills requirements within my Department’s three priority areas for innovation:  accelerating research and development, delivering more and better innovation driven enterprises and supporting more businesses across the wider economy to innovate. The report provides valuable insights that, alongside the wider evidence base, will help shape and inform future policy and delivery within this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has conducted research into the devolution of VAT.
answer_text: The devolution of VAT was considered by the independent Fiscal Commission that was established by previous Finance Minister, Conor Murphy MLA, and which published its final report on 19 May 2022. The Commission’s report included a comprehensive suite of recommendations to progress fiscal devolution in the North. However, the devolution of VAT powers was not a recommendation made by the Fiscal Commission. In the Commission’s view, the uncertainty regarding the significant compliance and administration burdens were sufficient that the Commission concluded that other taxes should be prioritised.The Interim Fiscal Framework, which I negotiated earlier this year, committed the British Government to further consideration of fiscal devolution. My department is currently prioritising those elements of the Interim Fiscal Framework in relation to our funding arrangements, which are of critical importance in the near-term. Once these elements are sufficiently advanced, I will bring my proposals to the Executive on the additional fiscal powers we might seek and negotiate for this with the British Government. These proposals will be informed by the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the specific steps his Department is taking to improve research and development in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Investment in research and development (R&D), and innovation more widely, is key to delivering my economic priorities of productivity, regional balance, good jobs and decarbonisation. The most recent NISRA statistics show a significant increase in R&D levels (£1.1bn in 2022) with the majority increase from businesses’ expenditure on R&D. This represents a 14.1% increase in real terms from 2018. However, action is being taken to increase this further.		My Department is playing a key role in accelerating the mobilisation and delivery of the of City and Growth Deal projects which will provide unprecedented investment in R&D infrastructure, helping to leverage further R&D investment.		A key means to improve R&D is to maximise external funding.  My Officials are working in collaboration with Innovate UK to publish an action plan that will increase links with our region, stimulating increased successful bids for funding in UK wide competitive funding. These actions will build on the success of projects such as Smart Nano NI and the Future Medical Institute.		Following our success in securing the presence of Digital Catapult in the North my officials are working to establish a further Catapults that will harness and build on existing expertise. 	The provision of recurrent institutional funding is critical to support our universities to develop and enhance their R&D capability and capacity and to compete effectively in leveraging additional funding from external sources. My Department provides circa £75 million of recurrent research and knowledge exchange funding to our universities, and in the current year, additional allocations of £3.3 million for core research, and £3.96 million for knowledge exchange activities, have also been provided.   In July 2024, I also announced funding for an additional 51 PhDs, costing £4m over a three-year period. This is in addition to the c.729 PhDs which my Department funds annually through the Postgraduate Award (PGA) Scheme.	Invest NI are working towards a target within their Business Strategy to support a minimum of 1,650 investments and £435m of investment in R&D, innovation and digitalisation. 		Work includes promoting the benefits of R&D, designating a ring-fenced £5m fund for businesses that have not engaged in R&D in the last three years, facilitating collaborations to access R&D funding and assisting businesses to access national R&D funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department plans to ensure that schools are providing a minimum of two hours of physical education to their pupils each week.
answer_text: Although, there is no statutory time requirement for Physical Education (PE) in schools (and in fact primary legislation does not allow for this in respect of any subject), my Department recommends that schools should provide children and young people with at least two hours of good quality curricular PE each week.My Department established a Task and Finish group to address the recommendations from the Education and Training Inspectorate's (ETI) review of PE in primary schools. The group is made up of representatives from the Department, the Education and Training Inspectorate, The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment and the Education Authority (EA).Work streams were established, looking at Initial Teacher Education and professional development opportunities, the school estate, resources, and providing support to schools to help them plan and deliver the recommended two hours of curricular PE per week. A circular is being drafted currently which will provide practical guidance to schools on how to address the recommendations of the report.Furthermore, I recently announced a curriculum-led capital investment programme. This programme will target areas of learning across the curriculum for investment, including PE. The application process, including eligibility criteria, for both the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces for 20 primary schools and the programme for synthetic pitches and sports halls in the post-primary sector, will be launched in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any work she has undertaken with the Minister for the Economy in relation to bringing forward a package of costed interventions to support the hospitality sector and business sector.
answer_text: The Member will be aware of the wider funding challenges being faced across Government Departments here in the context of requests for further financial interventions.Both Departments have met at official and Ministerial level with business representatives from the hospitality sector since the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget, and I am attending a joint meeting with the hospitality sector and the Minister for Economy. I have also arranged to meet with a range of wider business stakeholders before Christmas to discuss my planned work on the rating system.In terms of any additional support, that would need to both have a clear economic rationale and be aligned to the Executive’s priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the additional £3.7 million for homelessness will be spent, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“NIHE will be utilising the £3.7m allocated to homelessness through the October Monitoring Round on key community prevention and support initiatives. These activities are delivered within communities by 3rd party providers, many of which provide services across Northern Ireland. As a result, it is not possible to identify how this funding will be utilised across constituency boundaries.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 18818/22-27, to provide a timeline for the review of Higher Education Funding.
answer_text: It is currently anticipated the Review of Higher Education Funding will commence during 2025 and will take at least 12 months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the 20 primary schools chosen for the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The application process for the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces for 20 primary schools will be launched by my department in the coming weeks. The criteria for eligibility for funding for the pilot project will be included within the application process. The process will require schools to implement a minimum of two hours curricular PE as a condition of funding and to participate in both self and external evaluation of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure that more opportunities for career progression are available to women in the workplace.
answer_text: A key objective in my Economic Vision is to increase the proportion of working-age people in Good Jobs. It is not acceptable that being in work does not guarantee a reasonable standard of living. This is particularly the case for women and people with disabilities, who disproportionately make up the low-paid.  I am conscious that caring responsibilities are a key issue for women. Those with caring responsibilities may face barriers to entering or remaining within employment which may impact upon their opportunity for career progression.  I have made clear my intent to introduce paid carer’s leave as part of a forthcoming Employment Rights Bill, subject to funding being agreed at an Executive level. The existing right to request flexible working also offers those with caring responsibilities the opportunity to request working arrangements which allow them to balance their caring responsibilities with work.  I consulted on proposals to strengthen these rights, including making them available from the first day of employment and enabling each employee to make two statutory flexible working requests within a 12 month period. Consultation responses are currently being analysed. This work will inform enhancements to be made to statutory entitlements in the North of Ireland within the current mandate.Apprenticeships provide a high-quality pathway to good jobs in sectors across the economy. Male participation in Apprenticeships is considerably higher than female participation, accounting for 73% of current participants. It is important that apprenticeships are inclusive and we make progress in supporting underrepresented groups.Whilst apprenticeship gender balance reflect patterns within the labour market I am seeking to address barriers to participation in apprenticeships for women. My Department leads on the work of Sectoral Partnerships (SPs), who are a key conduit for enabling employer ownership of the content of Apprenticeships at all levels. The Department will be working with SPs to encourage the development of a wider range of occupations to try and address the current imbalance. Recently, funding was prioritised to support enhanced eligibility for apprenticeships to support participation of those of all ages. This has supported an increase in female apprenticeships, particularly in pathways such as Health and Social Care.  I also launched an Inclusion Challenge Fund for apprenticeships, worth more than £600,000, that will drive collaboration across the education, business and voluntary and community sectors to develop and deliver innovative solutions to this inclusion challenge. Projects are due to commence in January 2025.Finally, my Department has a range of external communications to promote apprenticeships to women, this includes the Apprenticeship Awards, Apprenticeship Week and advertising campaigns to promote Apprenticeships to women and to encourage a more diverse participation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to improve the Relationships and Sexuality Education curriculum to better inform children and young people about consent and healthy relationships.
answer_text: Developing healthy and positive relationships are important to ensuring that our children and young people gain the knowledge, understanding and skills necessary to make informed life choices.There is a significant focus on healthy relationships within the current curriculum and schools can develop their approach to teaching this issue which reflects the needs of its pupils and the school’s Relationships and Sexuality Education policy and ethos.  Teachers are best placed to reflect the needs of young people and it is important that their taught programme reflects those needs. Pupils can explore their management of a range of feelings and emotions and those of others; initiating, developing and sustaining mutually satisfying relationships, explore the qualities of a loving, respectful relationship and develop coping strategies to deal with challenging relationship scenarios.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the establishment of an oversight group that will help his Department deliver the actions set out in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Executive Office has published its Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls together with its Delivery Plan for 2024-2026.My officials have held an initial workshop with Executive Office colleagues and will formally establish an Oversight Group in early 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will establish a ministerial taskforce to focus on the acceleration of offshore wind energy.
answer_text: My Department established the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group in April 2022. This group brings together key delivery partners to develop and deliver the action plan for offshore renewable energy in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps he is taking to address economic inactivity caused by long-term sickness absence.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has lead responsibility for economic inactivity, but many other departments have a contribution to make, including my own. Education, training, and skills development opportunities, at all levels and for all ages, are available at our Higher Education Institutions, Further Education (FE) Colleges, and through a range of other training providers. There is a range of financial support available to FE learners, including the Additional Support Fund for those learners with a disability, as well as support towards childcare costs.We also run programmes such as Skills for Life and Work, Skill Up, and Step Up, which aim to make skills and education more accessible to a wide range of people, including people experiencing additional barriers such as disability or illness.  My department also works with DfC to support individuals into employment through the Labour Market Partnerships, where officials from my Skills team and Careers Service are represented.Ultimately, economic inactivity is a complex issue which will require a range of approaches and collaboration across government agencies and the private sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any correspondence he has had with (i) the Utility Regulator; and (ii) NIE Networks following the announcement that the Standard Connection Charge for a new build house will increase by 123 per cent in 2025.
answer_text: My Department has no role in setting or approving the Standard Connection Charges.  The independent Utility Regulator assesses changes proposed by NIE Networks.NIE Networks consulted on the proposed increases to the Standard Connection Charges for developments of 12 or more dwellings, publicly in August 2023.  It published a decision paper in May 2024.  NIE Networks  said that the charges reflect the actual cost increases which NIE Networks has faced.  These connections are “contestable”.  Developers may engage independent contractors for most of the works if they choose.The Utility Regulator approved NIE Networks’ proposed charges in November 2024, but asked that NIE Networks introduce them in a phased manner to allow builders time to adjust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 17156/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 17156/22-27 was answered on 29th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 17510/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 17510/22-27 was answered on 29 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered a review into the way in which the electricity grid is developed.
answer_text: The principal objective of my Department in carrying out its electricity functions is to protect the interests of electricity consumers.  Under Art 12(2)(a) of the Energy Order 2003, the Department, and the Utility Regulator, must have regard to the need to secure that all reasonable demands in the North or Ireland for electricity are met.  My Department is working with the Utility Regulator on a range of issues affecting consumers in relation to, for example, connection charges, but does not consider that a review into the way the electricity grid is developed is needed at present.The development of the electricity network is the remit of the network companies, who have consulted on and published their plans, all with the oversight of the independent Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any correspondence he has had with the Minister for Communities following the announcement that the Standard Connection Charge for a new build house will increase by 123 per cent in 2025.
answer_text: My Department has no role in setting or approving the Standard Connection Charges.  The independent Utility Regulator assesses changes proposed by NIE Networks.NIE Networks consulted on the proposed increases to the Standard Connection Charges for developments of 12 or more dwellings, publicly in August 2023.  It published a decision paper in May 2024.  NIE Networks said that the charges reflect the actual underlying cost increases that it has faced.  These connections are contestable, meaning that developers may engage independent contractors other than NIE Networks, for most of the works if they choose.The Utility Regulator approved NIE Networks’ proposed charges in November 2024, but asked that NIE Networks introduce them in a phased manner to allow builders time to adjust.This is separate from my Department’s ongoing work in respect of socialisation of connection charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Women in Northern Ireland 2023 report published by the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency.
answer_text: I welcome the report detailing the labour market experience of women. The report highlights that a lower proportion of females than males earn above the Real Living Wage and a higher proportion of females than males are economically inactive. Creating more Good Jobs, so that working people enjoy a decent standard of living is central to my Economic Vision. My department is addressing this through a range of measures including the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, programmes such as the Women Returners Programme and the provision of funding for Advice NI to promote and grow the number of Real Living Wage employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to introduce legislation which would allow disciplinary procedures to take place for cases where PSNI officers are retired from duty, similar to the legislation in the UK.
answer_text: The ability to conduct disciplinary proceedings in relation to a former PSNI officer is currently under consideration. As the legislation governing this in England and Wales is primary legislation, my Department is currently exploring how a similar provision can be achieved here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether he plans to introduce flexible student finance options to help support modular study.
answer_text: I have committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including financial support provided to students.  I have instructed my officials to bring forward detailed proposals for this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that a lack of a women's health strategy is having on economic inactivity rates for females.
answer_text: Economic activity is a primary focus of my department, providing a range of programmes designed to support individuals who face barriers to employment. This was a key feature of the Skills Fund that I launched in July with an investment of almost £12m.  This fund facilitated skills interventions such as ‘Step Up’ and ‘Skill Up’.	Through Skill Up we are supporting 6,000 individuals this year to gain new skills by providing free, accredited courses that enhance individuals’ employment prospects. The majority of these courses are delivered online, enabling greater accessibility. Eligible participants can also avail of carer and travel allowance.	Step Up provides opportunities for Women who are seeking to enhance their skills or simply building their confidence as they consider returning to the workplace.	The current research on the Gender Employment Gap has not yet been published but its focus is primarily on the impact of different types of caregiving - childcare, granchildcare, spousal, parental. The report does include a section on the menopause but it concludes - although this relatively small literature faces substantial data limitations, we observe mixed evidence that the menopause may be driving the gender employment gap of those aged 50-65 years.	Economic inactivity in itself, is however, a DfC matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered a strategy to help unpaid carers back into employment.
answer_text: The issues impacting unpaid carers, and the barriers they face to entering or remaining within employment, are complex and stretch far beyond the policy remit of my own department. To support our unpaid carers, I have made clear my desire to introduce paid carer’s leave, subject to funding being agreed at an Executive level. The issue of cost implications, including the financial and societal benefits of a paid entitlement, will be important considerations for the Executive. The existing right to request flexible working also offers carers the opportunity to request working arrangements which allow them to balance their caring responsibilities with work.  I have consulted on proposals to strengthen these rights. Access to Further Education is key for addressing equality of opportunity and creating pathways to good jobs. For those who face barriers to participation, such as people with caring responsibilities, a range of financial support is currently available including FE Grants, Hardship Fund, Care to Learn (NI) childcare scheme and the Additional Support Fund. Our colleges also actively provide up-to-date information about available support for carers through their websites, communication channels, and outreach activities. Examples of support include, for example:A dedicated Young Adult Carer section on their websites, in the prospectus, and within the Student Handbook.Comprehensive Young Adult Carers Guide to help students navigate studying while managing their caring duties.Young Adult Carers have access to a "listening ear" service and a 24-hour counselling service for ongoing emotional support.Partnerships with external organisations, such as Action for Children and Barnardo’s, to provide referrals for additional carer support services.Some colleges host Young Adult Carer Action Days.Several colleges arrange for each carer to meet with a member of the support team to create a tailored support plan.Colleges look at how they can offer flexibility to support carers in balancing their studies with their responsibilities. These adjustments can include, extensions to assignment deadlines, flexible start and finish times and additional support mechanisms to help manage workloads or assignments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they plan to expand the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan to include specific actions to prevent and address online violence against women and girls, in recognition of the scale of online violence facilitated by technology, in particular the technology used in online and digital environments.
answer_text: Online violence is a key area of focus identified through the development of the Strategic Framework and Ofcom were a co-design partner. We know that the increasing challenge lies in the influence of the online world on young people, particularly in shaping their attitudes and capacity to establish safe and healthy relationships. Consideration will be given to how to address all forms of violence, abuse and harm against women and girls, including online, as part of the implementation of the first Delivery Plan actions.Our officials continue to collaborate with Ofcom as the regulator for online safety under the Online Safety Act 2023, in particular in relation to the development of guidance for protecting women and girls online, which Ofcom plans to launch for consultation in early 2025. Work is also ongoing through the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) Challenge Fund on 6 proof of concept projects that will use data, tech and design innovations to identify solutions that make spaces safer for women and girls, including online.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the Task and Finish Group which was set up to address work life balance and other workload issues.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group consists of senior college staff and members of University and College Union and National Association of Schoolmasters Union of Women Teachers. I understand that Further Education colleges have taken the decision to pause the work of the Group until the review of the collaborative/structural working arrangements between the Further Education Colleges in respect of staff pay, terms and conditions has concluded.I am hopeful this review will conclude without delay and allow the important work of the Task and Finish Group to recommence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a lifelong learning strategy.
answer_text: Access to skills and lifelong learning are at the heart of my economic vision, and I intend to publish a skills action plan in the New Year that will reflect the importance of lifelong learning. My officials have been working with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning, with a view to co-designing approaches to embedding a culture of lifelong learning.  A number of initiatives are already underway, including All Age Apprenticeships, a further 23 projects supporting 8,000 people to upskill and reskill and research into impact measurement of non-accredited adult learning which will be published in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a timeline as to when he will bring the action plan for apprenticeships to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: A review of opportunities and challenges within the apprenticeship landscape has identified a range of opportunities to improve, grow and diversify the apprenticeship system. Officials are developing the draft Action Plan which will be presented for Ministerial Approval as soon as possible. The DfE Apprenticeship Action Plan will not be an Executive document, its purpose is to clearly outline the key activities and outputs for the 2024-2027 period that will support the delivery and development of DfE apprenticeship provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to provide greater maintenance support for part-time students.
answer_text: I recently agreed to proceed with a review of Higher Education Funding and will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available to higher education students going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the level of drug-related activity in Derry; (ii) to detail how the level of drug-related activity in Derry has changed in recent years; and (iii) to detail the resources her Department has provided to the area in order to reduce drug-related activity.
answer_text: Some information on drug-related activity is publicly available. This includes the most recent data (to 2022) on drug related and drug misuse deaths at local government district level, which is published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) on behalf of the Department of Health (DoH) and can be found at: Drug-Related and Drug Misuse Deaths 2012-2022 | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency. The PSNI also provide statistics on drugs seizures and arrests across district council areas on a quarterly basis. Drug Seizure Statistics | PSNI details the most recent available data up to 30 September 2024.From a criminal justice perspective, it is not possible to accurately assess the full extent of drug related activity as not all those engaged in such activity will come into contact with the justice system. Moreover, the assessment of, and response to, any criminal activity is primarily an operational matter for the Chief Constable. However, my Department, through the work of the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF), supports the ongoing efforts of law enforcement partners to tackle this issue. The OCTF Drugs subgroup brings together agencies on a strategic basis to ensure they are collectively, as well as individually, alive to emerging threats.  PSNI continues to work with partners, locally and regionally, to assist communities to build resilience against Organised Crime Groups and the harm caused by illegal drugs supply in Northern Ireland.Furthermore, my Department is responsible for management of funding from the Assets Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS) to various organisations.  The purpose of the ARCS fund is to support initiatives that will prevent crime or reduce the fear of crime and so contribute to safer communities where we respect the law, and each other.   Whilst funding has not been specifically allocated for drug-related initiatives in Derry, some of the projects that operate in and around the wider Derry area may have indirect benefits.  For example, some of the youth diversionary projects will have elements built in such as awareness of the impact of drugs, building resilience through virtual reality by giving students the opportunity to face real life drug scenarios and personal development by challenging negative behaviours which can have a damaging impact on their community.At a local and community level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) funded by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in the Departments operational response to community safety concerns, including drug related activity in local communities. In Derry and Strabane, the local PCSP currently deliver a number of initiatives that seek to tackle drug-related harms, including the installation of 21 Remove All Prescription and Illegal Drugs (RAPID) bins across the district which have removed 80,000 tablets from public circulation since 2018, funding towards addiction recovery and family support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road repairs that have taken place in Derry during the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: A total of 9,982 road defects were repaired in Derry in 2023-24 financial year.You will also find my Department’s official statistics on the condition of the road network published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is responsible for clearing obstructions on pathways that pose accessibility issues for wheelchair users or those with prams.
answer_text: Article 88 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 of the Order states that “any person who, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, in any way intentionally or negligently obstructs the free passage along a road shall be guilty of an offence." The footway is deemed part of the road by definition under the Order.Reports of footway obstructions should be directed to the PSNI in the first instance as the organisation responsible for enforcement under this Article.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider amending the carers allowance threshold to allow unpaid carers the opportunity to seek employment opportunities.
answer_text: From April 2025 my Department will increase the Carer’s Allowance weekly earnings limit to the equivalent of 16 hours at the National Living Wage. This change will better support unpaid carers to move into work or to work additional hours if they choose, whilst still remaining eligible for Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department is undertaking to support young carers to remain in education.
answer_text: In January 2024, my Department issued a ‘Young Carers in Education Digital Awareness Pack’ to all schools, with the aim of helping to raise awareness of, and the support available for, young carers in education.  The pack was developed by Action for Children and Barnardo’s Northern Ireland, supported by the Department and the Education Authority (EA). The pack included: a digital poster designed by Young Carers; EA and Health and Social Care Board (HSC) Guidance on ‘Supporting Young Carers in School’; a copy of Action for Children’s ‘Young Carers’ Education Research’ report; and a related letter to promote the poster, guidance and support services available to children and young people with a caring role.  Officials have subsequently met with Action for Children and Barnardo’s Northern Ireland to discuss the impact of the Digital Awareness Pack and there are plans to revise the ‘Supporting Young Carers in School’ guidance.I have nominated officials to sit on the Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for Carers. My officials will advocate for the needs of young carers whilst working collaboratively with other departments.  The EA will also be introducing a specialist post to support young carers regionally.  This post will be developed in collaboration with voluntary support services with a view to ensuring consistency of approach and positive outcomes for young carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department plans to introduce transition task forces to help with the Just Transition.
answer_text: The nature and scale of a future bespoke Just Transition Fund for Agriculture will be determined by emerging needs and the advice from the Just Transition Commission. Funding for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture will need to be considered in the context of budgetary pressures and priorities across the NI Executive.It is therefore too early in the process to specify any plans to introduce transition task forces to help with the Just Transition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he is taking to address traffic congestion in the Crescent Link area, Derry, including any update on the Caw roundabout signalisation.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has no immediate plans for traffic management interventions on the A514 Crescent Link.  However, officials are aware of traffic congestion issues which can arise in the Cresent Link area at the adjacent retail park. Traffic management within the retail park is the responsibility of the owners. The upgrade and signalisation of Caw Roundabout is included in the planning conditions for the adjacent H30 housing development and it is the responsibility of the developer to deliver these works.My Department have agreed a preliminary design, however, there will be a lead time associated with delivery of the highway improvements. Officials are working with the developer to bring forward the works as quickly as possible, which must be completed prior to occupation of the 301st dwelling at the latest. The timing is dependent on progression of a range of issues, including completion of the detailed design and construction on site.It is anticipated that the signalisation of Caw Roundabout will reduce congestion on the Crescent Link approach arm to this junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure the area surrounding the Peace Bridge, Derry, remains illuminated.
answer_text: Responsibility for the lighting on the Peace Bridge is shared between my Department and Derry City & Strabane District Council who work collaboratively to ensure lighting operates effectively. My Department are responsible for providing the functional lighting to pedestrians and cyclists that use the bridge and the Council are responsible for the aesthetic lighting which illuminates the structure. I can confirm that no street lighting faults in the vicinity of the peace bridge have recently been reported to the Department.  However, any street lighting faults can be reported to NI Direct, via the online facility or by telephone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the action she is taking to address any legislative gap preventing disciplinary proceedings to be taken against a retired PSNI officer, as exists in the UK.
answer_text: The ability to conduct disciplinary proceedings in relation to a former PSNI officer is currently under consideration. As the legislation governing this in England and Wales is primary legislation my Department is currently exploring how a similar provision can be achieved here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on repairs to the lights surrounding the Peace Bridge, Derry.
answer_text: Responsibility for the lighting on the Peace Bridge is shared between my Department and Derry City & Strabane District Council who work collaboratively to ensure lighting operates effectively. My Department is responsible for providing the functional lighting to pedestrians and cyclists that use the bridge and the Council are responsible for the aesthetic lighting which illuminates the structure. I can confirm that no street lighting faults in the vicinity of the peace bridge have recently been reported to the Department.  However, any street lighting faults can be reported to NI Direct, via the online facility or by telephone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women currently on a waiting list for a gynaecology appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment in Northern Ireland is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin was published on 28 November 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-september-2024  The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Gynaecology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is included in the associated ‘Outpatient Waiting Times Statistics by HSC Trust and Specialty September 2024’ Excel file, is also available for download at the same location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to bring forward a rates relief scheme for independent retailers and other high street businesses through increased Barnett consequentials, following the Autumn Budget 2024 announcement.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to my answer to AQW 17794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the delivery of the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: Following confirmation from the British Government that the £150m for the Enhanced Investment Zone (EIZ) Programme in the North will be made available, my Department is working with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government to agree the steps and timescales to secure approval for release of the funding. The EIZ is a 10 year programme, however it is anticipated that initial funding will be released during the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered reviewing the current firm access methodology for those who are wishing to invest in the electricity network.
answer_text: Connection access methodology is the remit of the network companies, and approved by the independent Utility Regulator.  SONI’s Forward Work Plan for 2024/25 includes a plan to issue a consultation paper on firm access in early 2025.The recent RP7 business plan approved by the Utility Regulator will bring £2.23 billion in investment and operational expenditure into the electricity networks here over 2025-2031, the largest in our history.  This network investment will materially increase access to our electricity networks for investors and increase the number of firm connections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work undertaken by his Department to make smart meters available to domestic electricity users, including any time frame for their introduction.
answer_text: My department has developed a design plan proposing principles for the roll-out of smart electricity meters. There is currently a public consultation on these design principles. The consultation will run until 12th December 2024. It is expected that the energy industry will provide information on the procurement process for the roll-out. An indicative timeline will be agreed by an Oversight Group, comprising the Department and the Utility Regulator, when the software and systems are fully designed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the PSNI's response to the Programme for Government outlining that policing numbers are the single most important issue the Executive needs to address this year.
To ask the Minister of Education whether the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will be extended beyond the end of the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Having listened to parents and providers and given the success of the scheme to date, it is my intention to bid for the funding required to sustain it beyond the end of March 2025.  Ultimately it will be for the Executive to decide if it wishes to allocate the associated budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department is undertaking to introduce more financial measures to support parents following the introduction of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: I have introduced a range of measures in the 2024/25 financial year to support the affordability and accessibility of early learning and childcare services, including the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.  Combined with Tax Free Childcare, this provides parents with a 32% reduction on their childcare bills. Additional measures include the stabilisation and expansion of a range of existing early learning and childcare programmes and commencement of the standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme.   All these measures will be evaluated to inform future proposals. Additionally, eligible parents access financial support through Universal Credit, where up to 85% of childcare costs can be covered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with Northern Ireland Electricity Networks in relation to an evaluation of power networks in the North West region.
answer_text: There is ongoing engagement at an official level between my officials, the Utility Regulator, SONI (the System Operator for Northern Ireland) and NIE Networks, regarding the development of the electricity network across the whole of the North.  The outcomes of this work can be seen in the RP7 price control, at a total 2.23bn, which will provide an unprecedented level of investment in developing the grid across the entire region.  My officials will continue to engage with stakeholders, including NIEN, regarding the grid in all regions of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has undertaken an evaluation of power networks in the North West region.
answer_text: There is ongoing engagement at an official level between my officials, the Utility Regulator, SONI (the System Operator for Northern Ireland) and NIE Networks, regarding the development of the electricity network across the whole of the North.  The outcomes of this work can be seen in the RP7 price control, at a total 2.23bn, which will provide an unprecedented level of investment in developing the grid across the entire region.  My officials will continue to engage with stakeholders, including NIEN, regarding the grid in all regions of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any correspondence he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to additional support that can be provided to dental practices following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: I have had no correspondence with the Finance Minister in relation to additional support that can be provided to dental practices following the Autumn Budget 2024.Despite the positive news about the budget settlement for 2025-26 it is still likely we will be facing a significant funding gap in Northern Ireland as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand. The Department of Health reported, during October Monitoring, a total short fall of £450m.  This included £130m to fund the continuation of existing services and £320m for non-contractual pay to match the English pay offers.Recognising that the funding available was unlikely to cover existing pressures, including pay, the Department of Finance did not invite bids at October monitoring outside of these pressures.I acknowledge there are a number of healthcare providers that will be affected by the rise in employer National Insurance contributions announced in the Budget and I understand that as well as General Dental Practitioners, GPs, care homes and hospices have voiced concerns about the impact this will have on them. Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to support older workers who wish to remain active.
answer_text: Whilst the Department for Communities (DfC) has policy responsibility for employability and reducing economic inactivity, my Department makes an important contribution through our skills and education remit, any of which are delivered on an all age basis.The six Further Education colleges and Universities across the region offer a wide range of courses open to individuals aged sixteen and over, who wish to learn new additional skills and remain active. These providers work in collaboration with employers to develop qualifications to upskill and reskill learners supporting older workers to remain active. There are also a number of supports specifically for adults, for example, the introduction of All Age Apprenticeships which allow older people more opportunities to gain a recognised qualification across a wide range of sectors.The Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis and can help older works who wish to remain active understand what opportunities or support (including financial support) would be available for them. Some further information on changing career, developing new skills or getting back into learning can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/returning-learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any recent engagement he has had with the British Government in relation to the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: Enhanced Investment Zone is a co-design policy with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG).  DfE colleagues along with MHCLG counterparts are commencing planning and governance work.I held a telephone conversation with Alex Norris MP Parliamentary Under-Secretary (Housing, Communities and Local Government) on the 24th October where we discussed the need for the EIZ to be funded in the Autumn Statement.  I am pleased to say that the funding position was confirmed and that we can now move from working at risk to actively delivering the co-design policy requirements. I am meeting with Alex Norris MP on the 13th November to discuss the delivery of the Enhanced Investment Zone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: Following a period of uncertainty surrounding the Enhanced Investment Zone (EIZ) funding I now welcome the confirmation from the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG)  that funding for the EIZ has been secured. EIZ is a co-designed policy initiative with MHCLG. My officials are collaborating with their counterparts in MHCLG regarding development and implementation. My officials are also building capacity and capability to deliver the programme.  MHCLG are estimating funding to be released during the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to alleviate economic inactivity within the elderly population.
answer_text: Whilst the Department for Communities (DfC) has policy responsibility for employability and reducing economic inactivity, my department has an important contribution to make through our skills and education remit. This includes extensive provision through our Colleges and Universities. To enhance this existing provision, I recently launched our Skill Up programme which provides a flexible approach to learning that may attract older people back to education.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work they are undertaking to address age discrimination, particularly in relation to the provision of goods and services.
answer_text: Consideration of extending age discrimination legislation to the provision of goods, facilities and services was the subject of consultations in a previous mandate.  A decision was not made in respect of the scope of that legislation. Officials have commenced a review to inform the potential scope of any legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Consumer Council's Electric Vehicles Consumer Research report regarding public charging infrastructure in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with the Consumer Council on public charging infrastructure in the North through membership of the Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task Force. The Electric Vehicle (EV) Consumer Research report published in February 2024 provided the Task Force with a helpful picture of some of the challenges faced by consumers and areas for future development within the EV infrastructure sector.  This work will be used to inform best practice within the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what objectives his Department has set for councils who are seeking to avail of extra funding through the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund.
answer_text: A Memorandum of Understanding has been issued to each of the 11 district councils in relation to the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund.The overall objective of the Fund is to ‘improve the uptake of free, independent regulated debt advice through a collaborative partnership approach’.The Memorandum of Understanding states that projects must:deliver holistic (income maximisation, debt and welfare) advice in community accessible settings such as health, education, faith-based locations;be a collaborative partnership with two or more organisations, one of which can supply the free, independent, FCA regulated debt advice. Projects can be led by a single organisation with the planned collaboration evidenced in their proposal; demonstrate improved outcomes (financial and wellbeing) for people accessing the service;include an aspect of face to face/in person delivery.
