question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the steps he will take to ensure there is support for pupils to develop healthy and respectful relationships.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14176/22-27, (i) when Prehen Park will be assessed for inclusion in the gritted route network; and (ii) when the outcome of such an assessment can be expected.
answer_text: A traffic count is required for the assessment of Prehan Park for inclusion within the gritted route network.  Officials will undertake this work during September 2024 with the results being available in early October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the development of the Women’s Health Action Plan, whether he plans to commission a women’s health strategy during this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Due to the severe financial constraints facing my Department and the absence of a long term budget, I intend to focus during the remainder of this mandate on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise our Health and Social Care system and address the health inequalities that contribute to poor health outcomes, rather than commit to any new long-term strategies which cannot be funded at this time. As outlined in my response to AQW 13039/22-27 there are a wide range of policy and service development initiatives in progress which aim to improve women's health services and outcomes. These will be highlighted in the women's health action plan which will outline my vision for what can be delivered during the current Assembly mandate, and provide a foundation for a longer-term women’s health strategy in the future, should the Executive decide to prioritise this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the decentralisation of staff in Invest NI following the publication of the Independent Review of Invest NI.
answer_text: Invest NI is currently finalising its 2024-27 Business Strategy. Details on the organisation's regional model will be finalised following agreement and publication of the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has engaged with the Public Prosecution Service in relation to sentencing for cases involving revenge porn.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 6 - AQO 742/22-27			ANSWERSections 51 to 53 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provide for the offence of disclosing, or threatening to disclose, private sexual photographs and films with intent to cause distress, often referred to as ‘revenge pornography’. It is an abhorrent and despicable crime and one which is used by perpetrators as a means to humiliate and intimidate their victims: a cruel crime often carried out by partners or ex-partners. The offence can be tried either summarily or on indictment: that is either in the magistrates’ court or in the Crown Court. On summary conviction, a maximum penalty of 6 months’ imprisonment is available. On conviction on indictment, a maximum penalty of 2 years’ imprisonment is available. While, as Chair of the Criminal Justice Board, I engage with the Public Prosecution Service, it is wholly independent of the Department. Decisions on where a case is tried are a matter entirely for the PPS and are made in accordance with the PPS Code for Prosecutors. This is not a matter where I could or would intervene. My role is to ensure that there is an appropriate and robust criminal legislative framework in place. The sentences handed down within that legislative framework are a matter for the independent judiciary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has engaged with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency on the scale and impact of all sources of pollution into the River Foyle.
answer_text: Question 12 - Sinéad McLaughlinTo ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has engaged with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency on the scale and impact of all sources of pollution into the River Foyle.AQO 762/22-27Answer:The River Foyle, as part of the wider Lough Foyle catchment, is subject to the same routine monitoring and assesement of water quality as all other waterbodies in Northern Ireland. Reports of pollution are investigated by both the NIEA and the Loughs Agency with enforcement action undertaken in line with existing policy. The most recent Water Framework Directive statistics show that more than two thirds of the river water bodies that flow into the River Foyle are at less than Good status. The drivers for status vary but are largely due to nutrient and hydromorphological pressures. While this is disappointing I am aware that water quality across Northern Ireland is not what it should be. I am therefore pleased that the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan has been approved and I am also pleased that as part of the draft Programme for Government for consultation, that the Executive has committed to publishing Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) which lays out my commitment to improving water quality. The draft EIP contains the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan for the period 2021-2027 taking an integrated approach to the protection, improvement and sustainable use of the water environment. The River Basin Management Plan will contain a Programme of Measures setting out the actions required to protect and improve all water bodies and will require collective action across government, the public and private sectors and wider society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he will take to address the provision of any misleading information or guidance in relation to abortion services through pregnancy care centres; and (ii) how he plans to engage with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority in order to ensure that such centres are not providing misleading information or guidance.
answer_text: During December 2022 and February/March 2024 my Department carried out public and professional awareness campaigns to inform on the provision of and access to abortion services for women and girls in Northern Ireland.  Women and girls are signposted to the NI Direct website Abortion services | nidirect which provides detail of how to access the central access point and signposts women to pregnancy choices and post abortion counselling services.My Department endeavours to include links to the NI Direct website within relevant publications.  The Public Health Agency website “SexualHealthNI” which is presently being updated, will include a link to NI Direct Abortion Services webpage.  The link will also be included in the planned “Living Well” campaign which will be running in October 2024.The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) have no powers to register or inspect pregnancy care centre services. These centres would only be required to be registered with the RQIA if private doctors offered medical services or treatments from within the establishment.Departmental officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy framework which considers a range of matters including principles of regulation, a broad scope of services that may be subject to regulation and a proposal that the regulator might have wider powers of enforcement. Work on this is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.My Department will be engaging with RQIA as part of the future review of regulation and this will provide the platform to consider any identified improvements in the regulation and monitoring of services across a wider range of areas, including pregnancy care centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11179/22-27, (i) for an update on the work of the GP Access Working Group; and (ii) what reasonable adjustments are in place for those with disabilities to access their GP without going through telephone services only.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. The GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, is overseeing work to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency and improve experience for patients. The Group is also considering how technology can help to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services for patients and practices alike.  GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department to provide primary care services.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce. Practices have a responsibility to ensure that patients have clear information on how to access their services, making reasonable adjustments for specific groups when necessary.  Each practice can determine how best to do this within their local context and for their patient population.  This includes ensuring they have appropriate systems in place for communication with their patients.  Each practice will decide which communication system suits their practice and patients.  They must, however, offer patients a range of options to contact the practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 12992/222-27, (i) for a timeline on the review of higher education funding; and (ii) whether he will task his officials to explore how the current student funding arrangements can be reformed to allow access to student funding for graduate entry medicine students.
answer_text: My officials are currently working on the details of the review, including timelines.In advance of the review officials have been considering the issue of access to tuition fee loans for students studying graduate entry medicine.  While this would normally be an issue for the Department of Health, I recently wrote to the Minister of Health who has agreed that my Department should take forward consideration of this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department will take in relation to the defective service strip at Great James Street in the Foyle constituency which is hindering traffic and causing delays.
answer_text: I have asked that the local Network Development team to assess the operation of these signals to determine if adjustments are required.  The local team will update your office once the assessment is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will amend the Education (Student Support) (No. 2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 to ensure that students from Northern Ireland, who are studying a higher education qualification in an EU country, are eligible to apply for tuition fees, the maintenance grant and loan support.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to provide me with advice on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what funding has been allocated to address the spending obligations that have arisen from the McCloud remedy.
answer_text: Pensions in payment are paid from Annually Managed Expenditure (AME). Treasury has given a commitment that additional costs accrued by members and payable by schemes in respect of any additional McCloud remedy related financial or tax losses will be classed as AME. These costs will be accounted for as part of the normal pension forecasting process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11714/22-27, for an update on his officials’ discussions with the local community to secure access to clean gullies at Cuthbert Street, Derry.
answer_text: My Department has arranged for a contractor to visit Cuthbert Street on Friday 19th July at 10am to clean all road gullies. Leaflets with details of the works including gully locations, and the dates/times of the proposed work have been placed on car windscreens and posted through all letterboxes on the street.The location of the gullies has also been temporarily marked on the footways to highlight the areas that should be kept clear.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s efforts to eradicate the operation of illegal taxis in Derry, particularly along Waterloo Place.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has primary responsibility for enforcing taxi regulations in the North.  Taxi enforcement is delivered through a combination of compliance audits conducted at operators’ premises and targeted checks on taxis and drivers at the roadside.  The DVA conducts roadside enforcement operations across the North, often in collaboration with other enforcement delivery partners, such as the PSNI. During these operations, enforcement officers undertake vehicle and licensing inspections to ensure that taxis and drivers meet all required standards.  Where offences are detected, DVA enforcement officers can seize suspected illegal taxis, impose fines, and pursue prosecution action.The DVA has conducted several enforcement operations in Derry this year, which have included Waterloo Place.  These operations have resulted in the detection of a number of illegal taxis, and the DVA is planning further operations in this area to help address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will extend the gritting route along Prehen Park, Derry, by approximately 200m, between Sandringham Drive and Sunningdale Dive, so that the bus route along this stretch of road is fully gritted.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department will assess Prehen Park for inclusion in the gritted route network in accordance with RSPPG E022 – Winter Service. If this section of Prehen Park meets the necessary criteria, officials will arrange to have it included on the scheduled gritted network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the 6.4 per cent uplift to Supporting People is intended to replace the 5.8 per cent cut in 2023-24 revenue funding experienced by the programme, or whether it will be applied in addition to reversing this cut.
answer_text: I provided an additional £3m in 2023/24 to the Supporting People Programme making a closing allocation of £75.8m, and this year I have provided a budget allocation of £80.7m. This financial year’s budget allocation remains indicative as it is subject to the outcome of the Department’s Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25, which was commissioned on 11 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of any challenges faced by same-sex couples in registering births under their own names.
answer_text: I am not currently aware of any challenges faced by same-sex couples in registering births under their own names.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the work of the Collaboration Test and Learn programme since the restoration of the Assembly; (ii) any key outputs of this programme; and (iii) how many specific meetings of the programme have focused on regional balance.
answer_text: Through the Test and Learn process improving collaboration is the primary issue that has been fed back to officials from my Department on numerous occasions during engagement sessions. It also reflects feedback that has been received by other Government Departments who are working on tackling deprivation and associated issues. NICS Board approved a Collaboration Strategic Framework and potential Test and Learn pilot areas. The pilot areas were identified through work with Departmental statisticians, in which localities with multiple government Departments operating in an area where identified. It was agreed that community background and departmental presence would be key data points considered in the decision-making process and analysis was carried out through an urban and rural lens. This approach has continued throughout and has been applied at all meetings.The initial pilots commenced in the Diamond (Derry/Londonderry) and Aughnacloy with the final evaluation workshops of this phase, held in May 2024. The findings from the Aughnacloy and Diamond pilots have been collated, analysed, and prepared in the form of an Evaluation Report which is currently being reported to Ministers and being presented to the NICS Board in July. These findings include that, in considering the next phase, that the initial pilot areas should be expanded.This Report shows the necessity for continuous improvement within Government and how it interacts with communities on place-based initiatives, so that services are people-centred, and outcome focused. Strategic Recommendations have been proposed within the Report, for NICS Board consideration.   Any decisions on additional pilots will then be taken after the report is considered by Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he has considered the introduction of a form of regional aid in order to assist in alleviating regional imbalances; and (ii) whether his Department has produced any analysis of areas that would be eligible for regional aid under EU rules.
answer_text: My Department is currently exploring   options for a new fund to support delivery of the forthcoming Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan.   Until 31 December 2027, all areas in the North are eligible for regional aid under EU rules.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they are taking to ensure their Department works with the local council to increase the number of local events at Ebrington Square, including to plan for summer 2025.
answer_text: The Department is working closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council and other stakeholders as we work to develop a vibrant and inclusive year-round events programme for Ebrington.   A series of community workshops have taken place recently to explore better ways of working together to realise the full potential of Ebrington.  The view expressed at these workshops and those from recent engagement with other stakeholders, will be used to inform the future approach to events and how to maximise the unique space at Ebrington. TEO wants to help event promoters and local groups to ensure that Ebrington is well used throughout the year for both large scale events and other cultural and community activities.  Derry City and Strabane District Council, on behalf of the Department, will shortly be calling for Expressions of Interest for large scale licensed events in both 2025 and 2026. This will allow for longer lead in times for events of scale. There is also activity planned for both the Halloween and Christmas periods.The Department has also engaged a local marketing company to deliver an enhanced animation and events programme to ensure Ebrington is used and visited by local people across the year.  This has included a series of activity during June and July.  Additional activities are currently being planned. The Ebrington event programme for 2024 is outlined below: DateEvent29 JuneMaritime weekend activity – free family fun, including mini golf, photobooths, and interactive acts30 JuneMaritime weekend – family fun as live dinosaurs take over Ebrington!Live interactive dinosaurs, mobile museums and laboratories.29 JuneFoyle Hospice charity cycle23 – 25 AugMusic festival weekend24 AugTour de Foyle – charity cycle race1 SeptWaterside Half Marathon14 & 15 SeptEuropean Heritage Open Days28/29 SeptFoyle Memory Walk - Alzheimer’s Society25 Oct – 3 NovCullen’s Amusements

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether the new Skill Up programme will cover the same 300 subject areas as its predecessor, across both further and higher education providers; (ii) whether Skill Up courses will be free or partially subsidised; and (iii) when the new Skill Up programme is expected to be fully rolled out.
answer_text: The Skill Up programme will support a wide range of subject areas that will directly address the skills gap in key sectors relating to my economic vision including Green Skills, Software, Advanced Manufacturing, Childcare, Health and Social Care, Hospitality, and other transversal Skills. Training will initially be delivered through both further and higher education providers. Skill Up courses will be free for all eligible participants and will be rolled out from September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he has instructed his officials to include population growth targets for urban centres as part of the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan; and (ii) for his assessment of which urban centres these targets will pertain to.
answer_text: The contents of the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan is still being formulated as co-design continues with stakeholders. It will be published in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 13281/22-27, (i) why his Department has not engaged with officials from the Department of Health on the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan; and (ii) whether he will instruct his officials to do so.
answer_text: My officials have had conversations with officials in the Department of Health (DoH) through the cross-departmental Collaboration Test and Learn Group and other forums, listening and learning from DoH (and other health sector organisations’) experience in developing initiatives, and exploring how enhanced place-based collaborative working between NICS departments and between the NICS and community organisations can improve delivery and outcomes for local communities.      Stakeholders co-designing the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan have not identified the need for formal DoH input at this stage, but I welcome any views from the sector and greater cross-government engagement will occur after the publication of the Plan which will set out a framework that enables wider NICS participation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department’s assessment of the drainage system in Hinton Park, Waterside, in the Foyle constituency; and (ii) the extent to which it has been added to the public system.
answer_text: The management of the sewerage network is an operational matter for NI Water.  However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:There are no known issues with the infrastructure that NI Water is responsible for at this location. NI Water officials are however aware of a problem  from a neighbouring  private drainage network, which does not fall under NI Water’s remit.  The drainage system in Hinton Park is adopted by NI Water.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has commissioned any research of existing literature in relation to sub-regional economic differences.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned research from the Ulster University’s Economic Policy Centre that will explore the economic evidence for regional differences across the North and provide evidence to further develop and deliver local economic strategies.Part of this research will also include a literature review, focusing on regional inequalities as well as the importance of regional cities in regional economic development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 13337/22-27, for his assessment of whether (i) higher education institutions have enough students and staff representatives on their governing bodies; (ii) those bodies are appropriately democratic; and (iii) the representation of students and staff in governance arrangements will be included in planned outcome agreements.
answer_text: As legally autonomous institutions, Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University are responsible for ensuring the composition of their governing bodies conform with their respective charters and statutes. Both universities are also members of the Committee of University Chairs and as such, adhere to applicable guidance including the 'Higher Education Code of Governance'.Outcome Agreements will not supersede the arrangements already in place. As per my previous answer, Outcome Agreements will show how each institution – consistent with its autonomous status, mission and values – will continue to play its part in achieving our shared ambitions for the North.The composition of Stranmillis University College’s Governing Body is prescribed by the Colleges of Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2005. The Governing Body of St Mary’s University College adheres with a Scheme of Management agreed with my Department.All of our Higher Education Institutions currently have staff and student representatives on their Governing Bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Executive Office in relation to the funding cut to Women’s Aid, including whether the Executive Office has offered to collaborate with his Department in sourcing appropriate monies to replace this funding.
answer_text: While my officials have kept Executive Office colleagues appraised of the core grant funding position, a discussion about how that funding could be replaced did not take place.Core grant funding will be maintained in 2024/25 at the same level as in 2023/24 and I made an announcement to that effect on 9 July 2024. Supported and facilitated by NICVA, work with the sector will take place over the next few months on the design of a new funding scheme for the sector, with the aim of having a new scheme in place in 25/26.  The first engagement event is scheduled to take place on 21 August 2024.  I want to make it clear that I am committed to supporting the sector, subject to the confirmation of budget allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps she will take to ensure that police officers, delivering training to fellow officers on sexual offences, will be properly trained.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of providing victims of crime with appropriate and timely support, information, advice and assistance and this is particularly true in cases of sexual crime.Police officer training is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Therefore, I cannot comment on specific police procedures or practices as I have a duty to respect that independence. You may therefore wish to address your question to the Chief Constable.Furthermore, I have been advised that over 500 frontline police officers have recently received training from Nexus in relation to their response to sexual crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether Making Life Better continues to operate as the Executive’s strategic framework for improving health outcomes; (ii) to detail the progress reports that have been published by his Department on this framework since 2015; and (iii) for his assessment of the effectiveness of this framework from 2013 to 2023.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is a mechanism in place to ensure that an individual reporting a domestic abuse or sexual offence related crime will be assigned a PSNI officer who has received training in those areas.
answer_text: I recognise how important it is that at every stage of the criminal justice process cases involving domestic abuse or a sexual offence related crime are handled with appropriate sensitivity. Police officer training is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Therefore, I cannot comment on specific police procedures or practices as I have a duty to respect that independence.However, the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (2021) and the Protection from Stalking Act (2022) provided requirements for mandatory training in relation to those issues for a number of criminal justice organisations. The legislation requires that training must be provided at least annually and is mandatory for personnel/staff that have responsibilities for dealing with cases involving domestic abuse or stalking, to ensure the effective discharge of their responsibilities in relation to such cases.Furthermore, I have been advised that over 500 frontline police officers have recently received training from Nexus in relation to their response to sexual crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what training and guidance is available for teachers, including those in school leadership positions, to address (i) bullying; and (ii) sexual assault in schools.
answer_text: The Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Northern Ireland deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes aligned with the Northern Ireland Curriculum (NIC), preparing future teachers for effective curriculum delivery. These programmes also ensure new teachers achieve the professional competences outlined by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI). The NIC emphasises the importance of diversity, respect and mutual understanding, while the GTCNI’s competences highlight the importance of: maintaining positive behaviour; promoting understanding of differing cultures, languages and faiths; understanding the interrelationship between schools and the communities they serve; encouraging inclusive behaviours; and preventing inappropriate behaviour. The emphasis on promoting positive behaviour and addressing intolerance varies between schools and individual classrooms, reflecting the specific needs and circumstances of pupils and evolving societal issues.  ITE represents the initial phase of a teacher’s professional learning journey. Teachers are expected to continually learn and develop professionally throughout their careers, driven by the needs of their pupils and schools, and in line with the Department’s Strategy for Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) – “Learning Leaders”. Support is provided by schools, the HEIs, and specialist teams within the Education Authority (EA).Recent measures my Department has taken to address bullying include the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, introduced in 2021. This Act defines bullying; establishes a new duty to record all incidents and motivations; and requires the Boards of Governors to take responsibility for anti-bullying policies. In 2022, my Department also established the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the EA. ABSIT supports schools in understanding and implementing the Act, embedding anti-bullying cultures, and offering training to schools.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) area of learning helps students develop knowledge, understanding and values about relationships and sexuality. Effective RSE, taught inclusively, encourages self-respect and responsible decision-making. The EA supports the delivery of RSE through TPL and school initiatives, and additional resources are available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) website.Guidance on harmful sexual behaviour in schools is provided in DE Circular 2022/02, and the Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools document. Schools have a statutory requirement to designate a Teacher (DT) and a Deputy Designated Teacher (DDT) for child protection, roles which are supported through a structured professional learning programme. These roles provide training on child protection to the whole school at least every two years, with new staff receiving training as part of their induction programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will set up a time-bound taskforce to address the issue of unregistered childminding; and (ii) what other steps he will take to address the safeguarding risk posed by unregistered childminding.
answer_text: My Department continues to raise awareness of the risks of using unregistered childcare and draw attention to the benefits of using registered childcare provision. Where a person suspects that unregistered childminding is taking place, they should contact their local HSC Trust. The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 gives Trusts the power to issue an enforcement notice upon anyone found to be providing unregistered childminding, and they will be guilty of an offence and subject to a fine if found to be childminding unregistered again in the following 12 months. The developing Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will provide further opportunities to tackle the issue of unregistered childminding, by promoting the value of registered childminding, as well as making childcare more affordable and accessible for parents who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of whether enough action has been undertaken by the justice system in order to address the issue of unregistered childminding; and (ii) to detail the number of unregistered childminders that have been prosecuted in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice has no role in addressing the issue of unregistered childminding. The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 requires childminders to be registered by their local Health and Social Care Trust and any assessment of the adequacy of current arrangements would be a matter for the Department of Health.   In relation to part (ii) of the question, prosecutions for offences associated with unregistered childminding may be brought under Article 132 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  Prosecutions and convictions at courts datasets held by the Department do not show any convictions for an offence under this Article of legislation for the period up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which information is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the representations he has made to the UK government in relation to the Trussell Trust’s Guarantee Our Essentials campaign.
answer_text: I have not made any representations to the UK government in relation to the Trussell Trust’s Guarantee Our Essentials campaign.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reinstate the School Holiday Food Grant.
answer_text: Following a decision by the Executive, the Department of Education made, on an interim basis, School Holiday Food Grant payments from July 2020 to March 2023.  The ringfenced funding for this ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued.In the private member's motion on holiday hunger debated in the Assembly on 19 March 2024, I outlined my view that food poverty is an issue that impacts all age groups on every day of the year and therefore holiday hunger for children should be tackled as part of a wider, more holistic solution to tackling food poverty across society.  Reflecting the close links between tackling food poverty (including holiday hunger), social welfare interventions and the Executive's Anti-Poverty Strategy, I have written to the Minister for Communities seeking to discuss how efforts to tackle food poverty experienced by children could align with the work of the Department for Communities on food poverty and the potential role other departments may have to play in trying to address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 12727/22-27, to detail the dates, since the restoration of the Assembly, on which his officials have undertaken formal meetings with (i) Department of Health officials; (ii) Department of Justice officials; (iii) Department of Education officials; and (iv) Department for Communities officials in relation to regional balance, where this was the initially intended purpose of the engagement.
answer_text: Benchmarking our actions and learning from colleagues in other departments is important to our approach to Regional Balance and is currently being pursued at official-to-official level.Department for CommunitiesMy officials are active participants in the DfC/TEO led Collaboration Test & Learn initiative and attended meetings on the following dates:8th March 202420th March 202422nd March 202416th May 202417th June 2024DfC officials also participate in the DfE Sub-regional Task & Finish Group – recent meeting held on 25th June. A meeting between DfE and DfC officials took place on 14th June 2023, as part of the Place 10x Call for Evidence.Department of EducationMy officials have had side conversations when meeting through the Collaboration Test & Learn group.The department also provided a response to the Place10x Call for Evidence.Department of HealthMy officials have had side conversations when meeting through the Collaboration Test & Learn group.Department of JusticeMy officials have had side conversations when meeting through the Collaboration Test & Learn group.The department also provided evidence to the Place 10x Call for Evidence, and a follow-up meeting with DoJ officials took place on 14th June 2023.Outside the mentioned departments, a meeting with DAERA officials took place on 22nd May 2024, and a meeting with DfI officials took place on 12th June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he is taking, in collaboration with the Minister of Education, to address economic inactivity.
answer_text: Economic inactivity is a significant and long-term challenge. According to the NISRA Labour Market Report, the latest estimate of the economic inactivity rate in the local working age population was 27.0% in the period from February to April.  The most common reason for economic inactivity in the working age population is long-term sickness (39.7% of economically inactive people). Other common reasons for economic inactivity in the working age population include students (25.8%), family and home care (15.0%) and retirement (9.4%). Tackling economic inactivity is a long standing and complex challenge that requires collaboration across the Executive.  While the Department for Communities is in the policy lead there are relevant interfaces across the Executive.The interface between my Department and the Department of Education is essential to address some of our most significant strategic challenges, including reducing economic inactivity.  This is why we have a joint programme of work on the 14-19 agenda that covers many areas of shared focus across the Departments. This includes Careers, which has been prioritised a key joint area of work.  Provision of effective careers guidance will ensure all our people are more aware of, and have access to, the full range of opportunities across our economy.  Another key area of work with DE to help address economic inactivity is childcare provision. Access to high quality affordable childcare provision is an Executive priority.  I am committed to working with the Minister of Education in taking forward this important area of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps he is taking to address recruitment challenges in the Irish-medium early years sector.
answer_text: Recruitment issues are a challenge across all of the early years sector and will be considered within the context of the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in conjunction with colleagues in the Departments of Health and Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the construction phase for the flood alleviation scheme at Drumahoe will commence.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified at this stage and consultants have been instructed to progress the detailed design phase of this project. It is hoped that the detailed design phase could be completed by early 2026. Following this, progress to procurement and ultimately work on site later in 2026 or early in 2027, will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will intervene where foliage overgrowth from private properties impacts on the safe use of pavements and roads.
answer_text: My Department can notify private landowners of their responsibilities under Article 50 of the 1993 Roads Order to remove obstructions caused by foliage overgrowth from private properties outside of the bird nesting season.Obstructions such as these are recorded as part of my Department’s routine inspection regime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commission large-scale research on the quality of work in Northern Ireland, similar to the University College Dublin Working in Ireland Survey 2021.
answer_text: Yes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how he plans to address regional inequalities in health outcomes.
answer_text: The ‘Health Inequalities Annual Report’, published by my Department on 27th March 2024, highlighted the scale of inequalities that continue to exist. For example, the report showed that in 2020-22 males in the most deprived areas live on average 7.2 fewer years than males in the least deprived areas. The gap for females was 4.8 years.Inequalities in health outcomes primarily arise because of inequalities in the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work and age. For example, research has shown that only 20% of health outcomes are driven by the clinical care we receive, with the rest related to the socio-economic and physical environment in which people live and a range of health and lifestyle behaviours.Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled and supported to lead healthy lives.The Framework is underpinned by a range of public health strategies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. It is further complemented by the Integrated Care System for NI which is underpinned by both a population health and outcomes-based approach.Reducing inequalities in health is a huge challenge. It needs continued, sustained collaboration across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.You will be aware from my recent statement in this Chamber that health inequalities are one of my main priority areas as Health Minister and I hope to be able to update Members further on my plans on the other side of the election later this week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he engages with businesses in order to inform the delivery of his economic vision.
answer_text: My officials and I engage consistently with business as a normal part of work on delivering my economic vision. This includes, direct meetings with businesses and their representatives, consistent feedback via my officials and Arms’ Length Bodies, including Invest NI and correspondence. As part of this work, I am also keen that I hear more from businesses outside of Belfast and particularly SMEs. I do this proactively through visits and meetings, and have also encouraged my officials to engage more with businesses on a sub-regional basis Particularly to inform development of the Sub-Regional Action Plan which will focus on Regional Balance.	Specifically on good jobs my officials and I have engaged with the LRA Engagement Forum, the membership of which is drawn from representatives of businesses and trade unions to take their initial views on a Good Jobs consultation that will be launched in early July.  The development of the right skills for our economy is a key component of raising productivity and to support our transition to a Net Zero economy. Sectoral Partnerships (SPs) have been established across 15 economic sectors, with a further three being rolled out.  SPs are a key conduit for enabling employer ownership of the content of Apprenticeships, Traineeships and HLAs. The SPs place local employers at the heart of curriculum development to ensure that apprenticeships and traineeships meet the skills needs of their industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department’s proposal, developed in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the £150 million Investment Zone, including an update on engagements with HM Treasury.
answer_text: Investment Zones are a British government policy led by the Levelling Up department (DLUHC) in London. Following publication of DLUHC’s Enhanced Investment Zone policy prospectus in March, my officials are working collaboratively with DAERA and others to shape a proposition that best meets the objectives of the policy and delivers maximum benefit for the north. That work is being taken forward via a co-design process between the Executive (led by my department) and DLUHC.  Similar processes in Scotland, England and Wales have been progressing over the past year with some closer to delivery than others. We are working at pace to deliver an IZ as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her proposed sentencing legislation will introduce stronger sentences for perpetrators of attacks against transport workers.
answer_text: No-one should face mistreatment whilst doing their job and serving the public. Such attacks are wholly unacceptable and must not be tolerated.  A number of offences against the person already exist, with maximum sentences ranging from 6 months at the lower end of the scale up to a potential life sentence for the offence of causing grievous bodily harm with intent.These offences apply to all victims, regardless of their occupation. However, I have been appalled by reports of aggression being displayed against people who serve the public in a range of occupations, including transport workers.To recognise this increased risk the Department’s proposed Sentencing Bill will include a new offence of assaulting a person who is engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty or providing services to the public.  The new offence will have an increased maximum sentence of 12 months’ imprisonment in the magistrates’ courts and a two year maximum if tried on indictment.  In more serious cases the fact that the victim was such a worker will become a statutory aggravating factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he will take to expand the number of Physician Associate places available in higher education institutions; (ii) how he will ensure these places are regionally balanced; and (iii) how his Department is working with the Department for the Economy to ensure that student places funded by his Department are regionally balanced.
answer_text: The Department is working with HSC Trusts to expand the Physician Associate role across the HSC. However, progress in creating new positions will largely be determined by the availability of the necessary funding.The Regional Physician Associate Forum, which coordinates the creation of posts, brings together employers from across all of Northern Ireland. There is only one Physician Associate programme available in Northern Ireland, located at Ulster University’s Magee campus in Londonderry. The programme is commissioned by this Department and therefore sits outside the Higher Education arrangements  operated by the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will progress with previously proposed universities public mission agreement legislation, including ensuring that institutional governance arrangements of higher education institutions contain adequate representation of students and staff.
answer_text: With a short mandate and a packed legislative agenda my focus will be on working with our higher education institutions to progress my four economic priorities and on responding to the financial challenges facing both institutions and students.My Department is also working with the three universities to conclude Outcome Agreements for academic year 2024/25. These will show how each institution – consistent with its autonomous status, mission and values – will continue to play its part in achieving our shared ambitions for the North.All of our Higher Education Institutions currently have both staff and student representatives on their Governing Bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the amount of any increase in student places for entry at Ulster University Magee campus in 2024-25;  (ii) any increase above the Maximum Student Number cap; and (iii) a breakdown of whether these places represent an expansion of existing courses or the establishment of new courses.
answer_text: Work remains ongoing regarding the number and composition of increased student places at the Ulster University Magee campus in 2024-25.  My officials are continuing to engage with this university on this and detailed proposals are expected to be finalised in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether negotiations on a new catering and cleaning contract will include discussions on pay enhancements for staff.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will reinstate funding to support the school transport provision for Fountain Primary School, Foyle.

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what steps he will take to encourage microgeneration of energy; and (ii) whether he will place a legal requirement on major electricity providers and suppliers to pay a statutory minimum price for electricity received from (a) private homes; (b) farms; (c) businesses; and (d) community schemes.
answer_text: My department has already been supporting microgeneration of energy by working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG). On 29 April 2024 the EECG opened for applications for a wide range of businesses across the North.		Businesses across a range of sectors from retail to advanced manufacturing to hospitality will be eligible for the grant and enable them to invest in technologies, including microgeneration, to reduce energy consumption and carbon emissions. It will also provide businesses the opportunity to access funding that will help them transition to more environmentally sustainable practices. 		The Windsor framework also enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar panels.		Additionally, Power NI already provides a micro-generation export tariff that is annually approved by the Utility Regulator and therefore offers current micro-generators a route to market. Other suppliers are also free to do the same and compete should they wish.	Further options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total number of students at each Ulster University campus as of 1 June 2024; and (ii) the expected number of students at each Ulster University campus as of 30 September 2024.
answer_text: Enrolments at Ulster University’s campuses for the 2021/22 academic year, the most recent year for which published data is available, are set out in the table below. 	Campus	Belfast	Coleraine	B’ham	J’stown	London	Magee	Total	Enrolments	13,315	3,995	2,200	3,250	2,770	4,135	34,550		The figure for the Derry~Londonderry (Magee) campus excludes 4,890 students undertaking the Objective Structured Clinical Exam (OSCE) of the Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) Test of Competence (ToC). The ToC is a part-time module for overseas nurses and midwives seeking registration as a nurse and/or midwife in Britain and the North of Ireland.	Figures for 2022/23 will be published later this year.		I am committed to working with the higher education sector to grow student numbers sustainably. Officials are in ongoing discussions with Ulster University about plans to expand its Derry~Londonderry campus to 10,000 students and these discussions will have an impact on the likely number of students for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the dates, since the restoration of the Assembly, on which he has held formal meetings with (i) the Minister for the Economy; and (ii) other Ministers in relation to regional balance, where this was the initially intended purpose of the engagement.
answer_text: While I have not met with the Minister for the Economy or any of my Executive Colleagues where regional balance was the sole purpose of the meeting my approach to delivery within my department is to tackle regional imbalance, therefore meetings with Executive colleagues are approached on that basis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered the Minister of Education’s request to reinstate the funding for the school transport previously provided to Fountain Primary School under the Urban Villages initiative.
answer_text: Considering the role Fountain Primary School plays in the wider community and the continuing importance of supporting a vibrant community ethos in the Fountain area, we have agreed to provide funding to support transport of pupils to Fountain Primary School for the academic year September 2024 to June 2025.  As at 31 March 2024, Urban Villages have contributed £92,600 of funding to Fountain School Transport Scheme.  In its design, Urban Villages was never intended to be a core funder, the aim was to provide additionality to existing provision within areas.  Therefore, as school transport is fundamentally a matter for Department for Education (and the Education Authority), we have written to the Minister of Education and recommended that his department considers any future support beyond this timeline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the input his Department has had into the development of the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan, including the dates of any formal meetings undertaken by any of his departmental officials in relation to the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department has not had any input into the development of the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan, nor have any of my officials had any formal meetings in relation to this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 12727/22-27.
answer_text: My response to AQW 12727/22-27 refers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he is taking to support dental practices to continue to provide NHS dentistry; and (ii) to detail the number of dental practices that (a) provide NHS dentistry; and (b) do not provide NHS dentistry, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.As at 31st March 2024, the latest publicly available annual Accredited Official Statistics, there are 364 General Dental Practices in Northern Ireland who have made arrangements with the Department to provide health service dentistry. A breakdown of these practices by Local Government District is detailed in the table below.Information is not held in relation to practices which are wholly private.General Dental Practices by Local Government DistrictLocal Government DistrictPractices1Antrim and Newtownabbey20Ards and North Down29Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon33Belfast84Causeway Coast and Glens25Derry City and Strabane26Fermanagh and Omagh25Lisburn and Castlereagh25Mid and East Antrim32Mid Ulster24Newry, Mourne and Down41Northern Ireland3641Practices may include a number of Orthodontic and Specialist Practices. Orthodontics is a specialist area of dentistry concerned with the growth and development of the teeth and jaws and the prevention and treatment of abnormalities of this development. Therefore, most patients are children. These practices do not typically have registered patient lists, with instead patients normally seen on referral from a GDS dental practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how he intends to address regional health inequalities, including to close the life expectancy gap between the most and least deprived areas of Northern Ireland; and (ii) any specific steps he intends to take in relation to the North West.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.Reducing inequalities in health is a significant challenge. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally. The COVID-19 pandemic has no doubt exacerbated existing inequalities and the PHA continues to deliver a range of actions to address the impact of this, and other health conditions and behaviours, with the most deprived communities to help reduce these health inequalities. Partnership working and collaboration between organisations is essential in this work.Across the North West area, the PHA works with a range of partners on health and wellbeing interventions/programmes, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, other NICS Departments, HSC Trusts, Primary Care, Local Government, Voluntary and Community Sector, local businesses and many other partners to promote activities that will encourage and enable healthier lifestyles that contribute to better health outcomes. Areas of particular focus include substance use; suicide prevention and mental health; supporting local community organisations; later years; accident prevention; sexual health and teenage pregnancy; obesity prevention; smoking cessation; and fuel poverty.Addressing health inequalities is one of my key priorities, and consideration is currently being given as to how we can further develop collaboration in this area, including a potential place-based approach by DoH, PHA and partners to help address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the funding challenges faced by the Supporting People programme; and (ii) whether he will consider allocating any further funding to this programme.
answer_text: I am aware that Supporting People providers have faced cost pressures in recent years, particularly in relation to staffing costs. I have decided to provide an additional £4.8m for the Supporting People Programme in 2024/25. This will provide total funding of approximately £80.7m for this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the subject of all papers his Department has submitted to the Early Learning and Childcare Task and Finish Group since the group was established; and (ii) the dates on which these papers were submitted.
answer_text: My officials continue to attend regular meetings of the Cross-departmental Task and Finish Group. Officials contributed to the development of the package of measures announced by the Education Minister on 23 May 2024, drawing on the Department’s knowledge and experience of the childcare sector. To date no formal papers have been requested by the Group as that has not been the nature of the engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 8835/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the main successes of the Enterprise Zone designation at Coleraine; and (ii) whether any lessons have been learned for the proposal for an Enhanced Investment Zone designation.
answer_text: The Enterprise Zone at Coleraine has attracted £20m in investment since 2018 and brought a global data storage company to the North of Ireland, which occupies a 45,000sq ft facility at the Atlantic Link Campus, the first of its kind in the north. In relation to the Enhanced Investment Zone, my officials are leading, on behalf of the Executive, a co-design process with the Levelling Up department in London to develop a proposal.  They are engaging widely with local stakeholders and interested parties in that regard.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) why junior Ministers have not provided an oral briefing to the Committee for the Executive Office since the restoration of the Assembly; and (ii) to detail the areas of responsibility of each junior Minister.
answer_text: The First Minister and deputy First Minister briefed the Committee for the Executive Office on 28 February as part of a broader programme of strategic briefing sessions upon the resumption of the Assembly.  Ministers, accompanied by Junior Ministers Reilly and Cameron, have committed to brief the Committee again in the new Assembly term, with 2 October provisionally scheduled in the Committee’s Forward Work Programme.       The Determination made under section 19 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 specifies that the function of the Junior Ministers is to assist the First Minister and deputy First Minister in the exercise of their functions in relation to the Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the public consultation on the draft optimum remediation strategy for the site at Mobuoy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to support Employment and Support Allowance claimants with the upcoming migration to Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department has a range of help and support in place to meet the individual needs of people moving to Universal Credit (UC), including:a dedicated telephony team;face to face support in local Jobs and Benefits offices;signposting to Independent Welfare Advice.In addition, my Department will provide enhanced support to legacy benefit claimants moving to Universal Credit, including those currently in receipt of Employment and Support Allowance. This will include an outbound telephone call during the migration period for those who have not yet made a claim by week 12 and consideration of a potential home visit.Further information is available on the nidirect website at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-you-receive-migration-notice-letter

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he will take to address the increased use of non-standard temporary accommodation.
