question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to list the recommendations from the Gillen Review that require legislative change and will not be introduced in the current mandate.
answer_text: I have been in discussion with the Justice Committee about my Department’s legislative programme with a view to ensuring that priority legislation, including measures recommended in the Gillen Review, can be progressed within the current truncated mandate, taking account of the implications of the budget settlement which the Department has received and is continuing to work through.My plans are not yet finalised, but I wish to offer you my assurance that delivering legislation to support victim focused reform within this mandate remains a key priority for me.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the ministerial decisions will be taken to establish the bodies provided for under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (The Act) for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: 	•	The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;	•	The Irish Language Commissioner; and	•	The Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.This will be in-line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10193/22-27 and AQW 28510/17-22, (i) whether the McCloud remedy has been fully implemented; and (ii) whether it is still her Department’s intention to introduce secondary legislation in relation to the McCloud Retrospective Remedy.
answer_text: The implementation of the McCloud retrospective remedy is ongoing. The main unfunded pension schemes are in the process of calculating pension benefits for in-scope members under both the pre-reformed and reformed schemes in order to provide these members with a statement of their remedy benefit entitlements and options. Under the remedy legislation, members of the unfunded public service schemes who are in scope of the remedy and have claimed benefits will now be given an ‘Immediate Choice’ of either pre-reformed or reformed scheme benefits for their remedy period service (1 April 2015 to 31 March 2022). Members must be provided with their ‘Immediate Choice’ by 31 March 2025 and they will have 12 months from receipt to make their choice. From 1 October 2023 active in-scope members are entitled to receive a choice of pre-reform or reform scheme benefits (“Deferred Choice Underpin”) at the point of their retirement. In the intervening years, those that remain in service will receive annual statements about their remedy entitlements, the first of which must be provided by 31 March 2025.The secondary legislation for the McCloud retrospective remedy for the Northern Ireland Civil Service Pension Schemes was made and laid on 21 September 2023, becoming effective from 1 October 2023, and can be accessed at: https://www.legislation.gov.uk/nisr/2023/141/contents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department provides any guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to the use of escalated bed spaces in hospitals; and (ii) whether Health and Social Care Trusts are expected to carry out a health and safety assessment on a patient-by-patient basis prior to the use of escalated bed spaces.
answer_text: My Department does not issue specific guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to the use of escalated bed spaces in hospitals, however, there is an overarching responsibility regarding patient safety which Trusts still adhere to when using escalated spaces.All additional care spaces being used have been risk assessed by Trust staff with the aim to use these areas for the shortest period of time as possible. Senior staff within the Trusts monitor the use and de-escalation of any additional beds and care spaces on a daily basis. As patient safety remains my Department and Trusts’ priority, careful consideration is made in identification of patients who are deemed appropriate for allocation to an additional bed/care space, utilising an agreed set of exclusion criteria.  A patient who is placed in an additional care space receives both medical and nursing care dictated by their clinical need to the same safety standard of all patients placed within a designated ward bed space. Trusts will increase the number of staff on duty for these respective areas so that these patients have access to appropriate care, although mitigations against deficits in the privacy and dignity of patients are much more challenging and, regrettably, at times insufficient to address privacy and dignity concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department has taken, since the publication of the National Union of Students UK and the Union of Students in Ireland report The Cost of Survival in March 2023, to address the financial pressures faced by students.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the impact that the cost of living is having on students. Former Minister Murphy recently met with the President of the National Union of Students – Union of Students in Ireland to discuss its report on the “Cost of Survival” and the issues faced by students. An additional £1m of hardship funding was made available in March of this year and academic year 2023/24 saw a 40% increase to maintenance loans. Officials have been asked to consider further options for improving the support available in future years, within the financial constraints faced by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 10017/22-27, to detail the dates, since taking up office, on which he has held any formal meetings with the (i) Minister of Health; (ii) Minister of Justice; (iii) Minister of Education; and (iv) Minister for Communities in relation to regional balance, where this was an initially intended purpose of the engagement.
answer_text: I have had no formal meetings with other Ministers since taking up office two weeks ago.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress made on the list of general repairs to properties in the Triangle estate, detailing (i) the number and nature of the issued repairs that are still outstanding; (ii) when each was first logged; and (iii) when they are due for completion.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive can advise that there are currently 53 jobs issued to the contractor to complete; Balcony walkway repairs, Parapet wall repairs, underside concrete repairs, roof repairs, gutter repairs, step repair & rain penetration repairs.Dates range from February 2022 to May 2024:12no. jobs issued 2022.13no. jobs issued 2023.28no. jobs issued 2024.The Sub-contractor is currently on site and the estimated completion date is Mid to End of August 2024.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the establishment of a Just Transition Fund.
answer_text: My vision for an economically and environmentally sustainable agri-food sector in Northern Ireland recognises the importance of supporting a just transition to a more sustainable farming sector by ensuring that we have in place policies and strategies that respond to the realities of climate change; benefit our environment; and support our economically and socially significant agri-food sector.		I am committed, as part of that vision, to setting up a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture and will be considering in conjunction with Executive colleagues the best approach to take to this and to the other requirements placed upon us by the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, learning where appropriate from the experience of others.		I will also be working closely with the Finance Minister to ensure that the future funding allocated for agriculture here fully meets Northern Ireland’s needs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will update his Department’s system for recording road defects so that it automatically records the number of temporary or recurring pothole defects.
answer_text: My Department undertakes routine road safety inspections and site investigations of customer enquiries to identify and record road defects, such as potholes along with the details required for carrying out a repair.  Contractors occasionally carry out an initial repair in temporary material in advance of planning a separate visit for a permanent repair, for example on a busy junction.  This is noted when the works have been completed and can be recorded on the maintenance system.  The maintenance system can also identify defect clusters where multiple defects have occurred, and this information can be used when developing future resurfacing programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9901/22-27, whether there is a standardised assessment scoring form used by his Department to inform decisions on annual maintenance programmes.
answer_text: In the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of the indicators outlined in my response to AQW 9901/22-27, as well as on the professional engineering judgements of our local engineers.The exact combination of indicators and metrics used within each Division will reflect individual needs and circumstances in the Division at that time.Programmes are subject to continual review, taking into account a wide range of factors, for example, worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in-year funding, contractor capacity etc. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9901/22-27, whether his Department uses any information in relation to the recurrence rate of defects on roads to inform decisions on annual maintenance programmes.
answer_text: My Department’s programmes are prepared on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage, general surface condition surveys, as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims. Contractors occasionally carry out an initial repair in temporary material in advance of planning a separate visit for a follow up permanent repair, for example on a busy junction, and this will not be included in the factors influencing the programme. However, where clusters of defects are identified, this information can be used when developing future resurfacing programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has recorded any actionable defects for repair on the pavement at Sutton Gardens, Derry, during the last 12 months.
answer_text: Two defects (damaged kerb and bitmac edge deterioration) and one third party referral (broken utility valve cover) were recorded as actionable defects during the last 12 months within Sutton Gardens, Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he will instruct his officials to consider the value and nature of specific financial incentives for students to study at the Ulster University's Magee campus as part of any review of student finance; (ii) whether his officials have updated him on a review of higher education funding; and (iii) for his assessment in relation to the merit of a review.
answer_text: I will be carefully considering the action plan of the Magee Taskforce that is due later this year. The Terms of Reference are clear that the Taskforce should consider any potential financial incentives needed to attract more students to the campus.My officials continue to brief me on the full range of issues around higher education funding and I will give proper consideration to the merits of a review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the public consultation for the proposed redress scheme legislation recommended in the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: We are currently considering a number of complex and sensitive issues which will inform the final shape of the proposed policy consultation, and in parallel to this, officials are finalising the necessary plans to launch the consultation before the summer recess.Following an analysis of these responses, the requisite draft Bill to establish both a Statutory Public Inquiry and financial redress schemes for those impacted by their experience in Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries, and Workhouses can be finalised and brought to the Executive for its consideration and, subject to the Speaker’s agreement, be introduced in the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of times his Department has attended to road surface defects on the Craigavon Bridge and John Street roundabout, Derry, in the last six months; and (ii) whether this roundabout will be scheduled for full resurfacing.
answer_text: My Department has carried out surface defect repairs 18 times on the roundabout at the junction of Craigavon Bridge and John Street (Carlisle Square roundabout) during the past 6 months. While Carlisle Square Roundabout would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those roads deemed most in need for intervention. The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once budgets have been finalised. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects on the roundabout in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the Royal Irish Academy's report entitled, Finding common ground, building community: strengthening and stimulating tertiary regional collaboration in the north-west of the island of Ireland; and (ii) whether he will ask the Ulster University Magee Taskforce to consider the establishment of, and produce a recommendation on, the establishment of an independent oversight body of tertiary education provision.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent Royal Irish Academy report on ‘Strengthening and stimulating tertiary regional collaboration in the North-West of the island of Ireland’ and have noted its conclusion that the future development of the greater North-West requires effective cross-border planning of tertiary education with enhanced concurrent and coordinated funding from both jurisdictions. Expansion of the Ulster University Magee campus is a priority for me, underpinning the economic development of the North-West and a more regionally balanced economy.  My focus will be on delivering the commitment to 10,000 students at Magee, in line with the commitment set out in the New Decade New Approach agreement. Given that the Ulster University Magee Taskforce is independent, the issues to be considered and the subsequent recommendations for inclusion within the Action Plan will be a matter for the Taskforce to determine.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the timeline for the implementation of service improvements in relation to Eating Disorder Services, as set out under Action 29 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031; and (ii) the support groups that are available to the families of someone who has been diagnosed with an eating disorder.
answer_text: the timeline for the implementation of service improvements in relation to Eating Disorder Services, as set out under Action 29 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.A business case is in development in relation to Action 29d and a number of workshops have been held with key stakeholders involved in Eating Disorder Services to shape direction and identify the case for change, based on the current service model, emerging need and setting future vision for the service.  The initial focus of the business case is an enhanced staffing model. A precise timeline has not been determined, as it will depend upon the availability of funding.the support groups that are available to the families of someone who has been diagnosed with an eating disorder.Children and Young People.Belfast TrustFamilies, specifically parents/ carers, are an integral part of the therapeutic treatment offered to young people, as per the NICE guidelines which highlight family-based treatment for anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa. The specialist CAMHS Eating Disorder Youth Service and Regional CAMHS Inpatient Service both offer a monthly support group, open to all parents/ carers, which offers support, psycho-education on eating disorders and ways families can specifically support their young person. This group also offers informal opportunities for parents to talk together about their shared experiences. To note Eating Disorder services are provided by Belfast Trust for children and adults living in the South Eastern Trust area.Northern TrustWith regards to Action 29 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 the Northern Trust CAMHS Eating Disorder Service provide both parent support groups and were appropriate Occupational Therapist groups for those young people who are diagnosed with an Eating Disorder. Southern TrustThe Southern Trust currently do not offer any formal support groups to the families of someone who has been diagnosed with an Eating Disorder.Treatment plans are based on need. All families assessed by the Southern Trust Eating Disorder Service are offered individualised Family Therapy.  Family Therapy can help those in close relationship to better understand and support each other when managing an Eating Disorder within the home.  The Family Therapist helps families to understand each other’s experience and views, appreciate each other’s needs, build on family strengths and work together to make useful changes in their relationships and their lives. The service offers Multi-Family Therapy. Multiple Family Therapy (MFT-AN) is a treatment designed for young people who are experiencing anorexia nervosa (AN) or other restrictive eating disorders, that involves several families participating in treatment together. The treatment aims to help improve outcomes for young people with an eating disorder and their families.  It brings together families who are struggling with similar difficulties with the aim of increasing knowledge and support, building solidarity to reduce

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the need for GP practices to provide reasonable adjustments to ensure equal access for disabled patients; and (ii) whether a practice operating a phone-only policy can act as a barrier to patient access.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. In response to increasing patient demand, most GP Practices  are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. With the level of pressure on GP services continuing to rise, this approach helps GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of patients who need their services.   I recognise the importance of ensuring that our HSC facilities and services are accessible to everyone who needs to use them. My Department is the contract holder for all GP practices in Northern Ireland and expects practices to address need within the requirements of equality, disability and human rights legislation.  Examples of how this expectation is operationalised might include availing of sign language interpreting services, installing premises improvements such as ramps and automatic doors and working flexibly to take account of patients’ circumstances, such as their level of family support and capabilities with respect to technology.Practices have a responsibility to ensure that patients have clear information on how to access their services, making reasonable adjustments for specific groups when necessary.  Each practice can determine how best to do this within their local context and for their patient population.  This includes ensuring they have appropriate systems in place for communication with their patients.  Each practice will decide which communication system suits their practice and patients.  They must, however, offer patients a range of options to contact the practice.GP practice staff are ordinarily familiar with the particular circumstances of their patients and can respond accordingly to reflect their needs. Whilst telephone consultations are one way of appropriately responding to patient needs, where a face to face appointment is required, for example where a patient has more complex needs, this will be arranged.My Department is aware of the need to support GPs in their continuing efforts to meet the growing demands for Primary Care services and has made significant investment to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.A GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, has been established to explore issues relating to access to services and to oversee work to improve the experience for both patients and practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to update the Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders from 2016, given it was based on National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines from 2004.
answer_text: My Department recognises The Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders (2016) requires updating especially as it references out of date guidelines and some of the terminology has changed.  Updating the pathway would not be cost neutral and would require additional resource. Clinical practice adheres to the most up to date guidelines as these reflect best practice. Action 29d of the Mental Health Strategy commits to enhance the regional eating disorder service. A business case is in development in relation to Action 29 d. and revising the pathway will be considered within this submission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 10864/22-27, for his assessment on whether his Department can legislate for other Departments, subject to the matter receiving the attention and agreement of the Executive.
answer_text: My department could only bring forward legislation that covers other departments in circumstances where the Executive had agreed a policy to place obligations on or to otherwise guide the activities of all departments, and in addition had determined that my department should lead on the introduction of that legislation to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how many formal engagements he has had with representatives of parents in relation to early education and childcare since taking office; and (ii) the dates on which those formal engagements took place.
answer_text: Since becoming the Minister for Education, I have met a wide range of early learning and childcare stakeholders, (including parents and the organisations which represent them) in a variety of contexts.   This has included group engagement, on-line consultation, meetings and visits to early years settings.  In a number of cases, a variety of stakeholders will have been present at the same time and therefore my engagements are not categorised in the manner requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Carers NI report, Delivering carer’s leave in Northern Ireland - Policy options and cost modelling.
answer_text: Policy officials have been engaging with Carers NI to inform policy options with respect to carer’s leave in advance of the launch of a consultation.The Delivering carer’s leave in Northern Ireland - Policy options and cost modelling paper was published by Carer’s NI on 29 April 2024. I have commissioned departmental analysts to consider the report’s findings to inform the policy options referenced above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has any plans to introduce legislation in line with the Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer provided to AQW 9411/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 10591/22-27, for his assessment of (i) whether Ministers can legislate for other departments, subject to Executive approval; and (ii) any other criteria that should be met before Ministers can legislate for other departments.
answer_text: The Ministerial Code requires any Minister to bring matters to the attention of the Executive Committee if it cuts across two or more responsibilities. Therefore, a Minister cannot unilaterally legislate for other departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the circumstances when GP practices are entitled to charge patients a fee for access to medical records; and (ii) for his assessment of the charging of patients for those records in any circumstance.
answer_text: Information on how to access medical records is published on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/accessing-medical-or-health-and-social-care-records This guidance sets out how, under Data Protection rights, an individual may use their right of access (commonly referred to as subject access) to , obtain a copy of their personal data, as well as other supplementary information. The Information Commissioners Office has published guidance for organisations responding to a Subject Access Request (including the issue of charging a fee for providing the information requested) at https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/individual-rights/individual-rights/right-of-access/#fee   This clearly states that an organisation may only charge a ’reasonable fee’ for the administrative costs of complying with a request if it is manifestly unfounded or excessive, or if an individual requests further copies of their data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has commissioned any research on the impact that unaffordable childcare has on (i) productivity; and (ii) economic inactivity in the economy.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly outlining my Economic Vision I mentioned how one way we can increase the number of people working in Good Jobs is by investing in affordable childcare and fair pay for childcare workers.  My Department is currently considering, alongside other Departments, what further research is needed in this area building on existing work on labour market impacts of parenthood - this includes research currently being undertaken by Queen’s University Belfast entitled "Closing the Gender Employment Gap in Northern Ireland".  This research recognises that childcare is a major influence on labour market outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the £25 million allocated to support a Childcare Strategy as announced in the Budget 2024-25, including the proportion of funding that will contribute towards immediate interventions for parents and providers.
answer_text: The £25m earmarked for an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy in the current financial year is 50% of what I had bid for.  I am currently reconsidering the options available within this allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the digitalisation of hospital and GP records; and (ii) the accessibility of those digital records to patients.
answer_text: Information has been held in digital medical records for many years. Clinicians require this information to provide all patients and service users with safe and appropriate care. Currently the encompass programme is deploying a regional Electronic Patient Record for use by Acute and Community Care services. The deployment phase is expected to conclude in Spring 2025. This will replace many, older individual systems currently in use. Primary Care providers have their own computer solution, tailored to their practice needs. There are currently 3 different commercial solutions in use.  The EPIC software suite, currently being deployed by the encompass programme to provide the Electronic Patient Record includes a patient portal, known in Northern Ireland as MyCare. This may be accessed via a mobile phone app, or a dedicated website. MyCare provides patients with personalised and secure online access to specific parts of their medical records including medications, appointments, and some test results. Information on General Practice interactions will not be available via MyCare. The MyCare portal is an evolving system and content will change and develop based on patient experience and improving technology.GP practices have the ability to offer patients access to an online app which patients can use to book appointments or request repeat medication. The services available to the patient are controlled by the individual practice.  Although other services are available via the app these have not been commissioned and are therefore not available in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how his Department is learning from the model of safe leave provision introduced in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) whether his Department will provide training, advice and support for employers on workplace policy support and pathways to support for employees.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with their counterparts in the Department of Children, Equality, Disability, Integration and Youth who have responsibility for Domestic Violence leave in the south of Ireland.As advised in my response to AQW 9792 22-27, there is a statutory requirement for my Department to issue guidance about the implementation or application of a provision of regulations made under the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (NI) 2022.  The content of guidance will be determined following the consultation exercise.   The consultation will also seek views on what other supports may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether his Department will reach 80 per cent of electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030.
answer_text: One of my four critical objectives for the Economy is to reduce carbon emissions; generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change.I want to build on our previous success in renewables development, so we are taking a collaborative, whole system approach to facilitate delivery of the 80% by 2030 target. A working group has been established by my Department with key delivery partners, the Department for Infrastructure, the Utility Regulator and the Strategic Investment Board. Collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of the renewable electricity target.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 9477/22-27, whether they will guarantee funding will be restored to Women’s Aid Northern Ireland which is commensurate to the funding lost by the organisation from the Core Grant in 2023-24, regardless of whether the organisation is successful for Core Grant funding in 2024-25.
answer_text: We are operating in a very difficult budgetary situation, and we are aware that the impact is being felt across government departments, within our communities and across the community and voluntary sector.   Core Grant funding remains the responsibility for the Department of Health.  We will continue to engage with the Minister of Health on this issue in the context of the recently agreed budget position. Work is ongoing to develop a package of measures to support the implementation of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy; the intention is not to displace existing funding arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether all Health and Social Care Trusts use both HPV primary screening and cytology in cervical screening processes; and (ii) how many severe adverse incidents in relation to cervical smear tests there have been in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the impact of the delivery of Levelling Up funding on the community and voluntary sector; and (ii) what steps he is taking in relation to the delivery of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to community groups currently in receipt of it beyond the March 2025 deadline.
answer_text: The delivery of Levelling Up Funding falls under the responsibility of the UK Government who are also responsible for the monitoring and evaluation of the impact of this funding.My Department and Executive colleagues led by Department of Finance engage regularly with the Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) and HM Treasury regarding all funding streams under the Levelling Up agenda. We will continue to press the UK Government on plans for post-2025 funding and seek to maximise opportunities and secure the best possible outcome for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 9606/22-27, (i) why he is unable to list any departmental policies and strategies which include addressing regional economic imbalances as an objective, other than the proposed sub-regional economic plan; and (ii) to detail the process by which his Department’s policies and strategies are being aligned to reflect his four key priorities, including the deadline for the completion of this alignment exercise.
answer_text: Work is underway in aligning existing policies and strategies with my four key priorities, including regional balance. The work supporting the development of the  Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan work stream informs this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of whether the current funding arrangements for graduate entry medical students presents challenges to workforce planning in the health service; (ii) whether he will ensure that graduate entry medical students can access student loans in the same way as students in Britain; and (iii) what engagement he plans to have with the Minister for the Economy in relation to this issue.
answer_text: (i)  Medicine has historically been a very heavily oversubscribed programme in Northern Ireland.  The Department’s Review of Medical Student Places in Northern Ireland, published in January 2019, recognised the contribution that Graduate Entry medical education could make to the supply of future medics here. Subject to GMC approval, students will begin to graduate under Ulster University’s Graduate Entry medical programme from 2025 and over the following few years it will be possible to begin to assess what contribution it is making to meeting NI’s future medical workforce needs.  (ii) Health and student finance are devolved matters. The levels of fees paid by students and the associated support arrangements vary across the UK, reflecting the resources available to each administration and their assessment of workforce supply priorities.  (iii) I am willing to consider any suggestions from the Minister for the Economy that will further improve the supply of medics in Northern Ireland.  However, they must be affordable for this Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to commit recurrent funding to running a FAST campaign for stroke awareness.
answer_text: My Department took the decision to pause most health-related advertising spend in the 2023/24 financial year as a direct result of the significant pressures on the budget. No decision has been taken as yet for 2024/25. However, the recently announced planned budget allocation for the Department means that we will now need to consider where savings can be made across all business areas. In this context I am currently unable to commit to funding the FAST campaign in 2024/25. In the interim, the Public Health Agency will seek to promote FAST messaging to the public through non-paid for channels including organic social media platforms and media activity. I remain clear that all patients with acute stroke symptoms should continue to call 999.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 9367/22-27, for his assessment on whether Ministers, upon consultation with other Executive Ministers, can legislate for other departments.
answer_text: Ministers cannot unilaterally legislate for other departments following consultation alone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 9716/22-27, to detail the job title of the official who acts as the strategic lead for regional balance in his Department; and (ii) how his Department measures the degree to which all officials at all grades are taking regional balance into account in all aspects of their work.
answer_text: The lead official in the Department is the Permanent Secretary. Measures to promote regional balance will be agreed with local areas.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps they are taking to ensure regional balance in the draft Investment Strategy.
answer_text: The draft ISNI, Infrastructure 2050, sets out a strategy for investment in infrastructure for the next 30 years to stabilise, deliver and transform.  The draft strategy recognises the need for regional balance across the investment priorities.It aims to ensure that projects help create thriving communities and places, a clean, secure and sustainable environment and a competitive and connected economy, and seeks to build resilience in public services. We will consider the current draft and consultation responses and aim to publish proposals in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to his meeting on 25 March 2024 with representatives of the Christian Institute to discuss Relationships and Sexuality Education, to detail the representatives he will meet from the (i) women’s sector; (ii) LGBT+ sector; and (iii) youth sector in relation to this issue.
answer_text: I have met with a number of representative organisations to discuss Relationships and Sexuality Education.  I recently met with the Mental Health Champion and the Commissioner for Victims Designate.  I am due to meet the Children’s and Young Peoples Commissioner on 30 April 2024.In addition, when I am not available to meet, officials will meet on my behalf and provide feedback to me where appropriate.  Officials have had meetings with a range of groups including, again, the Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Children’s Law Centre, NSPCC and jointly with the Rainbow Project, HERe NI, Cara Friend and the Women’s Policy Group.  Officials also met twice with the previous Youth Assembly and a number of Youth Groups from the Northwest and have invited RelateNI and the Secondary Schools Union Northern Ireland to meet.Engagement with a range of stakeholders will continue including direct engagement with young people and practitioners.  This will also include ongoing engagement with the Gillen Education and Awareness Group which has representation from a wide range of stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he will take to ensure that women can have confidence in the accuracy of cervical screening tests; and (ii) whether he will commence a public inquiry into cervical cancer screening in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Over 99% of cervical cancers are caused by human papilloma virus (HPV). Primary HPV testing, with cervical cytology as a second line test for those women who test positive for HPV, was fully implemented into the NI Cervical Screening programme from 11 December 2023. Primary HPV testing is an automated test which identifies the presence of HPV and was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than cervical cytology alone. To increase awareness of this change to the cervical screening programme, the Public Health Agency (PHA) has updated all public facing information resources (leaflets, and websites) and has delivered training sessions to primary care staff so that they are equipped to inform women and answer any questions in relation to the programme.  There is ongoing media engagement and posts on social media from PHA to promote the cervical screening programme.   Throughout April and May 2024, PHA supported the Community Pharmacy Living Well campaign. Training was delivered to community pharmacists, with written materials being available to the public, to promote the cervical screening programme. PHA continue to commission the Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA) to promote informed choice in cancer screening via a peer led community based programme. The programme targets groups where rates of participation in screening is likely to be lower, including those impacted by health inequalities and living in deprived areas. With regards to a public inquiry, I will await the outcome of the Cervical Cytology Review looking at cases in the SHSCT before considering its findings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps they will take to implement an integrated marketing campaign to ensure that victims and survivors, living both inside and outside Northern Ireland, are aware of entitlements to redress, support and services.
answer_text: Since March 2023, our department has been running a Public Information Campaign with the objective of raising awareness among victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse about their entitlements to advice, support services and redress.The campaign has included press advertisements, poster distributions to relevant organisations, social media engagement, a leaflet drop to every household here and a digital media advertising programme.In support of this, the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA) has and continues to promote entitlements to victims and survivors now living in other jurisdictions.As we have now entered the final year for applications to the HIA Redress Board, it is vital that victims and survivors are made aware of the final date for receipt of applications, 2 April 2025, as well as the availability of advice and support services.A working group has been established to draw expertise from different organisations, including COSICA, TEO and the Victims and Survivors Service, in planning further activity. We look forward to shortly receiving proposals from this group.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will commission a prevalence study of non-recent child sexual abuse, as suggested by the Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.
answer_text: We met the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse on 9th April 2024. Among other topics, we discussed her proposal for research into the prevalence of non-recent child sexual abuse.  This will be taken into account as part of our consideration of the overall research needs of the Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered appointing an Interim Advocate for victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, the Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: The Truth Recovery Design Panel Report did not recommend a dedicated Advocate role. Instead, a series of advocacy interventions have included dedicated and specialist support services, the monthly Victim and Survivor Consultation Forum (with an Independent Chair) and appointment of Independent Panel Victim and Survivor Representatives.While there is no immediate plan to appoint a single Advocate role, there is currently a series of workshops underway with Consultation Forum members which include views on advocacy.  On completion of this there will be opportunity to consider how the current advocacy model may be enhanced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to explain the reduction in his Department’s payments in relation to the Education Maintenance Allowance since 2013.
answer_text: The reduction in payments in relation to the Education Maintenance Allowance since 2013 is due to the  number of students in receipt of EMA having fallen from 22,015 to 10,915.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she will introduce legislation to overturn the convictions of sub-postmasters wrongly convicted in the Horizon scandal.
answer_text: On 22 April 2024, the UK Government announced its decision to include Northern Ireland within the territorial extent of the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Bill, by tabling amendments for consideration at the Bill’s Committee Stage on Monday 29 April 2024.  I am pleased that the UK Government has taken this decision, as I have always been of the view that Northern Ireland should be included within the scope of any Westminster legislation.  This represents the quickest solution and allows sub-postmasters here to be treated on the same basis as their counterparts elsewhere.Provided the Bill's provisions extend to Northern Ireland, there will be no need to introduce legislation in the Assembly to overturn these convictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment his Department has made of the impact of the standardisation of pre-school provision on the sustainability of childcare settings.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) is delivered through a partnership model of statutory and non-statutory provision, with approximately half of funded PSEP providers being registered childcare settings.  My Department has commissioned a Scoping Study to enable a costed implementation plan to be developed for the phased roll out of a universal, standardised, funded pre-school session length for every child whose parents want it.  The Scoping Study included independent consideration of the full costs of PSEP provision in order to determine a reasonable and sustainable funding rate for registered childcare providers participating in the programme.    I will be looking to Executive colleagues to prioritise the funding required to deliver this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to RTÉ Investigates: Ireland’s Abortion Services, broadcast on 15 April, what action he intends to take to ensure pregnancy care centres in Northern Ireland do not provide misinformation on reproductive healthcare.
answer_text: My officials engage with two independent Northern Ireland based organisations and the British Pregnancy Advisory Service which provide impartial information on reproductive healthcare. During December 2022 and February/March 2024 the Department of Health carried out public and professional awareness campaigns to inform on the provision of and access to abortion services for NI women and girls. Northern Ireland women and girls are signposted to the NI Direct website Abortion services | nidirect which provides detail of how to access the central access point and signposts women to pregnancy choices and post abortion counselling services.The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is the independent body with responsibility for the registration and inspection of establishments and agencies as defined within the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003.  Private pregnancy care centres do not require to be registered and inspected by RQIA. These centres would only be required to be registered with the RQIA if private doctors offered medical services or treatments from within the establishment. Prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required on this framework, including consultation on the draft policy. This work has had to be paused due to funding constraints and other priority projects which need to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of a ban on smacking children.
answer_text: I am fully supportive of taking steps to remove the defence of reasonable chastisement in Northern Ireland and am of the view that the current law creates uncertainty and can, in effect, provide shelter to abusive parents and fail to provide equal protection to children.   Whilst I recognise that some people have concerns that the removal of this defence may lead to prosecutions and the potential criminalisation of parents, I do not believe this to be the case.  I believe that the focus going forward has to be a positive one of improving parenting whilst ending the shelter of abusive parents under the current system.  I am keen to progress this matter but was unable to secure Executive approval for introduction of the necessary provisions as part of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Bill during the last mandate. Any change to the law would require Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of PSNI officers who have received training in dealing with the (i) Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021; (ii) Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; (iii) Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (iv) Criminal Justice (Committal Reform) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, broken down by Assembly constituency.
answer_text: I am unable to provide the number of PSNI officers who have received training by Assembly constituency in respect of the above listed Acts of the Northern Ireland Assembly.PSNI does not collate training statistics by Assembly constituency, but the following details of training for PSNI personnel across the 11 policing districts are available.Domestic Abuse & Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021In advance of the Domestic Abuse & Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021 commencing, a four module-training plan was developed which reflects the following:(1) Understanding Coercive controlling behaviour(2) Legislative module (3) Pathways to support (4) Impact of domestic abuse To date the modules have been undertaken by almost 5600 officers / staff members focusing on the understanding coercive controlling behaviour and approximately 2500 officers / staff undertaking the remaining modules in addition to module 1.  In advance of the legislation going live a new concept of Domestic Abuse Champion was used to facilitate awareness in teams and sections across local policing, custody, public protection and contact management. This involved approximately 250 officers / staff undertaking a training and awareness session with Domestic Abuse Policy lead and the Policy Detective Sergeant.  This highlighted the key areas of the legislation and an opportunity to review all case studies and support materials with front line officers / staff members.  As part of the legislative requirement for annual training (Year 2), in autumn 2023 a district training programme was rolled out to those officers across local policing command (across all 11 Districts, covering all consistencies) which featured a specific focus on the follow topics:•	What has worked well through the introduction of the offence – the learning from decisions made by PPS and evidence accepted by the courts•	What could be improved – this will focus on recognition of children in domestic abuse situations and application of child aggravators •	The consideration and use of Achieving Best Evidence Interviews  1462 officers undertook this programme of training across the 11 Districts. A similar programme of work was completed within Public Protection Branch as continuous professional development to ensure that the offence is being captured at all opportunities. In order to support the wider understanding of domestic abuse, there are also a number of internal training programmes that look to additional barriers for reporting and managing safeguarding.  This includes: •	Marginalised communities training – due to roll out in coming weeks •	Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure training – which is underway and will be completed by the end of May 2024•	Thematic days for Domestic abuse detectives within Public Protection Branch As we are now in Year 3 of this legislation, training has already commenced and will aim to focus on the following: •	Understanding the lived experience of victims of domestic abuse •	Understandin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions he intends to take to close the life expectancy gap between the most and least deprived areas of Northern Ireland; and (ii) the departmental policies, strategies or plans that include measures to alleviate this disparity.
answer_text: Health inequalities, including the gap in life expectancy between the most and least deprived areas of Northern Ireland, remain a particular challenge, which my Department actively seeks to address through all its activity.In particular, Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address these inequalities. The framework was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. It is important to note that many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing.MLB is also underpinned by a number of strategies designed to help improve population health and wellbeing and reduce inequalities through empowering people to live healthier lives. These include:The 2011 Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy which has been extended to September this year;The Tobacco Control Strategy for NI which has been extended and continues to provide strategic direction in this area of work;Protect Life 2 the long-term strategy for reducing suicides and the incidence of self-harm;The recently extended, Breastfeeding - A Great Start: A Strategy for Northern Ireland, which aims to improve the health and wellbeing of mothers and babies through breastfeeding;Healthy Futures - A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland, which was recently issued for consultation; andPreventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, the substance use strategy.The work undertaken under MLB also aligns with the development of the Integrated Care System for NI which will be underpinned by both population health and Outcomes-Based Accountability approaches to planning, focusing on how we can improve the health and wellbeing of the population and reduce health inequalities. This will be reflected through cross-cutting strategic considerations such as prevention and early intervention, wider social determinants of health and reducing health inequalities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he intends to take in relation to (i) the closure of Waterside Theatre; (ii) the recent funding loss experienced by organisations in Derry City and Strabane District Council from the Arts Council; and (iii) the regional imbalance in funding provided by the Arts Council.
answer_text: Funding to arts organisations is delivered on my Department’s behalf by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland and decisions in relation to the terms of its programmes, assessment processes and the allocation of funding are a matter for the Board of the Arts Council.I understand that the Arts Council remains open to discussing the possibility of support for the Waterside Theatre, but decisions on its viability and future are ultimately a matter for the management board of the organisation.The 2023-24 budget required very difficult decisions to be taken across the public service.  The Budget for 2024-25 has not yet been agreed by the Executive, however the financial outlook for 2024-25 is likely to be extremely challenging.  I will continue to seek to support all sectors within the Department’s remit, including Arts, to the greatest extent possible.	(iii)The Arts Council operates a range of revenue or capital support programmes which are offered on a region-wide basis. The issue of geographical reach and balance across Northern Ireland, in terms of access and funding, was raised in the report of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce and will be an important aspect of the work being undertaken by my department to develop a new Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to ensure that adults can access ADHD diagnoses and support services promptly.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, currently there are no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision of services between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals having to wait longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability.  Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many meetings have been held by his departmental officials in relation to the review of higher education funding since it was announced in October 2022.
answer_text: I understand that officials have engaged in approximately 20 formal meetings on establishing a review since the announcement by the previous Minister.  This does not reflect the full extent of engagement as the review will also have been discussed in meetings with a much broader agenda and often between colleagues informally.  It is not possible to quantify the number of broader agenda item meetings or informal discussions.I have asked officials to bring me proposals on the scope and terms of a possible review of higher education, and the future direction will be determined once I have considered those.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what timeline has been established for securing contributions from religious institutions to contribute to redress payments for victims of historic institutional abuse.
answer_text: The Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry Report recommended that any voluntary institution found by the Inquiry to have been guilty of systemic failings should be asked to make an appropriate financial contribution to the overall cost of the HIA Redress Board and any specialist support services. The Independent Facilitator, Mr Paul Sweeney, has drafted a framework for reaching an agreement between the Executive and the six voluntary institutions.  Two institutions, Barnardo’s and the Good Shepherd Sisters, have indicated their intention to make an interim contribution. We are continuing to engage with both institutions on receiving this payment in the coming weeks and will continue to engage on a final payment following the closure of the HIA Redress Board.  Final payments will not be able to be confirmed until all applications made to the Board, by the closing date in April 2025, have been processed.Constructive discussions on appropriate contributions from the other four institutions are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the preparation and introduction of secondary legislation on pensions and the McCloud retrospective remedy.
answer_text: The Public Service Pensions and Judicial Offices Act 2022 required devolved public service pension schemes to have secondary legislation for the McCloud retrospective remedy in place by 1 October 2023. Each of the relevant schemes, including the NICS pension scheme where my Department has direct responsibility, met this requirement. In most cases this means schemes can now provide affected members with a choice to have their pension entitlements during the period of age discrimination between 2015 and 2022, calculated under either the reformed or pre-reform scheme rules. There are some differences in how the remedy is applied in the Local Government Pension Scheme (Northern Ireland) due to the way the 2015 pension reforms were originally introduced for that scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the jobs announcement made by EY Northern Ireland and Invest NI on 13 September 2023, including the establishment of a hub in the North West.
answer_text: EY is progressing with its plans to establish a hub in the North West and  has carried out extensive due diligence on potential locations.  A final decision will be announced at a later date along with the confirmed location of a new hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department holds a central map detailing both DfI Roads and NI Water owned manholes; and (ii) what steps he will take towards developing a publicly available reporting map or tool for these manholes.
answer_text: The report-a-fault software system available on the NI Direct website allows customers to report a wide range of road related faults, including issues with manholes and gully covers. This facility can be found on the following NI Direct website link: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/report-problem-manhole-or-gully-cover.  This is also used by the NI Direct call-centre staff to submit a fault on a customer’s behalf.  While the system does not display manhole features, customers can place a marker on the map at the fault location, select the appropriate fault category and provide additional supplementary information. The system automatically assigns this to the local DfI maintenance office for investigation and forwarding to the relevant authority if necessary.  My Department does not hold a full inventory of all surface-level apparatus in a spatial format to display on a map. Developing such a system would take additional funding and considerable resources to maintain. Given the many competing priorities for my Department, there are no plans to take this forward at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his Department’s work to review the legislation underpinning the regulation and inspection of Health and Social Care services; and (ii) whether this will consider improvements in the regulation and monitoring of baby scan clinics.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment. Prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.However, I wish to advise that if ultrasounds are carried out in baby scan clinics by radiographers, the radiographers are regulated by the Health and Care Professions Council.  Also, independent hospitals and clinics may carry out private baby scans and these establishments are regulated by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority under the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 and the Independent Healthcare Regulations of 2005.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the business case submitted in relation to a new Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust have submitted a proposal for a new Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital.The Trust will be invited to submit a business case for this proposal once budget affordability is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the need to remunerate pharmacists in general practice services in line with Agenda for Change banding.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration for GP practice pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.DoH will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of why regional balance has not yet been achieved, given that it was included in the 2011 Programme for Government.
answer_text: My focus is on looking forward and driving sustained and inclusive economic growth across the North. The Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan will contain targets and actions to deliver a more balanced economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will increase the amount available through the Pathway Fund.
answer_text: The budgetary outcome for 2024-25 is still to be agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive. Based on the planning position which the Department of Finance has provided, the Department is facing a funding gap of c£900m.  The interim 2024-25 Pathway Fund budget has been maintained at 2023-24 levels i.e. £3.789m.Once the Executive agrees the 2024-25 budget, I will consider all additional requirements / pressures, including the Pathway Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 9367/22-27, (i) to detail his engagement with the (a) Minister of Health; (b) Minister of Justice; (c) Minister of Education; and (d) Minister for Communities in relation to regional balance; and (ii) to list any meetings he has held to discuss this issue with the Ministers; and (iii) to detail any representations he has made to them in written correspondence.
answer_text: Regional Balance is a core priority of my Economic Vision and, as such, is a frequent topic of all my engagements regardless of the initially intended purpose of the engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his Department's assessment of the standard of recent pothole repairs at (a) Camborne Park; and (b) Adelaide Avenue, Derry; and (ii) whether they will be revisited to carry out more substantial repairs.
answer_text: The potholes on Camborne Park and Adelaide Avenue were made safe using a temporary repair as the plant that supplies material for a permanent repair was closed for the Easter holidays.This work will now be followed up with permanent repairs as soon as resources allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 9367/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 9367/22-27 was answered on 12 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will amend the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 so that baby scan clinics are included in the list of regulated establishments and agencies as defined in the Order.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment. Prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.However, I wish to advise that if ultrasounds are carried out in baby scan clinics by radiographers, the radiographers are regulated by the Health and Care Professions Council.  Also, independent hospitals and clinics may carry out private baby scans and these establishments are regulated by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority under the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 and the Independent Healthcare Regulations of 2005.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the scoring criteria that his Department uses to determine when a road meets the threshold to be included in an upcoming resurfacing scheme.
answer_text: On trunk roads, information is routinely gathered and analysed from a variety of specialist machine surveys and visual inspections, and this information is used to help inform decisions regarding our annual maintenance programmes. On other roads Divisional programmes are prepared on the basis of a number of data factors including the road usage, general surface condition surveys, as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims. Programmes are subject to continual review, taking into account a wide range of factors, for example, worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, contractor capacity etc. In the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of the indicators outlined above as well as on the professional engineering judgements of our local engineers. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients have received Venetoclax treatment to treat cancer and how many received a uMRD test after twelve months in order to inform patients of the likely period of remission in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of average waiting lists for (i) a first appointment with an orthopaedic consultant; and (ii) orthopaedic surgery, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
