question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of where the enhanced Investment Zone should be located.
answer_text: Investment Zones are a British government policy, managed by Treasury and the Levelling Up Department in London. My preference is to use Investment Zone funding to support green technologies and the skills needed for a green economy, which I believe would have a wide geographical scope. Ultimately however any final decisions around the design and delivery of an Investment Zone will be for the Executive to make, in consultation with the British government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any additional student numbers generated through the lifting of the Maximum Aggregate Student Number cap will be exclusively directed towards the Magee campus of Ulster University.
answer_text: The expansion of the Magee campus is a priority project in creating a regionally balanced economy. 	‘New Decade, New Appraoch’ recognised that the MaSN cap may have to be increased to help realise the target of 10,000 places at Magee.	I will be setting out my approach to the expansion of Magee in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will allocate funding to Women’s Aid Northern Ireland which is commensurate to the funding lost by the organisation from the Core Grant.
answer_text: I understand that as a result of budget difficulties and the need to implement saving measures the Department of Health advised Women’s Aid that it was not possible to provide core grant funding for the full 12-month period in 2023/24.  My Department fully recognises the importance of the meaningful work carried out by Women’s Aid Federation Northern Ireland.  However, in light of budget constraints affecting the Department of Justice and in order to be able to deliver my Department’s statutory functions, I am not in a position to allocate any funding to cover the shortfall in the funding previously provided by the Department of Health.At the time of writing the 2024-25 budget has yet to be agreed and set by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what representations he has made to the UK government to express opposition to the step introduced to repeal its legal commitments to the all-island economy.
answer_text: The British Government’s commitment is to the removal of a reference to a report prepared in 2018. Repealing this legislation does not remove the commitments to improving North South cooperation which remain in international agreements, and indeed in the other parts of this legislation which is not being repealed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what representations he has made to the Republic of Ireland government to include the Northwest city region in Project Ireland 2040; and (ii) what steps he intends to take in relation to its inclusion in the future.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to initiate discussions with the Irish Government on the opportunities arising from Project Ireland 2040.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce greater transparency to the family courts system, following the recent expansion of the Transparency Implementation Group Reporting Pilot in England.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of opening up the courts and promoting a better understanding of our legal system, and that is why I recently welcomed the pilot scheme to film the delivery of judgments in the Court of Appeal. In relation to family courts however, the benefits of greater openness must be carefully balanced with the need to protect the privacy of the families and children involved. My Department has been following developments in relation to the Reporting Pilot in family courts in England and Wales with interest and will continue to do so.  In this jurisdiction, the issue of increased media access to and reporting of family cases has been consulted on in recent times by both the Gillen Review of Family Justice and the shadow Family Justice Board and proved very contentious. There are strongly held, differing views on this issue, which require further, detailed consideration before progressing any legislative change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take to decentralise his Department by locating a proportion of staff to areas outside Belfast.
answer_text: Given the nature of its work, my Department’s workforce is widely dispersed across the North.Of a workforce of over 2900 staff, over 75% are located outside the Belfast City Council boundary.  I, therefore, currently have no plans to further decentralise my Department’s workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will replace the funding lost by Women’s Aid NI from Core Grant funding.
answer_text: Once the budget position for 2024/25 is known, I will be in a position to consider the availability of funding for the Core Grant Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action his Department is taking in relation to gender inequalities in cardiovascular diagnosis and treatment; and (ii) whether he will ensure that improving public awareness of cardiovascular disease will be included as a goal within the Women’s Health Action Plan with time-bound targets and funding attached.
answer_text: I recognise that there are particular challenges in terms of improving awareness of cardiovascular risk factors among women and girls, and addressing any differences in terms of diagnosis and treatment. One of the guiding principles of the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network is to work to ensure equity of access to high quality cardiac care for all of the population. My Department will continue to work closely with the Cardiac Network to consider any improvements that could be applied to current diagnostic and treatment pathways.I welcomed the inclusion of women’s cardiovascular health in the Assembly motion debated on 13 February 2024, in which I outlined my Department’s plans to develop a short-to-medium-term women’s health action plan.  The action plan, whilst at an early stage, will aim to improve health outcomes for all women and girls in Northern Ireland. I also acknowledged the need for improved public awareness and information on the wide range women’s health issues, and this will be considered as work on the action plan progresses. As with all other areas of public health, any awareness campaign is subject to available funding, however, while budgets have not yet been confirmed I am keen to maximise what can be achieved within available resources in relation to key public health messages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps his Department is taking to ensure that new patients can register for and access NHS dental care; and (ii) what support his Department will provide to patients who do not live near a local dental practice which is accepting new NHS patients.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.An additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.In addition, a pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will introduce legislation to extend the statutory limits of flexible working to (i) part-time; and (ii) fixed-term contract workers.
answer_text: The right to make a statutory flexible working request is available to employees who meet the minimum 26-week continuous employment qualifying criteria. Part time and fixed term contract workers who hold the employment status of ‘employee’ are entitled to make a statutory flexible working request if they also satisfy the continuous employment criterion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will ensure that local sub-regional economic targets are standardised across Northern Ireland so that the levels of economic performance can be compared across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Sub-regional economic targets will be set in partnership with local areas, not imposed upon them. A wealth of data will allow for the relative economic performance in sub-regions to be compared.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the effectiveness of the UK Government’s Levelling Up agenda in Northern Ireland, including the impact of the Levelling Up & Regeneration Act 2023.
answer_text: The Levelling Up Agenda, to date, has been delivered directly here from Whitehall. It has largely bypassed Executive Departments, is not aligned to our priorities, and does not recognise our unique equality legislation. The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF), which is included within the Levelling Up Agenda, and described by the British Government as full replacement for past EU Structural Funds has fallen short of providing the funding we would have received had we remained in the EU.  The Levelling Up and Regeneration Act is an expansive piece of legislation which is largely focused on planning, housing and the powers of local authorities in Britain. Only Part 1 relating to the setting of Levelling Up Missions and their measurement is of relevance to my Department. Officials from my Department continue to engage with Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will introduce legislation for a right to disconnect.
answer_text: Good Jobs are a top priority for me and my department. As such, my officials are undertaking detailed work on a range of areas with the aim of bringing forward a comprehensive employment bill, alongside other policy interventions, later in this mandate. The right to disconnect is an issue I will be happy to consider as part of this exercise.  Only after I have considered all of the options will I be in the position to decide what should be legislated for, and what is achievable in the time available.  It should be noted that the Working Time Regulations 2016 already lay down minimum conditions relating to weekly working time, rest entitlements and annual leave.  They also make special provision for working hours and health assessment in relation to night workers. I would encourage all employers to ensure that they follow best practice guidance in this area in terms of ensuring staff have time to disconnect from work during the day. A healthy balance of connection and breaks from work benefits all in the modern working environment. It is important to note that there is no ‘one size fits all’ approach to this argument: flexibility is key and in the modern workplace a range of different working patterns exist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to improve stroke care to ensure that people have access to the appropriate support.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has worked with GP practices to establish best practice on online appointment scheduling systems; and (ii) what steps his Department has taken to ensure that all GP practices are supported to adopt this best practice.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for the day to day running of their practice, including how consultations for individual patients are delivered and how appointments are requested/booked.  I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  My Department has established a GP Access Working Group, which includes  GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients. Work is ongoing to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency.  The Group is also exploring how telephony and related technologies can be best deployed to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services  for patients and practices alike.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review his Department’s street lighting renewal policy to ensure that street lighting to rear footpaths is not removed when a new lighting scheme is designed or installed.
answer_text: When street lighting is deemed to have reached the end of its life and needs to be replaced, the area is assessed and new lighting is provided only in those areas that meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting.In residential areas, street lighting is provided on adopted pathways which provide access to the front entrances of properties, or where they are identified as through routes with no reasonable alternative lit route.  However, where a pathway is not adopted, or is identified as only providing access to the rear of properties, any existing lighting is removed and new lighting is not provided.This policy, which has been in place since 1993, ensures that my Department’s finite resources are directed at replacing lighting in those areas where maximum benefit to the public can be derived, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned, whilst also seeking to minimise environmental impact and reduce future maintenance and energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 has resulted in changes to policies introduced by departments in a way that has led to an increased demonstrable impact to the social and economic needs of people in rural areas.
answer_text: The sixth Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Report was published in January 2024 and the report shows that the Rural Needs Impact Assessment process is now well embedded within public authorities and that the needs of people in rural areas are being considered by decision makers in the development and implementation of policies and in the design and delivery of public services.The Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 has led to the development of the Rural Needs Toolkit for Health and Social Care which helps inform health and social care service providers and decision makers of the needs of people in rural areas in relation to health and social care provision.I recognise that there is still scope for more to be done to help ensure that the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 delivers better outcomes for rural dwellers and my Department will be considering how this can be achieved going forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the Executive’s discussions on affordable childcare, whether he will announce a package of immediate support measures to bring childcare costs down for parents and support providers.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to the Assembly on 12th February and Executive colleagues on 15th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  However, I am conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place and I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet parents, providers, and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand the issues which they are facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the funding model for community pharmacies, including the suitability of the drug tariff model used for community pharmacies.
answer_text: I can assure the member that I am committed to working with Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI) to deliver fair, sustainable and affordable funding for community pharmacy. Following a meeting I held with representatives of CPNI on 21st February 2024, I have committed to an inflationary uplift of £6.1m for pharmacy services in 2023/24. Furthermore, an additional £4m was provided in recognition of service pressures for the 2023/24 year. Following the commitments in relation to £10.1m, arrangements were made with The Business Services Organisation to invest 10/12th (£8.4m) within the February payment. Arrangements have been put in place to make the remainder available with the March and April payments. Further work will be taken forward to review community pharmacy financial arrangements in the coming months with CPNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to improve collaboration between Invest NI and the International Development Association, in order to promote the all-island economy, especially in the North West.
answer_text: As a result of the Windsor Framework, we alone can export goods to the British and EU markets, without the frictions and paperwork that others now endure. The Windsor Framework also protects the all-Ireland economy, which has tremendous unrealised potential.We must take full advantage of this all-Ireland economy, and Invest NI has a key role to play in achieving this. Invest NI has well-established relationships on an all-Ireland basis with IDA Ireland as well as organisations such as InterTrade Ireland, Enterprise Ireland, Skillnet Ireland and many others. 	I have tasked Invest NI with building on this, including identifying potential opportunities for further and more systematic collaboration with IDA Ireland and other organisations.	As we take action to maximise the potential presented by the all-Ireland economy a clear area of focus will be regional development. Invest NI will build upon existing Shared Island projects and proposals, for example, an International Regulatory Cluster in the North-West and the successful model of collaboration already in place with Enterprise Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of flood risks from the Creggan Reservoirs, Derry; and (ii) what steps his Department will take in relation to those risks.
answer_text: My Department encourages all reservoir managers to manage their reservoirs in a responsible manner. My officials have regularly communicated with officers from Derry City and Strabane Council to discuss how, they as reservoir owners and managers, should take forward safety measures at their Creggan reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the implementation of the recommendations from the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services; and (ii) whether he will establish a separate arm's-length body for children’s social care services.
answer_text: (i) The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services was published in June 2023 and a public consultation on 49 of 53 recommendations ran for 12 weeks between September and December 2023. Work to analyse consultation responses is underway and it is hoped that this will be completed by mid-late March. (ii) Any decision regarding a separate arm's-length body for children’s social care services ALB will be subject to the outcome of consultation and would be a decision for the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of (i) the recommendations from the Gillen Review that have not been implemented; and (ii) the reasons why these recommendations have not been implemented.
answer_text: I remain committed to the full implementation of all accepted recommendations under the Gillen Review. To date, 128 recommendations (just over 50%) have been fully completed and a further 48 (19%) are partially completed. Where a recommendation has been partially completed that means that some provision is in place and is delivering improved outcomes for victims (often as a pilot), however further work or funding is required to establish longer-term, mainstreamed service provision.  A further 45 recommendations (18%) are in progress. This means that work is in train, and in many cases well advanced, but the associated service or measure is not yet operational. There are several reasons why these recommendations have not yet been fully or partially implemented.  In some cases, this is because of complex operational or policy issues that remain to be resolved.  In others, delays have been incurred because of the uncertainty around future budgets and further progress will be subject to additional funding becoming available. Additionally, the absence of the Executive has meant that those recommendations that require cross-departmental input and agreement could not be implemented. Examples of recommendations currently in progress include:multi-agency preparatory work to establish a pilot to test pre-recorded cross-examination in serious sexual offence cases involving very young complainants. This project has complex interdependencies with other child-focused workstreams. Significant progress has been made, however launch of the pilot will be subject to resolving remaining issues and to securing funding;multi-agency research on attrition in both serious sexual offences and domestic abuse. This research will help to inform future policy interventions to reduce attrition and will also help us to assess the impact of interventions under the Gillen Review Programme; cross-departmental work to consider and make recommendations in relation to the establishment of a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland; work in partnership with PSNI and The Executive Office to deliver strategic communications aimed at challenging attitudes and behaviours  that can underpin gender-based violence. It is anticipated that a PSNI-led campaign will launch in the coming months.Some workstreams, such as those relating to Relationship and Sexuality Education, do not fall within the functions of my Department and are for the Department or Education to take forward.  Further, 25 recommendations (10%) were deferred because of the need for legislative change to support implementation.  These include those recommendations relating to laws around consent, additional powers for the judiciary, and changes to legal aid fee structures for serious sexual offences. While my legislative programme is currently under review, the loss of two years from the mandate means that it may not be possible to deliver all these recommendations within the remainder of this mandate.I will be meeting with my offic

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps she will take to implement the recommendations from the Law Centre NI report Redressing Users’ Disadvantage: Proposals for Tribunal Reform.
answer_text: Since 2010 when the Law Centre report “Redressing Users’ Disadvantage: Proposals for Tribunal Reform” was published, a number of reforms have been taken forward by the Department of Justice, including the transfer of nine devolved tribunals from other departments in 2011, which now fall within the responsibility of the Department. The Department consulted on wider tribunal reform in January 2013, publishing its summary of responses in July 2013. Further policy development was undertaken and a consultation was planned to explore issues raised by consultees, however, the suspension of the Assembly in 2017 prevented the publication of this paper. The 2017 and 2022 Assembly suspensions also affected the transfer of the Appeals Service from the Department for Communities, which is an important element of the reform plans. The Appeals Service transfer work programme will need to be progressed by the Executive in advance of any revised tribunal reform consultation. More recently, the need to prioritise Departmental staff time on necessary reform of judicial pensions has limited the Department’s ability to take this matter any further.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will extend eligibility of the Northern Ireland Concessionary Fares Scheme to include (i) free travel for disabled people who currently qualify for a Half Fare SmartPass; and (ii) a companion pass for disabled people who qualify for a pass and are unable to travel alone.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme closed on 24th August 2023 and included an Option to extend half fare travel to free travel for eligible people with disabilities. The consultation also included an option to introduce companion travel, for eligible disabled people who are unable to travel alone, due to their disability. While I fully recognise the potential benefits of both these Options for disabled people, any decision to extend the Scheme must be affordable within the budget made available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will establish an independent oversight body for higher and further education, with the aim of expanding student numbers at Ulster University Magee campus to at least 10,000 students.
answer_text: One of my strategic objectives is to create a regionally balanced economy. The expansion of the Magee campus will be a priority project to advance this. I will be setting out how to achieve this in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will establish a time-bound taskforce to deal with the issue of unregistered childminding.
answer_text: My Department will continue to raise awareness of the risks of using unregistered childminding and draw attention to the tax and other benefits of using registered childminding. The developing Early Education and Childcare Strategy will also assist in tackling this issue, by promoting the value of registered childminding, as well as making childcare more affordable and accessible for parents who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the plans and progress of the Mobuoy Remediation Project.
answer_text: My department is continuing to progress work in relation to monitoring the site, pursuing a criminal justice outcome against the former operators, and planning for remediation.Safeguarding public health, protecting the water quality of the river Faughan and working in partnership with NI Water to safeguard drinking water in the north-west are of utmost importance.I intend to issue a draft Optimum Remediation Strategy, developed by my department’s appointed Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT), for public consultation within the next 2 months. This strategy, to deliver long term remediation of the site, is based on the best balance of environmental, social, and economic factors.The public consultation will give all interested parties the opportunity to comment on the draft Optimum Remediation Strategy. There is no agreed preferred option, and there will not be, until consultation has been completed. Responses will be carefully considered and presented for the next stages of scrutiny and approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on progress of the recommendations from the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland report on Child Sexual Exploitation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland report on Child Sexual Exploitation in Northern Ireland contains two strategic and seven operational recommendations. All recommendations are either achieved or substantially achieved, with good progress being made on a small number of remaining actions.Implementation is monitored by the cross-Departmental Child Protection Senior Officials Group to ensure a strategic joined up approach across justice, education and health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he is taking to update the Empty Homes Strategy.
answer_text: A Review of the Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was completed and published on the Department’s website in 2021.  As a result of this work the Department is better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant. The Department consulted on a new Housing Supply Strategy in 2021/22. It is intended that this Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and adaptive reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently reviewing this Strategy and considering options for progressing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made in the review of speech and language therapy recruitment.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will consider introducing legislation to provide unpaid carers a legal right to five days unpaid carer’s leave, similar to that in Great Britain.
answer_text: Following a co-design and consultation process, it is my intention to bring forward an employment rights Bill within the current Assembly mandate to ensure the employment law framework remains fit for purpose and operates effectively for both businesses and workers. Carer’s Leave is an issue that I will be happy to consider in that context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he will take to address the growth of non-standard temporary accommodation placements by the Housing Executive, particularly in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: Since the onset of the pandemic there has been an unprecedented increase in demand for temporary accommodation, which is not unique to Northern Ireland and has been experienced across England, Scotland and Wales.  The number of people in Temporary Accommodation is unsustainably high, and the use of non-standard accommodation (hotels and Bed and Breakfasts) in particular represents a very poor housing option for households.The number of Temporary Accommodation placements provided annually has increased from 4,527 placements in 2019/20 to 10,253 in 2022/23. In the first six months of 2023/24 the Housing Executive provided a total of 5,575. It is important to note that an individual or household could receive more than one placement in any given year, so the number of placements does not amount to the total number of people staying in temporary accommodation at a given time.   Despite this unprecedented demand, the Housing Executive has been clear that it will only use non-standard temporary accommodation in the absence of other options such as voluntary sector hostels, Housing Executive hostels and Single Lets (properties in the Private Rented sector acquired on behalf of applicants by the Housing Executive).  Non-standard accommodation currently accounts for approximately 10% of total placements in temporary accommodation across Northern Ireland.  The Housing Executive has told me that it is committed to actions that seek to reduce use of non-standard accommodation and has recently increased its focus on reducing non-standard placements in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area, due to a recent concentration of use of hotels in the city centre in particular.  The Housing Executive has established a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group which is chaired by the Chief Executive and is considering options for alternative and more financially sustainable options for temporary accommodation. In addressing longer-term goals on temporary accommodation the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation Year 2 Action Plan can be found at Homeless To Home (nihe.gov.uk) and contains 25 actions that support the Housing Executive’s delivery of the following objectives:Minimised need for temporary accommodation;Customers receive appropriate accommodation and support at the point of need; Customers moving on from temporary accommodation sustain their tenancies; andA sustainable supply of good quality, affordable temporary accommodation which is safe, warm and well managed.The Housing Executive is currently developing the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation Year 3 Action Plan which will guide their strategic objectives in relation to temporary accommodation for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will work with the Minister for the Economy to implement a gender budgeting pilot.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that it fulfils its Section 75 duties in relation to the Budget, including duties in relation to gender.The OECD Best Practices for Gender Budgeting states that “Gender budgeting can be used to ensure that the budget, as far as possible, reflects the priorities of the government in relation to gender equality. This is not done through creating a separate budget for women’s policies: rather, it is done through encouraging departments to table budget proposals in line with the government’s priorities, and bringing information to the fore on the gender impact of budget proposals to support better-informed and more impactful budget decisions.”The gender impact of budget proposals should be captured as part of equality impact assessment information provided by departments as part of the budget process.That is not to say that improvements cannot be made. An important step towards that will be the development of the Executive’s Social Inclusion Strategies, including the Gender Equality Strategy.  The work on this is being led by the Department for Communities and I understand this may include consideration of gender budgeting and gender budgeting pilots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will lift the maximum student number cap for Ulster University's Magee campus, Derry.
answer_text: One of my strategic objectives is to create a regionally balanced economy.  The expansion of the Magee campus will be a priority project to advance this.  I will be setting out how to achieve this in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a funding revenue has been secured for the completion of the Mother and Baby Unit at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: A business case for the proposed Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital site is currently being prepared; however delivery of this project remains subject to capital and revenue affordability in the context of an overall challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will instruct his officials to (i) assess the impact of the loss of European Social Funding in March 2023; and (ii) quantify the loss of funding for each organisation, with a view to fully replacing this funding for those organisations affected.
answer_text: The British Government pledged to replace EU Structural Funds with the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) being the primary replacement for ESF. The funding provided by the British Government to date has not represented full replacement. The £42m per annum SPF allocation (£127m three-year total) compares poorly with the £65m average per annum (£195m across three years) Executive Departments would have expected from ESF and ERDF, before taking inflation into consideration.The Executive is united in calling on the British Government to fully replace EU funding and to give the Executive control over its distribution so that the excellent work of groups previously funded by ESF can be continued.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will legislate for an early education and childcare act to provide a statutory basis for the financing, supervision and coordination of the delivery of early education and childcare services.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority and while I have stated that our ambition should not be limited, it is important that we take time to carefully consider our options and to secure the necessary funding.  This will include reviewing any legislative requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he will take to ensure that the Derry and Strabane City Deal projects are delivered in full, including any representations he will make to the British government for additional funding required.
answer_text: The Derry City & Strabane City Deal will see investment of approximately £250m in the North-West and play a key role in creating new and better jobs for the region. I want to see progress in delivery of the Deal accelerated and I have instructed my officials to play a much greater role in supporting project promoters to develop proposals that are commenced as soon as possible. I recently met with Ulster University, a key partner in Deal delivery and look forward to moving its projects into delivery stage in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure that the relevant funding is allocated to Men’s Action Network to prevent its closure in Foyle.
answer_text: The 2023/24 Health Budget has resulted in all sectors within health and social care experiencing severe financial pressures and I appreciate this has been particularly challenging for those such as MAN operating in the VCS sector. I will always carefully consider requests for additional funding across health and social care; however, you will be aware that there are many compelling but competing demands for extra funding in the context of the significant budgetary constraints we are facing. Overall, my department is forecasting a deficit position in the current financial year, and it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging for 2024/25.I’m however very supportive of the work underway to change the nature of the funding relationship between government and the VCS and I’ve directed my officials to commit to further discussion regarding the nature of the wider funding relationship between the sector and the HSC.While I’m faced with many challenges particularly in terms of the funding position I remain committed to improving all our services across health and social care.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the North West Strategic Growth Partnership.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to decentralise his Department by locating a proportion of staff, including senior officials, to the North West.
answer_text: Currently there are staff from DfE Careers Service and Trading Standards Service located in offices throughout the North including Orchard House in Derry. There are NICS Connect 2 hubs in the North West which provide workspaces with access to IT facilities for civil servants. These Hubs promote regional economic balance and make jobs in the civil service more accessible to those living further away from Belfast. In addition the majority of DfE staff work from their homes for a proportion of each week, benefitting local economies and lowering carbon emissions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps his Department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: My Department is committed to making decisions in the interests of every child and young person across Northern Ireland. Every policy, strategy and plan is subject to equality screening and rural needs impact assessments. My Department and its Arm’s Length Bodies are also engaged at a local level through community planning partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps her Department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: ANSWERI am fully committed to driving regional balance across the North for those areas within my responsibility as Finance Minister, to ensure all areas and people share in the benefits of prosperity. My department, in developing the 2022 NICS Hybrid Working Policy, took account of regional needs by ensuring that technology, and accommodation, in the form of Connect2 hubs, were located at six regional locations.  As a major employer, my department has also taken steps to ensure that hybrid working is available, where possible, for all employment opportunities offered by the Civil Service.In addition, our independent statistical agency, NISRA, provides data and analysis to provide robust regional evidence to inform policy making and monitoring right across a suite of Executive policies and actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: My Department seeks to achieve regional balance across its remit by ensuring that policies, strategies and plans are evidence-based and compliant with relevant statutory obligations, including section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016. The Department’s Economic and Social Research Programme and core analytical work informs consideration of regional issues and aims to ensure that policy development takes account of high quality research and evidence.The Departmental Business Plan 2023/24 outlines the key activities the Department aims to deliver during the current financial year.  Developed with regard to the Department’s constrained budgetary position, the Business Plan prioritises the provision of continued support to meet the needs of people, families and communities across the whole of Northern Ireland.	Work is underway to develop next year’s Departmental Business Plan, which will continue to be targeted at addressing Executive and Departmental priorities throughout Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for (i) an update on the design of the flood alleviation scheme in Ivy Mead, Drumahoe; and (ii) his Department’s estimate of the capital funding required to complete the scheme.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified and consultants have been instructed to commence the design phase of this project. Progress to procurement will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding. The current estimated cost to deliver the preferred option is in the region of £4-5m

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of the review of higher and further education funding, in the light of the National Union of Students and Union of Students in Ireland's Cost of Survival Report.
answer_text: A thriving Further and Higher sector is critical for a strong, regionally balanced economy. I am acutely aware of the impact that cost of living increases are having on students and will be engaging with my officials to fully scope the options for financial and other means of support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will ensure that Invest NI develops and publishes detailed sub-regional economic targets which are standardized across Northern Ireland, to include specific targets for the North-West city region.
answer_text: Promoting regional economic balance is a priority for me. To this end, sub-regional economic targets will be established, including for the North-West region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Executive improvement scheme for the Triangle area, Clooney Terrace, in Foyle.
answer_text: The improvement scheme for the Triangle area, Clooney Terrace in Foyle is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will undertake to commission a women’s health strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is significant work already underway across our health service and across many specialties providing care to women and girls of all ages, however, I recognise the need for a greater focus and co-ordination on women-specific issues and to prioritise limited resources where they will be most effective. My Department is currently in the process of developing a short-medium term Women’s Health Action Plan which will build upon this ongoing work and identify the priority actions across women’s health services which can be taken forward within our current budget, as well as those where additional investment may be required. This action plan is an important first step which could help inform a more comprehensive long term Women’s Health strategy in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: My Department will develop and implement comprehensive policies, strategies and plans that tackle a range of key issues including climate change and promoting nature friendly farming, with the aim of benefitting all regions and providing a shared future for all.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what they can do to ensure that Fountain Primary School retains funding from Urban Villages for its school transport.
answer_text: Urban Villages has provided funding of £92.6k to the Fountain Primary and Nursery School transport scheme from September 2017 to March 2024.In its design, Urban Villages was never intended to be a core funder, the aim was to provide additionality to existing provision within areas.  The emphasis of Urban Villages’ work in the Fountain area is changing as was always envisaged; against this backdrop the request from Fountain Primary School has been received and is being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take to engage with his counterpart in the Republic of Ireland to ensure that Fermanagh is included in the final All Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The Draft All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) was released for public consultation for the purposes of Strategic Environmental Assessment from 25 July to 29 September 2023. Approximately 470 consultation responses were received and work is ongoing to analyse these responses and identify the key themes.As we consider the issues raised, my officials and I will engage with our counterparts in the Department of Transport to finalise the Review and ensure that the final recommendations deliver on the objectives of the Review. This will include consideration of the viability of rail services to Fermanagh.It is currently anticipated the Review will be finalised and presented to the governments of both jurisdictions for approval and publication in late-Spring 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: The Department of Justice mission is “working in partnership to create a fair, just and safe community where we respect the law and each other”.  To achieve that mission, my Department will continue to fulfil its statutory requirements and have due regard to the need to promote equality of opportunity and consider rural needs when developing and implementing policies, strategies and plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what steps he will take to urgently prioritise the publication of the draft Early Education and Childcare Strategy; and, (ii) how he will ensure that parents are fully included in the consultation.
answer_text: I am committed to bringing forward a paper for an Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy to my Executive colleagues as a matter of priority. It is my intention to meet with a wide range of sector representatives and stakeholders over the coming days, including parents, to hear firsthand, the issues which they face and seek their views on what the Strategy needs to address.Independent research commissioned by the Department in 2022 to assess the key challenges which the Strategy needs to address also included extensive engagement with parents, and I will wish to consider the findings from this exercise also.  A copy of the final report can be accessed at RSM (2023) DE Review of Childcare Services in NI - Final Report.PDF (education-ni.gov.uk).    It is essential that we have input from a wide range of stakeholders as we take this work forward and I am committed to ongoing and meaningful engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: The need to secure balanced regional growth and tackle regional imbalance is outlined within the Regional Development Strategy 2035. The strategies and plans that I am bringing forward will reflect this.  From a transport perspective, the Department is preparing a new suite of Transport Plans that will continue to improve the connectivity and sustainability of the transport system across the region. This will also support the ambitions outlined within each council’s Local Development Plan. In addition, my Department is continuing to support the development of the City and Growth Deals and play our part in delivery. I am committed to delivering the upgrade to the A5 and the Phase 3 upgrade of the Derry to Coleraine railway is continuing to be progressed. These and other projects, taken in combination with the recently completed A6 scheme, will transform our social and economic geography by delivering greater accessibility, improved safety, reduced journey times and will promote inclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his department will take to achieve regional balance in its policies, strategies and plans.
answer_text: My Department’s Strategic Business Plan 2023/24 sets out the Department’s strategic objectives and priority actions within the limits of the financial and other resources allocated to the Department for the current financial year. It was informed by the draft Programme for Government and includes actions aimed at reducing health and social inequalities and delivering improvements across population health and wellbeing, quality, safety, improved service and access across the region. Work is underway to develop the 2024/25 Plan which will be informed by both my priorities and those of the wider Executive in the context of any updated Programme for Government and agreed Budget.My Department will continue to fulfil its statutory requirements and have due regard to the need to promote equality of opportunity and consider rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising a policy, strategy or plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to achieve a regionally balanced economy, including the expansion of higher education student numbers in the Ulster University's Magee Campus.
answer_text: Regional balance and the Magee expansion are priorities for me and I will outline my plans in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will instruct her department to review Section 12 of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, in relation to defendant anonymity.
answer_text: As is good practice with all new policies, I am open to reviewing whether the policy relating to the anonymity of suspects in sexual offence cases, which was given effect by this legislative provision, is operating as intended in practice. However, I am fully conscious that the policy change has only been in effect since 28 September 2023 and so I consider it would be somewhat premature to make an assessment on its effectiveness at this early stage.  It is important that sufficient time is allowed for any new policy to bed in so that its practical operation can be tested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps are being taken to improve careers guidance in schools.
answer_text: The careers system in Northern Ireland is the responsibility of both the Department of Education (DE) and the Department for the Economy (DfE).  The two departments work in partnership to address the careers education and guidance needs of young people.  DE has responsibility for the policy and delivery of careers education in schools while DfE has statutory responsibility for the policy and delivery of an all-age careers guidance service delivered by suitably qualified careers advisers.  The Careers Advisory Service has a Service Level Agreement with all post-primary schools. In June 2022 my Department and DfE jointly published Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training – A Framework to Transform 14-19 Education and Training Provision.  The report makes a number of recommendations around careers and careers guidance and my Department is working closely with DfE and key stakeholders progress these recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what assessment his Department has made of the need to provide further student finance support for those students who undertake medicine as a secondary undergraduate degree.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on her engagement with childcare providers in relation to the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging with childcare providers as part of the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy.  It is my intention to meet with a wide range of sector representatives over the coming days, to hear firsthand, the issues which the Strategy needs to address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider the introduction of a Women’s Health Strategy.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Gender Equality Strategy.
answer_text: I am currently considering work to date on the development of a Gender Equality Strategy and will decide on the next steps in due course.  Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what assessment his Department has made of the impact that a vacancy in the Ministerial office will have on businesses, post 28 October 2022.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 4140/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is taking to tackle the hidden costs of higher education.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3624/22-27, what steps his Department has taken to promote the Delay-Repay scheme to Translink customers.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration his Department has given to the call from the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists for two dedicated endometriosis regional centres in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the following questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment her Department has made of the benefits of introducing modern languages as a compulsory element of the primary school curriculum.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland , the teaching of an additional language is not compulsory for primary school children; however, the Northern Ireland Curriculum provides flexibility for primary schools to provide pupils with the opportunity to learn an additional language either during core teaching hours or as part of their extracurricular provision.  Modern Languages have never been a compulsory element of the primary curriculum.  The wide-ranging benefits of learning a language are well-documented by academics.  These range from developing children’s mental agility, emotional well-being and improved cognitive skills, global perspective, communication skills and inter-cultural awareness - all which add value in terms of health and wellbeing, social cohesion, life and work opportunities.Modern Languages has been identified as a priority area for development within the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  Officials have engaged extensively with counterparts in the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to consider how best to support languages and guide the Department’s focus to encourage the development of language skills.  As part of these ongoing discussions, CCEA has recently provided proposals to enhance and strengthen the Modern Languages Strategy moving forward.  My Department will consider these proposals, taking account of the current budgetary position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many schools in each constituency have availed of the Extended Schools Programme in (i) 2020-21; and (ii) 2021-22.
answer_text: The breakdown of schools participating in the Extended Schools programme from each Constituency in the years 2020/21, 2021/22, 2022/23, 2023/24 is provided in the table below.  Constituency 2023/24 2022/232021/222020/21Belfast East19202019Belfast North41454645Belfast South22222222Belfast West52535353East Antrim12162020East Londonderry25333129Fermanagh And South Tyrone28353737Foyle40454545Lagan Valley12131414Mid Ulster11161616Newry And Armagh37424443North Antrim21222424North Down8999South Antrim13181615South Down20232929Strangford13161514Upper Bann17202020West Tyrone31313333

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assessment her Department has made of the recent findings from research, conducted by Renters’ Voice, which found that 52 per cent of renters did not believe their current renting situation was sustainable.
answer_text: I note the Renter’s Voice survey referred to and the follow up survey, conducted in February 2023, had relatively small sample sizes. That said, there is wider evidence about the difficulties that lower income tenants have faced in meeting increasing rents in the private rented sector. It is these households that are most likely to have been most impacted by the freeze in Local Housing Allowance. The increase in Local Housing Allowance rates from last month, some of which have increased by over 40 percent, will then go some way in helping these tenants meet their housing costs and likely enable their current renting situation to be more sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with the Utility Regulator on reducing the cap on energy market profits.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of abortion services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: On 24 October, the Secretary of State announced that he will use his statutory powers to commission Abortion Services in Northern Ireland and ensure that appropriate funding is made available for these services.The Department will continue to work closely with the Northern Ireland Office and NI Health and Social Care Trusts on the implementation of these services, in line with legislative requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department is encouraging schools to participate in World Ventil8 Day.
answer_text: It is a matter for individual schools to determine their annual programme of awareness and celebratory events such as participating in events such as World Ventil8 Day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he plans to take following publication of research by Amnesty International on 21 October 2022, which found that 60 per cent of adults think the Government should be doing more to make abortion services available and accessible across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) CO2 monitors; and (ii) HEPA filters in schools, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: CO2 MonitorsAn initial supply of 11,500 CO2 monitors were secured and distributed in December 2021 across the education estate.  Following the initial distribution, Education Authority (EA) advised schools that they could request additional monitors. Schools who have requested additional monitors to date have now received them.  A total of 13,506 monitors have been distributed.  Unfortunately, the distribution records are not held in a constituency format.HEPA FiltersThe table below details the number of HEPA filters, totalling 200, installed by EA maintenance service, following school requests, broken down by constituency:ConstituencyCount of FiltrationUnits DeliveredBELFAST EAST4BELFAST NORTH7BELFAST SOUTH6BELFAST WEST43EAST ANTRIM11EAST LONDONDERRY11FERMANAGH AND SOUTH TYRONE13FOYLE20NEWRY AND ARMAGH6NORTH ANTRIM2NORTH DOWN7SOUTH ANTRIM10SOUTH DOWN5STRANGFORD30UPPER BANN20WEST TYRONE5

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on his Department’s work with the Northern Ireland Office and the British government to deliver on additional funding for the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre in Foyle.
answer_text: In the context of the agreement to restore the NI Executive, the UK Government outlined Financial and Economic Commitments to Northern Ireland in Annex A to the “New Decade, New Approach” (NDNA) document. Under the section Addressing Northern Ireland’s Unique Circumstances, the UK Government stated that it would make funding available for a range of projects aimed at supporting community and reconciliation initiatives to remove barriers, bring the people together and build a safer, more secure society, which could include areas such as additional funding to support the Derry/Londonderry addiction centre.Following discussions with the Northern Ireland Office (NIO), my officials, along with the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) as the commissioners of substance use services, have been looking at how best to develop potential proposals for the allocation of funding under this commitment.As an initial step, a population needs-based assessment of substance use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area is now underway and a review of inpatient Tier 4 services across Northern Ireland is also being commissioned.In addition, my Department is leading on the implementation of Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, NI’s new substance use strategy. As part of this, SPPG in association with PHA are in the process of developing an approach to Multi-Agency Strategic Planning and Commissioning. It is the intention that a comprehensive, whole-system planning approach to substance use will ensure that demographic trends of increasing demand and complexity, current and future resource, gaps, service pressures and workforce issues will be addressed.All of these pieces of work will be utilised so that my Department can make informed proposals to the NIO around this NDNA commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment his Department has made of the recent findings by Carers NI in their research Unpaid Carers and the Cost of Living Crisis in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise that unpaid carers are a lifeline for those that they care for, and the health service could not survive without each and every one of them. While the current cost of living crisis is impacting on all areas of society across Northern Ireland, I am mindful of the particular challenges that face informal/family carers, as highlighted by the recent Carers NI research.I note that a substantial number of the recommendations made by Carers NI in their recent white paper, would fall under the remit of other government departments, rather than directly for the Department of Health. It would be for those departments to consider these as appropriate. I recognise however that there are many improvements to be made, and more action needs to be taken to support carers. You will be aware that my Department has recently undertaken a public consultation on proposals to Reform the Adult Social Care system in Northern Ireland. A central priority of the plans is supporting family and unpaid carers and this consultation included a number of proposals to improve the support available.Now that the consultation period has ended, my officials are analysing the responses and a summary and evaluation report will be prepared for Ministerial consideration. The Department will publish the consultation report, which will then inform the development of a new strategy for adult social care. I look forward to seeing the outcome of the consultation and views in relation to the proposals to further support carers.Additionally, in recognition of the particular challenges facing carers, I was pleased to launch the Support for Carers Fund last year. This Fund is actively providing assistance and support for carers on the ground, with funding of approximately £2.5m awarded to date. I was also pleased to announce an uplift of £7.1m for 2022-23 for Direct Payments on 6 October 2022. In real terms, this should see Personal Assistants receiving an uplift of around 12% in their hourly rate. This funding will allow individuals who employ personal assistant carers via Direct Payments to offer improved rates of pay and in turn support the continued provision of care in the context of an increasingly competitive employment market, including in the social care sector. My Department remains committed to supporting carers, and seeking to find additional ways to provide support where we can, and where pressures allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what discussions his officials have had with Translink on the Delay-Repay scheme and the extent to which eligible users of public transport are appropriately compensated.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to his statement on 11 October 2022, to outline the key reasons that have resulted in the current levels of departmental overspend.
answer_text: A large proportion of the forecast overspend is due to energy costs and pay pressures resulting from the cost of living crisis. The impact of inflation also means that the Executive Spending Review outcome is worth considerably less than when it was set in 2021.The position has been exacerbated by the lack of an Executive agreed Budget to help planning, inform decision making and prioritisation and constrain departmental spending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what actions her Department has taken to address the non-payment of substitute teachers for work completed in September 2022; (ii) whether she will launch a review into the use of the NI Supply Teacher Register system; and (iii) what actions her Department will be taking to ensure this situation does not occur again.
answer_text: This question relates to events in August 2022.A new and improved Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) was procured by the Education Authority (EA) which administers the system, and it went live in August 2022.A number of issues were identified when the system went live which resulted in the late salary payments for some supply teachers. NISTR is accepted as the only system through which schools can engage supply teachers in Northern Ireland. The EA continues to monitor activities through NISTR.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what support is in place for parents of young children who are unable to afford the rising cost of baby formula; (ii) what steps are being taken to promote the NHS Healthy Start Scheme; and (iii) how many parents from Northern Ireland applied to the NHS Healthy Start Scheme in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Healthy Start Scheme helps eligible parents of young children with the purchase of baby formula.  Since the scheme was digitised, two national communications campaigns have been delivered. The first campaign targeted those on the paper voucher scheme to further raise awareness that the paper voucher scheme was ending in March 2022, and to highlight that in order to continue receiving their benefit they need to apply online for a prepaid card. The second awareness campaign began in February 2022 to promote the digitised scheme to all those who may be eligible for a prepaid card.Data is not available in the format requested; however, between reporting years September 2018 to September 2022 there have been over 24,000 applications for the Healthy Start Scheme in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to raise awareness of the impact of menopause symptoms.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response to AQW 3666/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is in place for GP surgeries to establish telephone systems to ensure appropriate triaging of patients.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 4453/22-27, (i) what is meant by the term same category of spend; (ii) why In-year Monitoring of Public Expenditure Guidance was not issued for 2022/23; and (iii) whether Ministers may move funding over £1 million within Departments, without Executive approval.
answer_text: The Budget provided by Treasury is broken into different categories of spend with a separate control total for each.  These are:Ring-fenced Resource (used only for depreciation and impairments) Non Ring-Fenced Resource Capital Financial Transactions Capital. In-year Monitoring of Public Expenditure Guidance was not issued as in the absence of an Executive a budget cannot be set and therefore monitoring rounds cannot take place. The monitoring guidance in 2021-22 allowed Ministers flexibility to move funding within their department without recourse to the Executive.  I would anticipate the same principle to apply in the absence of an Executive

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to his announcement of increased student maintenance loans from 2023/24, (i) whether any increased financial support is planned for postgraduate students; (ii) whether the planned review of student funding will include a review of funding available for postgraduate students and students in further education; and (iii) what opportunity student representatives will have to engage in the review.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why the Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics covering Quarter one of 2022-23 have not yet been published.
answer_text: Official Statistics for the Driver and Vehicle Agency are produced and published by a small team of NISRA statisticians.  The delay in publishing Quarter one statistics for 2022-23 is due to the long term sick absence of a member of the team.The Quarter one report is now due for publication on 30 November 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1902/22-27, whether he will commit to including a workforce plan and sustained funding for bereavement care as part of the new Maternity Strategy.
answer_text: The Department will be taking forward a new Maternity Strategy drawing on the learning from the last Maternity Strategy and reflecting other strategic drivers.  There is, as yet, no timeline for the commencement of this process but the needs assessment to inform the Strategy is underway.In terms of workforce planning, Delivering Care aims to support the provision of high quality care which is safe and effective in hospital and community settings, through the development of a framework to determine staffing ranges for the nursing and midwifery workforce in a range of major specialities. Delivering Care Phase 11 has been progressing and will be outlining the workforce requirements for midwifery.In respect of bereavement care, all healthcare staff working in maternity and neonatal services in Northern Ireland follow the Department of Health’s Regional Bereavement Guidance on evidence-based, holistic care of parents and their families after the experience of miscarriage, stillbirth or neonatal death. This guidance establishes a regional standard of care throughout Northern Ireland. Pathways are in place for the emotional and practical support of parents who experience miscarriage, stillbirth or the death of a baby shortly after birth.  The team involved in providing care to the bereaved woman will work with her and the family to navigate the legal, procedural and emotional challenges following the death of a baby.  In addition, each Trust has a bereavement midwife resource to provide additional support to parents and families who have lost a baby through miscarriage, stillbirth or where their baby has died shortly after birth. Bereavement midwives also offer support and training to other members of staff to ensure that they can provide support to parents and families in these challenging circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what consideration her Department has given to compulsory training for jurors who sit on trials related to domestic abuse and other forms of gender-based violence.
answer_text: My Department is looking at a number of recommendations relating to jurors’ responsibilities emanating from the Review by Sir John Gillen and Criminal Justice Inspection NI reports on the handling of sexual violence and abuse cases and modern slavery and human trafficking. I recognise there is a variety of juror related issues and my Department intends to conduct a holistic review and public consultation on these.   It is anticipated this work will provide the opportunity to consider jury awareness raising and training more widely.More generally, I recognise the importance of increased awareness raising in relation to domestic and sexual abuse for all of our society. Over the remainder of this year there will be further bursts of the ‘Still Abuse’ awareness raising campaign on domestic abuse, the domestic abuse offence and the support available through the 24hr Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline.  My Department will also be undertaking awareness raising over coming months in relation to new offences to address stalking, non-fatal strangulation, up-skirting, down-blousing and cyber flashing.  We are also working on an active bystander public awareness campaign with PSNI.In addition, together with the Department of Health we are developing a new seven year strategy to further progress work to tackle domestic and sexual abuse.  It is intended to publish this for consultation later this year, to take effect from April 2023.  That strategy, and associated action plans, will consider how best we can keep people informed about this issue, as well as increasing knowledge about healthy relationships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to reduce re-offending in domestic abuse cases.
answer_text: The strategic approach of my Department, together with our partners, is to align activities, support rehabilitation, improve outcomes for offenders and ultimately build a safer NI through a long term reduction in offending behaviour.  However, the criminal justice system on its own cannot provide the knowledge, expertise or access to the services needed to truly reduce reoffending.In terms of addressing domestic abuse, I have brought forward a range of measures including the introduction of a domestic abuse offence and associated aggravators that assists the police to intervene early in the abuse cycle and prevent repeat offending.  This will be complemented by Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders which, when introduced, will also assist in early intervention for those subject to and at risk of abuse.  I have also legislated to address a number of other abusive behaviours including stalking and non-fatal strangulation.  Within the custodial environment, those who have engaged in domestic abuse will have the opportunity to work with the Prisoner Development Unit to develop plans to address their specific needs.  Development can include addressing addictions, mental health, counselling and education/training skills, as well as addressing more specific offence related behaviours and attitudes. Individuals can also be referred to specific programmes where appropriate.Once released, the Probation Board offer a range of programmes and interventions to offenders/service users to address the issue of domestic abuse for those under statutory supervision, or who are subject to public protection arrangements.  These programmes effectively and robustly ensure that those who have perpetrated domestic abuse are able to address their abusive behaviour, challenge negative attitudes and beliefs and increase awareness as to the impact their behaviour has had on victims.  In addition, the Public Protection Arrangements for Northern Ireland (PPANI) bring together a number of agencies including Probation, Police, Social Services, the Prison Service and the Housing Executive to share information and to provide effective assessment and management of risks posed by individuals convicted of violence in a domestic setting.
