question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what his assessment is of the recent report on racial equality in medicine; (ii) what steps he will put in place to implement the recommendations within this report; and (iii) whether he will investigate why the number of medical staff leaving the profession due to racism is not recorded.
answer_text: (i) & (ii) Racism, in any shape or form, is not something that any colleague in HSC should have to endure. I want to send a clear message, from the very top of our health and social care system, that such behaviour is totally unacceptable and will not be tolerated. We fully recognise and respect the hard work, commitment and dedication of HSC staff from within the BAME community.HSC Trusts including NIAS have a responsibility to ensure that all appropriate steps are taken to ensure staff know they can come to a workplace that is free from abuse and harassment. It must be clear to everyone who works across HSC that they have the full support of senior management and the Department of Health in tackling any form of racism.The five Health and Social Care Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service have worked collaboratively to develop an Equality Action Plan which sets out the actions that are being taken forward collaboratively over five years. Actions in the Plan are intended to make sure that the promotion of equality of opportunity and good relations are mainstreamed across health and social care. The Trusts have established a Joint Consultative Forum so that they can continue to work closely with the Community Relations Council (CRC), the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI) and the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission (NIHRC) in taking forward the Action Plan.  Considerable work has been done across health and social care to promote good relations such as a regional training manual for HSC Staff; guidance for Non-Executive Directors on equality, human rights and good relations; celebrating good relations week; and establishing Minority Ethnic staff networks.Each HSC Trust have also in place employment policies to support staff including the Equality, Diversity and Inclusion Policy, which supports fair employment.  Additional mechanisms in place for reporting incidents of racism and discrimination include the Conflict, Bullying and Harassment in the Workplace Policy and the Complaints Policy, as well as an ECNI approved Equality Scheme. Additional measures includeBespoke welcome packages for the International Recruitment Program;mandatory training in the form of the Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights E-Learning Programme “Making a Difference”; andStaff Support Network to support ethnic minorities colleagues.The Department recognises and welcomes the increasing number of medics from a diverse range of backgrounds who are coming to train in Northern Ireland. Accordingly, the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency is working with trainee representatives and the HSC Trusts to provide the necessary support for these medics, and their families, as they settle in to life in Northern Ireland and pursue their careers in the HSC.(iii) HSC Trusts have indicated that medical staff are invited to share their views on their experiences in working within HSC and whether they have any recommendations for change. Th

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will publish an update on his Department’s progress on the preparation of legislation to ban fracking.
answer_text: In the last mandate I circulated a draft Executive paper to Ministerial colleagues on future onshore petroleum licensing policy. It sought agreement to the Department’s preferred policy option to introduce a moratorium and eventual legislative ban on all forms of onshore petroleum exploration and production in Northern Ireland, including fracking. No decision was taken on the paper by the Executive. 	As I have stated previously, Executive agreement will be required prior to public consultation on a preferred policy option as onshore petroleum licensing is a cross cutting and controversial issue. Appropriate legislative action will be taken forward once a policy position is adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure can a meeting take place between an elected representative, Translink and Into the West without Department officials present.
answer_text: I am ambitious for our rail network and I am committed to addressing regional imbalance by improving connectivity to the North West area. I, along with my officials, have engaged with Into the West with respect to our ambitions for the North West. While meetings can take place between political representatives, Into the West and Translink without officials present, I am keen for my officials to be involved in any discussions relating to improving rail connections in the region to ensure that we can continue to work collaboratively on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice the number of prosecutions for racist (i) abuse; and (ii) assaults, directed at health care staff in Northern Ireland, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the location where an offence occurred or, unless specified in the offence description, on the occupation of the victim of abuse or assault.  In the period 2017 to 2021, the most recent five years for which information is available, there were no prosecutions or convictions at courts for offences which specified the victim as a health or ambulance worker, and where there was a racist motivation, as assessed by the Public Prosecution Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with the British Government to advocate for the necessary funding to remove the Maximum Student Number cap on university student places in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Higher Education is a devolved matter. The ability of local Higher Education Institutions to recruit more NI domiciled students is limited by the Executive’s decision on the amount of budget available to my Department to provide teaching grant to support student places. The provision of teaching grant means that student fees charged in Northern Ireland are considerably lower than in England and Wales. In Scotland, which also applies teaching grant to Scottish domiciles studying in Scotland, a number cap is in place due to the need to remain within the teaching grant funding provided by the Scottish Government.In contrast, there is no similar constraint in England and Wales as student teaching is funded in the main via student fees, with extremely limited levels of teaching grant being provided.  I have had no engagement with the UK Government as it is a devolved matter on which the Executive has decided funding arrangements. It is only on the basis that the Executive follow the same funding approach as in England, that the cap could be removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following her announcement on childcare provision on 7 September 2022, what consideration was given to offering all pre-school children a minimum of 30 hours per week.
answer_text: The offer of a minimum of 22.5 hours pre-school education per week is a core component of an integrated Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, and viewed as a vital first step, to ensuring equality of provision for all children aged 3-4.  Further proposals, including any additional hours for children aged 3-4, will be considered as part of the ongoing development work on the wider Strategy and informed by the planned research and stakeholder engagement I outlined in my statement on 7 September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support he will put in place for small businesses during the winter months.
answer_text: I recognise the challenges our local businesses are facing due to rising costs. To identify avenues of assistance, I have been engaging with representatives of the business community. Further meetings on the matter are being convened as we work to support businesses across our economy through the winter months.On 8 September 2022, Prime Minister Truss announced energy bill price caps for businesses for a period of six months. The UK Government has indicated that Northern Ireland businesses will receive the same support as that being rolled out across the rest of the UK. In addition, to help businesses, Invest NI delivers a suite of support, including specialist advice to enable NI businesses to become more energy efficient. This includes a fully funded Technical Consultancy and a Resource Matching Service. This service helps businesses optimise and futureproof their operations by implementing green efficiencies. Such efficiencies enable carbon savings and cost savings, mitigating the impact of rising energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what consideration he has given to mandating that all hospitality venues offer spiking kits; and, (ii) whether he has engaged with the Minister of Justice on any such proposal.
answer_text: I have not considered mandating that all hospitality venues offer spiking kits as it is not within the remit of my Department. Other Northern Ireland Departments are better placed to provide a response on this matter.	I have not engaged with the Minister of Justice on any such proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what plans he has made to support students throughout the cost of living crisis; (ii) how many times he has met with student representatives since March 2020 and the dates of those meetings; and (iii) when he plans to next meet with student representatives.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that the cost of living crisis is having on households across Northern Ireland, and in particular on students. In addition to the wide range of financial support already provided by my Department to support students within further and higher education, I recently announced that for academic year 2022/23 additional funding of £2.8million will be provided through the Higher Education Student Support Funds to help those students who encounter financial hardship during the course of their studies at the five local higher education institutions.For students studying within the Further Education sector and facing hardship they will continue to have access to a range of financial support measures to assist them to overcome barriers which could prevent their continued participation in Further Education. I encourage any Further Education or Higher Education student facing financial difficulties to contact the student support office within their respective college or higher education institution for advice and support.My Department has also engaged the Strategic Investment Board to undertake a review of student support within Further Education.  In relation to your query as to how many times I have met with student representatives since March 2020, any external meetings I have attended up to and including 30 June 2022 are published publicly at www.opendatani.gov.uk. Since 1 July 2022 I have not met any student representatives, however, my officials are in regular contact with NUS-USI. Going forward, I have recently received an invitation from Chloe Ferguson, NUS-USI President, and will meet with her in due course, diary permitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Early Years review will include a recommendation on the permanent expansion of Surestart coverage to the wards of Tulleyally and Drumahoe in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Some addresses in the Tullyally and Drumahoe areas are now served by Waterside Sure Start under temporary expansion which, since 2021 has extended Sure Start services to an additional 22 Super Output Areas (SOAs), now falling within the 25% most disadvantaged in Northern Ireland. Targeting of Sure Start is being considered (including the 22 SOAs) within the ongoing, Independent Review of Targeted Early Years Interventions, which is expected to conclude by autumn 2022.  I will take account of findings in the Review Report when this is made available to the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why routine asbestos inspections are not carried out on buildings that are due for demolition and were built before the 1999 asbestos ban; and (ii) whether he will commit to introducing these inspections.
answer_text: The management of asbestos is regulated under the Control of Asbestos Regulations (NI) 2012, for which the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI), an Executive Non-Departmental Public Body sponsored by the Department for the Economy, are the competent enforcing Authority.		 As HSENI are the competent enforcing Authority it is not for my Department to conduct such inspections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many individuals were awaiting the outcome of their Free School Meal application on 1 September 2022; and (ii) how this figure compares to applications awaiting income on (a) 1 September 2021; and (b) 1 September 2020.
answer_text: The number of parents/guardians awaiting the outcome of their free school meals application on 1 September 2022 was 10,460. This is a significant reduction on the equivalent figure on 1 September 2021 which was 12,694. The EA is unable to provide this information for 1 September 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what further support he intends to provide to community pharmacists to help address pressures on the health service.
answer_text: The 3 year Community Pharmacy Commissioning Plan 22-25, has been agreed and is funded for year one (2022-23) which includes the development of a number of new clinical services in community pharmacy. Officials in my Department are working closely with Community Pharmacy NI to deliver this Commissioning Plan, within the context of difficult economic pressures which indeed all businesses in NI are facing, including community pharmacies.The absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 has limited my ability to commit additional funding to services.  I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with funding allocations, which will continue to fund essential activity. However based on our current spending trajectory, we will have spent roughly 60% of our baseline by the end of the second quarter of 2022/23.I am very conscious of pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will commit to delivering a campaign targeted at parents, employers and childcare providers to increase awareness of the financial support available for childcare.
answer_text: My Department, in conjunction with the Department of Health, ran a promotional campaign throughout 2021/22 to raise awareness of the financial support available to help families with the costs of childcare.  This included radio and digital media advertising, advertisements in local publications and information to childcare providers.  Planning is underway for further promotional work in 2022/23.My officials have been engaging with HMRC to raise awareness specifically of the UK-wide Tax-Free Childcare Scheme.  In addition, my Department has worked closely with the Department for the Economy and the Labour Relations Agency on their recent ‘Employers Guide to Childcare’ publication that encourages employers to consider family-friendly working practices and policies and to signpost their staff to available financial assistance towards their childcare costs.As well as the ongoing promotional work to encourage uptake of existing financial support available to families, I have written to the UK Chancellor asking him to uplift the rate of the Tax-Free Childcare Scheme from 20% to 30% to support families with their childcare costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to increase the school leaving age to 18.
answer_text: The upper limit of compulsory school age is set out in primary legislation and any change would require agreement from the Executive and the Assembly.  I have no plans to increase the school leaving age to 18 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Disability Employment Strategy.
answer_text: The Department is within the final development stages of the next Disability and Work Strategy, which will seek to set a long term commitment to increasing the rate of employment for those with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work will begin on the Sevenoaks Traffic Calming Scheme in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that the traffic calming scheme in the Sevenoaks area of the Foyle constituency is scheduled for delivery this autumn.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether a criminal conviction debars a person from being a director of a Housing Association; (ii) if so, what type of conviction debars a person; and (iii) what is recognised as time spent.
answer_text: A criminal conviction does not, in itself, debar a person from being a director of a housing association but may do so in certain circumstances.Registered Housing Associations (RHAs), as registered charities, must comply with the Charities Act 2008 (the Act). Some people are disqualified by law from acting as charity trustees (directors). These disqualifications are set out in the Act and broadly include, but are not limited to, anyone who:has been convicted of an offence involving deception or dishonesty, unless the conviction is spent under the Rehabilitation of Offenders Order 1978;has previously been removed as a charity trustee by the Charity Commission or the courts; oris subject to disqualification under company legislation.RHAs under their own rules of association may adopt additional criteria for determining whether a person can serve as a director. The Federation of Housing Association’s model rules, upon which several of the RHAs’ rules are based include provisions to debar a person from serving as director if:they are disqualified from acting as a director of a company, as a Board Member of another registered society or as a charity trustee for any reason;they have been convicted of an indictable offence which is not, or cannot be, spent; or they have been convicted of any other offence at any time which, in the opinion of the Boardbrings the association into disrepute, oris incompatible with the role of Board Member, committee member or co-optee, and:the Board resolved (by a two-thirds majority) that they should be removed.The determination of whether a conviction is spent is not within the remit of this Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to address the challenges facing unpaid carers.
answer_text: I recognise that, for many carers, this has been a particularly difficult and challenging period of their lives. I am committed to supporting carers, and to seeking to find additional ways to provide support where we can, and where pressures allow.Trusts continue to proactively reach out to identify informal, unpaid carers to ensure they have the support they require in their caring role, and are able to access appropriate advice and support. As standard practice, when Trust staff identify carers, they are offered carer assessments or reassessments and if accepted, arrangements are made for the assessments to be carried out.Throughout the pandemic Trusts have continued to grow and adapt their services to enhance support for carers through the use of online activities, increasing the information available, and the provision of face to face events. Trusts also disseminate regular information to keep unpaid carers informed of support and services to assist in the caring role.In recognition of the ongoing challenges, I was pleased last year to be able to establish the Support for Carers Fund to support family/unpaid carers. When I launched the Fund my focus was to support projects to deliver on the ground to make a tangible difference to individual carers and I am pleased to advise that approximately £2.5m in funding has now been awarded to projects that are making a real and practical difference to people’s lives.I appreciate that many carers continue to experience significant challenges and it is important that we continue to work to strengthen the supports available. The ‘Power to People’ report highlighted the need to acknowledge the essential contribution of the family and informal carers who assist in supporting the sustainability of the broader adult social care system.As you will be aware my Department has recently undertaken a public consultation on proposals to Reform the Adult Social Care system in Northern Ireland. Through this consultation, the Department has brought forward proposals to address the challenges facing the adult care and support system in Northern Ireland. A central priority of the plans is supporting family and unpaid carers and this consultation included a number of proposals to improve the support available.Now that the consultation period has ended, my officials are analysing the responses and a summary and evaluation report will be prepared for Ministerial consideration. The Department will publish the consultation report, which will then inform the development of a new strategy for adult social care.I look forward to seeing the outcome of the consultation and views in relation to the proposals to further support carers. Ultimately the focus needs to be on delivering tangible improvements to the lived experiences of people on the ground.We, as a society, need to be more appreciative of the role that carers carry out in our communities, and we in the Executive, need to be better at valuing their contribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what urgent steps his Department intends to take to progress flood alleviation schemes in Foyle.
answer_text: My Department is developing flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe and Eglinton and these projects have now progressed to detailed design. However, the development of these proposals are complex and have required re-evaluation to determine if a viable solution can be found. This re-evaluation continues and will also now include a review of the recent flooding to ensure that any proposals would be effective. It is hoped this re-evaluation will be completed by the end of 2022. Subject to the identification of a viable proposal the detailed design will be further developed, in conjunction with the environmental impact assessment, and I have asked my officials to progress this work as quickly as possible. A further flood alleviation scheme is currently proposed for the Derry City area, mainly focusing on tidal flood risk. It is intended this project will progress to design in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will take steps to introduce paid menstrual leave.
answer_text: Good employers recognise the challenges that many women can face within the workplace as a result of severe period pain.  They have flexible supportive working arrangements in place such as homeworking or flexible hours on painful period days and they create supportive working environments in which employees can openly discuss periods in the same way they discuss other health issues.  I would encourage all employers to offer such flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff.There are no plans at present to introduce paid menstrual leave.  An immediate focus and priority for my Department is the progression of paid miscarriage leave and domestic abuse safe leave.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the repair and refurbishment of the Triangle estate, including (i) the number of fire safety assessments that have been carried out in the last five years; (ii) when she expects the refurbishment scheme to be completed; (iii) for her assessment of the likelihood of any delays; and (iv) whether she will undertake to encourage the Housing Executive to write to residents to provide them with an update on the scheme's progress.
answer_text: The work in relation to the repair and refurbishment of the Triangle estate is ongoing.NIHE should engage fully with all stakeholders at the earliest opportunity on any issues that may affect them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will increase the bursary available to nursing students, in the light of the cost of living emergency.
answer_text: The bursary arrangements for healthcare students are kept under continuous review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in the light of the cost of living emergency, (i) what further steps her Department will take to address the cost of school uniforms; (ii) whether she will consider using departmental powers to address this as a welfare issue; and (iii) whether she will direct schools to amend uniform policies to remove the requirement for certain items to be branded.
answer_text: I understand the challenges that families are currently having to address in relation to the cost of living, including the pressures associated with school uniform costs at this time of year.  In relation to the costs associated with school uniforms, I recently approved a 20% uplift in the levels of uniform grant ahead of the 2022-23 academic year. This measure provides immediate additional support in this area while work continues with my Department’s review of free school meals and uniform grants. This review will include consideration of the rates of uniform grant, and the criteria determining who can access the grant. I also recently announced that ahead of the conclusion of the ongoing review, I have instructed my officials to identify an approach that will seek to ensure, on a statutory basis, that school uniforms are more affordable for families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the Italian study, overseen by the Hume foundation think-tank, that showed mechanical ventilation systems can reduce school Covid-19 cases by 82%; and (ii) when she will install HEPA air filtration units in all schools.
answer_text: (i) My Department has noted the David Hume Foundation study and will maintain a review of this area. The Department continues to follow the guidance on ventilation and air conditioning in the workplace provided by the Public Health Agency. (ii) The most effective mitigating measure in relation to Covid-19 is natural ventilation and my Department and the Education Authority are supporting schools in prioritising the effective implementation of this measure. Air cleaners were reviewed in detail by SAGE and its Environment and Modelling Group concluded that there is currently very little evidence that air cleaners are an effective control to prevent COVID-19, however the principle of air cleaning suggests that they may be useful in some cases and may be suitable for spaces where there is insufficient ventilation and the ventilation can’t be improved. Given that advice from SAGE, my Department does not plan to install HEPA air filtration units in all schools. All schools have received guidance in relation to ventilation, and supplied with CO2 monitors to assist in identifying areas where ventilation needs to be improved.  Schools with concerns about ventilation have been advised to contact the EA maintenance helpline for advice/support on the best approach for their individual circumstances. There is a sufficient stock of air filtration units to meet the current demands and additional units will be purchased if there is a requirement for them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A2 Buncrana Road scheme, including (i) whether designs have been finalised; (ii) when the work will commence; and (iii) the estimated total cost.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the significance of the A2 Buncrana Road Improvement Scheme for the city of Derry in terms of improved transport links, road safety, active and sustainable travel, and its contribution to the regeneration of the area. My Officials are currently finalising the design to ensure that it is appropriate and the project is future-proofed before consideration is given to bringing forward draft statutory Orders. The timescales for progressing to procurement and construction will depend upon on a number of factors including the need for a public inquiry, the successful completion of statutory procedures and the availability of funding in future years’ for which budgets for my Department  have not yet been agreed.The preliminary estimated cost for the project is £85 million but this is currently under review as part of the detailed design and will also need to be revised to take account of current inflationary pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many people are living with Ulrich Muscular Dystrophy; (ii) to detail the recommended treatment; and (iii) whether this treatment can accessed on the NHS.
answer_text: Specific data on the number of people in Northern Ireland living with Ullrich Muscular Dystrophy is not recorded by my Department. However, the number of hospital admissions for people diagnosed with Muscular Dystrophy and Congenital Muscular Dystrophy (including Ullrich Muscular Dystrophy) are provided in the table below.  17/1818/1919/2020/2121/22PG710 (Muscular Dystrophy)Admissions13014711166109Individuals17373774963G712 (Congenital myopathiesCongenital muscular dystrophy:)Admissions3317321527Individuals112815517Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Dept. of Health, NI.Patients receive supportive and pragmatic help from a multi-disciplinary team comprising physiotherapy, occupational therapy, nutritional support, genetics, respiratory, cardiology and orthotic services. All of these services can be accessed through the health service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether an Equality Impact Assessment was carried out on changes to the pension awarding process for the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers' Superannuation Committee members, as a result of the Local Government Pension Scheme Regulations (NI) 2014 Regulations.
answer_text: An equality screening exercise, carried out in respect of changes introduced by the Local Government Pension Scheme Regulations 2014, did not conclude that a significant discriminatory or negative differential impact on any section 75 group was likely therefore an Equality Impact Assessment was not carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action he is taking to mitigate the current wave of COVID-19; (ii) whether he is considering bringing forward the autumn booster programme; and (iii) whether he intends to reconsider the current rules in relation to face coverings on public transport and in healthcare settings or other public buildings.
answer_text: (i) What action he is taking to mitigate the current wave of COVID-19.Data indicates that the current wave of COVID-19 is declining.  While prevalence remains relatively high and pressures on health and social care remain significant, no further actions are planned in relation to the current wave although the situation will be kept under review.  Vaccinations remain the best form of defence against serious ill health, hospitalisation and death from COVID-19 amongst those likely to be at highest risk - the elderly and those with underlying health conditions.Throughout the course of the COVID-19 vaccination programme, the Department of Health has been guided by the expert advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).  This has involved making primary and booster vaccinations available as and when recommended by JCVI.  In response to a JCVI recommendation, a spring booster campaign, aimed at care home residents, those aged 75 years of age and over and the immunosuppressed, was completed in the period between March 2022 and June 2022.Current public health advice available via NIDirect remains valid:  Coronavirus (COVID-19): how to stay safe and help prevent the spread | nidirect(ii) Whether he is considering bringing forward the autumn booster programmeIn line with further advice from the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) plans are now well advanced for an autumn programme, to begin next month, the primary aim of which is to protect the most vulnerable over the winter period.JCVI have recommended a gap of at least three months between an individual’s last dose and a booster. For those vaccinated as part of the spring booster this will mean that the autumn programme can only start from September 2022 onwards.  GPs, Community Pharmacies as well as Trust sites are expected to begin vaccinations in September 2022, in line with JCVI advice and the rest of the UK.(iii) Whether he intends to reconsider the current rules in relation to face coverings on public transport and in healthcare settings or other public buildingsAt present, there are no plans to reconsider guidance in relation to the use of face coverings. All guidance in relation to measures to reduce COVID-19 transmission will be kept under review as we move into the autumn and winter period.While no longer a legal requirement, all public transport passengers are advised to wear face coverings on buses and trains and at stations, if they are able to do so. This message reflects the current public health advice which continues to ‘strongly recommend’ the use of face coverings in indoor areas where you come into contact with people you do not usually meet. As we approach autumn and our health service starts to face additional winter pressures, Translink will keep this messaging under review to ensure it reflects the most up to date public health advice and promotes passenger confidence, particularly among older and more vulnerable passengers who are often

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he will introduce financial incentives for consumers to decarbonise their energy sources; and (ii) to detail his reasoning.
answer_text: Our Energy Strategy, The Path To Net Zero, outlines our long-term vision to achieve net zero carbon and affordable energy for Northern Ireland consumers. The strategy sets the policy direction for increasing renewable energy generation and achieving a target of at least 70% of electricity consumed in Northern Ireland to be from renewables.  This target has since increased to 80% following the introduction of the Climate Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which received Royal Assent last month. Our strategy also commits to delivering energy savings from buildings and replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy. My Department intends to launch a pilot domestic and small business support scheme to help with the transition to lower carbon forms of heating in 2022/23, which will then lead to a wider rollout of support in the future.Action twelve of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for this year also outlines our intention to consult on a renewable electricity support scheme, with officials currently focused on securing a scheme that will support large scale development offering maximum benefit to Northern Ireland as a whole, whilst achieving the greatest value for money. In line with Energy Strategy objectives of putting the consumer at the heart of the energy transition and delivering a smart, decentralised energy system, my officials are also considering how to support other forms of generation, including micro-generating technologies such as residential solar photovoltaics, in a considered way, as we bring forward future Action Plans. Further information on proposed support schemes will be published at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when his Department will carry out essential road resurfacing on (i) Lisaghmore Avenue, Derry; and (ii) Lyndhurst Road, Derry.
answer_text: It is my Department’s intention to resurface Lyndhurst Road during the current 2022/23 financial year, subject to a new resurfacing contract being successfully awarded in the autumn 2022.The resurfacing of Lisaghmore Avenue is programmed for 2023/24 financial year, subject to funding.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action his Department is taking to track and trace cases of monkeypox; and (ii) whether he will roll out a public awareness campaign on monkeypox, with specific regard to identifying the signs and the advised isolation period.
answer_text: (i) Schedule 1 of the Public Health Act (Northern Ireland) 1967 was amended on 7 June 2022 to make monkeypox a notifiable disease.  This requires clinicians to report known or suspected cases to the Public Health Agency (PHA). Information has been disseminated to all clinicians to notify them of the need to report known and suspected cases in accordance with nationally agreed case definitions.   Each confirmed case is contacted, with their consent, for contact tracing purposes.  As part of any incident response, the PHA Health Protection surveillance services maintains a database of all case notifications. (ii) In response to the outbreak, the PHA has activated an incident management response plan and established a regional steering group with all relevant stakeholders.  The regional steering group has been coordinating communication and promotion activities in Northern Ireland. Press releases and social media messages are shared regularly and work is being undertaken with relevant organisations to share messages with the individuals most at risk.  The PHA website contains materials on signs, symptoms and isolation periods.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the smallpox vaccine will be made available to healthcare workers and others who may have been exposed to monkeypox, or are at increased risk of contracting monkeypox.
answer_text: In adherence with UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) guidance relating to pre-exposure vaccination against monkeypox, the smallpox vaccine has been prioritised for healthcare workers with high risk of exposure and certain groups within the community at greatest risk of infection in order to control the recent outbreak of the virus. Others who have been in close contact with a confirmed case of monkeypox are being offered vaccination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department is responsible for equality issues in local government pension decisions, including adherence to Section 75 Equality of Opportunity Screening Analysis.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for maintaining, reviewing and updating the legislative framework for the Local Government Pensions Scheme and, in doing so, to have due regard to its equality duties.The Department is not responsible for equality issues in relation to local government pension decisions.  NILGOSC is responsible for administering the Local Government Pension Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the progress in updating the mandate of the Utility Regulator; (ii) when legislation to enact the mandate will be ready; and (iii) the principles of the new mandate.
answer_text: The extent to which new or amended frameworks will be needed to support the delivery of the Executive’s ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ Strategy is being assessed by my officials, in conjunction with the Utility Regulator. The Utility Regulator currently operates under an extensive legislative and regulatory framework and is required to exercise its functions in line with a broad range of duties. These include an obligation to exercise its functions in a way best calculated to secure a diverse, viable and environmentally sustainable long-term energy supply, which includes the need to guard against climate change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1826/22-27, (i) why there is no target wait time for an ADHD assessment; and (ii) whether he will introduce a target.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will expand the terms of reference of the Independent Review of Invest NI to include (i) the effectiveness of its complaints procedure; and (ii) the views of complainants using the complaints procedure.
answer_text: The Terms of Reference for the Independent Review of Invest NI have been finalised and work on the Review has now commenced. The Review is entirely independent and I would therefore encourage you to engage directly with Sir Michael Lyons on any issue you believe will be relevant to his assessment of Invest NI’s performance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the findings of the latest report on Prevalence of Autism in School Age Children in Northern Ireland, that (i) the urban population had a statistically significant higher prevalence rate of autism than the rural population; and (ii) males were almost three times more likely to be diagnosed with autism than females.
answer_text: My concerns in relation to autism are well documented and it is evident that considerable work must be undertaken to ensure that children are supported and services are readily accessible to support presenting need across the population.Whilst there is no definitive reason apparent for the disparity, international research (Lauritsen – Urbanicity and Autism Spectrum Disorders) has indicated links to autism and urbanicity. However, this would require extensive research in the context of our population.Within our region there are considered to be a number of contributing factors which include some areas having a higher population of school children and therefore an increased rate of referral and diagnosis and additionally the need for referral criteria for autism assessment to be aligned across the region; work is underway to address this.Comparison in male diagnosis and female diagnosis is highlighted on a regular basis.  The overarching reason for this is considered to be that characteristics of autism may present in a different way in females and they are also more adept at masking characteristics, many will not access or seek assessment or support until adulthood.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action he is taking to expand cross border heath care; and (ii) whether he has had any meetings since May 2022 with his counterparts in the Republic of Ireland to discuss future plans in this area.
answer_text: Cross border health care initiatives are generally advanced through the North South Ministerial Council (NSMC), the Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT) partnership and through local Service Level Agreements between relevant providers in both jurisdictions, where it is demonstrated that there is a clear population health need that could be sustainably met through a collaborative approach and without detriment to current service provision.The NSMC Health Sectoral work programme focuses on Accident and Emergency Planning; Major emergencies; Co-operation on High Technology Equipment; Cancer Research; Health Promotion and; from 2008 onwards, Child Protection.CAWT is the cross-border health and social care partnership for the Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts, my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, the Public Health Agency and the Health Service Executive in the Republic of Ireland.  CAWT seeks to add value to existing health and social care activity by bringing a cross border dimension to on-going collaboration between the health systems in both jurisdictions and accessing EU funding in support of such activities where appropriate.The NSMC has not met since May 2022 as in the absence of a First Minister and deputy First Minister, the statutory requirements to enable the NSMC to meet cannot be fulfilled.  Therefore, the Council is unable to meet at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of NHS dentists, per 100,000 population, in each local council area.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce an annual minimum five per cent passenger growth target for Translink.
answer_text: The Department entered into a new Public Service Agreement (PSA) with Translink effective from 1st April 2022. The PSA includes a performance management framework which sets out the performance standards Translink is required to meet when delivering services. This includes Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and targets in key areas of importance including passenger numbers. The target for passenger numbers for 2022/23 aligns to the projections in Translink’s Corporate Plan 2022-25 of 71m passenger journeys. The Department will review the KPIs and targets on an annual basis and revise and update these where appropriate. Given the continued suppression of passenger numbers following the COVID-19 pandemic, and the uncertainty regarding the potential for future waves of the virus and the long-term recovery of patronage, it would not be feasible at this point to commit to introducing an annual minimum growth target. Instead, at each annual review, a new stretching target for passenger growth will be set for the year ahead which is based on performance over the previous year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what action she is taking to ensure that Relationship and Sexuality Education is LGBTQ+ inclusive; and (ii) whether she is engaging with schools to encourage them to provide up to date Relationship and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: It is important that Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is relevant to all children and young people and that schools provide an environment where they can discuss issues which are important to them.  Each school is required to have a RSE policy and it is the responsibility of the Board of Governors to ensure that a comprehensive programme is delivered.A range of resources is already available to schools to support the teaching of RSE. Further guidance from the Department and the publication of the RSE Progression Framework by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment will issue in due course and will strengthen further this support.  These will remain under review and will be updated as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he last met with the British Dental Association NI; and to detail his engagement with the British Dental Association NI in relation to rising operational costs and a new contract.
answer_text: I last met with the British Dental Association (BDA) NI on 21 October 2021 to discuss a range of business issues affecting General Dental Practitioners.  Senior officials have met regularly with BDA representatives on issues such as Covid-19 response and contract reforms issues.At that meeting I stated my commitment to reforming the current dental contract and followed this with the establishment of the General Dental Services Contract Reform Group in December 2021, to develop options for a new contract. The group, consisting of Departmental officials and BDA representatives, has met on 3 occasions and rising operational costs are part of the discussions. While any new contract will likely require legislative change and take time to implement, this group is keen to, during this process, identify and implement any shorter term but impactful actions that will not require such significant changes, in order to provide better services to patients. More recently, the Permanent Secretary of the Department met with representatives of BDA NI on 16 June 2022. The primary topic of discussion in this meeting was the rising operational costs faced by the industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Hate Crime Advocacy Service.
answer_text: The Department of Justice and PSNI continue to fund the Hate Crime Advocacy Service (HCAS). The Service has moved from a grant funded model to a publicly tendered contract from 1 April 2022, following a procurement exercise through Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD). The contract has been awarded on a three year term plus two optional 12 month extensions. This has strengthened the sustainability of the Service as recommended by Judge Marrinan in his Review of Hate Crime Legislation.The Service is provided on a consortium basis led by Victim Support NI and comprising Leonard Cheshire Disability, the Migrant Centre NI and The Rainbow Project.The Advocacy Service encourages members of the community to report hate and signal incidents/crimes as a victim or witness and supports victims of hate and signal crimes throughout the criminal justice process. In addition, the Service can signpost victims to a range of other support organisations and services.The Service now has its own branding and a new website, Facebook and Twitter page have been developed, to increase reach to victims, which are now operational.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the initial results of the Identification and Referral to Improve Safety (IRIS) programme; and (ii) how and when the programme will be evaluated.
answer_text: There are currently 38 practices signed up to the Identification and Referral to Improve Safety (IRIS) programme across the Belfast, Craigavon and Newry and District GP Federations.  Two Advocate Educators are in place (one from Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid and one from Women’s Aid Armagh Down) and they are working across these areas to deliver the IRIS training. Initial feedback from patients referred through IRIS so far has been positive.  My officials are currently liaising with IRIS colleagues to determine how best (and when) to undertake an evaluation and will continue to work in partnership with Women’s Aid, Nexus NI, Men’s Advisory Project and Victim Support NI who have come together to help deliver this programme in partnership with primary care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a progress report on the adoption of unexplained wealth orders in NI.
answer_text: Ensuring that law enforcement agencies in Northern Ireland have access to the full range of asset recovery powers in line with their counterparts in England, Scotland and Wales is a key priority.  It is essential that the relevant agencies investigate, disrupt, prosecute and bring to justice those involved in serious organised crime. In June 2021, I asked the Home Secretary to commence the outstanding proceeds of crime provisions of the Criminal Finances Act 2017.  This included measures relating to unexplained wealth orders (UWOs) and accompanying interim freezing orders.   UWOs and interim freezing orders are civil investigation powers granted to specified enforcement agencies under the Criminal Finances Act 2017: the National Crime Agency; Her Majesty’s Revenue and Customs; the Financial Conduct Authority; the Director of the Serious Fraud Office; the Director of Public Prosecutions (in relation to England and Wales) and the Director of Public Prosecutions for Northern Ireland (in relation to Northern Ireland).  An application by an enforcement agency for a UWO is a civil process; these orders have the potential to enhance the ability of enforcement agencies to recover the proceeds of crime and tackle money laundering using a wider range of asset recovery powers.  An order requires an individual to set out the nature and extent of their interest in the property in question, and to explain how they obtained that property in cases where their known income does not explain ownership of that property.The powers to bring forward an application for a UWO to the High Court are in place.  Codes of Practice providing guidance on their use is published and training has been provided.  It is my understanding that while the High Court in Northern Ireland has not made a UWO at this time, UWOs may be under consideration in ongoing investigations.  The enforcement authorities with the powers to seek UWOs are operationally independent.  As an operational matter I cannot comment further at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the pilot scheme for a domestic violence court in Belfast.
answer_text: My officials, along with the Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Public Prosecution Service, are continuing to work on the development of a domestic violence court at Laganside Courts focusing on improved case progression, processes and procedures ahead of cases reaching court.  A range of operational practicalities are currently being worked through and my officials are liaising with the Presiding District Judge (Magistrates’ Courts).  All of those involved are committed to ensuring that the changes are effective as soon as possible and can be brought forward at the earliest opportunity.  It is hoped that a pilot will commence later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: I refer you to the response provided to AQW 234/22-27 dated 24 May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is making any preparations to secure and administer an updated Covid booster to protect against recent variants.
answer_text: While I am aware that work is currently ongoing on new variant/ modified vaccines, these still require approval by the MHRA/EMA.  If any of these vaccines gain the necessary approval they will also have to be considered by JCVI, who will recommend if they should be used in the UK and if so, who they should be offered to.  All COVID-19 vaccines are currently procured by the UK Government on behalf of the four UK countries.  Agreements are in place with the manufacturers of the current vaccines to supply their new vaccine should it gain regulatory approval and be recommended for use by JCVI.  If this occurs, the vaccine(s) will be supplied to Northern Ireland under the current arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a Letter of Offer for funding for Phase 3 of the Coleraine to Derry railway line has been received from the Department for Finance.
answer_text: I can confirm that an Outline Business Case (OBC) for the Phase 3 upgrade to the Coleraine to Derry railway line is currently being assessed in line with normal processes. If approved, my Department will be in a position to provide a letter of offer to take forward this important project for the North West.

answer_text: My Department provides funding through the ApprenticeshipsNI (AppsNI) and Higher Level Apprenticeships (HLA) programmes for the delivery of off the job training for apprenticeships at levels 2 to 7 but does not allocate any apprenticeship roles. The apprentices are in employment, with responsibility for their recruitment and selection resting with the employers. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider permitting the interval between first and second doses of the Pfizer Covid vaccine to be reduced to three weeks.
answer_text: While the Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) have approved the use of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine (also known as Comirnaty) in the UK, with an interval of 21 days, the COVID-19 vaccination programme has been guided by the advice and recommendations of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent expert advisory committee.  JCVI consider all the available scientific evidence, both published and unpublished, before offering their advice to the four UK Health Ministers. Based on evidence of a better immune response where longer intervals between doses in the primary schedule are used, JCVI currently recommends a minimum interval of 8 weeks between doses of the COVID-19 vaccines currently in use in the UK for adults.  For those aged under 18 years of age, who are not in a high-risk group, a 12-week interval is recommended. While there are some exceptions to this interval period, such as for those who are about to commence immunosuppressive treatment etc, I am not aware of any plans by JCVI to recommend a reduction to the interval between the primary doses of the COVID-19 vaccines and I will continue to be guided by JCVI advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he is taking to ensure that people with disabilities are included in the workforce and receive adequate support.
answer_text: The Department for Communities leads on the development of the new Disability Strategy and the Disability Employment Strategy which aims to support people with disabilities into employment.  The Department for Communities also provides a range of support for those in employment.  Minister Hargey would be best placed to take further related enquiries.In relation to skills and education, my Department offers a range of practical, financial and other support to those with disabilities across the further and higher education sectors, as well as to those participating on vocational training programmes, to enable them to build the skills and qualifications needed to compete for employment opportunities at all levels.Examples of the type of support include the Disabled Students Allowance for Higher Education students, a focus on disability through the Widening Access and Participation Plans, the Additional Support Fund in Further Education, extended eligibility and flexibilities on the Skills for Life and Work programme, and specialist support through the Disability Support Service contract.  The Department’s Careers Service also offer careers guidance interviews, during which any barriers to training or employment are discussed.  Careers Advisors also participate in Transition Meetings, including significant follow-up work for young people with a disability at key transition points throughout their education and training journey. The recently published Skills Strategy includes a commitment to support those under-represented in the workforce, including those with a disability, to gain appropriate skills and qualifications to enable them to progress towards and into employment.In the context of the workplace, the Labour Relations Agency which is also within my Department:advise and provide guidance to employers and employees on the Disability Discrimination Act’s responsibilities, rights and protections;aim to encourage best practice in others through participation in the ‘Every Customer Counts’ and JAM (Just a Minute) initiatives, which aim to promote accessibility; and,work closely with the Equality Commission to promote the Mental Health Charter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is available to children with a diagnosis of ADHD; and whether he intends to expand those services.
answer_text: Following an ADHD diagnosis, appropriate care and medical intervention is discussed and agreed with the child and their family. In circumstances where a child requires medication following diagnosis, they will remain under the care of a lead professional within CAMHS or Community Paediatrics. HSC Trusts offer a range of additional post-diagnostic ADHD support to children and their families, such as online parental workshops, family support packs, signposting to community or voluntary organisations and post-diagnostic interventions by ADHD Nurse Specialists.Access to integrated assessment and support pathways for people with neuro-developmental presentations, such as ADHD, is currently being considered as part of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Services Framework. These services may not be identified as ADHD specific but will be designed to meet the needs of all children and young people including those with ADHD, and those awaiting an assessment for diagnosis of ADHD.  These pathways will include help and support for parents and families that are identified as in need of emotional health and welling support.  In the longer term, the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, which sets out the strategic direction of mental health services over the next 10 years, commits to continued development of our understanding of specialisms within a general mental health service and this includes services for those with ADHD.  In particular, Action 23 acknowledges there are some individuals who will always need specialist help and support that is often long term.  All practicable help and support will be provided to people in need, keeping in line with the vision of person-centred care and a “no wrong door” approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will take any action to ensure that the PSNI do not use spit and bite guards on people under the age of 18.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my answer to AQW 2050/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the PSNI Equality Impact Assessment on the Use of Spit and Bite Guards, which outlined that 81% of uses were on people with a disability, for her assessment of the PSNI’s decision to make the use of spit and bite guards permanent.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my answer to AQW 2050/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 299/22-27, to detail (i) the response maintenance repairs that have been carried out on the Waterside Triangle as a result of water ingress problems first noticed in 2012/2013; and (ii) the cost of these repairs.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that a considerable number of response maintenance repairs have been carried out at the properties in Waterside Triangle. Unfortunately, it is difficult to ascertain which of the total number of repairs are associated with water ingress as it is dependent on the way the repair is reported to the Housing Executive.  The Housing Executive does not record repairs to its properties under the category of “water ingress”. Some repair requests from tenants may be related to water ingress but are not described as such in their request therefore the Housing Executive do not hold the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will ensure that the PSNI publish the results of a public consultation on the equality impact of spit and bite guards.
answer_text: The use of Spit and Bit Guards is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the NIPB.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.You will, therefore, wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) at what stage in the School Enhancement Programme are (a) Greenhaw Primary School; and (b) Chapel Road Primary School; and (ii) when will work commence in these schools.
answer_text: A design team has been appointed for the School Enhancement Programme project at Greenhaw Primary School. The project has progressed to RIBA Stage 1, with the Technical Feasibility complete. The project business case is currently being developed. Pending approval of the business case, the next milestone for the project is the commencement of RIBA Stage 2 (Concept Design) and the submission of a Planning Application. It is currently anticipated that the project will commence on site in summer 2024, however this is dependent on statutory approvals and budget availability.A design team has also been appointed for the project at Chapel Road Primary School. RIBA Stage 1 was approved in December 2021. The Stage 2 design and cost information have been submitted by the design team and are currently under review in the Department. It is currently anticipated that the project at Chapel Road Primary School will commence on site in summer 2023, however this is dependent on statutory approvals and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will answer AQW 300/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 300/22-27 was answered on 23rd June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will answer AQW 302/22-27 and AQW 379/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 302/22-27 was answered on 23rd June 2022.	AQW 379/22-27 was answered on 28th June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Schools' Entrance Assessment Group will be able to produce practice papers for the new single transfer test for Primary Six pupils in 2022/2023.
answer_text: The arrangements for the Transfer Tests from the 2023/24 school year are a matter for the test provider, the Schools' Entrance Assessment Group.  I am advised that, as the test is still being developed, practice papers will not be available to pupils.  However, I have also been advised the test will focus on the literacy and numeracy content of the curriculum at Key Stage 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many housing applicants have been placed in temporary accommodation that is outside of their area of choice in each of the last ten years, broken down by council area.
answer_text: The Housing Executive, has provided this information.this information is not available as gathering it would require manually considering each Homelessness placement for the last ten years, which would incur disproportionate cost.When assessing the suitability of the temporary accommodation, relevant factors to be considered include size and location of the property; personal circumstances of the household such as affordability, proximity to hospitals, schools, care services and family and the accessibility of public transport. This is not a prescriptive list that encompasses suitability as each applicant’s circumstances will be different and Housing Solutions staff take their own view on the evidence available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to bring forward a renewed maternity strategy.
answer_text: In March 2020, I gave approval for officials to commence a clinically-led review of Maternity and Neonatal Services, following which an updated Maternity Strategy would be developed. Unfortunately, during the pandemic resources had to be redirected and the review was subsequently paused.  I recognise the importance of developing a new strategy and therefore it is my intention to recommence work on a new Maternity Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will take on clinics that mislead clients into believing they provide abortion services, that provide inaccurate information on pregnancy and abortion, or that refer people for unnecessary scans.
answer_text: The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is the independent body with responsibility for the registration and regulation of establishments and agencies that fall within the requirements of the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003.  RQIA would review such clinics before making a determination as to whether they are required to be registered under the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005.  For those establishments and agencies that are required to be registered with the RQIA they will be subject to inspections based on care standards which will ensure that both the public and the service providers know what quality of services is expected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1386/22-27, for his assessment of the impact that the absence of a public health awareness campaign on local abortion service availability is having on patient access to services.
answer_text: It would not be possible to assess the impact of the absence of a public health awareness campaign.  At this stage it has not been determined whether information about access to local abortion services should be provided in the form of a public health awareness campaign or through other means.  What is important is ensuring that accurate information about services and how to access them is available through official sources.  Where there is evidence indicating the need for or the potential benefits of an awareness campaign, my Department will give this due consideration as part of ongoing planning for the commissioning of abortion services, which I referred to in my response to AQW 1386/22-27.  In the meantime, as I previously advised, Health and Social Care providers in Northern Ireland provide information about their services through their websites which is always available to the public and to those who may require access to them, including in relation to abortion services. I would, of course, discourage anyone from accessing medical advice or treatment from any unofficial or unregulated source.  I would therefore repeat my previous response that women residing in Northern Ireland who require pregnancy advice should contact their GP or local Family Planning Services in the first instance, and those requiring advice, counselling or access to abortion services should contact the central access point provided by the British Pregnancy Advice Service (BPAS).  Contact details are provided on Health and Social Care Trust and on BPAS websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why the Housing Executive has suspended their discretionary grants schemes to new applicants; (ii) when these schemes are due to reopen; and (iii) whether she will provide financial support to help avoid any delays in the schemes reopening.
answer_text: The three discretionary private sector grants administered by the Housing Executive are awarded in exceptional circumstances and where the applicant meets the relevant eligibility criteria which can be found here:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Housing-Help/Grants/Types-of-grants-available.In the ongoing absence of an Executive and an agreed 2022-23 Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive's Home Repair Assistance Grant and Repair Grant have the necessary budget to accept new applicants.
answer_text: The continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget is having serious consequences across a wide range of areas. It is critical that the Executive is re-established and a budget is agreed. My Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope. The Housing Executive is currently unable to process further applications to approval stage unless the work is required to enable disabled facilities adaptations to be installed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the Office for National Statistics report on COVID-19 Schools Infection Survey, England: long COVID and mental health, March 2022; and (ii) whether she will introduce HEPA air filtration units in all classrooms to help reduce the risk of COVID-19 infection and reduce the number of school children developing Long COVID.
answer_text: The report shows that COVID-19 continues to have a negative impact on the health and wellbeing of children and young people in Northern Ireland. My officials are working collaboratively with the Department of Health on the development of the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  Launched in February 2021, the Framework comprises a range of initiatives aimed at delivering better mental health outcomes for children and young people, and will be especially important at this time given the impact of the pandemic. The Department of Health have confirmed that CAMHS has faced significant pressures in recent years as a result of increased demand for services, resource constraints and the COVID-19 pandemic. The Department of Health continues to work collaboratively with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (formerly known as the HSCB) and the HSC Trusts to manage the pressures on CAMHS and to put in place measures to increase capacity and improve performance. This includes a formal review of waiting lists on a weekly basis, ongoing recruitment campaigns, providing initial assessments via telephone, zoom or face to face, and exploring greater involvement of the community and voluntary sector.In 2021, an extra £500k was agreed to provide additional support to CAMHS to increase specialist staff across all Trusts and to implement new initiatives to support young people who are affected by eating disorders.As well as additional financial support, a number of structural improvements have been recently implemented across CAMHS to aid greater collaboration between services providers.  For example, the establishment of the Managed Care Network (MCN) for acute CAMHS.  The MCN enables clinical expertise to be shared across the region, particularly in response to children and young people in crisis, and ensure consistency of treatment and care.  It is anticipated that the MCN will be well placed to drive progress in CAMHS service development, planning and strategic priorities for the future.The Department of Health has allocated £1.5m recurrently from 2021/22 for the development of CAMHS Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools (EWTS). The new EWTS service aims to promote the emotional wellbeing of children, build individual and collective resilience and most critically, provide intervention at the earliest opportunity.  It is anticipated EWTS service delivery will commence later this year.While it is anticipated the actions currently being taken will help to address existing pressures within services, the new Mental Health Strategy, published on 29 June 2021, includes a number of key strategic actions to achieve longer term improvements in CAMHS, including increasing investment in CAMHS to 10% of the overall mental health budget.The most effective mitigating measure in relation to Covid-19 is natural ventilation and my Department and the Education Authority are supporting schools in prioritising the effective impleme

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will introduce a public health awareness campaign to provide information on the availability of local abortion services; and for his assessment of the impact that the current absence of such a campaign is having on access to abortion services.
answer_text: The ongoing planning for the commissioning of abortion services will include consideration of how providers make information available and accessible to those requiring these services.In the meantime, women residing in NI who require advice, counselling or access to abortion services, including early medical abortion services currently available within Northern Ireland or other services currently being provided in England, should contact the central access point provided by the British Pregnancy Advice Service (BPAS). Contact details are provided on Health and Social Care Trust and on BPAS websites. Women can also consult their GPs who can advise and signpost them to the appropriate information sources in accordance with their healthcare requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the current structure of direct payments for carers and increase the rates available.
answer_text: Direct Payment funding is provided in lieu of statutory provided services and is calculated on the basis of individual assessed need. Direct Payment hourly rates are adjusted each April to reflect changes such as increases to the National Minimum Wage.In November last year I announced a £23m support package which was primarily designed as a response to the pressures facing the domiciliary care and care home workforces. There has been positive feedback on how this additional funding has helped to stabilise the sector during a very challenging period. While this funding package does not extend to Direct Payments, I am considering if there would be scope to address direct payment rates which are an extremely important part of our care system, particularly in allowing for a person centred approach, whilst also ensuring that an individual’s assessed social care needs are fulfilled. As you are aware however, we face and are likely to continue to face, an extremely challenging fiscal environment and the absence of an agreed three year budget makes this position even more challenging.A consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care is currently ongoing to consider the longer term strategic approach. The consultation document includes a number of proposals in relation to Self-directed support and Direct Payments and I look forward to seeing views on these proposals as the consultation proceeds.
