question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to carry out a study on how many patients develop additional health complaints in the time waiting for a first outpatient appointment and the resulting annual costs to his Department.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the physical, mental and financial tolls taken on our population by long periods spent on a waiting list. However, my Department currently has no plans to commission such a study.In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists. The Framework sets out a five year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future.There are a number of initiatives in the Framework that are already well underway:The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput. In May 2022, 600 patients had already been treated already this financial year, in addition to the 6,240 patients treated last year at these clinics. The Day Procedure Centre (DPC) at Lagan Valley Hospital (LVH) is working extremely well, supporting a range of specialties from across the region to undertake the most urgent clinical cases. Approximately 3,000 patients have been treated at the Regional Day Procedure Centre at LVH, and approximately 5,000 patients have received endoscopy procedures at the centre. This is additional regional capacity to help reduce long waits.Omagh Hospital has also been set up as a Regional Day Procedure Centre and has been provided with additional funding to enhance capacity for Elective Care Services.Allowing a private Independent Sector provider to use vacant theatres in the South West Acute Hospital in Enniskillen has already seen hundreds of patients on waiting lists for hip replacement surgery being treated. This service provides good patient outcomes with over 67% of patients discharged from hospital on day of surgery and the vast reminder discharged the day after.Theatre lists for paediatric general surgery day procedures have been created or reinstated across the region and this will allow children to be treated in a more timely manner with the intention to tackling the backlog of children waiting.Opportunities to deliver more elective surgery on a regional basis are currently being explored in other units across the Health and Social Care (HSC) service. Funding has been maintained for additional waiting list activity at levels set out in the Elective Care Framework. This involves £46m in the first two quarters of 2022/23, covering the period April to September. I also recently announced further recurrent funding in excessive of £16m to further enhance capacity for our elective care services. This funding is for further developments at Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital.  It will also fund developments at Musgrave Park Hospital Duke of Connaught as an orthopaedic day procedure centre and will fund a regional urology stone services at Craigavon Area Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is taking to support businesses that are experiencing increases in their running costs as a result of the rise in energy prices.
answer_text: There is no price regulation for business consumers in electricity, those who use heating oil or LPG and all, bar the smallest businesses, who use natural gas.Price regulation remains in place for the largest electricity domestic supplier in Northern Ireland, Power NI, for SSE Airtricity in the Greater Belfast and West gas supply areas and firmus in the ‘ten towns’. With a larger number of suppliers for business consumers, supply competition sets prices in the market.  All consumers, including business customers, have the right to switch energy supplier and are protected in their dealings with energy companies through requirements in all energy supply licences.  Invest NI delivers a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable NI businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies. This includes: fully funded Technical Consultancy; a Resource Matching Service; and, the Resource Efficiency Capital Grant. This support helps businesses to optimise and future proof their operations by implementing green efficiencies to achieve carbon savings and cost savings to mitigate the impact of the rise in energy costs. One of the key objectives of the ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’, the Energy Strategy published on 16th December 2021, is to ensure that all businesses have access to essential and affordable energy to facilitate their competitiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider rates relief for businesses that have been impacted by rising energy costs.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive I cannot bring forward any new rate reliefs for businesses.As the Member is aware, the Executive provided considerable support for businesses since returning in January 2020, including the provision of over £560 million in rate relief to eligible businesses during the pandemic.  This rating year (2022/23) saw businesses provided with a further rate holiday package. This all builds on the critical assistance provided to the business sector in the form of the 18% reduction in the regional rate I implemented in 2020, and have frozen in the two years since.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will review the landlord allowance on rates.
answer_text: Generally speaking, it is the occupier of any property that is charged rates, however there is mandatory liability for landlords where the Capital Value of the property does not exceed £150,000.  		Landlords who pay rates by a set time receive a 10% discount on the rate bill for each property. Landlords are in a better position than LPS to collect the rates in what is often a transient sector of the housing market. This policy is primarily designed to increase rate collection with the discount intended to compensate landlords for the work involved in collecting the rates from their tenants and the potential risk of default.  It is therefore beneficial to both LPS and landlords. 		However, the appropriate level of discount was consulted on in the 2016 Rates Rethink and may be considered when an Executive is in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will introduce a rate relief for domestic households, that are not eligible for benefits, to help with the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: For the third year in a row the domestic regional rate has been frozen in order to keep rates as low as possible for households.The cost of living crisis needs to be tackled by all Executive Departments as a whole but without an Executive, no new cross-cutting fiscal policies can be introduced nor can the £435 million of available funding be allocated. This funding could undoubtedly be used to help families, workers and businesses with the cost of living and to support public services, particularly our health service. I hope an Executive can be reformed as quickly as possible to enable us to allocate these funds.

answer_text: The Inter-Departmental Business Register contains information on businesses registered for Value Added Tax (VAT) or Pay As You Earn (PAYE). question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of training places on the Assured Skills programme which were requested by employers and were not approved in (i) each of the last three financial years; and (ii) the current financial year, broken down by council area.
answer_text: The Department does not retain records of the number of training places requested and not approved, instead, where a company did not avail of the Assured Skills programme, it will have been deemed that the Programme was not a suitable intervention for the company at that time. The Programme has not, where conditions have been met and budget is available, turned down any prospective employer for Assured Skills, but the Programme is not always the best skills solution for companies.As Northern Ireland’s main economic development organisation, Invest Northern Ireland (NI) works to create and grow business in NI by increasing investment, through attracting Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), and helping NI companies grow their business.When a company is interested in creating jobs in NI, Invest NI makes introductions to the Assured Skills team who, where appropriate and through liaison with our training provider(s), aim to provide the right skills solution through delivery of industry relevant, pre-employment training content, to upskill individuals and help them compete for guaranteed job vacancies in new FDI companies and locally expanding businesses.	Both DfE and Invest NI have developed complementary roles supporting both companies and individuals in the NI economy, working together to increase competitiveness, increase productivity, and improve the skills base of the NI workforce. This approach is reflected in the Assured Skills programme, with DfE and Invest NI working together throughout the negotiation period with companies, to establish whether the Programme can fulfil the skills requirements for their prospective workforce, and whether the project fulfils the criteria of the Assured Skills programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what criteria is used for employers' needs to be met through the Assured Skills programme.
answer_text: The Assured Skills pre-employment training programme offers new and expanding Invest NI companies the opportunity to work closely with the Department for the Economy (DfE), Invest NI and the Further and Higher Education sector to recruit high quality employees.There are two types of Assured Skills academies available: academies that are supported by a single company (must be an Invest NI client), that is recruiting to fill at least two academies of 20 people; and, for companies growing on a smaller scale, the Collaborative Academy model where a group of companies (two or more, with at least one being an Invest NI client) with guaranteed job vacancies can identify a skills need that can be successfully delivered through the Academy model.  In return, participating companies must guarantee an interview for those who successfully complete the Academy.Through extensive discussions with the participating company and the local Further Education (FE) colleges and Higher Education (HE) institutions, training is developed to meet the skills in demand, whilst ensuring the skills delivered are transferable, within the industry more widely. The companies play a key role in the development of the training content, and the selection process for places on the Assured Skills academies. Once the training plan and content has been agreed, the Department will lead, manage, and cover the costs associated with the recruitment process, development, and delivery of the Academy but no financial support is paid to the companies involved in the Academy.The eligibility criteria for Academy applicants are agreed with the participating companies, in consultation with the college and the Assured Skills team. In response to industry demand many academies are aimed at graduate level, with applicants requiring a 2:2 degree in any discipline, however, some companies accept Level 5 (e.g. HND), or no academic requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he is taking to assist the aviation sector with the increasing numbers of (i) queues at airports; (ii) missed flights; and (iii) cancelled flights, coming into the summer.
answer_text: From the Office of the Minister 	Gordon Lyons MLA								As Minister for the Economy my responsibility is with maintaining and enhancing Northern Ireland’s air connectivity, both domestically and internationally. In doing so, I fully recognise that civil aviation is a reserved matter for the UK Department for Transport (DfT). The Secretary of State for Transport has pointed out that this is a private sector industry and responsibility for cancelled flights and lengthy queues lies with the airlines and airports.  They are commercially responsible for manging their businesses and providing their customers with an appropriate and reasonable service.Not all operators have been affected by the issues in the same way, and I understand the pressures which the airlines and operators are working under.  However, the scenes we have witnessed at airports are distressing, with holidaymakers missing out on their first trips abroad after the pandemic. I welcome the fact that the UK Government is working closely with the sector to ensure that there will be no repeat of these scenes this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many applications have been received to transfer between schools for pupils (i) starting Year 9 in September 2022; and (ii) moving from Year 8 to Year 9 in the last five years, broken down by council area.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are plans to increase Sure Start provision.
answer_text: The Sure Start programme is delivered via 38 Sure Start projects, in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland (NI), as measured by the NI Multiple Deprivation Measure (NIMDM) 2010; research indicates this is where targeted early intervention can be most effective. Provision is universal within the Sure Start catchment areas, therefore all children under four (and their families) in these areas, can access services. Increased Department of Education (DE) funding of £1.56m in 2021/22 and 2022/23 is facilitating  temporary extension of services to an additional 22 Super Output Areas falling within the 25% most disadvantaged areas when applying the latest (2017) NIMDM.To ensure optimum early intervention, DE will take account of the findings of the ongoing Independent Review of Targeted Early Years Interventions (including Sure Start and its geographical scope), which is expected to complete by autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what immediate steps he intends to take to address the shortage of HRT products; and whether he will consider permitting pharmacists to provide alternative medication without the need for a prescription, as is the case in England.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in England has responsibility for all UK medicine supplies and is working directly with suppliers on this issue. My Department continues to work closely with DHSC on supply issues affecting Northern Ireland and with suppliers here to monitor the situation closely.  I am a standing member of the UK Menopause Taskforce which brings together Government MPs and Health Ministers from each of the Devolved Administrations and professional representatives with expertise in this area. The shared aim of that Forum is to strengthen coordination, raise awareness and improve care and support for women across all of our Jurisdictions.  In addition, DHSC recently established a new HRT Taskforce, Chaired by Madelaine McTernan, with the specific remit of addressing HRT supply chain issues.  I have already formally requested close working with the Devolved Administrations, through existing structures, to help drive the HRT Taskforce agenda forward.In line with similar steps in England, my Department recently introduced Serious Shortage Protocols (SSPs) in NI to manage and control the supply of a number of HRT products in the interim until these wider supply issues can be fully resolved. The SSPs relate to five commonly used HRT products that are experiencing supply chain pressures. They allow the community pharmacist to substitute a product with an alternative, with the patient’s agreement, or to limit the number of packs supplied.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the British Government's Department for Education's evaluation of the Pause project; (ii) for her assessment of its continued and expanded use in Northern Ireland; and (iii) whether her Department will provide assistance for the project to be continued and expanded.
answer_text: In December 2018, the Pause Project was introduced on a pilot basis in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust. The pilot was subsequently extended to the Western and Belfast Trust areas.  It’s continued and expanded use, including any decisions regarding funding, would be a matter for the Minister of Health. I welcome the multi-agency approach used by the Project and officials in my Department and in the Housing Executive are happy to provide advice in relation to housing matters if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the opportunities for community asset transfers in the Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: I believe that there is significant potential for community asset transfer in all local government areas, including Derry City and Strabane District Council.  My Department continues to support the implementation of the Executive’s CAT policy in partnership with the Development Trusts NI.  Current CAT opportunities are promoted on the DTNI website: 	https://www.dtni.org.uk/asset-transfer/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) priorities; and (ii) deficiencies, in the Derry and Strabane District Council area, that will inform the Labour Market Partnership programme.
answer_text: My Department is leading the development of the Labour Market Partnership initiative through a collaborative co-design process with input from all 11 local councils.A Local Labour Market Partnership has been established in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area since 2021-22. The Partnership has recently developed a 2022-23 Action Plan, evidenced by statistical information and informed by local knowledge.  The Derry and Strabane LMP 2022-23 Action Plan received approval from the Regional LMP in March 2022 and my Department has issued a funding offer in connection to the proposals detailed within the Action Plan.  I would encourage the member to contact 028 7125 3253 or email info@derrystrabane.com to discuss the finer details of their LMP action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether legacy tenants can apply to the Right to Buy scheme if they have been a lead tenant for less than five years but have lived in the property for longer than five years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that under the statutory House Sales Scheme, the right to buy only arises when the tenant has been a secure tenant of the Housing Executive or another qualifying landlord listed in the Scheme (e.g. a registered housing association) for a period of not less than 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he intends to take to alleviate the financial crisis in the travel agency sector.
answer_text: I welcome the fact that world travel markets have re-opened, and confidence has returned in respect of both inbound and outbound tourism. I continue to work with our tourism bodies to encourage more people to visit Northern Ireland, and with local airports seeking to attract new routes for domestic and international travellers

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the results of fire safety assessments in the Triangle estate in Derry's Waterside in the last five years.
answer_text: I refer you to the response issued for AQW 28077/17-22. The Housing Executive has advised us that the answer to AQW 28077/17-22 remains unchanged.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what expenditure has been undertaken on repairs and refurbishments to properties in the Triangle estate in Derry's Waterside in each of the last ten years, broken down by (i) leaseholders; and (ii) tenants.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that there have been three planned schemes in the last ten years that involved properties that are in the Waterside Triangle scheme:A Heating scheme in 2012/13 that included 27 Housing Executive tenanted properties at an estimated cost of £122,450.A Flat Entrance Door scheme in 2013/14 that included 26 properties (18 Housing Executive tenanted properties and 8 leasehold properties) at an estimated cost of £55,919.  The latter comprises an estimated cost of £38,713 for the works to tenants’ properties and £17,206 to leaseholders’ properties.  The leaseholders were not charged for this work. A Kitchen scheme in 2016/17 that included 33 Housing Executive tenanted properties at an estimated cost of £141,649.It should be noted that the costs provided are estimates - because the Housing Executive does not have costs for individual properties - and are based on applying the proportion of Waterside Triangle properties in the schemes to the schemes’ total costs.The Housing Executive does not hold the expenditure figures for repairs during this time period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Northern Ireland Housing Executive became aware of water ingress at properties in the Triangle estate in Derry's Waterside.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has established from Response Maintenance records that it first noticed water ingress problems at the properties in the Triangle in approximately 2012/13.  The Housing Executive has continued to carry out response maintenance repairs throughout this time and is still continuing to carry out repairs where necessary while the improvement scheme is finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why the agreed National Joint Council award for Housing Executive staff has not been signed off; and whether she will she ensure that this is signed off immediately.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why the outcome of the April 2021 Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme Future of the Scheme consultation has not been published; and when he plans to publish it.
answer_text: The consultation on options for the future of the Northern Ireland Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme closed on 9 April 2021. My Department has analysed the 427 responses received. I am keen that the report of the consultation be comprehensive, indicating how responses were considered in the formulation of policy on the way forward. This requires a decision on the next steps by the Northern Ireland Executive. It is regrettable that several draft Executive papers circulated on this matter were not included on an Executive Committee agenda. I hope that a new Executive will be in a position to consider the future of the Scheme at the earliest opportunity. A comprehensive report of the consultation will be published after a decision on the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30797/17-22, whether funding will be allocated to secure the future of the Pause Project.

To ask the Minister of Education (i) why children with a Statement of Special Education Needs will not receive the outcome of their post-primary application from the Education Authority at the same time as their peers; (ii) whether the 10th June deadline for notification of outcomes can be brought forward; and (iii) for her assessment of the impact that this delay will have on the emotional well-being of those pupils.
answer_text: The transfer process for Primary 7 children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) is a statutory process managed entirely separately to ensure that each child receives a suitable placement that will support and cater to their individual needs.  Education Authority (EA) SEN Link Officers work closely with schools, parents/carers and educational psychologists to review the individual needs of each child on a case by case basis and families are informed once a school placement has been finalised, rather than on a fixed date.  Many children with a statement of SEN had already been informed of their placement for year 8 in advance of their peers.   The EA SEN Team is working hard to confirm the remainder of Year 8 places that best meet the children’s needs, as quickly as possibleThe 10th June is not a deadline for notification of outcomes, SEN Link Officers will contact parents to provide an update on progress where a placement has not yet been secured. 	I recognise that that change or fear of change is a stress factor for many children with SEN and their families, at this time. The emotional health and wellbeing of the children continues to be a priority for my Department and a range of supports are available through the ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ including Healthy Happy Minds, a counselling and therapeutic service for primary school pupils. Parents of children with a statement of SEN who have any concerns are encouraged to contact their Link Officer directly or the SEN Helpline which is open Monday – Friday from 9am – 5pm on 028 9598 5960.  Further information is available on the EA website: www.eani.org.uk/parents/special-educational-needs-sen/sen-contact-details

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to reduce waiting times for an endometriosis diagnosis.
answer_text: Patients with Endometriosis requiring diagnosis, treatment or surgery are included within the general Gynaecology lists with the appropriate priority coding.  As you will be aware I was able to recently confirm that, despite ongoing budgetary uncertainty, I have been able to allocate £46m for additional waiting list activity for the first six months of 2022/23.This additional funding involves creating additional in-house health service capacity over and above normal day to day work, as well as paying independent sector providers to assess and treat patients who are on waiting lists.  Trusts are currently drawing up their plans as to what can be achievedI would like to reassure you that I am very conscious of the pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care, including Gynaecology/Endometriosis.As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources that I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action his Department is taking to address the waiting list in endometriosis treatment and surgery; and (ii) whether he will consider cross border and GB referrals to alleviate wait times.
answer_text: As you are aware HSC is in the process of rebuilding services.  A key priority for all Trusts, including the Belfast Trust, is to maximise all elective capacity so as to ensure that as many patients as possible are seen and treated in an environment that is safe for both patients and staff. I have recently confirmed that, despite ongoing budgetary uncertainty, I have been able to allocate £46m for additional waiting list activity for the first six months of 2022/23. This is in addition to the £90m we allocated last year that delivered over 216,000 patient contacts by the end of February. This included 35,000 new outpatient appointments, 120,000 diagnostic appointments and 13,000 in-patient day case treatment.Patients with Endometriosis requiring treatment or surgery are included within the general Gynaecology lists with the appropriate priority coding.  The additional funding will enable Trusts both to optimise in-house capacity and to enable them to access additional capacity within the independent sector where it safe and appropriate to do so. There are currently no plans to send endometriosis patients to GB however local IS and cross border options will be considered as part of this new allocation.Whilst the additional £46m allocation will help to minimise waits in the short term, the longer-term solution lies in the implementation of the Elective Care Framework (2021).I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.  Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans she has to introduce mandatory and age appropriate menstrual wellbeing education.
answer_text: The statutory curriculum already provides schools with the flexibility to educate young people about menstrual wellbeing.  For example, in primary schools at Key Stage 2, pupils learn about how the body grows and develops and the physical and emotional changes that take place during puberty.  In post-primary schools at Key Stage 3, pupils learn that menstruation is a normal and natural part of a girl’s physical development and have opportunities to explore the practical challenges of menstruation including the importance of access to period products and personal hygiene as well as issues such as pain, fear and embarrassment.To support schools in the delivery of the curriculum, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) develops and produces curricular guidance and teaching support materials.  CCEA has produced a range of guidance and resources for schools about personal development, including menstrual wellbeing, which are available on its Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) Hub.  In December 2020, the Executive introduced a pilot scheme to address period poverty in schools to bring practice here into line with the rest of the UK.  The pilot was launched in schools in September 2021 and will continue to June 2024, subject to positive evaluation and available funding each year.  As part of this, CCEA was tasked to help promote the scheme and embed menstrual wellbeing within existing RSE curriculum materials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to secure an endometriosis specialist in Health and Social Care Trusts, in line with NICE guidance.
answer_text: The need to improve provision of endometriosis services in Northern Ireland is acknowledged.Conventional gynaecological medical and surgical treatments are not always successful in achieving improvements in quality of life for these patients and the input of members of a dedicated multidisciplinary team is often necessary.National criteria (produced by the British Society of Gynaecological Endoscopy [BSGE]; www.bsge.org.uk) now exist on the standards of service and workload required to undertake surgical excision of advanced endometriosis and this is driving the establishment of endometriosis centres where such work can be undertaken by specialist multidisciplinary teams.  Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have developed an investment proposal to establish a Level IV Regional Endometriosis service which would be accredited by BSGE (British Society of Gynaecological Endoscopy).  This development will require significant investment to support the necessary multi-disciplinary inputs including access to pain, psychosexual services and specialist gynae physiotherapists. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there was any underspend from the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme; and whether the scheme will be reopened.
answer_text: I am fully committed to supporting people in need. I would like this scheme to reopen and I have been assured by the Finance Minister there is money available, however this requires Executive approval. I am deeply frustrated that the absence of a functioning Executive prevents support for this essential scheme.The Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme was operated by Bryson Care, an arm of the Bryson Charitable Group. My Department made a contribution of £2 million which was fully spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the rationale for the decision to close Ballougry Primary School in Foyle; (ii) whether she will reconsider this decision; and (iii) what will happen to the pupils of the school if the closure goes ahead.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) are currently conducting a statutory pre-publication consultation regarding future educational provision at Ballougry Primary School (PS) which runs until 29 June 2022.  My Department becomes involved at the point a Development Proposal (DP) is published.  This triggers a statutory two month objection period.  During this two month period anyone is able to submit their views on the published DP directly to my Department. At the end of the statutory objection period, Departmental officials will gather and assess all the relevant information and evidence and will present a submission to me for my ultimate decision.
